actions.thought: UTG in a 6-max game with K♠9♦ (K9o) is too weak to open. From early position I need a relatively strong, non-dominated range; K9o is frequently dominated by better kings and broadways and has poor postflop playability out of position against five players to act. Calling 100 to see a flop would put me out of position with a marginal hand that rarely realizes equity, and a raise here would be a thin play with a weak holding. I’ll fold and preserve my 100BB stack for a better spot.

KJs is a premium suited broadway hand - definitely raising here in middle position. Standard open to 250 (2.5x) to build the pot and narrow the field. Good hand to play postflop with position on the blinds if called.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Seven suited (Ad 7d), a hand that plays well in position but is slightly marginal against an early-middle position raise. Claude raised to 2.5x BB from Middle Position. A7s is a solid candidate for a call to see a flop in position, or a 3-bet bluff. Given it's the very first hand of the tournament, I will opt to call and see a flop to gauge the table dynamics and Claude's playing style without bloating the pot too early with a marginal hand.

Js9c is a marginal offsuit hand but plays much better in position. With a middle-position open and a cutoff flat, a 3-bet would bloat the pot with a hand that’s often dominated when called (AJ/KJ/QJ, JT, bigger pairs), and it’s less likely to get folds given the caller. Taking the good price and realizing equity on the button is the highest-EV line here; we can apply pressure postflop on favorable textures.

Preflop in SB with 56o, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability OOP. Facing a raise from MP and two callers (CO and BTN), making it 4-way if I call. Pot odds are about 4.5:1 (200 to call into 900), but equity realization is low multiway with dominated potential and reverse implied odds. Deep stacks don't help much as 56o needs to flop strong draws or pairs to realize equity, which is unlikely heads-up let alone multiway. BB still to act. Standard fold to avoid difficult spots OOP.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 250 from Middle Position (Claude) and two callers (Flash and FiveTwo). The pot is 900, and I need to call 150. My pot odds are roughly 14.3% (150/1050). While the price is very attractive, my hand (Jc 6s) is extremely weak (bottom 15% range) and plays poorly multi-way out of position. Reverse implied odds are a concern as I can easily be dominated on Jack-high boards, and a pair of sixes is rarely good at showdown. Despite the good odds, the realization of equity with J6o in a 4-way pot is too low to justify a call.

Flop came A-2-3 with two clubs. I completely whiffed - no pair, no flush draw (I have hearts), and my overcards are dominated by the Ace. With two callers behind me who likely called with Ax hands or pocket pairs, c-betting into this board is risky. Best to check and control pot size with my air.

I have top pair with a 7 kicker on an Ace-high board with straight and flush draws. The pre-flop raiser (Claude) has checked to me. While I could bet for value, checking back here is a solid way to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand, especially with another player (FiveTwo) still to act behind me. If FiveTwo also checks, I get a free turn card. If he bets, I can evaluate based on the size and Claude's response. This keeps the pot manageable in the early stages of the tournament.

Both opponents checked on an A-high, dynamic flop (flush draws and wheel draws available). In a multiway pot, many strong Ax and big draws often bet for value/protection, so the double-check caps their ranges somewhat and gives us a good spot to stab from the button. Js9c has little showdown value here, but a small c-bet can fold out a lot of whiffed broadways and deny equity while keeping our risk low versus a check-raise. If we face significant resistance, we can comfortably give up.

I have KhJh on As-2c-3c board. Complete miss - no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw. FiveTwo's 300 bet into 750 is likely value with an Ace or a draw. I'm getting 4:1 odds but only have about 12% equity to pair up (6 outs), and even hitting a K or J I'd still lose to any Ax. Clear fold.

I have top pair on an Ace-high board. While my kicker is relatively weak (7), I cannot fold to a 1/3 pot-sized bet after checking the flop. FiveTwo could be betting a wide range of hands including flush draws, straight draws, or weaker Aces. Raising would likely only get called by better hands and fold out the bluffs I want to keep in. I will call the 300 to see a turn and re-evaluate, keeping the pot manageable with a medium-strength top pair.

On the turn, I have improved to top two pair (Aces and Sevens). The board is quite draw-heavy with both club and spade flush draws possible, as well as a straight draw (4-5 already makes a straight). Since FiveTwo was the aggressor on the flop, I will check to him again to see if he continues his story. This allows him to bluff or bet with a draw or a weaker Ace. If he bets, I plan to check-raise to extract value and protect my hand against the numerous draws on the board, as my hand is currently very strong but vulnerable to many river cards.

We’re on a pure bluff with essentially no equity (no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw). After our small flop stab gets called, Flash’s range is now weighted toward Ax, pocket pairs, 2x/3x, and club-draw type hands that generally continue versus a second barrel. The 7s is a fairly blank/negative card for our barreling story (it doesn’t improve our perceived value range much, but can improve his continues like A7/77/76s), so firing again will run into too many stubborn hands and burns chips. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our small chance to improve to a J/9 on the river, and lets us reassess versus a river lead.

I have top two pair (Aces and Sevens) on a board of As 2c 3c 7s Th. The turn went check-check after FiveTwo bet the flop, which suggests he might have a marginal Ace or a draw that he didn't want to semi-bluff again. The river Ten is a relatively safe card, though it does mean AT and TT would now beat me. I am betting for value here, targeting a weaker Ace (like A4, A5, A6, A8, or A9) that might check behind if I check, but will likely call a reasonable value bet. A bet of 800 into the 1500 pot is a standard size to extract value from those hands.

We have pure air (J-high) with essentially no showdown value. Flash’s line (check-call flop, check turn, then 800 into 1500 on the river) is heavily weighted to value hands that didn’t need protection on the turn: Ax, some 7x that improved/decided to go for it now, or pocket pairs turning into a thin/value-block bet. There are missed club draws and some gutshots in his range, but not enough to justify a call needing ~26% equity, and we don’t block his value while only lightly blocking a few bluffs with 9c. Turning this into a raise bluff is also poor: his continuing range versus a raise is mostly Ax+, which won’t fold often at this stack depth. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack.

Pocket 3s from UTG. Small pocket pair with set-mining value at ~97BB effective stacks. Standard open to 2.5x to see a flop cheaply and try to hit a set. If I face significant 3-bet action, I can fold. The implied odds make this playable.

I am holding Jack of Hearts and Six of Diamonds in Middle Position. Claude has opened with a raise to 250 from Under the Gun (UTG). J6 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor equity against an UTG opening range, which usually consists of strong pairs and high cards. There is no strategic reason to call or raise here; folding is the standard and most profitable play to preserve my stack.

UTG opened to 2.5bb in a 6-max game, which is typically a fairly strong, condensed range. T5 offsuit is heavily dominated (by AT/KTs/QTs, etc.), plays poorly postflop, and doesn’t realize equity well in position versus an early-position raiser. With deep stacks there’s no incentive to defend this hand; a 3-bet bluff is also too loose with this combo. Best decision is to fold and preserve stack.

58o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against an UTG raiser's range. Even on the button with position, it's not worth calling a 2.5x raise early in the tournament with deep stacks. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

Facing a 2.5x raise from UTG while in the Small Blind with Ac6h. A6o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against a strong UTG range. It suffers significantly from reverse implied odds (being dominated by better Aces). Although the pot odds are decent, the equity realizability is low. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

UTG opened to 250 (2.5x). I’m in the BB with T♥8♣ and ~99 BB effective. The pot is 400; calling 150 more (250 total) gives ~27% pot odds (150/550) — a cheap price to see a flop with a hand that has some showdown equity and backdoor play. 3-betting out of position with offsuit T8 is unattractive (will bloat the pot and leave me OOP with a marginal holding), while folding is too tight given the price and opponent’s raise size. I’ll call to see the flop and then play cautiously — fold to strong aggression on unfavorable boards, continue when I connect or can apply pressure.

I have Th8c and the flop is 9h Js 7d — I’ve made a straight (7-8-9-10-J) using both hole cards. This is a very strong hand versus UTG’s raising range; the only real dangers are full houses if the board pairs (or very specific runner-runner straights if a Q comes and an opponent holds the exact cards). I should be extracting value and denying free cards. A half‑pot size will get called by worse hands (Jx, 9x, some pocket pairs) while charging the combos that can improve to a full house. Bet ~1/2 pot (slightly rounded) to maximize value and protection.

I was set-mining with pocket 3s and missed. The flop of J97 gives me an underpair with only 2 outs (~4%) to improve. Minni's 300 bet into a 550 pot suggests they connected with this board. Calling here with no set and no draws is burning chips. Clear fold.

Eight-six offsuit is a weak hand, especially from Under the Gun (UTG) position. In a 6-max game, opening from UTG requires a much stronger range to avoid being out of position or dominated by callers and 3-betters behind me. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

K7s is too weak to open from middle position in a 6-handed, no-ante structure. With four players behind (including BTN and the blinds) we’re likely to face 3-bets or get called by better kings that dominate us, and limping is not a profitable baseline strategy. Preserving stack and keeping our MP opening range tighter is best here.

Preflop in cutoff with 32o, an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability. UTG (Flash) and MP (FiveTwo) have folded, leaving it unopened, but 32o has no high card strength, straight potential is marginal, and it's dominated against any calling range. Limping is poor as it plays poorly postflop OOP or even IP. Raising is a bad bluff due to low fold equity against tight players and unplayable postflop. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with K9o and the action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play the pot in position. K9o is strong enough to open from the button given the positional advantage. I will raise to 2.5x BB (250) to put pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind.

I have J♦5♣ in the small blind facing a button open to 250. I’ve already posted 50, so calling costs 200 to play out of position. This hand is weak offsuit with poor playability and will often be dominated by the button’s opening range. 3‑betting would be a pure bluff from the SB out of position and is unnecessary with ~100bb effective stacks early in the tournament. Folding preserves chips and lets me wait for a better spot. I fold.

A3s in the BB facing a button open to 250. This is a standard defend - I have a suited ace with decent playability. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (need to call 150 into 400), and A3s plays well enough against a button stealing range. Easy call.

I flopped top pair with A3s on Ac Kh 6h. Weak kicker but decent hand against button's wide opening range. Standard play is to check to the preflop aggressor. I can check-call a reasonable c-bet since many worse hands (Kx, flush draws, air) will bet. If he checks back, the board is dangerous with the flush draw.

I hold Kc9h on a board of AcKh6h, giving me middle pair. The pot is 550. My opponent (Big Blind) checked. This board texture hits my Button opening range very hard (I have all the strong Aces), but my specific hand serves as a medium-strength showdown hand (SDV). Betting here has little value: worse hands (like 77-QQ or 6x) might fold, and better hands (Ax, K10+) will call. By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and can comfortably call a bet on the turn or river to bluff-catch. I want to get to showdown cheaply.

I have top pair weak kicker (A3s) on AcKhQs6h. Pro checked back the flop, which often indicates a weak/marginal hand or draw. The turn Q makes the board scarier (JT now has a straight, more broadway cards). I should lead for a small bet to get value from Kx, Qx hands and charge the heart flush draw. Checking risks giving free cards. A 1/3 pot bet of ~175-200 is appropriate.

I hold middle pair (Kings) on a board of [Ac Kh 6h Qs]. After I checked back the flop, the Big Blind leads the turn for a small sizing (200 into 550). This line often represents a blocking bet with a draw (hearts or straight), a weaker pair (Qx), or a probing weak Ax. My hand is too strong to fold to a small bet, as I beat all bluffs and many value hands like weaker Kings or Queens. Raising would only isolate me against better hands (Ax, J10, sets). Calling keeps his range wide and allows me to re-evaluate on the river in position.

I have top pair weak kicker (A3s) on AcKhQs6h5d. Pro checked back the flop (indicating weakness) and just called my turn bet. Their range is likely Kx, Qx, or missed draws (hearts, JT straight draw). The river 5d is a blank. Betting thin for value is risky - better aces call, worse hands fold. By checking, I can induce bluffs from missed draws and control pot size with my marginal holding. If Pro bets, I can make a decision, but I likely call a reasonable sized bet.

I hold second pair (Kings) on a board of Ac Kh 6h Qs 5d. The pot is 950. My opponent, the Big Blind, bet small on the turn (200) and has now checked the river. His line is consistent with a marginal made hand (like Qx or a weak Kx) or a missed draw (hearts or straight draw like JT, though JT got there on the turn, he might have bet river with a straight). My hand has decent showdown value but is unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet (Qx might call, but missed draws fold). Conversely, better hands (Ax, better Kx) will certainly call. Betting offers thin value and exposes me to a check-raise. Checking back allows me to win against missed draws and weaker pairs without risking more chips.

UTG 6-handed with 4♦2♦ is too weak to open: it has low raw equity, gets dominated when it makes a pair, and plays poorly out of position versus multiple callers/3-bets. With ~95 BB effective stacks, preserving EV by folding this bottom-tier suited hand is best.

Preflop, unraised pot after UTG fold. My hand is 5h3d, a trash hand with poor connectivity, low cards, offsuit. No playable equity from middle position early in tournament with deep stacks. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold 8h 7h (suited connectors) in the Cutoff, which is a strong candidate for opening the pot due to its post-flop playability (straight and flush potential). The action folded to me. Opening to 2.5BB (250) is a standard size to steal the blinds or play in position against the blinds if the Button folds. I want to take the initiative rather than limping or folding a playable hand in late position.

Cutoff (Pro) opened to 250. I’m on the button with Kd Tc and ~102 BB effective. This is a hand I want to play in position and it fares well as a positional 3-bet: it has decent equity vs a cutoff opening range and provides blockers to strong Kx/Tx combos. I’ll 3-bet to apply pressure and isolate the opener rather than just flatting and inviting multiway action. I choose a practical size — raise to 800 (≈3.2x the open) — big enough to fold out weaker hands and thin the field, but not so large as to bloat the pot out of position if called. Plan: if called I’ll play postflop in position; if I face a large 4-bet I’ll give KTo up to avoid tricky dominated spots. I avoided the minimum raise to 400 because it’s too small to achieve isolation and just invites cheap callers.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni (800) after Pro opened (250). I have AJs which is a decent hand but tricky spot OOP against a 3-bettor. AJs has good playability postflop but I'm concerned about being dominated by AK/AQ. However, folding AJs seems too tight here - it's in the top of my calling range. 4-betting would be aggressive and commit too many chips. I'll flat call and play carefully postflop, looking to hit a strong hand or fold to heavy pressure.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qs 4h. The action before me involves a raise to 250 from Pro, a 3-bet to 800 from Minni, and a cold-call from Claude in the Small Blind. Q4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that is heavily dominated by the ranges of players involved in a 3-bet pot. Even with the pot odds provided, the reverse implied odds and poor post-flop playability make this a clear fold.

I am facing a 3-bet to 800 from the Button and a cold call from the SB. The pot is currently 1950, and I need to call 550. This gives me pot odds of 550/2500 = 22%. With 8h 7h (suited connectors), I have around 35-40% equity against strong ranges and significant implied odds due to the deep stacks (~90BB). Although I will be out of position against the Button, I am in position against the SB and closing the action. The price is too good to fold a hand with this much post-flop playability in a multi-way pot.

I completely whiffed this 5-9-2 rainbow board with AJs. Minni was the 3-bettor and has the initiative in this 3-way pot. I have two overcards but no pair, no draw. Best to check and see what the aggressor does - I can fold to significant action or catch a free card if checked through.

I have 8h 7h on a 5c 9s 2c board. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 6) but no immediate showdown value. I am out of position relative to the pre-flop 3-bettor (Minni on the Button) but in position relative to the Small Blind. Leading out (donk betting) into two opponents, including the aggressor, is risky and unlikely to generate folds from overpairs or strong hands. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser to see their action and price. If they check back, I get a free card to hit my straight.

I 3‑bet preflop with KdTc to play in position, but both the SB and the CO called so we’re heads‑up/multiway in a bloated pot (2,500). Flop 5c‑9s‑2c is very dry — I only have two overcards (K,T) and a backdoor club (one club in hand) so my immediate equity is modest (~24% to pair K/T by river, small double‑club backdoor). In a 3‑bet multiway pot continuation‑betting as a bluff is thinly profitable and will be called by many of the villain ranges (pocket pairs, Ax, broadways), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with just overcards. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity in position; plan to call small bets to see a turn, fold to large pressure or a check‑raise, and start betting/raising only if I pair K/T or pick up a meaningful draw on the turn.

Board paired with the 2h on the turn. Everyone checked the flop including Minni who 3-bet preflop - suggesting nobody connected strongly with this low board. I still have AJ high with no pair. While I could bet as a bluff to represent strength, checking is better: 1) I have showdown value if it checks through, 2) I can see a free river and potentially hit an A or J, 3) Betting OOP and getting check-raised would be disastrous with no equity. Check and reevaluate on river.

I have picked up significant equity on the turn with the 2h, giving me a heart flush draw alongside my gutshot straight draw (need a 6). The board is 5c 9s 2c 2h. The pre-flop 3-bettor (Minni) checked back the flop, which suggests she may not have a strong overpair, or she is exercising pot control. While a probe bet here could take down the pot against Ace-highs, betting into two opponents in a 3-bet pot is risky; if I get raised, I would likely have to fold my equity. Checking allows me to try to realize my equity for free or evaluate a bet from the Button. With 8-high, I have no showdown value, but my draw is strong enough to play passively to try to hit the river.

I remain on the button with KdTc in a multiway 3‑bet pot (2,500). The turn (5c 9s 2c 2h) pairs the board and leaves me with only K/T overcards and no realistic draws (the runner‑runner club draw is dead). I have ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (~13% chance) but against two opponents calling my 3‑bet, bluff equity is low and a bet here will be called too often by medium pairs and Ax/broadway hands. Also a check allows me to control the pot size, gather information (both checked flop/turn — could be weak or slowplayed pockets like 22) and realize my equity in position. Plan: check the turn to see a cheap river; if I face a small bet I can call to see the river given my pair‑outs, but fold to large pressure. If I pair K/T on the river I will then extract value or target specific lines based on how my opponents have acted.

River brought the Jd giving me top pair top kicker (Jacks up with Ace kicker). After everyone checked flop and turn, I've hit a great card. I should bet for value - can get called by worse Jx, 99, 55, or even stubborn pocket pairs. Betting around 1/2 pot for value.

I hold 8-high with a missed gutshot straight draw and a missed flush draw on the river (5c 9s 2c 2h Jd). The Small Blind (Claude) has led out for 1200 into a 2450 pot after checking two streets. Given that I have zero showdown value and there is still the pre-flop 3-bettor (Minni) to act behind me, I cannot call. Bluff raising is not viable because Claude's lead into two opponents in a 3-bet pot indicates strength (likely a Jack, 9, or full house), and I block no value hands. The only correct play is to fold.

Fold. Claude called my 3‑bet preflop and played very passively on the flop and turn, then comes out raising 1,200 into a 3,700 pot on the river. Calling 1,200 requires roughly 24% equity (1200/(3700+1200) ≈ 24.5%). With K♦T♣ I have no pair and essentially zero clean outs on this river — my showdown equity versus a realistic SB calling range (Jx, 9x, 5x, medium pocket pairs, and the occasional slowplayed 2s/quads) is well below the required threshold. Raising as a bluff is unlikely to fold enough of his value combos and would unnecessarily bloat the pot out of position. Consistent with my turn plan (fold to significant river pressure without pairing up), the +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

UTG in a 6-handed game with deep stacks (~100BB). 67o is an offsuit connector not in any standard UTG opening range. Speculative hand that benefits from position or multiway pots, but raising OOP invites trouble postflop. Previous hands show aggressive play, but folding weak hands early preserves stack for premium spots. Clear fold.

I hold King-Queen offsuit (KQo) in Middle Position with 6 players active. With the UTG player folding, this is a standard opening hand due to its high card strength and playability. Limping would be weak, so I will raise to take the initiative. A standard raise size of 2.5BB (250 chips) is appropriate to build the pot and thin the field, consistent with previous open sizes observed at the table.

I have Q♦3♥ in the cutoff facing a 2.5x open to 250 from Middle (Pro). This is a weak, poorly connected hand with a bad kicker and limited postflop playability — it will be dominated by most of the raiser’s continuing range. Calling 250 into a 400 pot requires ~38% equity and is likely to become a multiway pot with the button and blinds still to act, making the call even worse. 3-betting as a bluff is possible but risky against an active opener who has shown aggression; I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with such a hand. With a healthy stack I’ll conserve chips for better spots and fold.
J6o is a weak offsuit hand. Even with button position, calling a raise with this hand is unprofitable. The hand lacks connectivity and has poor high card value. Folding is the disciplined play here.

J9s is a strong speculative hand. I am getting 3:1 odds to call the raise from the Small Blind. While playing out of position is not ideal, the suited connectivity of the hand allows for good playability on many flop textures.

We’re getting a very good price in the big blind: 150 to win 600 (and we realize equity multiway). Even though 8-5 offsuit is weak and out of position, BB defense should be wide here with no squeeze incentive and poor fold equity versus MP open + SB cold-call. Calling keeps SPR high and avoids bloating the pot with a hand that doesn’t play well as a bluff 3-bet.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on a very dry board (4d Jh 3c). While I likely have the best hand currently, my kicker is vulnerable to better Jacks like AJ, KJ, or QJ. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is the standard play here to see their continuation betting strategy and to control the size of the pot. Since the board is rainbow and lacks significant draw potential, I don't feel the immediate need to bet for protection yet.

We’ve picked up a strong enough draw for this spot: with the 5 on J-4-3 we have an open-ended straight draw to a 2 or 6, plus some backdoor flush equity. Still, we’re out of position and multiway versus the preflop raiser, so leading (donking) doesn’t generate great fold equity and risks getting raised off our equity. Checking keeps ranges wide, lets Pro continuation-bet his overcards/pairs, and we can realize equity via a call (or occasionally a check-raise if sizing/line makes sense).

I am in a multi-way pot (3-way) on a dry J-4-3 rainbow board. While I have a range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, betting into two opponents with just King-high is risky, especially given FiveTwo's history of check-raising on dry boards (Hand #1). The board texture is static, meaning few draws exist, so giving a free card isn't disastrous. Checking allows me to realize my equity (trying to hit a K or Q on the turn) and pot control without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. I will check back and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the Queen of Clubs is an overcard to my Jack. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and the Big Blind (FiveTwo) both checked the flop, their ranges remain wide, but the Queen likely improves some of Pro's overcard combinations like AQ or KQ. Given that I now have second pair with a mediocre kicker and am out of position, I will check again to control the pot size and see how the other players react to this card.

Flop checked through three ways, which heavily caps both Flash and Pro away from their strongest value (many Jx/overpairs would often c-bet for protection). The turn Q is a strong card to represent from the BB defending range (Qx suited, QJ, etc.) and puts pressure on exactly the ace-high/king-high and small pocket pairs that commonly check back flop. With essentially no real draw or showdown value, our best route to winning is taking the pot now with a small probe that risks little relative to the 750 out there. Plan: bet/fold versus a raise; if called, proceed very cautiously on most rivers and often shut down unless a very favorable scare card/range interaction appears.

The turn card is the Queen of Clubs, giving me Top Pair with a King Kicker. This is a strong hand, especially after the flop checked through. FiveTwo leads for 300 (about 40% of the pot). This small lead often signifies a marginal hand, a draw, or a blocker bet. Raising here might fold out worse hands (like Jacks or weak Queens) that I want to keep in the pot, or isolate me against sets/two pair. Calling allows me to keep FiveTwo's range wide, keeps bluffs in, and utilizes my position to re-evaluate on the river. Flash is still to act, but calling is safer than bloating the pot immediately with one pair.

The Queen on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Jacks, which weakens my hand significantly. FiveTwo's lead from the Big Blind and Pro's subsequent call suggest that at least one of them may have a Queen or a better Jack. However, I am being offered 4.5:1 pot odds (300 to call into a total pot of 1,350). With these odds, I have enough equity to call once more to see if the river improves my hand or if the action checks down, providing a cheap showdown. I will call and re-evaluate on the river.

The river 7d is a complete blank. My hand remains second pair (Jacks) with a nine kicker. After the Queen appeared on the turn, FiveTwo led out and Pro called, which strongly suggests that my Jacks are now behind a Queen or a superior Jack. Leading out with a bet here would likely only result in being called by better hands and folding out hands that I already beat. Checking is the most logical play to minimize losses and potentially reach a cheap showdown if the action checks around.

Turn probe got called by both Pro and Flash, which greatly reduces fold equity on the river—multiway, their continuing ranges contain a lot of Qx/Jx and pocket pairs that will not fold to a reasonable barrel. The 7d doesn’t improve our story (no flush completes, no obvious scary overcard beyond what’s already there), and with 8-high we have no credible thin value line. Best is to check, preserve stack, and fold to significant aggression rather than fire a low-EV third barrel into two callers.

I hold Top Pair with a King Kicker (Qs Kd) on a board of [4d Jh 3c Qc 7d]. The pot is multi-way (3 players). On the turn, FiveTwo led for a small sizing and Flash called, suggesting they likely hold marginal made hands (like Jx or a weak Q) or draws that have now missed on the river. With both opponents checking to me on the river, they have capped their ranges. While I likely have the best hand, betting is thin value; worse hands like mid-pairs (Jacks) will likely fold, and I risk being called only by two-pair or better, or getting check-raised. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity and win the pot against missed draws and weaker pairs without reopening the betting.

I am in UTG position with 7d 5s, which is an extremely weak hand (unsuited one-gapper). Opening from UTG requires a strong range because I have to act first and will be out of position post-flop against any callers. With 97 BBs deep early in the tournament, there is absolutely no incentive to play this hand. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I hold 6s5c — a small, connected offsuit hand. There's been no raise and it costs 100 to see the flop (pot 150). With deep stacks and several players still to act this is a cheap, speculative spot to try to realize equity, not a hand I want to isolate with a raise. Calling 100 preserves my stack and lets me play the flop for a low price; I will fold to a large re-raise preflop or proceed cautiously postflop if the board is unfavorable.

I have pocket Jacks in the cutoff - a premium hand. Minni limped from middle position. I should raise for value and to isolate the limper. Standard raise size with a limper would be around 4x BB, so 400.

I am holding 6h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand with no real potential for straights, flushes, or high-card strength. Claude has made a 4x raise from the cutoff, and Minni has already limped. While I am on the button, playing such a low-equity hand against a raise is a negative expected value play. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

K6o from the small blind versus a limp-raise is too dominated and plays poorly out of position with reverse implied odds. Calling invites a tough multiway spot with Elon still in the big blind, and turning it into a squeeze bluff risks getting called by ranges that have us in bad shape (broadways, better kings, pairs). With plenty of stack depth and better spots ahead, I’ll just release it.

AhKh is a premium hand, one of the best in poker, especially suited. Facing a CO raise to 400 after an MP limp, with SB and BTN folding, this is a standard 3-bet spot to isolate and build the pot. Calling risks multiway pot with Minni still to act behind after me, and folding is -EV. Claude's range is wide from CO, and Minni's limp suggests weakness. 3-bet sizing to 12bb (1200 total) is standard over a 4bb raise, denying equity to worse hands and extracting value. Deep stacks allow for postflop play if called. This maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

Elon 3-bet Claude’s open to 1200 after I already called 100, so I face 1100 to continue into a 1750 pot (calling would make it ~2850). I hold 6♠5♣ — a small, offsuit connector that plays poorly multiway and out of position against an opener and a reraiser. The call requires roughly 38.6% equity to break even, which is unlikely versus these ranges, and raising would irresponsibly bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Sunk cost is irrelevant here — I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Facing a 3-bet from BB with JJ. This is a strong hand but not strong enough to 4-bet/get it in against a typical 3-bet range (AA, KK, QQ, AK). I have position and good pot odds (800 to win 2550). Calling to see a flop in position is the best play - I can evaluate based on board texture and Elon's action postflop.

Heads-up on flop with AhKh on 2s8dTh board. I have the nut flush draw (10 clean outs) plus AK overcards for additional equity, giving me roughly 40-50% equity against Claude's CO calling range after my 3-bet (which includes AxKx, broadways, suited connectors, pocket pairs). The board is dry and low, missing much of his range, favoring my pre-flop aggression. As first to act but with initiative, a continuation bet builds the pot, charges his draws/weak pairs, denies equity to floats, and sets up check-raise if he shoves. Sizing 2/3 pot (1700) balances value from medium strength and protection while leaving room for turns/rivers. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards or gutshots; betting maximizes EV long-term.

I have an overpair (JJ) on a dry 2s-8d-Th board. Elon 3-bet preflop and is now c-betting 1700 into 2550 (~67% pot). This board is very favorable for my hand - only AA, KK, QQ, and TT beat me from his 3-bet range. Against AK, AQ, and other 3-bet bluffs I'm way ahead. Raising would fold out worse hands and only get called by better. Folding an overpair on this texture is too weak. Calling to control pot size and re-evaluate on turn is optimal.

Heads-up on turn with AhKh on 2s8dThQh board. The Qh is a fantastic card, bringing the nut flush draw (9 outs to nuts), plus AK overcards for gutshot potential and pair outs, giving me 40-50+% equity against Claude's CO 3-bet calling range (pairs like 88/TT/22, broadway Ax like AQo/AJs, straight draws like J9/97, weaker heart draws). Board texture hit some draws and pairs, but my equity dominates. Pot 5950, stacks deep (~7k effective). As preflop aggressor first to act, betting leverages initiative, fold equity against his medium strength (e.g., T9, 89, mid pairs without diamond block), charges his draws/improvements, and builds massive pot for river shove if called. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/brick-out draws or letting him bet weakly. Sizing to ~3/4 pot (4500) balances value from his T/Q hits and protection while leaving maneuverability; too small risks cheap calls, all-in premature without max value. Claude's history shows passivity postflop (folds to aggression in hands 1/2/4), maximizing EV by applying pressure now.

The Qh is a disaster card for my JJ. Elon 3-bet preflop and has now bet aggressively on both flop and turn. His range that takes this line (3-bet pre, big flop bet, huge turn bet) is weighted heavily toward AA, KK, QQ, TT, and AQ - all of which now beat me. The only hand I'm ahead of is AK. He's betting 4500 into ~5850 (77% pot) and only has 2550 behind - he's committed. If I call and lose, I'm down to 4300 chips (43bb). The Q is such a bad runout that I think folding JJ here is correct despite the pot odds. His line and sizing screams value.

UTG with K6o (K♠6♥) is a marginal, easily dominated hand with poor postflop playability. In a 6-handed game UTG should have a tight opening range; K6o is not in that range. The pot is small (150) and calling/limping invites multiway pots and difficult OOP decisions. Several opponents have shown aggression (Pro, Claude, Elon) and could 3-bet or isolate, which would put me in a bad spot with this hand. Early in the tournament I should preserve my 9,350 stack for stronger opportunities. Fold.
95 offsuit is a very weak hand in middle position with 5 players behind. Not worth opening or limping - easy fold.

I am in the Cutoff with 8s3h, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no straight or flush potential and very poor high-card value. Two players have already folded, but with the Button, Small Blind, and Big Blind still to act, there is no merit in attempting a steal or entering the pot with this hand. This is a standard fold.

Button is effectively on the steal with three folds ahead; K7o is comfortably within a 6-max button opening range. Raising is better than limping to maximize fold equity versus the blinds and keep initiative, while using a small size to risk less against potential 3-bets from Elon/Pro. Plan is raise/fold to larger 3-bets and play in position when called.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 225 (2.25x) after three folds. My hand J♥8♣ is offsuit but has decent equity against a wide button opening range (~40-50% of hands), around 38-40% equity. Pot odds are excellent: calling 175 more (total 225 committed) into a 375 pot for ~3:1 implied. Deep stacks (86+ BB effective) allow for postflop playability. J8o can flop strong draws, pairs, or straight potential. OOP but cheap call to realize equity and control pot OOP. Folding too tight early in tournament; 3-bet not justified without blockers or premium. BB to act but unlikely to squeeze wide. Standard mixed spot but call maximizes EV.

I am facing a raise to 2.25 BB from the Button and a call from the Small Blind. I need to call 125 chips into a pot that will be 675 total, offering me pot odds of approximately 5.4 to 1 (requiring ~18.5% equity). My hand, 6s4c, is a weak offsuit one-gapper. While the price is enticing, 64o performs poorly in multi-way pots and I will be out of position against the aggressor. The raw equity of the hand is close to the required odds, but my ability to realize that equity (Equity Realization) out of position is low due to the hand's playability issues and reverse implied odds. Therefore, folding is the disciplined choice to avoid bleeding chips in a marginal spot early in the tournament.

Flop A♥ K♥ 9♦ is a scary, coordinated board with two broadway cards and a flush draw. My J♥8♣ is jack-high with only a backdoor flush draw (one heart) and no pair or straight draw potential. Equity is very low (~10-20%) against FiveTwo's wide button opening range post-three folds, which includes Ax, Kx, suited connectors, broadways, and hearts that connect well here. OOP first to act with no showdown value or credible bluff range for a donk bet. Checking allows pot control, realizes equity if checked behind (possible with his missed hands), and preserves stack by folding to a likely continuation bet. Deep stacks but early in tournament (hand 7/150); no need to bluff into position with air. Standard check.

We have second pair (K) with a weak kicker on an A-K-9 two-heart board. As the preflop raiser we retain a meaningful range advantage here (we have many strong Ax and AK), so a small range c-bet is profitable to deny equity to hands like QJ/JT/heart draws and to get thin value from worse Kx/9x that defend preflop. I’ll keep the sizing small to control the pot versus Ax-heavy continues from the small blind and to make it easy to fold if Elon check-raises big on this texture.

Facing a 200 bet into 750 pot on A♥ K♥ 9♦ flop from button raiser with J♥8♣ (jack-high, backdoor heart draw only). Equity <20% vs. wide button range that connects strongly (Ax, Kx, broadways, suited connectors, hearts). Poor pot odds long-term without fold equity or implied odds OOP. No showdown value; calling invites turn pressure. Standard check-fold preserves stack early (hand 7/150, deep stacks). Bluff-raise too thin without blockers or history.
Q2o from UTG is a clear fold. This is way too weak to open from early position - poor kicker, offsuit, and 5 players left to act behind me.

I am holding 6d 3d in middle position. This is an extremely weak hand and does not meet the requirements for an open-raise in a 6-max game. Folding is the only viable long-term strategy here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Unopened pot in the cutoff with A7o. This hand is strong enough to open in position, has an ace blocker for blind/button 3-bets, and plays reasonably well in single-raised pots where we can leverage position and initiative. Limping invites multiway/awkward domination spots; folding is too tight here. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to pressure the blinds and keep sizing consistent with the table. Versus a larger 3-bet (especially from Elon on the button), I’ll generally fold; versus flats I’ll c-bet favorable boards and control pot on ace-high boards when action indicates domination risk.

Preflop on the button facing a cutoff raise to 250 into 150 (pot now 400). Q8s is a speculative suited hand with good playability, straight and flush potential, and excellent postflop position. Deep stacks (90-125BB) provide strong implied odds if I connect. FiveTwo has shown aggression from late position in prior hands (e.g., Hand 1 called pre then led flop, Hand 5 bet turn, Hand 7 raised and c-bet). Calling leverages position to realize equity and outplay postflop, while folding is too tight and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand's capping range. Pot odds are favorable (400:250 ~1.6:1, plus blinds likely to call/raise). Standard call.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o, facing a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly multi-way and out of position. Despite getting decent pot odds, the reverse implied odds are significant as I am likely dominated if I hit a King, and playability is low. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I have pocket Jacks in the big blind facing a 2.5x cutoff open (250) with the button cold-calling. Calling here leaves me out of position in a multiway pot against two callers — not ideal. A min-raise would be too weak and invite more callers; JJ is strong enough to 3-bet for value/isolation. With ~90BB effective stacks, I can size for value without committing. I’ll 3-bet to roughly 3.5x the open (~875) and round to 900 to maximize fold equity and isolate the opener while still leaving post-flop maneuverability. If I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate, but this sizing is the best long-term EV play here.

Minni’s 3-bet is a squeeze after my CO open and Elon’s flat, which heavily weights their range toward strong value and good broadways. A7o is dominated by the hands that continue (AJ+/AQ+, bigger aces, strong pairs) and plays poorly in a 3-bet pot—especially with Elon still behind, creating awkward multiway/position dynamics. A 4-bet bluff is possible with the ace blocker, but at ~85bb effective this sizing will get defended/jammed often enough that A7o is not a good candidate. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing a BB squeeze to 900 after CO open to 250 and my button call with Qd8d suited. Pot 1450, calling 650 more (pot odds ~3.2:1, requiring ~23% equity). Q8ds plays well multiway or heads-up postflop with straight/flush potential, position, and deep stacks (85-125BB) for implied odds. Minni's squeeze range includes QQ+, AK, suited broadways, and bluffs (as shown in Hand 4 wide 3-bet). Q8s has 35-45% equity vs. typical squeeze range, good playability, Q blocker. Folding too tight given position and odds; 4-betting caps range and risks domination/shove vs. her value. Call to realize equity OOP opponent with superior position.

I 3-bet preflop to isolate and got one caller, so this is heads-up but I’m out of position. Flop Td Ah 4d is an A-high board that hits a large portion of the button’s 3-bet-calling range (AXs/Axo, AT/TT, some broadways). My JJ is decent vs non-Ax hands but is vulnerable to any Ace and can be crushed by a check-raise. Leading out will often be called by better hands and may induce aggression I don’t want to face out of position. I’ll check to control the pot size, allow bluffs/weaker holdings to reveal themselves, and call reasonable-sized continuation bets to realize equity. If Elon checks back I can take a stab on many blank turns; if he bets small I’ll usually call and re-evaluate; if he check-raises or barrels big I’ll fold unless the sizing/texture gives clear odds to continue.

Flop board Td Ah 4d is dryish Ace-high with diamond flush draw threat. Holding Qd8d gives me a strong flush draw (9 outs) plus Queen overcard blocker, ~35% equity heads-up vs. Minni's BB squeeze range (AA/KK/QQ/AK/broadway bluffs, per prior 3-bet wide in Hand 4). She checked OOP after squeeze, potentially weak Ax, trap with monster, or missed value hands. Deep stacks (118BB effective) and button position favor semi-bluff betting: charges her draws/worse Ax, builds pot for when we hit flush, high fold equity vs. polarized range. Sizing 3/4 pot (1575) balances value from Ax/KQ and protection/foldout from overpairs. Check-back risks free card to her draws/improves; overbet unnecessary without nut advantage.

I checked the A♦ T♦ 4♣ flop with JJ planning to call reasonable c-bets and keep the pot controllable OOP. Elon leads 1,575 into a 3,675 pot (~43% pot). That sizing is small enough that calling is the +EV line: my J♠J♣ is ahead of a lot of his non‑A holdings (Kx, Qx, missed broadways, some T‑x) and has decent equity vs a cold‑call 3‑bet range, while I only need ~23% equity to justify a call (1,575 / 6,825 ≈ 23%). Raising to the minimum (3,150) would bloat the pot OOP, commit a lot of chips when I’m often behind to any A, and open me to a 4‑bet or difficult turn decisions. So I’ll call, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to clear strength (big turn barrel) if the board favors A‑heavy lines, or continue/raise on a favorable blank if he shows weakness.

I hold JJ on Td Ah 4d Qh after calling Elon’s small flop c-bet. The Q on the turn is a full overcard to my pair and also brings a second heart — I now have only two clean outs (two Js) and remain behind any Ax or Qx. Elon checked the turn, which can be either a capped/medium-strength range or a trap, but I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that’s often behind. A bet here would either fold out some Kx hands or get called by most A/Q/Tx holdings and commit a lot OOP with poor equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see a river; I’ll require a clear improvement (a J for trips) or a very cheap river to continue — otherwise I’ll fold to large river aggression.

Turn board Td Ah 4d Qh brings a Queen, pairing my Qd for second pair (Queens) with a nut diamond flush draw (9 outs) and backdoor straight potential. Pot 5250, Minni checks OOP second time after squeezing pre and calling my flop c-bet 1575. Her range: polarized BB defense (Ax like AK/AQ/AJ/AT, sets, flush draws, some bluffs/straights). My hand has ~35-45% equity vs. her calling range (directs vs. Ax ~25%, crushes draws/bluffs ~70%+), blocks QQ/AQ, excellent playability in position. Deep effective stacks (her 6875 ~78BB into pot, my 103BB), but betting builds pot for river shoves/flushes and denies her equity (hearts/spades/straight draws like KJ/J9). Check-back risks free river to improving draws/Ax pairing or checking down equity. Bet ~2/3 pot (3500) balances value from worse Ax/draws and thin value from overpairs, high fold equity vs. weak Ax/missed draws while preserving stacks vs. monsters. Continues my aggressive line repping strength (flop bet), exploits her check-check OOP tendency.

Fold. I 3‑bet preflop to isolate and called a small flop c‑bet on Td Ah 4d to control the pot OOP. The Q♥ on the turn, plus Elon’s sizable turn bet (3,500 into a 8,750 pot), is a clear show of strength — it improves or heavily favors Ax/Qx/value combos and puts me in a dominated/marginal spot. I only have two clean outs to make trips (2/46 ≈ 4.3%), and calling 3,500 to win a total pot of 12,250 requires ~28.6% equity. Against a realistic cold‑call/continuation/c-barrel range (many A♣/A♠/A♥ combos, Qx, some T‑combos and value hands), my equity is well below the break‑even threshold. Raising is unattractive OOP — it bloats the pot when I’m often behind and risks commitment to worse spots. Preserving ~6,875 chips for better spots is the higher EV play here, so I fold.

I am in the UTG (Under The Gun) position with Js 8s. In a 6-max game, this is the tightest position to play from. While J8s is a suited hand with some potential, it is generally below the threshold for a standard opening range from UTG, which typically starts at higher suited connectors like T9s or JTs. Raising here would likely lead to playing a post-flop pot out of position against several opponents, and there are aggressive stacks like Elon and Claude yet to act. Given we are only 9 hands into a 150-hand tournament and my stack is healthy at 103 BB, I will choose the conservative and standard play of folding.

9♠7♦ offsuit is too weak to open from middle position in a 6-max lineup, especially with two strong late-position players still to act (CO/Button) who can 3-bet or isolate. Limping invites multiway pots where we’re often dominated and out of position postflop. Best EV is to conserve chips and fold.

Folded to me in the cutoff with 65s, a suited connector that plays well post-flop in position. Raising to steal the blinds or build a pot when I hit. Good fold equity, and position allows me to control the action. Standard sizing around 2.5x in these games as seen in history. Min raise is 200, but 250 is a solid open.

I hold AJo on the Button facing a standard 2.5x open from the Cutoff (Elon). Elon has demonstrated an aggressive style in previous hands (e.g., large overbets in Hand 6 and Hand 8), and he is currently the chip leader. AJo is a strong hand that dominates much of a Cutoff opening range, but it is vulnerable to 4-bets. By just calling, I accomplish several things: 1) I keep the aggressor's range wide, including hands I dominate; 2) I utilize my positional advantage without bloating the pot pre-flop against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent; 3) I avoid a high-variance spot where I might be forced to fold to a re-raise. While a 3-bet is a valid option to isolate, flat-calling is a lower-variance line that allows me to navigate post-flop effectively.

I’m in the small blind with Kd9h facing Elon’s CO open to 250 and a button call — I’ll be out of position to two players. Calling 200 (to complete my SB) gives ~23% immediate pot odds to see a flop in a multiway pot and is a low-cost way to realize equity with a playable king. A min-raise to 400 is too small to isolate and will likely get called, and a larger 3-bet is unjustified with K9o out of position. Plan: call 200, evaluate the flop — continue only with top pair/top kicker or strong draws, otherwise fold to significant aggression or a 3-bet preflop.

I have 76o in the BB with 150 to call into an 850 pot (5.7:1 odds). Three players already in. While 76o is marginal, the pot odds are attractive and I can flop a straight draw or two pair occasionally. Calling is reasonable given the price.

Flop is Jc-8c-5h and I hold Kd-9h — no pair, no club, only a single overcard (K). This is a 4‑way pot and the board is draw‑heavy (two clubs), so betting out as a bluff from the small blind out of position is thin and will get called by flush/straight/paired draws. My preflop plan was to continue only with top pair/top kicker or strong draws; this hand doesn’t meet that criteria. I’ll check to see how the field behaves and fold to meaningful aggression. If everyone checks through I’ll reassess on the turn and only invest further if I improve (pair the K or pick up a real draw) or face a very small bet that gives correct pot odds to call as a bluff‑catcher.

I have 76o on Jc8c5h - an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 9 gives me the nuts/straight). With 8 outs and 3 opponents, I'll take the free card rather than semi-bluff into a multiway pot. If I hit on the turn I can extract value.

Multiway pot with three opponents after my preflop raise. Flop Jc8c5h hits me with bottom pair (5s) and poor kicker (6c), no draws. Board is wet with flush draw and possible straight draws. Acting third after checks from blinds, but still vulnerable to Button (Pro). Checking allows control of pot size, realizes equity cheaply if checked through, and avoids bloating pot OOP with marginal hand. Will call small bets or fold to aggression, evaluating turn based on action.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (AJ on Jc-8c-5h) in a multi-way pot (4 players). The pre-flop aggressor (Elon) checked, signaling weakness or a draw. The board has a flush draw (clubs) and some straight connectivity. Checking back gives three opponents a free card to hit a flush or straight, or a higher pair. I need to bet for value (targeting worse Jacks like KJ/QJ, middle pairs like 99/8x) and protection (charging flush draws). A bet size of roughly 55% of the pot (550 into 1000) strikes a balance between extracting value and denying equity to draws without over-committing against a potential trap.

Fold. I completed preflop with Kd9h intending to continue only with top pair or real draws; the flop Jc-8c-5h is four‑way and I have no pair and no club backdoor. Pro’s bet of 550 into a 1550 pot gives ~26% pot odds to call, but my true equity (only ~6 outs to pair by the turn, no draw to the clubs/straight) is well below what’s needed, and calling OOP in a multiway spot invites further action from the BB/CO. Pro has shown aggression in prior hands and this is not a spot to bluff‑catch. Preserve the stack and fold.

I have 76o with an open-ended straight draw - any 4 or 9 gives me a straight. Pro bet 550 into ~1000 pot. I need to call 550 to win 1550 (2.8:1 odds, ~26% equity needed). With 8 outs I have ~32% to hit by river, ~17% on turn alone. Getting reasonable odds to continue with my draw. If I hit, I can extract good value from Pro.

Facing a bet of 550 into 2100 from Pro on the button after three checks, with Claude calling from the BB. My 65s gives bottom pair with a weak 6 kicker on a draw-heavy Jc8c5h flop (clubs flush draw, open-enders like 97o/s, T7). Preflop I raised as suited connector, but checked flop to pot control. Pro's bet after checks suggests strength (top pair, overpair, set, or semi-bluff draw), and Claude's call indicates at least a draw, weak pair, or float. My equity is marginal OOP against their ranges; I beat pure bluffs/draws but crushed by Jx, 8x, sets, two-pair. Pot odds ~3.8:1 require ~20% equity, but reverse implied odds hurt on turns that improve board or them. Folding preserves stack early in tournament (hand 9/150), avoids tough spots multiway deep-stacked, and maintains image as preflop raiser who doesn't call down lightly. Better spots await.

Board paired with Jd on turn. I still have my OESD (4 or 9 gives me a straight). With ~16% to hit on river and action checked to me, I should take the free card rather than bluff into a paired board where Pro showed strength on the flop. If I hit the river, I can bet for value; if Pro bets, I can evaluate pot odds.

I hold Trip Jacks with an Ace kicker (AJo) on a Jc-8c-5h-Jd board. The turn card improves my hand significantly, but the board still contains a flush draw (clubs). My opponent, Claude, defended the Big Blind and check-called the flop, putting him on a range consisting of flush draws, middle pairs (8x), or a worse Jack (though I block Jacks and the board has two). I hold the Ac, which blocks the nut flush draw, making it slightly less likely he is on a high-equity draw, but plenty of other flush draws exist in a BB defending range. Checking back is an option to induce bluffs on the river, but given the flush draw presence, I prefer to bet to deny equity and extract value from draws and sticky pairs. A bet of roughly half the pot (~1050) charges draws appropriately while keeping worse made hands in the pot. If he has a boat (J8, 55, 88), I will likely hear about it now, but my hand is too strong to slow play.

I still have my OESD (4 or 9 gives me a straight), but the math doesn't work here. Pro bet 1050 into 2100 pot, so I'm getting 3:1 odds (need ~25% equity). With 8 outs I only have ~17% to hit on river. The board pairing with Jacks is concerning - Pro could easily have trips now and even if I hit my straight, I could lose to a full house. Implied odds aren't great on a paired board. Fold is correct.

UTG 6-max, K4s is too weak to open profitably and plays poorly out of position (often dominated by better Kx, reverse implied odds). Limping also invites isolation from the more aggressive players (Elon/Minni/Pro) and leaves us guessing postflop. Best EV is to discard and wait for stronger holdings/spots.

Preflop in middle position with J5s. FiveTwo folded UTG, first to act effectively after blinds. J5s is a marginal suited hand with poor playability, easily dominated by better Jx or spades. Deep stacks (153BB), early in tournament (hand 10/150), no need to speculate with weak hand from MP. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold 5d 3s in the Cutoff, which is a very weak unsuited hand with poor playability. Action has folded to me. While the Cutoff is a steal position, opening with this specific hand is too loose, especially given that the players in the blinds (Claude and Flash) have shown a tendency to defend or call raises in previous hands. At this early stage of the tournament (Hand 10/150), there is no incentive to get involved with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold.

On the button with pocket fives (5c5h) and ~66 BB effective, this is a clear spot to open-raise and take the initiative against just the blinds. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300) — larger than the minimum to maximize fold equity and discourage loose defend/callers, while keeping the pot manageable if I miss. If I get called I’ll plan to proceed cautiously (check/call on many flops, look to set-mine or realize equity), and I’ll respect sizable 3-bets from deep, folding to very large reraises unless the sizing and reads justify continuing.

9h6c is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing a button raise from Minni, I'd be out of position for the entire hand with a speculative holding that doesn't play well postflop. The pot odds (250 to win 450) aren't sufficient to justify continuing with this marginal hand. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 6d. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), Q6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a 3x open. Given the tournament's early stage and my healthy stack, there is no need to defend such a marginal hand that lacks connectivity and strong post-flop potential.

Preflop UTG in a 6-handed game with TJo offsuit. This is a marginal hand at best, lacking the strength to open-raise from early position. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots where our hand plays poorly postflop. Folding preserves our deep stack (153BB) for premium opportunities. Player tendencies from prior hands show loose calling, but no need to enter with this weak holding.

I am in Middle Position with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). The Under-the-Gun player has folded. In a 6-max game, K9o is generally considered too weak to open-raise from this position as it is often dominated by calling ranges (KQ, KJ, KTs) and plays poorly post-flop out of position against the button or cutoff. With a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, there is no pressure to play marginal hands. Standard play is to fold.

Cutoff with 7♠8♦, no raisers and pot is 150 with 100 to call. This hand is connected and plays well in position — I want fold equity and to avoid getting multiway where its equity falls. I'll open to a standard ~3x BB (300 total) to apply pressure to the button and the blinds while keeping the pot manageable with ~68BB stacks. If I get isolated or a single call I'll proceed to see the flop and c-bet favorable textures; if I face a sizable 3-bet I'll fold (7-8o is too weak vs a strong 3-betting range) and only continue vs a small, demonstrably light 3-bet from the blinds.

I have 2d8s on the button facing a 300 raise from Minni. This is a very weak hand with poor playability - 8-2 offsuit is near the bottom of starting hands. Even with position, calling here would be burning chips. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong hand in a 6-max game. Minni has raised to 300 from the Cutoff, which is a wide opening position. While calling is an option, it would put me out of position against both Minni and potentially the Big Blind. To take control of the hand and isolate Minni, I will put in a 3-bet. A raise to 950 is a standard size that exerts pressure on the late-position raiser and protects my equity.

We’re in the BB with 5♥2♥ facing a CO open (300) and a small blind 3-bet to 950. Even with decent immediate pot odds, we’ll be out of position against at least one strong range, often multiway, and this hand realizes equity terribly (dominated, poor playability, reverse implied odds). A 4-bet bluff is too ambitious with 52s versus a SB 3-bet range that can continue aggressively. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I opened to 300 from the cutoff for fold equity and position, but Flash (SB) 3-bet to 950 — leaving 650 to call into a 1,350 pot. 7♠8♦ offsuit is a marginal hand and plays poorly out of position against a standard-sized 3-bet: calling requires ~32.5% equity (650/(1350+650)) and I’ll be OOP on the flop with a hand that’s often dominated or has low playability. A 4-bet/raise to 1600 or a shove would be a thin, high-variance play with very little equity and is unnecessary with ~64 BB effective. Given the sizing, seat and my earlier plan to fold 7-8o to a sizable 3-bet, the correct, EV-positive line is to fold and wait for a better spot in position.

I am in UTG position with [2d, 3h], which is effectively the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em (3-2 offsuit). Being first to act with 5 players behind me and holding trash cards, there is zero strategic merit in entering the pot. I will fold immediately to preserve chips.

I have Q♥9♥ in middle position with ~64 BB effective and UTG (Pro) already folded. Limping from MP is weak — it gives the aggressive players behind (Elon in the BB, Flash on the BTN, Claude in the cutoff) cheap odds to isolate or raise. Q9s is playable and has good postflop playability and blocker value, so I want initiative and some fold equity. I’ll open to a standard ~3BB size (raise to 300) to pressure the blinds and unopened callers without overcommitting. Plan: take the lead postflop and proceed cautiously — fold to a large, committed 3-bet; continue with top pair, strong draws, or favourable board textures; otherwise control pot size and avoid marginal bloats.

Ad8d is a decent suited ace in the cutoff. Minni raised to 300 from MP. I have position on the raiser. This hand plays well postflop with its suited nature and decent high card strength. Calling to see a flop is solid here - I can outplay Minni with position if I connect, and the suited ace gives me nut flush potential. 3-betting is also viable but flatting keeps the pot manageable with this medium-strength hand.

I have pocket deuces on the button. There's a raise and a call before me. This is a perfect spot for set mining. The effective stack sizes are deep enough (around 60 big blinds) to provide the implied odds I need if I flop a set. I'll call to see the flop and fold if I don't improve and face significant pressure.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action in the SB (250 more to win 1050), and QTo has enough raw equity plus some playability (broadway top-pair potential, occasional straight draws) to continue. However, squeezing here is unattractive: we’re out of position, the open is already called twice (less fold equity), and QTo is too dominated to bloat the pot versus strong continuing ranges. Best is to flat and play postflop cautiously, folding to heavy pressure on bad textures.

AK suited is a premium hand, top of our range for 3-betting. The min-raise from Minni in middle position followed by three loose calls (Claude, Flash, FiveTwo) suggests a bloated pot with likely medium-strength hands like suited broadways, Ax, or pocket pairs. Calling would result in a 5-way pot of ~1500 OOP, which is suboptimal with position disadvantage post-flop. A 3-bet thins the field, isolates primarily against Minni's range, extracts value from worse (AQo/s, KQo/s, AJs, etc.), and applies fold equity against the limpers who show loose calling tendencies from hand history (e.g., FiveTwo and Flash frequent callers). Sizing to 1200 (4x the raise) is balanced: large enough for fold equity and value but not overcommitting stacks early. Minni's stack of 6175 allows room for further action if called. This maximizes EV over tournament.

Elon’s sizable 3‑bet to 1,200 after I opened to 300 is a firm, isolating action and I’m out of position to the button/cutoff if they peel. Calling 900 into a 2,400 pot gives only ~27% pot odds and Q♥9♥ is easily dominated by typical 3‑bet ranges (AA/KK/QQ/AK/AQ) — it’s marginal equity at best and I’ll be playing a bloated pot out of position. My earlier plan was to fold to a large, committed 3‑bet unless I had top pair/strong draws or a clear read; this spot meets that cutoff. Preserve ~62 BBs to pick better spots—fold.

Elon 3-bet to 1200 from BB after 4 callers - likely a squeeze play or strong hand. Ad8d is a decent suited ace but not strong enough to continue against a 3-bet with Flash and FiveTwo still to act behind me. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds but the reverse implied odds are bad if I'm dominated, and I could face a 4-bet behind. Elon has been aggressive (hand 6 showed he plays big pots). Better to fold and preserve stack for clearer spots.

Elon's large squeeze from the Big Blind to 1200 signifies a very strong range, likely JJ+ or AK. I am holding pocket deuces on the button, and while set mining is an option, the call of 900 represents nearly 9% of my stack. The implied odds are approximately 11:1, which is marginally low for set mining against a 3-bet, especially with the Small Blind (FiveTwo) still left to act and the potential for a 4-bet jam. Given the stack sizes and the strength shown by Elon, I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Elon’s squeeze after an open + 3 callers is weighted toward strong value (big pairs, AQ+/AK) with some bluffs, and QTo is heavily dominated by the value portion. Even though the immediate price is decent (900 to win ~3300 after calling), we’ll realize equity poorly out of position in a 3-bet pot and face high-pressure barreling lines from an aggressive opponent. 4-bet bluffing is too thin here given our hand quality and stack depth. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

UTG with K♦8♠ is a marginal, easily dominated hand in 6‑max. There are four players to act behind me (including aggressive players like Elon, Flash and Pro who have shown frequent big preflop raises), so calling 1BB to see a flop puts me out of position into likely raises and tough post‑flop decisions. Opening here would be thin and invite 3‑bets/squeezes. With ~62 BBs and plenty of better spots to pick, the correct play is to conserve chips and fold.
Ac Td is a solid hand from middle position. Standard open raise to 250 to build the pot and take initiative. This hand plays well postflop with top pair potential and decent kicker.

I hold King-Queen suited in the Cutoff, which is a premium starting hand in a 6-max game. Claude has opened for a standard 2.5x raise. To take control of the hand and capitalize on my position, I will 3-bet to 750. This puts pressure on the remaining players to fold and forces Claude to play a larger pot out of position or fold. If called, KQs plays very well post-flop, and I have the benefit of acting last on all subsequent streets.

We’re on the Button with 6♣3♥ facing an UTG+1 open (250) and a cutoff 3-bet to 750. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated when we make a pair, and it realizes equity badly versus a tight/strong 3-bet range. Calling 750 creates a bloated pot where we’ll be forced to fold frequently postflop, and a 4-bet bluff is not appropriate with this hand (no blockers, no connectivity). Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Preflop in SB facing a raise from Claude (MP, 2.5BB) and 3-bet from Flash (CO, to 7.5BB). Pot 1150, to call 700 (pot odds ~2.6:1 but irrelevant). A4o is ace-high with poor kicker, offsuit, no blockers to sets/straights. Vulnerable to domination by AQo+, AK, and any pairs. BB still to act, so potentially 3-way. Deep stacks but no reason to speculate OOP with trash. Standard fold preserves stack for better spots.

I hold 7-2 offsuit in the Big Blind, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The action ahead of me involves an open raise from Claude to 250 and a 3-bet from Flash to 750. Facing a raise and a re-raise with trash cards, I have zero equity or playability. Cold calling is impossible due to pot odds and hand strength. Bluffing (cold 4-betting) is reckless against a 3-bet range this early in the tournament with such a weak holding. The correct and only play is to fold immediately.

Flash 3-bet to 750 from cutoff. I have ATo which is decent but not premium. I need to call 500 into a pot of 1150, getting ~2.3:1 odds. ATo plays okay against a cutoff 3-bet range but can be dominated by AJ+. However, the pot odds are decent enough to call and see a flop in position. Folding is too tight, 4-betting would be overplaying this hand. I'll call and evaluate postflop.

I missed this flop with AT on Qd4s4c. Flash 3-bet preflop so likely has a strong range (AQ, KQ, QJs, pairs). Betting here as a bluff would be risky since Flash likely connects with this board or has overcards to my ten. Checking to control pot size makes sense - I have showdown value with Ace high and can evaluate based on Flash's action.

On a flop of Qd-4s-4c, I have top pair (Queens) with a King kicker. The board is paired (Fours), which reduces the likelihood that my opponent has a set of fours, though it's still possible. Claude checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. This is a board where I have a significant range advantage and a very strong hand. I will bet around 1/3 of the pot (550) to extract value from pocket pairs (77-JJ), weaker Queens (QJ, QT), and some Ace-high hands that might peel, while also protecting my equity against overcards or backdoors.

I have Ace-high on Q44 board after Flash 3-bet preflop and now c-bet 550. Flash's 3-bet range likely includes QQ, JJ+, AQ, KQ, AK - many of which hit this board. I have no made hand, just two overcards to the 4s. Even if I hit an Ace, I could be dominated by AQ/AK. The 4:1 pot odds aren't enough when my equity is likely under 20% against Flash's continuing range. Folding is correct - no need to chase with Ace-high.
T4o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a very weak hand - offsuit, unconnected, and I'm in the worst position at the table. No reason to play this trash hand from early position.

Hand 14, Middle Position. I have 8h 9d. While 8-9 offsuit is a connector, it is generally too weak to open from middle position in a 6-max game, especially with several active players left to act behind me. Given my recent aggressive plays in previous hands, I should tighten up and wait for a better spot rather than playing a marginal offsuit hand out of position.

We’re first in from the cutoff after two folds; Q3s is near the bottom of an opening range but still playable as a suited queen with position and reasonable steal equity versus the blinds. Open-limping is too weak and invites multiway pots, so I prefer an open-raise that can win the blinds immediately and gives us initiative. If Elon (BTN) 3-bets with his likely aggressive range, we can comfortably fold and lose a small amount; otherwise we can play in position versus the blinds and proceed cautiously postflop.

Jd6c is an offsuit, unconnected hand with low equity against a cutoff raiser's range. FiveTwo's raise to 250 (2.5x BB) from cutoff is standard and can include a wide range, but J6o is dominated by better jacks, has poor postflop playability, and doesn't make strong hands often. On the button, position is an advantage, but calling 250 into a 650 pot (dead money 150 from blinds) offers low reward for the risk. Folding preserves my deep 16450 stack for premium spots in this 6-handed tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with J9o facing a 2.5x raise from the Cutoff (FiveTwo). While J9o has some playability, it is a marginal hand that is easily dominated. Playing out of position against a Cutoff open is difficult, and calling also invites the Big Blind to overcall or squeeze. Given the stack depths and early tournament stage, avoiding bleeding chips in marginal spots out of position is optimal. The pot odds of 33% are not compelling enough to overcome the positional disadvantage and reverse implied odds.

I have 8♣3♦ — a very weak offsuit hand that performs poorly out of position. FiveTwo raised to 250 (2.5x BB); calling 150 into a 400 pot gives ~27% pot odds, but 8-3o has much less showdown equity versus a raiser’s stealing range and is often dominated. 3-betting would be a poor bluff here (no blockers, terrible postflop playability) and I don’t want to play multi-street out of position with this holding. With ~60 BB effective I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position with 8c 3c. This is one of the weakest hands in the deck. In a 6-max game, UTG ranges should be quite tight since there are five players left to act. 83s does not meet the requirements for an opening range from this position, and calling or raising would be a significant mistake. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

K9o is a reasonable open in 6-handed from middle position after UTG folds. Limping invites multiway play and gives up initiative; raising lets us pressure the blinds, realize equity with position vs the blinds, and keeps our range uncapped. Use a standard size that fits the table’s prior opens and discourages over-calling.

Preflop, in cutoff with 79o facing a middle position raise to 250 into 400 pot. My hand has some playability as a speculative offsuit connector with a gap, but good position postflop. FiveTwo has shown loose-aggressive tendencies in prior hands (e.g., raising weak hands like 56o, bluffing flops). Deep stacks (164xBB for me, others 60-170x) allow for implied odds if we connect on flop (straight/flush potential rare, but two pair possible). Pot odds ~1.6:1 direct, better implied multiway or heads-up. Raising is unnecessary risk without premium; folding too tight early in tournament for stack maximization. Call to realize equity in position, control pot postflop.

I am on the Button with Qd Jc. Facing a raise from Middle Position (FiveTwo) and a flat call from the Cutoff (Elon), QJo is a marginal hand. While I have the advantage of position, QJo suffers from significant reverse implied odds as it is often dominated by hands in the opener's range (AQ, AJ, KQ, KJ) and the caller's range. It does not play particularly well in multi-way pots compared to suited connectors or pairs. Given the action ahead and the potential for the blinds to squeeze or call, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot or a situation where I can be the aggressor.

I have 9♦3♦ in the small blind facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo and a call from Elon — a multiway pot with me out of position. 9-3s has very low equity and poor playability in multiway pots; calling 200 to see a flop is a speculative, often-dominated play, and a min-raise to 400 to isolate is too thin given possible 3-bets (Claude in the BB) and the callers. I’m ~60BB and should conserve chips early in the tournament and pick better spots in position. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket 7s in the BB with a raise and a caller. Getting over 4:1 pot odds (150 to win 650). Great spot to set mine. Calling is standard here - 3-betting is an option but with position disadvantage and a medium pair, I prefer to see a flop cheaply and try to hit a set.

Flopped A-T-6 with pocket 7s - I missed my set. My underpair is weak on this Ace-high board against two opponents (FiveTwo who raised preflop and Elon who called). Both could easily have an Ace or Ten. Checking is standard here - no reason to lead into two players with a weak holding.

Multiway on an A-high board, our K9o has very little immediate equity (no pair, no flush draw, only very backdoor straight potential). Being sandwiched (BB checked, but CO still to act) makes a bluff c-bet worse because we can get called/raised and end up playing a bloated pot out of position to Elon. Checking keeps the pot small, protects our checking range on ace-high textures, and lets us potentially realize equity or take a stab on a better turn card if it checks through.

Flop Ac6hTs with 7d9h gives me a weak gutshot straight draw to the 8 (for 6789T) and a backdoor heart flush draw. No pair, no strong equity. Multiway pot (3 players: Claude BB checked weak, FiveTwo preflop raiser checked which ranges from weak Ax to draws/traps). In position as CO, checking behind is optimal: controls pot size deepstacked (162xBB), realizes implied odds if turn brings 8/H/connector, avoids bloating pot with marginal hand. Bluffing multiway flop is low EV vs LAG FiveTwo who may call/raise light and passive Claude. Pot odds perfect to check/call turns if improves or float.

Turn brought the 6d pairing the board. I have pocket 7s - an underpair that loses to any Ace, Ten, or trip 6s. Both opponents checked the flop which is somewhat encouraging, but on this paired board my hand is very marginal. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a terrible spot. Checking keeps the pot controlled with my showdown value. If they have air, they might check through and I can win at showdown. No need to bloat the pot OOP with a vulnerable underpair.

We have K9o with no pair and essentially no draw on A-T-6-6. While the paired turn is a decent card to represent (it reduces their strong made-hand combos), this is still multiway with Elon behind, and both opponents can easily have a lot of Ax that checked flop for pot control, plus some 6x that will never fold. Betting here is mostly a one-and-done bluff with very poor equity when called and risks getting peeled/raised, putting us in a tough spot.
