Checking keeps the pot small, lets us realize our modest showdown value (K-high can still win if they both whiffed), and we can reassess on the river if it checks through or improves us.

Turn board pairs the 6s (Ac6hTs6d), giving me no improvement from flop's weak gutshot (still need 8 for straight) and backdoor FH draw. Equity remains low (~10-15% vs ranges: FiveTwo's checked MP raise likely Ax/sets/draws, Claude's BB call speculative/weak). Two checks ahead suggest weakness or traps, but multiway pot deepstacked (162xBB), no position advantage lost since I'm last. Betting/raising as bluff low EV: poor fold equity multiway, risks stacks on marginal spot early tournament. Checking realizes equity cheaply to river (hoping 8/H/J), controls pot, avoids spew. Consistent with preflop/flop plan of playing speculative hand postflop in position.

River completes with 8s. Board: Ac-6h-Ts-6d-8s. I have pocket 7s - an underpair. Everyone checked flop and turn, indicating weakness. My 77 has some showdown value but betting is risky - I only get called by better hands (any Ace, Ten, 8, or 6). The checking pattern suggests opponents have marginal holdings too. Best play is to check and try to win at showdown. My 77 beats missed draws and lower pocket pairs.

River is A♣ T♠ 6h 6d 8♠, three-way, after everyone checked flop/turn and Claude checks river. With K♦9♠ we have pure K-high and essentially no credible value target; any ace, ten, pocket pair, or 6x beats us and will often call. Bluffing into two players is low-fold-equity (especially because 6x/slow-played Ax exist), and betting also exposes us to a raise from Elon with us having to forfeit extra chips. We take the free showdown—K-high can still win versus complete whiffs—and avoid lighting chips on fire in a low-success multiway bluff spot.

River 8s perfectly completes my gutshot straight draw from flop/turn (7-9 makes 6-7-8-9-10 straight on Ac-10-6-6-8 board). This is a very strong hand, likely the best multiway: beats Ax (no kicker issues for straight), any single pair, two-pair (A6/A8/66/68/106 etc.), and most draws missed (no flush). Two checks ahead indicate weakness (Claude passive BB, FiveTwo checked preflop raise range likely Ax/draws/sets but no betting). Deep stacks, early tournament (hand 15/150), position last: optimal to value bet for thin value from worse Ax/pairs/traps. Bet sizing 2/3 pot (~533) balances value/protection vs draws (spade backdoor missed), induces calls from LAG FiveTwo/history aggressive, avoids overbetting into potential monsters (sets unlikely). Checking risks giving free showdown or missed value.

Elon bets 550 into 800 on the river after everyone checked flop and turn. I have pocket 7s - an underpair. The passive action throughout suggested weakness, but now Elon is betting. With this board (A-T-8-6-6), my 77 loses to any Ace, Ten, 8, 6, or higher pocket pair. While the bet could be a bluff exploiting the weakness shown, the pot odds of ~29% don't justify calling when so many holdings beat me. Elon's bet sizing looks like thin value with an 8 or even a weak Ace he was checking down. Folding is the disciplined play here.

We get to the river with pure K-high after checking three streets. Elon's small river stab into a checked line can include bluffs, but his value range is still very wide here (any Ax that pot-controlled, Tx, 8x that improved, and pocket pairs like 77–99), and our K blocks some of the natural KQ/KJ-type bluffs we’d like him to have. With no pair and no relevant blockers to his strong hands (we don’t block Ax/6x), this is too thin to pay off. Raising would just torch chips because Ax/6x never fold and worse hands mostly fold anyway. Preserve stack and fold.

UTG in a 6-max game with several capable/aggressive players behind, J8 offsuit is too weak to open and performs poorly when called/3-bet. Limping invites multiway play where we’re dominated and realize equity badly. Best stack-preserving, +EV choice is to fold and wait for stronger UTG holdings.

K3h suited is a weak hand from middle position in a 6-handed game. King-high with a poor kicker (3), vulnerable to domination by better kings. Preflop, only blinds posted (pot 150, to-call 100), UTG folded. Raising would be overplaying; limping invites multiway pots where this hand plays poorly postflop due to lack of showdown value and reverse implied odds. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in a deep-stack tournament setting. Opponents have shown aggression, but no need to speculate here.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Seven offsuit (Q7o). The action has folded to me. While the Cutoff is a late position suitable for stealing the blinds, Q7o is too weak to include in a standard opening range (typically top 30-35%). It has poor playability post-flop and creates reverse implied odds issues. With three players left to act (Button, Small Blind, Big Blind), the likelihood of running into a better hand is significant. The standard and disciplined play here is to fold.

I'm on the button with 5♠3♦ and ~60bb effective. With only the blinds to act I should open a wide stealing range rather than limp — the positional advantage and fold equity make a steal profitable even with a weak hand. I’ll raise to 250 (≈2.5bb): big enough to pressure the blinds but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. If I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll play cautiously in position and look for spots to c‑bet or check back depending on the flop texture.
2-6 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a button raise, I have no equity worth investing here - no connectivity, no high cards, poor playability OOP. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack suited, a premium hand. The Button (Minni) has opened for 2.5x. Given the previous hands, Minni has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets. Even if called, AJs plays very well post-flop, though I will be out of position. I will 3-bet to 850 for value and to take control of the hand.

I opened to 250 on the button with 5♠3♦ intending a steal, but Flash (BB) 3‑bet to 850. That leaves 600 to call into a ~1,150 pot, so I’d need ~34% equity to break even (600/(1150+600)≈34%). 5‑3 offsuit has very poor equity and post‑flop playability versus a typical BB 3‑bet range, and I already planned to fold to a sizable 3‑bet. 4‑betting would be a pure bluff and unnecessarily risky here, so I fold and preserve my ~57bb stack for better spots.

UTG with TJo offsuit is a standard fold in a 6-handed game. This hand has poor playability, dominated equity against typical raising ranges, and low strength post-flop from early position. Limping is weak and invites multi-way pots where it plays poorly. Raising risks playing out of position with a marginal hand against competent opponents. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a deep-stack tournament.

I am in Middle Position with Q4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and no connectivity. With several players left to act behind me, opening the pot or limping is not profitable. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I have T♠3♦ in the cutoff. With UTG and MP folded there are still three players to act behind me (button Claude, SB Flash, BB FiveTwo). Pot is 150; call is 100 and min-raise is 200. T3o is a weak, offsuit, poorly connected hand that is often dominated and has very poor playability in multiway pots. The table has shown willingness to defend and 3‑bet, so my fold equity is limited. With ~57 BB I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot (button or late-position steal with a stronger hand). I will fold this marginal holding rather than limp or open into multiple active opponents.

I have pocket Kings on the button - a premium hand. With 3 players folded to me, I should raise for value. Standard open raise to 250-300 (2.5-3x BB) to build the pot while potentially getting calls from worse hands. KK is too strong to slow play preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with As5s, a strong suited Ace that performs well against a standard Button opening range. Claude has raised to 275 from the Button. While calling is an option to see a flop, 3-betting allows me to take control of the hand, potentially win the pot immediately, or build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop equity (nut flush potential and an Ace blocker). I will raise to 975 to put pressure on the Button and the Big Blind.

We’re in the BB facing a BTN open and a SB 3-bet, and we’ll be out of position against at least Flash (often also Claude continuing). 6♠3♠ has very poor playability here: it’s too weak to 4-bet (no blockers, low fold equity versus a SB 3-bet range) and too low to profitably defend as a call given how often we’ll be dominated or forced to fold on many flops. Despite the price, our equity realization OOP in a 3-bet pot is bad and the reverse implied odds are high. Best is to preserve chips and wait for a better defend/3-bet candidate.

I have KK and Flash 3-bet me to 975. This is a clear 4-bet spot with pocket Kings. Flash could be 3-betting light against my button open, or have a strong hand like QQ/AK/JJ. With my stack at 6325 and the pot already building, I should 4-bet to around 2500. This commits me to the hand and gets value from worse hands while building the pot. I'm happy to get it all in preflop with KK against Flash's range.

My initial 3-bet with As5s was a semi-bluff designed to use my Ace blocker and fold equity against a standard Button opening range. However, Claude's 4-bet to 2500 is a very strong response, especially considering he started the hand with only 4100 chips (41 big blinds). By 4-betting to 2500, he has committed more than half of his stack and is effectively pot-committed to any shove. His range here is likely extremely tight, consisting of hands like QQ+, AK, and maybe JJ. Against this range, As5s is a significant underdog (roughly 25-30% equity). While the pot odds to call are somewhat attractive, I would be risking nearly 40% of my stack on a hand where I am almost certainly dominated. In a tournament setting, preserving my 9500-chip stack is more prudent than gambling as a large underdog against a short stack's shown strength.

I am holding King-Deuce offsuit (K2o) in UTG position. This is a very weak hand that falls well outside the standard opening range for Early Position in a 6-max game. With active players yet to act and being out of position post-flop, playing this hand has a negative expected value. The correct strategic move is to fold immediately.

I have J♦9♦ in middle position with ~57 BB. This is a playable, suited connector that performs well heads-up and has good post-flop playability. It's early in the tournament and folded around to me, so I want to seize the initiative and avoid a multiway limp. A standard 2.5xBB open (250) gives decent fold equity against the blinds, keeps the pot manageable versus the deep stacks, and allows me to play position versus the cutoff/button/SB. If I face a large 3-bet I can fold and preserve my stack; versus a cold call I can proceed to use my positional advantage and flush/straight equity. Therefore I open-raise to 250.
J5 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't play well against a middle position raise. No compelling reason to get involved with this holding - easy fold.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand. Minni opened from middle position. In previous hands (Hand #11, Hand #16), Minni has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets. By raising to 750, I can potentially take the pot down immediately or isolate Minni while playing the rest of the hand in position. This also discourages the blinds (FiveTwo and Elon) from entering the pot cheaply.

We’re facing an MP open to 250 and a fairly large BTN squeeze to 750 while we’re out of position in the small blind with A7o. This hand has poor playability OOP in a bloated pot and suffers from heavy reverse implied odds when we make top pair (dominated by AK/AQ/AJ that are very live in the 3-bettor’s range). Calling also keeps the BB in and invites a squeeze/overcall, further reducing equity realization. A 4-bet bluff is unattractive given the sizing and stack depths: we’d need a very high combined fold frequency and we’re not happy continuing versus a jam or a call in a low-SPR pot. Best is to cut losses and preserve the stack.

J3s is a marginal suited connector but too weak to defend against a 3-bet from the button after an MP open-raise. I'm OOP postflop, and the hand has poor playability and equity against likely strong 3-bet ranges from Flash, who has shown aggression on the button in prior hands. Pot odds are about 1.77:1 effective (650 to 1150), requiring ~36% equity, which J3s doesn't have vs. polarized 3-bet range. Folding preserves stack for better spots in deep-stack tournament.

I have Jd9d (~55 BB). I opened to 250 from MP and Flash (button) 3-bet to 750. Folding is too tight with a suited connector that has good equity and implied odds; a 4-bet (min to 1250) would commit too many chips with a marginal hand and will often face a stronger range or a call that leaves us OOP. Pot is 1150 and I must call 500 more (750 total), giving ~3.3:1 pot odds (need ~23% equity) — reasonable versus a likely wide button 3-bet range. I’ll call 750 total and play the flop cautiously out of position: look to continue on favorable diamond/connected flops, control pot on dry boards, and fold to heavy, polarized aggression.

I opened J♦9♦ and called Flash’s button 3‑bet. Flop Q♣8♣3♠ gives me no pair and only a 4‑out gutshot to a Ten (plus negligible runner‑runner diamond equity) — roughly ~8.7% to hit on the turn, ~16% by the river. I’m out of position against a 3‑bettor on a board that favors their c‑betting range (Qx and club combos). Pot is 1,650 (~16.5 BB) and I shouldn’t build the pot with a naked draw and marginal equity. Plan: check and control the size. If Flash checks back I keep the initiative on later streets; if he bets a very small size (uncommonly tiny, ~<1/6 pot) I can call for the odds to chase my gutshot, but I will fold to normal or large continuation bets (~1/3 pot or bigger) because my equity (~16%) doesn’t justify calling bigger bets out of position. No bluff‑raise — too risky OOP with no fold equity against a 3‑bettor.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker (TPKK) on a board of Qc-8c-3s. After 3-betting pre-flop and being called by Minni, her check on the flop indicates weakness or a pot-control line. I need to bet here for value against hands like QJ, QT, JJ, TT, or club flush draws. A bet of around 40% of the pot is a standard continuation bet that keeps her range wide while protecting my hand.

I opened Jd9d and called Flash’s preflop 3‑bet. On Qc‑8c‑3s I checked and Flash bet 650. The pot after his bet is 2,300 and calling 650 would make the pot 2,950, so I need roughly 650/2950 ≈ 22% equity to break even. My hand has meaningful equity here: 3 jacks + 3 nines + 4 tens = ~10 clean outs to make a pair or a straight and roughly ~38% to improve by the river (plus a tiny amount of backdoor diamond equity). Flash (button 3‑bettor) will often c‑bet this board with a wide range (Qx, clubs, broadways and bluffs), so my equity against that continuing range comfortably exceeds the ~22% threshold. Raising OOP as a bluff is unattractive — I lack reliable fold equity and would bloat the pot with a marginal hand — so the correct line is to call the 650, keep the pot controllable, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy turn aggression if I completely miss).

The turn (Qc 8c 3s 3h) leaves me with J♦9♦ — no pair, no club draw and essentially no realistic equity. Flash checked the turn after c‑betting the flop, which is consistent with either a missed hand exercising pot control or a medium-strength holding (Qx, 8x, paired 3, small pocket). Betting here would be a pure out‑of‑position bluff with poor blockers and limited fold equity against a 3‑bettor’s continuing range, and would bloat the pot and risk a large portion of my ~43.8BB stack for little expected gain. I’m preserving stack and controling pot size: check and re‑evaluate on the river (fold to meaningful river aggression, consider a river bluff only with a very favorable runout or clear blocker).

I have top pair with a King kicker on a board that hasn't changed much with the 3h on the turn. Minni has checked to me twice now. I need to continue extracting value from weaker Queens like QJ or QT and mid-pocket pairs like JJ or TT that might still be calling. I also want to charge any club flush draws that are sticking around. A bet of 1200 into the 2950 pot is approximately 40%, keeping the price reasonable for her to call with marginal hands while continuing to build the pot in my favor.

Facing a 1,200 turn bet into a 4,150 pot (I must call 1,200 to win ~5,350), I need ~1,200 / (4,150+1,200) ≈ 22.4% equity to justify a call. With J♦9♦ on Qc‑8c‑3s‑3h I have only 6 clean outs (three jacks + three nines) to pair on the river ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% — well below the required equity. I have no backdoor flush/straight and I’m out of position vs a button 3‑bettor who has shown willingness to barrel — his line is consistent with a wide value/continuation range (Qx, clubs, small pairs) that beats a single pair if I hit. Raising as a bluff (min to 2,400) is too risky OOP with poor blockers and would bloat the pot unnecessarily. Preserving ~43.8 BB for better spots and folding marginal, low‑equity holdings is the best long‑term decision here.

Holding 6♥3♠ UTG in a 6-max game is an automatic fold. This is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand that will be dominated by virtually any standard opening range and plays terribly out of position against five opponents. Several players at this table have shown willingness to make large raises and 3-bets, so attempting a steal or limp/call here is low EV. With ~44 BB I want to preserve chips for better spots in late position or vs single opponents — fold and wait for a stronger hand.
8-4 offsuit is a trash hand - not connected, no flush potential, no high cards. Easy fold from middle position with 4 players still to act behind me.

I am holding T3 offsuit in the cutoff. This is one of the weakest hands in the game and is well outside any standard opening range from this position. With three active players behind me and no significant chips invested, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot. Folding is the only viable play.

Folds to me on the button with QJo, a clear steal/open in 6-max: strong enough to realize equity in position and apply pressure to blinds’ wide defending ranges. I’ll use a standard ~2.5bb sizing to build the pot while keeping risk reasonable versus 3-bets.

7h2d is one of the worst possible starting hands, completely uncoordinated and offsuit. Facing a button raise from FiveTwo after folds around, this is a clear fold from the small blind. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives poor implied odds out of position with no playable equity or hand potential. No value in raising or bluffing preflop here. Preserve stack for better spots.

I hold AcQd in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x open from the Button (FiveTwo). With 80BB effective stacks, AQo is a strong hand that plays well against a wide Button opening range. While a 3-bet for value is a viable option to deny equity and build a pot, calling is a standard, lower-variance play that keeps the opponent's range wide (including hands I dominate like AJ, AT, KQ) and avoids being forced to fold to a 4-bet. Playing out of position, I opt to flat call to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold AcQd (Top Pair, Queen Kicker) on an Ad Kd 5h board. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Button), checking is the standard and correct play. This board texture (Ace-King high) heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, so leading out (donk betting) would generally fold out their bluffs while isolating me against their strongest hands (AK, AA, KK, two pair). By checking, I allow the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wider range, including bluffs and worse value hands (like weaker Aces or Kings). I plan to check-call if they bet.

Heads-up in position as the preflop raiser on A-K-5 (two diamonds). My QJ has no made hand but does have a gutshot to the nuts (T) and benefits from folding out Pro’s weakest BB defend hands (small pairs, some suited connectors, random floats). I also want to deny equity to diamond/runner-runner holdings. This board is good for my perceived range advantage (lots of strong Ax/AK), so a small, range-style c-bet is appropriate and keeps me from bloating the pot with a marginal holding. If Pro check-raises, I can comfortably fold and preserve stack.

I hold Top Pair with a Queen Kicker (AcQd) on an Ad-Kd-5h board. The opponent (Button) c-bet 200 (approx. 36% pot). This board texture strongly favors the pre-flop raiser, so their wide c-betting range includes value hands I beat (AJ, AT, KQ), draws (diamonds, gutshots), and pure bluffs. Raising here would likely fold out their bluffs and isolate me against hands that beat me (AK, sets, two pair). Calling is the optimal line to keep their range wide, control the pot size while out of position, and allow them to fire again on later streets with inferior hands or bluffs.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AcQd) on a board of Ad-Kd-5h-5c. The turn card pairs the board, which slightly reduces the combinations of two-pair hands my opponent might have (like A5 or K5), though it introduces trip fives possibilities. Being out of position against the Button aggressor who c-bet the flop, checking remains the best strategy. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs and weaker hands while isolating me against their value range (AK, AA, KK, 5x). By checking, I induce bluffs from missed draws or weaker holdings and allow the opponent to value-own themselves with worse Aces or Kings. I intend to call a reasonable bet on this street as my hand is still very strong relative to a wide Button range.

Turn pairs the 5, and after my small flop c-bet gets called, Pro’s continuing range is still heavy Ax, some Kx, and diamond draws. My QJ has only a gutshot to the nuts (T) and no diamond, so a second barrel doesn’t generate great fold equity (Ax rarely folds, Kx/draws often peel) and risks getting check-raised off my equity. In position I can take the free card, realize my 4 clean outs to Broadway, and keep my range protected (I can also check back some strong Ax/AK here). If Pro checks river, I can decide whether to bluff based on the river card; if I hit T, I can go for value.

The opponent (Button) checked back the turn on an A-K-5-5 board, which strongly suggests a capped range consisting of marginal showdown value (like weak Ax or Kx) or missed draws, rather than strong hands like AK or trips which would likely continue betting. With Top Pair Good Kicker (AcQd), I am likely ahead of their checking range. Checking the river risks allowing the opponent to check back hands like Kx or weaker Ax that have showdown value but won't bet themselves. Therefore, I elect to lead out with a small value bet (block bet) to extract value from these holdings. The board texture is static on the river (no completed flush), making a bluff from the opponent less likely if I check. A bet of 425 (approx 45% pot) is small enough to get called by Kx or splitting Ax.

River spot is a pure bluff-catch: Q♠J♣ has no pair and no straight/flush, so we only win versus missed draws / air. Pro’s line (BB defend → check/call small flop c-bet on A-K-5♦♦ → check turn on paired 5 → small-ish river lead 425 into ~1.3–1.5k) is very consistent with taking a stab after we checked back turn and look capped. This runout also produces a lot of natural bluffs in his range (missed diamond draws, some floats/pairs turning into bluffs), and our hand blocks some of his more common thin-value Ax continues like AQ/AJ (one Q and one J removed), slightly increasing the bluff density. With pot odds needing only ~24% bluffs, calling is higher EV than folding, while raising as a bluff is unnecessary/high variance because many Ax hands won’t fold to a raise here. ამიტომ we call and expect to pick off enough bluffs.
4d8d is a weak suited connector from UTG - the worst position at the table. This hand doesn't have enough equity to open from early position where I'll face 5 players behind me. Easy fold.

I am in middle position with pocket sixes. This is a standard opening hand in a 6-max game. I will raise to 2.5 times the big blind to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set mine if I get called. My stack is healthy, and a standard raise is appropriate here.

Flash is opening from middle position to 2.5bb and has shown a fairly aggressive preflop posture. In the cutoff, A8o is too weak to flat purely for value (domination issues when we make top pair), but it’s a reasonable light 3-bet candidate because it blocks strong continues (A-K/A-Q/A-J) and plays better when we take the betting lead. Raising also reduces the chance Elon (button, deep) squeezes over a flat and forces us out. If Flash 4-bets, we can comfortably fold; if he calls, we can c-bet favorable textures and keep pots controlled when we pair the ace with a weak kicker.

Js7h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing a raise from Flash (MP) and a 3-bet from FiveTwo (CO), their ranges are strong (pairs, broadways, suited aces, etc.). Calling 800 into a 1200 pot gets ~1.5:1, but postflop I'll be OOP to FiveTwo, multiway likely if Flash calls, and my hand doesn't connect well enough to justify. 4-betting has no value with this hand—no fold equity worth risking against aggressive opponents. FiveTwo has shown aggression historically, but folding marginal hands preserves stack for better spots. Deep stacked tournament, no need to speculate here.

I am in the Small Blind holding 2s 5s. The action has been a raise to 2.5bb by Flash in Middle Position, followed by a 3-bet to 8bb by FiveTwo in the Cutoff. Calling a 3-bet cold with 5-high out of position is a losing play. Additionally, the original raiser (Flash) is still to act behind me and could 4-bet. My hand has very poor playability and equity against a 3-betting range. This is a straightforward fold.

I’m in the big blind with Qc3c — a suited but otherwise marginal hand — facing a 3-bet to 800 from the cutoff after a 250 MP open. Folding is too tight given the pot odds and my ~42bb stack, and 4-betting as a squeeze with Q3s would be over-aggressive and often dominated. The pot is 1,200 and I need to put in 700 more (so 800 total committed), which gives me roughly 36% equity required — borderline but reasonable to see a flop with a suited queen. I’ll call to 800 and proceed cautiously postflop: if I miss and face significant pressure I’ll fold; I’ll continue only with clear equity (top pair, strong draws, two-pair+, or favorable runouts).

I originally opened for 250 with pocket sixes. FiveTwo 3-bet to 800, and Minni cold-called from the big blind. I need to call 550 more into a pot of 1900. These are excellent pot odds (roughly 3.5:1), and the effective stacks are deep enough to justify set-mining. Minni's cold-call of a 3-bet usually indicates a strong range, which increases my implied odds if I hit a six on the flop, as I'm likely to get paid off by a big overpair. I will call to see the flop and fold if I don't improve, unless the board texture is extremely favorable.

Flop (7c 9d 4c) with Qc3c: I have a four‑card club draw (9 outs) and very little showdown value without improvement. Pot is 2,450 and I’m first to act. Preflop I called the 3‑bet so I’m not committed — I want to use initiative to either take the pot now or realize my strong draw at a reasonable price. A ~1/3 pot semi‑bluff pressures single‑raised/missed overcard holdings (AK/AQ/KQ/etc.) and small pairs, but doesn’t overcommit me vs two opponents. I’ll bet 800 chips: that gives fold equity, builds the pot when I have equity, and keeps enough stack behind to fold to a raise (I’m folding to heavy flop aggression). If called I’ll plan to continue only with clear improvement (flush/pair/two‑pair) or the correct odds; if checked around I’ll often check the turn to control the pot.

Pre-flop, I called a 3-bet from FiveTwo, and Minni (BB) also called. The flop is 7c-9d-4c. Minni has now 'donk-bet' 800 into a pot of 3250. This lead into the pre-flop 3-bettor and the original raiser usually indicates a hand that connected with the board (like a pair of 7s, 9s, or a flush draw). My pocket sixes are now an underpair to two cards on the board. Furthermore, FiveTwo is still to act behind me and could easily have an overpair or a stronger hand. Given the strength shown by Minni and the risk of FiveTwo raising, my hand is not strong enough to continue. I am folding to preserve my stack.

This is a 3-bet pot where we should have the range advantage, but Minni’s small donk lead (800 into 3250) from a short stack is still very often at least a made pair (9x/7x/44/77/99) or a draw that won’t fold. With Ac8h on 7c9d4c we have essentially ace-high with no real draw (only a runner-runner club possibility) and only 3 clean outs to top pair, which is not enough to profitably call 800 needing ~20% immediate equity. Turning this into a bluff raise is also poor because Minni’s remaining stack is shallow and they’re unlikely to fold any pair. Best play is to cut losses and fold.

I am UTG with Five of Hearts and Four of Clubs, which is a very weak hand. In early position, it is standard to fold such holdings as they have low equity and are difficult to play profitably post-flop against multiple opponents yet to act.

With 66 in middle position at 6-max, this is a standard open. We have decent immediate fold equity vs the blinds and can realize strong implied odds when we flop a set, while also being able to c-bet selectively on favorable boards. I’ll use a small, consistent sizing that keeps my range wide and doesn’t bloat the pot unnecessarily; if faced with a large 3-bet from late position, I can mostly continue only at good price/implied odds and otherwise let it go.

Preflop in cutoff facing a middle position raise to 250 from FiveTwo (6350 stack). T9d is a strong suited connector with great playability, position, and implied odds. Pot is 400, calling 250 offers good odds (~1.6:1 direct, better implied). FiveTwo has shown aggression but folds to resistance sometimes. Prefer calling over 3-betting to keep pot multi-way potential and realize equity postflop in position rather than risk a cooler or shove. Folding too tight early with deep stacks.

I am on the Button with 8h 9s (98o). Facing a raise from MP and a call from the Cutoff, my hand is too weak to continue. Although I have position, 98o plays poorly in multi-way pots and is often dominated. The pot odds do not justify calling with an unsuited connector against this action. Folding is the standard play.

I'm in the SB with K♠2♣ facing a 250 open from FiveTwo plus a call from Elon — a multiway, out-of-position spot. K2o is frequently dominated and has very poor equity against an opener and a caller; calling 200 to play OOP (risking 200 into a ~650 pot) requires ~23% equity, which K2o doesn't have here. A min-raise/bluff is unattractive because Claude (BB) can still reraise and callers behind will often continue, putting me in tough spots. With ~60 BB effective I should preserve chips for better, cleaner spots. Fold.

Q7 suited in the BB facing a raise to 250 with one caller. Pot is 650 and I need 150 to call, getting over 4:1 odds. Suited hands with flush potential play reasonably well in multiway pots. This is a standard defend from the BB with decent implied odds.

I flopped middle pair with Q7h on 872 rainbow. Decent showdown value but vulnerable. Two opponents behind me (FiveTwo who raised preflop and Elon who called). Checking is best here - I can evaluate their action before committing more chips. If they check behind, I can reevaluate on the turn. If they bet, I can decide whether to call or fold based on the sizing.

We’re multiway and I’m out of position to Elon, holding a marginal underpair (66) on 8-7-2 rainbow. This flop connects well with both callers’ ranges (lots of 7x/8x, pairs, and some straight-ish hands), so a c-bet is likely to get called and put me in a tough spot on many turns with very limited improvement (mostly just 2 outs to a set). Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes my showdown value vs overcards, and lets me respond to any stab based on sizing/action.

Flop 8d-7c-2s gives me an open-ended straight draw (6 or J makes the nuts) with 9-Td, plus backdoor diamond flush draw on a dry, uncoordinated board. ~32% equity heads-up against a pair, higher multiway. Both opponents checked (FiveTwo preflop raiser might have overpair/missed flop; Claude wide BB defend). Position allows control. Pot 800, deep stacks (mine 16.6k, others ~6-7k). Betting ~2/3 pot (550) for value (beats bluffs/draws), protection (deny equity), and fold equity while building pot with strong draw. Checking risks free card or multiway pot where my equity dilutes. Prefer betting over checkback with initiative.

I have middle pair (7s) with a queen kicker on a dry 872 board. Elon bet 550 into ~800, a standard c-bet size. FiveTwo checked and is likely done. Getting about 2.5:1 odds (550 to win ~1350). My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable to overpairs and top pair. On this dry board, I can call and reassess on the turn - if he barrels again, I can fold. The odds are reasonable enough to peel one street.

We have an underpair (66) on 8-7-2 rainbow in a 3-way pot. After we check, Elon fires 550 into ~800 and Claude cold-calls, which heavily weights at least one opponent toward a made hand (7x/8x/overpairs/sets) and significantly reduces bluff frequency. Our immediate equity is poor (mostly two outs to a set), we have no meaningful backdoors, and being sandwiched between caller and bettor makes it hard to realize whatever thin showdown value we have. The implied odds to peel for a turn set aren’t clean here either (we can be up against 77/88, and future action often forces us to fold when we miss). Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

Elon bet the flop but now checked the turn when the King came. This is either pot control with a medium hand (like 8x), giving up on a bluff, or occasionally slowplaying. My middle pair (7s) with Q kicker has some showdown value but is quite vulnerable. The King is a scare card that could have helped his range. With the action checking to me, I should check back for pot control - betting only gets called by better hands and I can try to see a cheap showdown.

Heads-up on turn with pot 1900, Claude checks. My T9d holds the nut flush draw (9 diamonds outs) + open-ended straight draw (8 straight outs, ~15 unique outs for ~30-35% equity vs. typical calling range). Strong combo draw on a board that got more coordinated with Kd (diamonds now prominent). Previous flop bet/continuation rep range includes overpairs, Ax, sets, now turning draw stronger. Claude called flop as BB defender (likely mid-pair, weak Ax, stubborn draw), double check suggests weakness or trap but unlikely given history. Position + deep effective stacks (his 6875) favor semi-bluff bet: extract value from worse (pairs <K, missed Ax), protect equity, fold equity vs. floats/Ax, build pot for river shove potential. Check risks giving free card to draws or check-behind with showdown value. Size ~2/3 pot (1300) balances value/protection/fold equity without overbetting.

Elon double-barreled with 1300 into a pot of ~1900. I have middle pair (7s) with Q kicker on 8-7-2-K. The King turn is a bad card for my hand - it either helped Elon's range (KQ, KJ type hands that called preflop) or he's continuing with an overpair/top pair. My hand beats only bluffs and worse 7x. Getting ~2.5:1 odds, I'd need to be good about 28% of the time, but against a double-barrel on this runout, middle pair weak kicker is just too vulnerable. Fold and preserve chips.

UTG 6-handed with 7-2 offsuit is at the very bottom of the range: poor high-card value, dominated when we make a pair, and weak playability multiway. Limping invites over-limps/isolations and puts us in tough postflop spots out of position; raising has little fold equity versus this lineup and burns chips. Preserve stack for better UTG opens.

3s5d is a complete trash hand: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with minimal playability. In middle position with 6-handed play and deep stacks, this is an automatic fold preflop. No stealing incentive as blinds are tight based on history, and pot is small. Preserve stack for premium hands.

I am in the Cutoff with Jack-Deuce suited (J2s), a very weak hand. While it is folded to me, the opponents in the blinds (Claude and Flash) have shown tendencies to defend or 3-bet aggressively. Opening this hand would be a loose steal attempt with poor post-flop playability and negative expected value against active players. With a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, there is no pressure to force action with sub-optimal holdings. The disciplined play is to fold.

On the button with J♥3♦: the hand itself is weak and often dominated, but being last to act postflop gives significant leverage. With ~60 BB I want to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds rather than limp and allow cheap multiway flops where this hand plays poorly. A standard 2.5x BB open (250 total) is a small investment that picks up the pot a lot of the time and keeps my range wide. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, call if I get isolated and the price is reasonable, and use position to c-bet/fold on dry boards or realize equity on connected/flush-draw boards.

KTo in the SB facing a button raise from Minni. This is a decent hand to defend with, though I'm out of position. Minni has been active with raises. I could 3-bet to take control, but KTo plays better as a call since it has good showdown potential but isn't strong enough to build a big pot OOP. Flash could squeeze behind, but I'll take that risk. Calling 200 to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 3h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni opened from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call the remaining 150 chips, this hand has extremely poor equity and playability, especially out of position against two opponents. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand rather than risk further chips in a multi-way pot with garbage cards.

Flopped top pair with KTo on K89 board. I'm OOP heads up against Minni who raised from the button. This is a strong hand that should bet for value - I can get called by worse kings, nines, eights, and straight draws (JT, T7). No flush draw present. Betting around 1/3 pot for thin value and to charge draws.

I opened preflop and am on the button with J♥3♦ — I have no pair and only remote backdoor outs on K♣‑9♥‑8♦. Claude’s small 225 probe into a 825 pot looks like a wide c‑bet (Kx, 9x, small pairs, and bluffs). Calling 225 is cheap: I invest 225 to win ~1275 (break‑even ≈18%), and with position I can see a turn and fold to real aggression if I don’t improve. Raising here would commit more chips bluffing with very little backup equity, and folding surrenders too much to a tiny bet. Plan: call to use positional advantage, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a large turn raise unless I improve or pick up a good read.

Turn 6d doesn't change much. I still have top pair good kicker on K986. Minni called my flop bet - could have worse kings, middle pairs, or draws like JT/T7. Two diamonds now but no made flush yet. I should continue betting for value - around half pot to extract from worse hands and charge draws.

I’m on the button with J♥3♦ and I’ve missed — board Kc‑9h‑8d‑6d gives me no pair and no realistic draw. Claude’s turn bet is 500 into a 1550 pot; calling requires roughly 24% equity. My only raw outs are three Jacks and three Threes (≈6 outs ≈13%), and several of those will still lose if Claude holds Kx/9x or a small pair. Raising to 1000 would be a large semibluff with very little backup equity and risks a costly call or 3‑bet. Claude’s line (call pre, small flop probe, bigger turn bet) looks consistent with value hands that dominate me. I planned to fold to meaningful turn aggression if I didn’t improve — I haven’t, so fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

Pocket 77 is a strong hand UTG in a 6-handed tournament. Standard play is to open-raise for value and to narrow the field. With deep stacks (mine 17950, others 5k-11k), raise to 3x BB (300) to build the pot while maintaining fold equity against speculative hands. Limping is weak and invites multiway pots where 77 is vulnerable to overcards. Folding is too tight preflop. No history indicates passive play, and recent hands show aggressive opening.

I hold Qc 2c in Middle position facing a raise to 300 from the UTG player (Elon). Q2 suited is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated by an UTG opening range. With several players left to act behind me and poor equity against the raiser, I cannot profitably call or raise. Folding is the only clear option.

Elon opened UTG to 300. I have J♥T♠ offsuit in the cutoff with 5,500 (≈55bb). Calling 300 into a 450 pot requires roughly 40% equity to break even — JTo offsuit is well below that versus a reasonable UTG opening range. There are three players left to act (button, SB, BB) who can 3‑bet or call and turn this into a multiway pot where JTo performs poorly. A light 3‑bet is an option but is thin here given opponent tendencies and stack depths (I can be 4‑bet or face callers), so the lowest-variance + EV play is to preserve chips and fold this marginal hand.

K2o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise from Elon. Even with button position, K2o plays poorly against a UTG opening range - I'll often be dominated when I hit a King, and the 2 provides little support. The hand lacks playability to justify calling 3BB. Fold is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 5h. Elon raised from UTG to 300. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against a UTG range, especially with several players still to act and a large stack opening. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

Elon’s UTG 3x open represents a relatively strong range, and we’re out of position with a very dominated offsuit hand (T3o). Even getting a decent price (200 to win 450 now), the combination of poor playability, reverse implied odds (making weak tens/third pair), and positional disadvantage makes defending unprofitable. A bluff 3-bet is also too loose here versus UTG. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I am in UTG position with Ace-Six offsuit (Ah 6s). In a 6-handed game, A6o is generally too weak to open from early position as it is often dominated by better Aces and has poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. The Big Blind (Elon) has a large stack and has shown aggression in previous hands, making stealing blinds or playing marginal hands riskier. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from UTG.

J♣9♣ is a decent, speculative hand but not a premium opener from middle with four players left to act and an aggressive, deep big blind (Elon). The pot is 150 and calling 100 gives good pot odds (~28.6% break-even equity) to see a flop and realize implied odds with flush/straight and two-pair possibilities. Raising here risks getting 3‑bet or bloating a multiway pot out of position; calling keeps my options and I can play the flop based on texture (continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression with air). I will call the 100 to see the flop.
92o is one of the worst hands in poker - no connectivity, not suited, no high cards. Even in the cutoff with a limper, this is a clear fold. No reason to get involved with this trash hand.

I am on the button with 9h 3s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Minni has limped in from middle position. While I have position, this hand has no structural value (low card connectivity or suit) and is not worth a raise or even a call to see a flop. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here to preserve my stack.

Minni has limped and it folds to us in the SB. With 7h6d (offsuit) we have some connectivity, but it’s still a marginal hand and we’ll be out of position postflop versus at least one player, often two if the BB comes along. Raising to isolate is possible, but it tends to build a bigger pot with a hand that can be dominated and also invites the BB to defend/3-bet, putting us in tough spots. Completing keeps the pot small, realizes our equity cheaply, and lets us proceed fit-or-fold on unfavorable flops while still having some ability to make strong disguised hands on the right textures.

Preflop in the big blind with T8o, facing a limper (Minni, short stack ~54BB) from middle position and a check from the small blind (FiveTwo, ~62BB). Pot has 300 in dead money including my blind. T8o is a speculative hand with straight potential and decent playability. With position postflop and deep effective stacks, raising isolates the limper, generates folds often from weak ranges, and steals the pot preflop frequently given players' folding tendencies in history. Min-raise would be too small; pot-sized raise to 400 pressures without overcommitting. If called, continue based on board texture.

I call to see the flop. J♣9♣ is a speculative, playable hand with strong post‑flop equity (flush/straight/two‑pair potential) and good implied odds against deep stacks. I’ve already put in 100; Elon’s raise to 400 means 300 more to call (total 400). The pot is ~600, so calling 300 requires ~33% equity — reasonable for J9s. FiveTwo is already in, so this is likely a multiway pot which reduces the effectiveness of a small 3‑bet; raising here would bloat the pot out of position and commit chips with a marginal hand. I’ll keep the pot manageable, see the flop, and continue only with strong top‑pair/strong draws or fold to heavy aggression.

We’re facing a fairly large isolation raise from the BB (to 400) after we completed and Minni limped/called. With 7♠6♦ offsuit we have some straight potential, but it’s a weak, non-suited connector that realizes equity poorly out of position in a raised pot—especially multiway versus an aggressor who can keep pressing and a caller who can have a lot of suited/connected hands that dominate our draw/board coverage. The immediate price isn’t terrible, but the reverse implied odds when we make a marginal pair and the difficulty of navigating OOP outweigh it. No good squeeze/bluff-3bet candidate here either. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

Heads-up on the flop as the preflop raiser OOP against Minni, who limped from middle position and called my isolation raise. My T8o hits middle pair with a Ten kicker on Ad-8h-6h board (pot 900). This is a strong but vulnerable holding: second pair, no draws. The board is draw-heavy with a heart flush draw and possible straight draws (5-7, 7-9, 9-T), which hits limper calling ranges hard (suited connectors, Ax suited, mid pairs). However, my range as PFR is perceived stronger (overpairs, sets, top pair). Minni's stack is 5100 (~51BB), so she's pot committed if I bet large. C-betting for thin value and protection against draws denies equity and takes it down often given history of folds to aggression (e.g., multiple spots where Minni folds to postflop bets). Half-pot bet sizes well given pot odds and her stack; 600 applies pressure without overbetting, allowing room for turns/rivers if check-raised (unlikely from her range). Folding equity high OOP.

I have Jc9c on Ad 8h 6h — no pair and no club on the board (flush impossible), only weak backdoor/paired-card outs. Elon’s 600 into a 1500 pot gives ~28.6% immediate pot odds to call, but vs a reasonable c-bet range (Ax, heart draws, overcards) my true equity is much lower and I’m out of position. Elon has shown aggressive postflop tendencies and is deep-stacked, so a call will likely cost more on later streets or force difficult decisions. I planned preflop to continue only with top pair or strong draws; this spot doesn’t meet that bar. Fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

UTG in 6-max with 9♠6♦ — a weak, unsuited, unconnected hand. Preflop there’s already 150 in the pot (SB+BB) and my cost to see a flop is 100; min-raise would be 200. From UTG I need to open very tight; 9-6o plays poorly out of position, is often dominated, and has poor postflop playability multiway. The table has several capable raisers (we’ve seen large opens and aggression), so calling or attempting a blind raise here is negative EV. Preserve 51 BB stack and wait for a better spot—fold.
9-3 offsuit is a trash hand from middle position. No connectivity, no high cards, not suited. Easy fold - no reason to invest chips here.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak hand and does not justify an open raise from this position, especially with active players still to act behind me. Standard play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Folds to me on the Button, so this is a prime spot to attack the blinds with a wide opening range. Kh4d is weak and won’t continue versus a 3-bet, but it has enough blocker value (Kx) and positional advantage to profit as a steal when the blinds fold often enough. I’ll use a standard small open to apply pressure while keeping my risk controlled and plan to fold to significant resistance (3-bet).

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 250 (200 to call into 400 pot). My hand is Ah5c offsuit, ace-high with a weak kicker. FiveTwo on the button is an active raiser based on history (frequent opens, some bluffs postflop), but defending OOP with A5o is marginal at best—it's easily dominated by better aces, pairs, or broadway, and plays poorly postflop without position or nut potential. Pot odds are decent (~3:1), but implied odds are poor due to reverse domination risks and multiway potential if BB defends. Deep stacked (185BB), better to fold and preserve stack for premium spots rather than speculate OOP.

I hold Pocket Tens (Tc, Ts) in the Big Blind facing a standard button raise from FiveTwo to 250. FiveTwo has approximately 60BBs. Reviewing the hand history (Hands 8, 12, 13), FiveTwo has consistently folded to 3-bets or significant pre-flop aggression. If I 3-bet here, it is highly likely FiveTwo will fold, limiting my winnings to the pre-flop dead money. Since TT is a strong premium hand that dominates a wide button opening range, I prefer to keep FiveTwo in the pot to extract value post-flop rather than forcing a fold immediately. Calling allows me to defend my blind, disguise the strength of my hand, and play against a wide range, despite being out of position.

I hold Pocket Tens on an Ace-high flop (Ac 4h 2c). As the pre-flop caller out of position against the Button raiser (FiveTwo), standard strategy is to check to the aggressor. The Ace hits the opponent's range harder than mine, so leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to accomplish much; it folds out bluffs I beat and gets action from hands that beat me. I will check and evaluate based on FiveTwo's action. If they c-bet, I will have a decision based on sizing, as I have a vulnerable underpair but often the best hand against a wide button range.

BB checks on an A-high flop where my button open has a big range advantage. Even though I have a marginal made hand (pair of 4s) and not a great hand to face heavy pressure, a small c-bet is still profitable: it folds out a lot of BB’s whiffs/overcards, denies equity to hands like KQ/KJ/QJ, and lets me define the hand at a low cost. I’ll use a small sizing and be prepared to fold if Pro check-raises big, since my hand can’t comfortably continue versus strong Ax/sets/strong draws.

I hold Pocket Tens on an Ace-high flop (Ac 4h 2c). The opponent (FiveTwo) has c-bet 200 into a pot of 550. This is a small bet (~36% of the pot before the bet, or ~27% of the total pot). Reviewing Hand #19, FiveTwo c-bet 200 on a similar A-high board (Kd Ad 5h) with second pair (Kd), suggesting they c-bet these textures frequently with a wide range. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 950, roughly 3.75:1). My hand is ahead of all their lower pocket pairs (99-55), draws (club draws, gutshots like 3x or 53), and complete air. Raising isolates me against Aces, so calling is the most profitable line to keep their bluffs and weaker value hands in the pot.

I hold Pocket Tens on a board of [4h Ac 2c 5d]. The turn card (5d) increases the connectivity of the board, creating a possible wheel straight if anyone holds a 3. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo), who showed strength by c-betting the flop, my best course of action is to check. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (Aces, sets, straights) or cause worse hands to fold, denying me the chance to induce bluffs or get to a cheap showdown. I will check to the aggressor and evaluate their action.

Turn brings the 5d, giving me a weak one-pair (4s) plus a small gutshot to a 3. After Pro check-calls the flop on A-high, his continuing range is weighted toward Ax, some 2x/4x/5x, pocket pairs, and club draws—hands that either won’t fold to a second barrel or have decent equity. If I bet again, I mostly fold out worse (air) and get called/raised by better, inflating the pot with a marginal hand at a healthy SPR. Checking back takes the free card, keeps my showdown value intact, and lets Pro bluff rivers with missed clubs while I can still consider a thin value bet on safe rivers if he checks again.

I hold Pocket Tens (Tc, Ts) on a board of [4h Ac 2c 5d Qd]. The pot is 950. Pre-flop, I defended my Big Blind against a button raise. I check-called a small c-bet on the flop, and the turn went check-check. The river Queen is a bad card for me as it is another overcard to my pair. FiveTwo's check-back on the turn indicates they likely do not have a strong Ace, but they could hold a weak Ace, a Queen, or a weirdly played 3 (straight). They could also have missed club draws or lower pairs (99-66). Betting here achieves little: worse hands (like 88) likely fold, and better hands (Ax, Qx) will call. Checking allows me to potentially win at showdown if they check back with a weaker pair or missed draw, or allows me to evaluate if they decide to bluff. Given the board texture with two overcards and a possible straight, my hand is a pure bluff-catcher/showdown hand.

Pro’s line (check-call flop, check turn, check river) still contains a lot of Ax that will never fold, plus some pocket pairs (66–JJ) and occasional 3x that turned into a wheel—hands that either beat my pair of 4s or will look me up. The Qd river also improves parts of his range (Qx floats/pairs) and doesn’t give me a credible polarized value story that generates enough folds. My hand has some showdown value versus missed club draws and pure floats, so the highest-EV play is to take the free showdown rather than turn a weak pair into a thin/low-fold-equity bluff.
A8o from UTG is a marginal open but playable at a 6-max table. With 74.5 BB, I have a comfortable stack. I'll open with a standard 2.5x raise to 250.

Claude has opened from UTG for 2.5x. I am holding pocket threes, which is a prime candidate for set-mining. Given our stack depths (Claude has 7200 and I have over 11000), the implied odds are favorable to call and attempt to hit a set on the flop. Claude's UTG range is typically strong, meaning if I do hit my set, I have a high probability of getting paid off. I will call the 250 and see a flop.

Facing an UTG 2.5x open and a flat call in front, 42s is at the very bottom of playable suited hands. It plays poorly multiway (dominated flushes, weak straight potential) and has little squeeze equity versus an UTG range plus caller. With ~57bb effective, best EV is to avoid putting money in with a dominated, low-realization hand.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, dominated by better kings and vulnerable to any pair or suited connectors in opponents' ranges. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (who shows decent preflop strength in history) and a flat call from Flash (middle position), the combined range is strong. Even on the button with position, calling into a likely multiway pot (blinds behind may continue) is -EV long-term. Pot odds of ~2.6:1 aren't enough to justify with such low equity and playability. Deep stacks allow waiting for better spots to maximize stack over 150 hands.

I hold 6c5c in the Small Blind. Claude raised to 250 from UTG, and Flash called from Middle Position. I am getting excellent pot odds to call (200 to win 650, roughly 3.25:1). Suited connectors play well in multi-way pots due to their potential to make strong hands (straights, flushes) that can crack the stronger ranges of an UTG opener. Although playing out of position is a disadvantage, the deep effective stacks provide sufficient implied odds to justify seeing a flop cheaply. I will call and look to fit or fold post-flop.

I’m in the big blind with J♥9♥ facing an UTG open to 250 that’s been called by two players (pot 850). Calling 150 more to make my total 250 gives me very attractive pot odds (about 15% equity required) and J9s has good playability (flush/straight/top-pair potential) in multiway pots. A raise here would be small and puts me out of position against the raiser and callers, so I prefer to defend and see a flop. Plan: call to see the flop and proceed cautiously — continue aggressively with made top pair/two pair or strong draws, otherwise control the pot or fold to heavy pressure.

I am out of position in a 4-way pot with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and a backdoor flush draw. The board texture (Q-8-4) favors the pre-flop raiser (UTG), who has a strong range advantage. Leading out into three opponents with just a gutshot is not profitable. The standard play is to check to the aggressor and evaluate the action. If the action is heavy, I can easily fold; if it checks through, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or pick up a flush draw.

Flop: Q♦ 4♣ 8♠; I have J♥9♥. I missed — no pair — but I have a decent one-card straight draw (four tens) plus pair-outs (3 jacks, 3 nines) = ~10 outs (~21% to hit on the turn, ~38% by river). This is a multiway pot (UTG open + callers) and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to build the pot with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, preserves my SPR and lets me realize equity without committing chips. Plan: check and see the turn; call a small bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot) to continue, fold to large pressure unless I improve; if I hit the straight or pair the J on the turn I’ll transition to more aggressive betting.

I flopped middle pair (8s) with top kicker (Ace). Board is Qd4c8s - fairly dry texture. Both Pro and Minni checked to me. I should c-bet for value and protection against overcards. A bet around 50% pot seems appropriate to get value from worse hands and deny equity to overcards.

I have pocket threes and the flop is Qd-4c-8s, which did not improve my hand. Claude, who raised pre-flop from UTG, has now continuation bet 500 into three opponents. This is a sign of strength or at least a standard C-bet on a board that likely favors his range (containing high cards like Queens). With two players still left to act behind me and having missed my set, there is no value in calling with a weak pair of threes. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold 6c5c on a Qd 4c 8s board. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and a backdoor flush draw. The UTG player (Claude) has bet 500 into a pot of 1000 (total pot now 1500). I need to call 500 to win a total pot of 2000, requiring 25% equity. A gutshot has approximately 4 outs (roughly 8-9% equity on the next card), and even adding value for backdoor draws, my total equity is likely below 20%. Being out of position against a strong UTG range that c-bet into three people makes realizing this equity difficult. The price is too expensive for the draw I have. Therefore, the correct play is to fold.

Call 500. Claude’s 500 c-bet is about 1/3 of the pot, so I’m getting ~25% pot odds. My J♥9♥ has ~10 clean outs (4 tens + 3 jacks + 3 nines) — roughly ~21% to hit the turn and ~38–40% to improve by the river — so calling to realize equity is +EV. I’m out of position and facing an UTG raiser, so a raise here would be iso-bluffing into a player with a strong range; I prefer to control the size of the pot and see the turn. Plan: call to see the turn; if I pair a J/9 or hit a 10 (straight) I’ll go for value/pressure. If I miss and face a large turn barrel (>>1/2–2/3 pot) I’ll fold, but I’ll call small-to-medium turn bets to continue.

I just paired my J on the turn (J♥9♥) giving me top pair with a decent but not premium kicker on Q♦ 4♣ 8♠ J♦. Pot is ~2,000 and action checked to me after Claude (UTG) c-bet the flop and then checked the turn. Claude’s line suggests a range of Qx, some overpairs (AA/KK), broadways and occasional bluffs — his turn check is often pot-control with a Queen or a missed hand. I beat a lot of his checking combos (worse Jx, small pocket pairs, some floats) and I also want to charge any straight/river outs. Betting for value is the right play rather than slowing down. I’ll make a moderately sized value bet (about 1/2 the pot) to extract from worse jacks and medium pairs while keeping the pot manageable if he raises. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river; if he raises big, I’ll give credit to a stronger range and fold.

Minni check-called my flop bet and now leads out 1000 on the turn when the Jd hits. This is a concerning line - the turn card brings another Broadway card. Minni likely has Qx, Jx that just improved, or possibly a straight draw like T9. My middle pair of 8s with ace kicker is in rough shape against this range. Getting 3:1 pot odds but I'm likely behind and will face more pressure on the river. Folding is prudent here.

I am in the UTG position with T6 offsuit. This is a very weak hand to open from early position in a 6-handed game. My range should be much tighter here. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to avoid being out of position with a marginal hand against multiple opponents.

We’re in middle position 6-handed with 7♠4♦ offsuit, a very weak hand that plays poorly postflop (dominated, low connectivity, and minimal high-card equity). Limping invites multiway pots with reverse implied odds, and raising doesn’t generate enough folds against these opponents’ calling/3-betting tendencies shown in prior hands. Preserving stack and waiting for better spots is higher EV.

K3o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by any other king or pair. In cutoff position after two folds, this is still a clear fold preflop. No need to speculate or steal with trash against competent blinds like Minni and Claude, who have shown willingness to defend. Preserve stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament.

I am on the Button with 9h3h, a very weak suited hand. Although it is folded to me, offering a potential steal opportunity, the Big Blind (Claude) has shown a tendency to defend wide in previous hands. Since my hand has very poor post-flop playability and low equity when called, the expected value of a steal attempt is likely negative or marginal compared to simply folding and waiting for a better spot, especially given my healthy stack size.

Everyone folded to me — heads-up against Claude in the big blind. K♥2♦ is a marginal hand but has decent heads-up stealing value and blocks some strong Kx/Ax combos. Limping gives up initiative and leaves me OOP with a weak hand; a small min-raise to 200 (the minimum) buys fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if Claude fights back. Plan: raise to 200 to try to take the pot now or play a capped pot postflop; fold to a large 3-bet, and proceed cautiously if he merely calls (look to c-bet appropriate flops or check back and fold without improvement).

Minni min-raised to 200 from SB. I'm in BB with J5o - a weak hand, but getting 3:1 odds (call 100 to win 300). At this price, defending with Jx suited would be standard, and even J5o is playable given the excellent odds. Will call and evaluate flop.

I raised preflop and flopped top pair (K) against one opponent who merely called. This is a textbook c-bet spot: I have value vs worse Kx and many single-pair hands, and I need to charge any diamond draws and medium pairs. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (200) to get value while keeping the pot controllable — if Claude jams or makes a large raise I’ll assign him very strong hands (7x trips, Kx with a big kicker, or a strong draw+pair) and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn (be cautious to a diamond turn and continue barreling only when it improves my equity or he shows weakness).

I have J5o on a K-7-7 board with no diamonds. I completely missed - no pair, no draw. Minni bet half pot and likely has a King, a 7, or a pocket pair. Even with 3:1 odds, I have almost no equity here with J-high and no backdoor draws. Folding is the clear play.

Pocket Tens are a clear UTG open in 6-max. Limping invites multiway play and lets the blinds realize equity too cheaply; raising builds the pot while we’re likely ahead of most continuing ranges. Given typical sizing in prior hands, a standard 2.5x open keeps our range consistent and avoids overinflating with a medium-strong pair at ~57bb.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 from FiveTwo, who has shown loose-aggressive tendencies in previous hands but still likely has a decent range from early position. My 4s5s is a speculative suited connector, but in middle position out of position to button and blinds, with multiple players left to act including deep-stacked Pro on my left. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 400 for ~1.6:1), and while it has implied odds potential, the risk of domination or getting raised behind outweighs it early in the tournament with a deep stack (185BB). Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I hold J6 offsuit in the Cutoff facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo. J6o is a very weak hand with poor equity against a typical strong UTG opening range. There are no strategic reasons to float or 3-bet bluff in this spot given the position and hand strength. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I will call 250. 6♣8♥ is a marginal, speculative hand but being on the button gives me a lot of post‑flop leverage — calling is cheap relative to my 6,550 stack and lets me realize equity in position. I don’t want to 3‑bet as a bluff vs an UTG opener (FiveTwo) because the hand lacks blockers and fold equity; a 3‑bet could put me in a tough spot vs a 4‑bet. Plan: see the flop (pot control) and continue only with a good pair/draw or a favorable board; fold to significant aggression on dry, high‑card boards or large squeezes out of position.
Q5o is a weak hand out of position. Facing a UTG raise and a call, even with decent pot odds (200 to win 650), this hand plays poorly multiway and OOP. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7-2 suited, which is historically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 150 to win 650), I am facing a raise from UTG (FiveTwo) and a call from the Button (Minni). Calling would put me out of position against two opponents for the entire hand with a hand that has very poor playability and realization of equity. In a high-roller tournament format, it's better to preserve my stack and avoid marginal situations with trash hands.

Heads-up OOP versus the Button on a dry paired board (9-9-7), my UTG range has a big advantage and can c-bet very small at high frequency. With TT I’m ahead of all unpaired overcards and many pocket pairs (22-88) and some 7x, and a small bet denies equity while keeping the pot controlled versus the portion of Minni’s range that has trips/boats (9x/77/99). If I face significant aggression, I can comfortably let it go.

Call 175. With 6♣8♥ on 9♠9♦7♥ I have an open‑ended straight draw (any 5 or 10 completes — 8 outs). The pot is 825 and the bet is 175, so I’m getting ~17.5% pot odds which roughly matches my chance to hit on the turn (~17%); by the river my two‑card equity is ~31.5%. I’m on the button so I can realize equity and control the pot size; FiveTwo opened UTG so his range is relatively strong and his small flop sizing means a raise‑fold bluff is unlikely to succeed often. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy aggression on a blank, and if I hit the straight or see weakness on the turn I’ll extract value or take the pot.

Turn is a brick relative to ranges (9-9-7-3) but it does introduce some heart-draw floats. After my tiny flop c-bet gets called, Minni’s range is still heavy on pocket pairs (22-88), some 7x, and some overcards/backdoors; 9x/77 is possible but not the bulk. With TT (Th blocks some heart continues) I want to keep extracting from worse pairs and charge any hearts/overcards while keeping the pot controllable versus trips. A small second barrel accomplishes that, and I’m comfortable folding if I face a significant raise.

Call 325. I’m on the button with 6♣8♥ on 9♠9♦7♥3♥ — I have an open‑ended straight draw (any 5 or 10 = 8 outs, ~8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river). The immediate pot odds require ~19.7% (325 / (1325+325)) so it’s slightly short as a pure odds call, but the bet is small (~3.25 BB) relative to my ~61 BB stack and I have position. That gives me implied odds and post‑river maneuverability to extract value if I hit and to control/abandon the pot cheaply if I miss. Raising as a bluff is thin (no meaningful blockers and UTG’s range is likely strong), and folding surrenders too much equity for a small price. Plan: call to see the river; if I hit the straight I will look to extract value, if I miss I’ll check and fold to large river aggression unless pot odds or reads justify a call.

River completes the front-door heart flush on a paired board (99-7-3-4 with three hearts), which is a big shift in relative hand strength: TT is still an overpair to all 7x/underpairs, but it now gets called less often by worse and is much more likely to get raised/called by better (any 9x, 77/44/33, and especially two-heart holdings that floated/called small bets). Given we’ve already bet flop/turn small and built a pot, the best EV line is to take the showdown value and avoid value-owning ourselves. I’ll check and be prepared to bluff-catch versus small stabs, but comfortably fold to large polar bets.

Check. I missed my straight on 6♣8♥ — the river (9♠ 9♦ 7♥ 3♥ 4♥) gives the board three hearts and a paired nine board, so many hands in FiveTwo’s UTG raising/barreling line beat me: any A/K/high kicker, pocket pairs, trips (9x) and two‑heart combos that completed a flush on the river. FiveTwo's line (raise pre, barrels on flop+turn, then check river) is ambiguous, but UTG continuing range is weighted to hands that will call a small probe bet. A tiny bluff would need only a small fold frequency to be mathematically profitable (100 into 1,650 needs ≈6% folds), but given his range and history the actual fold equity is thin and callers will often have nut or better showdown hands that beat my 8‑kicker. I preserve stack and showdown equity by checking; if he bets I’ll fold to meaningful river aggression unless sizing or tells make a call reasonable.

Q8o is a speculative offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by any raising range from UTG in a 6-handed tournament. Position is worst (UTG), so folding preserves stack for better spots. No reads from this hand yet, but history shows opponents can be aggressive post-flop; better to wait for premium hands early in tournament to build stack without variance.

With active players at 6, I am in Middle Position (MP). After the UTG fold, action is on me. ATo (Ace-Ten offsuit) is a standard opening hand from this position in a 6-max game. It dominates many hands that might call (like weaker Ax, Kx, or connectors) but is easy to fold to a 3-bet. A standard open size at this table has been around 2.5x (250 chips). I will raise to 250 to take the initiative and thin the field.

I’m in the cutoff with J♠3♥ facing a 250 open from middle (to call 250, min‑raise to 400). J3o is a marginal, easily dominated hand with very poor postflop playability. Calling 250 into the 400 pot requires meaningful equity (~28%), which this hand generally doesn’t have versus a standard open range, and I don’t want to play a bloated pot out of position. A 3‑bet would be a pure bluff here with limited fold equity and unnecessary risk given my 58BB stack. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

2s3s is a very weak hand even suited. While I have button position, this is at the very bottom of playable hands. Pro raised from middle position indicating some strength. I still have two players behind me (blinds). The implied odds aren't great enough to justify calling with such a low suited gapper - I'd need to hit a flush or miracle flop to win. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am holding Queen-Ten of hearts in the Small Blind. This is a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop, though I will be out of position against the initial raiser, Pro. Pro raised to 250 from middle position, which is a standard opening size for them. Calling the 200 extra is a high-value play given the potential to flop strong draws or top pair, and it keeps the pot manageable while I am out of position. I don't see a strong need to 3-bet here as I'd prefer to see a flop and evaluate the board texture first.

We’re getting a very good price in the BB (150 more to win a pot of ~600) and closing the action. A9o is a reasonable defend here, especially multiway, with decent top-pair potential and some blocker value with the ace. A squeeze/3-bet would often put us in dominated spots when called (worse aces don’t continue much; better aces and strong broadways do), so the higher-EV line is to realize equity and play postflop.

The flop 6c-3c-7d does not hit my Queen-Ten of hearts. I have two overcards but no direct draw or pair. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and the Big Blind (FiveTwo), the most standard and disciplined play is to check and see how the action develops. Leading out into two opponents on this board with just overcards is unnecessarily risky.

Multiway (PFR Pro + SB caller) on a low, coordinated flop (6c-3c-7d), A9o without a club has modest equity: two overcards and little ability to barrel profitably if we donk. Leading into two players is unlikely to generate enough folds (they’ll continue with pairs, club draws, and gutters), and we don’t benefit from building a pot with ace-high. Best is to check, realize equity, and let the preflop raiser c-bet his range; we can then respond based on sizing/texture (often fold vs big pressure, continue vs small with our overcards/backdoors).

I raised pre-flop with AcTs and got two callers from the blinds. The flop comes 6c 3c 7d. This low, connected board hits the defending ranges of the blinds (sets, two pairs, straight draws, pairs) better than my broadway-heavy range. Being multiway with two opponents makes a continuation bet less attractive, as I have only Ace-high and the likelihood of someone connecting is high. I do have the Ace of clubs, giving me a backdoor nut flush draw, and two overcards. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and potentially hit a strong top pair or flush draw on the turn. I avoid getting check-raised and can re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn 5d makes the board texture very coordinated, bringing potential straights (any 4) and a second flush draw. My Queen-Ten of hearts still hasn't connected with this board in any meaningful way. Since the flop was checked around, it's possible everyone missed, but I have no reason to bluff into two opponents on such a wet board. I will check and hope to see a free river card.

Turn goes check/check on a very dynamic board (6c-3c-7d-5d) after a flop that checked through, which caps both opponents’ ranges a bit and creates a good stab spot for the BB. This 5d is a better card for my defending range than Pro’s MP open (I have more 4x, 65, 75, 76, etc.), so a small donk can generate folds from unpaired overcards and deny equity, while still giving me some improvement equity (8 for a straight; A/9 to make a pair that can win vs floats). I’ll use a modest sizing to risk little, keep worse ranges in, and comfortably fold if I face a raise from either player.

The turn card [5d] makes the board [6c 3c 7d 5d], which is extremely coordinated and connects heavily with the Big Blind's pre-flop defending range (containing many 4x straights, two pairs, and sets). The Big Blind leading out for 300 into two opponents after the flop checked through demonstrates strength or at least a strong piece of this board. I hold [Ac Ts], which is merely Ace-high. My only 'draw' is a gutshot to a 4, which would put a straight on the board likely resulting in a chop, but even that is thin. I have no flush draw. With a player still behind me and facing aggression on a board that hits my opponent's range much better than mine, folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten of hearts. The board is 6c-3c-7d-5d. I have no pair and no draw. The turn 5d completed several potential straights (4-8, 4-2) and FiveTwo, the Big Blind, has bet 300 into a 1050 pot after the flop was checked around. While the bet is small, I have zero equity on this board and no reasonable path to winning the hand other than a pure bluff on a later street, which is risky given the coordinated board. Folding is the most disciplined play.

I am in the UTG position in a 6-handed game with a deep stack (115 BB). King-Ten suited (Kh Th) is a strong enough broadway hand to open from this position, as it plays well post-flop and can make strong flushes and straights. My standard opening size has been 2.5x (250), which balances well with my previous opens including suited connectors and other broadways. Limping is weak, so I will raise to take the initiative.

I have A♠ K♦ in middle position facing an UTG open to 250. This is a premium hand and I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener and avoid a multiway pot against the button/SB/BB. A min-raise to 400 is too small and will invite callers; a standard 3-bet to about 900 total applies solid pressure, builds value and gives good fold equity while leaving plenty of stack to navigate a 4‑bet if needed. I’ll 3‑bet to 900 to play this hand aggressively and either get heads‑up or a narrowed field.

Holding 9s5d - a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from UTG and a 3-bet from Middle position. This is an easy fold - 95o has no playability and I'm facing significant aggression. No need to burn 900 chips here.

I am on the button holding 52s. There has been an open from UTG and a 3-bet from the middle position to 900. 52s is a very weak hand and does not play well against a 3-bet range. Cold-calling or 4-betting with this hand in this spot would be a massive overplay and a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We’re facing an UTG open plus a 3-bet from Minni, and we’re out of position in the small blind with Q9o. Cold-calling 850 creates a bloated pot where our hand is frequently dominated (Qx and 9x dominated, poor playability OOP vs a strong range), leading to reverse implied odds.
