A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here because Minni’s 3-bet range versus UTG should be relatively strong and UTG can still continue/jam behind. Best EV is to avoid spewing chips and wait for a better spot.

Facing an UTG raise from Pro followed by a 3-bet to 900 from Minni in middle position. My J6h is a speculative suited hand with some playability but poor equity against likely strong ranges from both opponents. Currently getting pot odds of about 800 to 2100 (38% needed), but out of position in the big blind, and if I call, action returns to Pro who may continue or 4-bet, potentially making it multiway or facing further aggression. J6h is dominated by better jacks or suited broadways in their ranges, and while suited offers flush/straight potential, it's too weak for the spot. Minni's stack of 4900 post-3bet commits them somewhat, but Pro UTG open suggests strength. Deep stacked tournament, hand 30/150, better to fold and wait for premium spots to maximize long-term EV.

I opened to 2.5bb from UTG with KTs and faced a large 3-bet (to 9bb) from Minni in MP. While I have pot odds of roughly 2:1 (calling 650 to win 1950), my hand plays poorly out of position against a strong 3-betting range which likely includes TT+, AQ+, and potentially hands that dominate me like AK or KQs. The Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) would be around 2 post-flop, making it difficult to navigate without hitting a very strong hand. Given the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage, folding is the disciplined decision.

UTG with 9♦5♥ — a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and no position. This is well outside any reasonable open-raise range from UTG in a 6-handed game. Calling 100 to see a flop out of position with many opponents behind gives poor equity and frequent domination; limping/raising is also suboptimal against deep, capable stacks to my left. Preserve my 62BB stack for better spots — fold.
8-3 offsuit is a very weak hand - not suited, poorly connected, no high cards. Easy fold from middle position.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Deuce offsuit. While it is a King, it is one of the weakest King-X hands and does not perform well in a 6-handed game, even from late position. With the Button and both Blinds still to act, there is a high likelihood of being called or raised by a superior hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folded to us on the button 3-handed vs blinds. Pocket 4s is strong enough to open at high frequency here: we can often win the blinds immediately, and when called we have reasonable playability (set potential, can continue on favorable low boards) while having position. I prefer a standard small raise sizing consistent with the table to apply pressure without bloating the pot too much versus larger stacks in the blinds.

Preflop from SB facing a button raise to 250 after three folds. FiveTwo is an aggressive opener from the button with a wide range, likely 40-50% of hands based on history of frequent raises from late position with marginal holdings. QsJs is a strong suited connector with broadway potential, excellent playability, and good equity (around 40%+) against a button opening range. Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into a pot that will be 600 (3:1). Deep stacks allow for implied odds postflop. Defending OOP is standard with this hand; folding is too tight, 3-betting adds unnecessary risk without premium value. Calling keeps the pot multiway potentially (BB behind) and realizes equity flexibly.

I am in the Big Blind facing a Button raise to 250 and a Small Blind call. The pot is 600 and I need to call 150, offering me 4:1 (or 20% equity required) on a call. However, my hand [7c 4s] is extremely weak (7-4 offsuit), has poor playability, and performs badly in multi-way pots. Additionally, I will be out of position relative to the aggressor on the Button. The likelihood of realizing my equity is low due to the positional disadvantage and the weakness of the hand. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips.

On this Ace-King-8 rainbow flop, QsJs gives me a gutshot straight draw to broadway (needing a Ten, 4 outs), backdoor spade flush draw, and two overcards that are live against non-Ax/Kx villain ranges. Equity is decent (~25-35% vs. a polarized button c-bet range that includes Ax, Kx, bluffs, and pocket pairs). However, OOP heads-up with no pair or strong draw, I have poor showdown value and want to realize equity cheaply. Preflop call was correct for playability; now checking is standard to pot control, induce bluffs from FiveTwo's aggressive button range (frequent cbets observed), and fold to a large bet while check-calling smaller ones for implied odds given deep effective stacks (villain 6800 into 600 pot). Donk betting is suboptimal as it bloats the pot OOP with a marginal draw. Pot odds favor checking first.

Heads-up in position as the preflop raiser on A-K-8, I have a significant range advantage (lots of strong Ax/Kx/sets) while Elon’s SB flatting range is capped and contains many hands that will miss or hate facing a c-bet. With 44 I don’t want a big pot, but a small continuation bet pressures his air/weak pairs, can fold out hands with overcard equity, and keeps my risk low. If I get called, I’ll mostly shut down unless the turn is very favorable (low cards improving my perceived range / giving me equity).

Facing a small 200 bet into an 800 pot (1/4 pot size) from the aggressive button FiveTwo after my check OOP on A-K-8 rainbow. QsJs blocks some broadway combos, has a live gutshot straight draw to Broadway (4 tens), backdoor spade flush draw, and two live overcards (Q,J live vs many non-Ax/Kx hands in wide button range). Equity ~28-35% vs polarized c-bet range (Ax, Kx, sets, bluffs, mid pairs, draws). Excellent pot odds (4:1 direct, better implied). Deep effective stacks (my 18200, villain 6600) justify calling for playability and fold equity on later streets. Folding is -EV given odds and history of FiveTwo's frequent small cbets; raising marginal draw OOP risks bloating pot without enough equity/fold equity. Check-call small bets standard with playable draw OOP to realize equity cheaply and induce further bluffs from aggro opponent.

Turn board pairs the 8s on an A-K-8 board, making it K-high with a paired board and club flush draw possible. QsJs holds a live gutshot straight draw to Broadway (4 outs to any Ten), two live overcards (Q,J likely good vs non-Ax/Kx in button range), and minimal showdown value. Equity remains solid (~25-35% vs FiveTwo's polarized continuation range including Ax, Kx, 88/99, bluffs, and missed draws from wide button open). First to act OOP into 1000 pot with villain's 6600 behind (shallow-ish effective, but my deep 18k). Donking is poor OOP with marginal draw as it bloats pot, denies equity, and invites raises from better hands. Check-raising too strong without made hand or combo draw. Folding spew given playability and history. Standard check to pot control, realize equity cheaply on favorable odds if bet (villain frequently small cbets postflop as seen in multiple hands), induce bluffs from aggro button range, and keep pot small OOP. River will be straightforward depending on action.

Elon’s flop call on A-K-8 keeps him weighted toward Ax/Kx/8x plus some pocket pairs and occasional floats. The turn pairing the 8 is a good barrel card for my range as the preflop raiser (I credibly have 8x/boats and strong Ax that benefit from protection), while it puts maximum pressure on his marginal continues (pocket pairs like 99-QQ, weak Kx, floats) and denies him the chance to realize equity/bluff river for free. With 44 I’m not trying to build a big pot, so I’ll use a small sizing and comfortably fold if check-raised.

Turn pairs the 8c on A-K-8 rainbow board (now two-tone clubs). QsJs holds a clean gutshot to Broadway (4x Tens), live Q/J overcards vs villain's wide button range, and backdoor spade draw. No showdown value but excellent playability OOP. Equity ~28-35% vs FiveTwo's polarized small c-bet range (Ax/Kx/88/sets/value combos + bluffs/missed draws from frequent aggressive button opens). Pot odds stellar (1350:350 ~3.85:1, need <20% equity). Villain's tiny 350 bet (~1/4 pot) into deep effective stacks (6250 behind) screams thin value or bluff inducement; history shows frequent small continuation bets on flops/turns (e.g., Hands 19,25,28,29). BB behind but unlikely to interfere much. Check-call continues pot control OOP, realizes equity cheaply, induces further small bets/river bluffs from aggro opponent, preserves fold equity if raised (unlikely small size), and sets up potential river value if hit or villain weak. Folding -EV with superior odds/playability; raising bloats pot OOP with marginal draw, low fold equity vs value-heavy range. Standard check-call spot to balance range and exploit villain's aggression.

River board completes with 6h, bringing possible heart flush draws and straight possibilities (e.g., 97, T7), but primarily pairs the 8s earlier, keeping it A/K/88 heavy. QsJs holds a missed gutshot straight draw (4 outs to T for Broadway, clean), Q/J overcards that block some value but no showdown value against villain's polarized button range (Ax, Kx value; 88/99/77 sets; mid-pairs/draws turned semi-bluffs; pure bluffs from wide preflop open and multi-street small continuation bets). Equity ~20-25% on river vs. his range, as villain's aggression (small flop/turn bets into checked pots OOP) suggests thin value or inducement rather than nuts. OOP first to act into 1700 pot with deep stacks (my 17k, villain 6k), betting/donking is -EV: no thin value (villain calls with better), poor fold equity vs. calling station tendencies observed, bloats pot OOP, denies equity realization cheaply. Folding impossible. Check is mandatory: pot control OOP with bluff-catcher/draw, induces bluffs/value from worse (e.g., missed draws, weak Ax/Kx, mid-pairs) from aggro FiveTwo's history of small bets (Hands 1,5,15,19,21,25,28,29), sets up cheap call to further small bets (~1/4 pot pattern), balances range (some value like AT/A9 check), exploits villain's aggression profitably long-term in tournament. BB folded preflop, heads-up.

River after Elon has check-called flop and turn on A-K-8-8, his range is now heavily concentrated in made hands (Ax, Kx, 8x) that are structurally very hard to fold on this runout. The 6h is a brick (no flush completes, no obvious straight completes), so it doesn’t add credible pressure points that force strong folds. With 44 specifically, we have essentially no relevant blockers (we don’t block Ax/Kx/8x), making this one of the worst hands to choose as a triple-barrel bluff. Given the low fold equity versus a range that has already continued twice, the highest-EV/lowest-variance play is to check back and preserve stack rather than fire a likely losing bluff.
52 suited in UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest hands in poker, and UTG requires a tight range. No reason to invest chips here.

I am in Middle Position with Queen-Nine suited (Qc 9c). The action has been folded to me. In a 6-max tournament structure, Q9s is a standard opening hand from this position. It has good playability post-flop, particularly with flush and straight potential. I will raise to 250, which has been the standard opening size at this table, to take control of the pot and narrow the field.

72o is at the absolute bottom of the range and performs poorly even in position versus an early/middle open. There’s no profitable defend here (dominated, terrible playability), and turning it into a 3-bet bluff is unnecessary given Flash’s demonstrated willingness to apply pressure and the depth of stacks. Preserve stack and wait for better CO spots.

Pocket sixes is a strong hand on the button facing a middle position raise to 2.5BB. Flash's range is likely wide including Ax, broadway, suited connectors. With position on all players, 3-betting provides fold equity to take down the pot immediately (pot is 400), isolates if called, and sets up post-flop aggression. Set-mining potential if multiway. Sizing to 900 (about 3.6x their raise) is standard for value/protection. My deep stack allows flexibility. Opponents like Minni in BB may defend wide but I have blockers and position.

I am in the Small Blind holding QJo facing a raise from Flash (MP) and a 3-bet from Elon (Button) to 900. QJo is a marginal off-suit hand that is often dominated by a 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, AJ, JJ+). Cold-calling a 3-bet from the Small Blind puts me in the worst possible position post-flop, likely sandwiching me between the initial raiser and the 3-bettor. The pot odds do not justify calling with a hand that has poor playability and reverse implied odds in this spot. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I’m on the big blind with 5♠3♦. Flash opened to 250 and Elon 3-bet to 900 with the pot at 1300, so calling would cost me 800 more (I’d be committing 900 total). That requires roughly ~38% equity to break even, but 5-3 offsuit has very poor equity and awful post‑flop playability in a multiway/3‑bet pot. A squeeze or 4‑bet bluff here is too risky and unlikely to work against these stacks and likely ranges. Preserve the ~61 BB stack and fold.

I opened with Queen-Nine suited (Qc 9c) from Middle Position, which is a standard opening play. However, Elon has 3-bet me to 900 from the Button. This 3-bet is significant in size (3.6x my open), and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Elon has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands (Hands #6, #8, and #12), often using large sizing. Q9s is near the bottom of my opening range and does not perform well enough against a typical Button 3-betting range to justify calling out of position, especially when facing a player who applies significant post-flop pressure. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to contest a pot against him.

I am under the gun (UTG) with Queen-Jack offsuit in a 6-max game. While QJo is a semi-strong broadway hand, it is generally considered a fold from the earliest position because it is easily dominated by better hands (AQ, KQ, AJ, QJ suited) and I will likely have to play the rest of the hand out of position against five other players. Given the stack depths and the aggressive tendencies of players like Elon and FiveTwo, folding is the most disciplined and safe play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

6-handed, after UTG folds, QJo is comfortably within a middle-position opening range. We have ~80bb, and raising is much higher EV than limping because we deny equity to blinds and can often take it down preflop or with a single c-bet on favorable boards. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open that this table has been using to keep fold equity high without bloating the pot unnecessarily versus the deeper stacks behind.

K4o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing a raise from middle position by FiveTwo, who has been aggressive but my hand lacks equity against his likely range. In cutoff position with a deep stack, calling gives poor implied odds and risks multiway pots with players behind. 3-betting is not justified without strong blockers or suited connectivity. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a tournament setting.

I hold 5s 2s on the Button. This is a very weak hand, even though it is suited. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from Middle Position. Despite having position, calling a raise with a 3-gapper low suited hand is generally not profitable due to poor equity and reverse implied odds. The pot odds do not justify a call, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary with this specific holding. The disciplined and standard play is to fold.

Ad♦ K♥ in the SB vs a MP open to 250 (2.5x). This is a premium hand with strong equity and good fold equity preflop; out of position I prefer to 3-bet for value and isolation rather than just calling and playing OOP. I’ll raise to 900 total — big enough to put pressure on his opening range and reduce multiway action, but small enough to keep worse hands calling. If called I’ll continue as the aggressor (c‑bet favorable A/K high and dry flops, pot‑control on highly coordinated boards). If I face a polarized 4‑bet I’ll reassess (likely fold to a large 4‑bet, consider shove/call only vs small, noncommittal 4‑bets from loose players). This maximizes my EV while preserving plenty of stack depth to maneuver post‑flop.

73 suited is a very weak hand. Facing a 3-bet from Minni after FiveTwo's open, I'd need to call 800 into a 1250 pot. Even with suited connectors-ish potential, 73s doesn't have good playability or equity against 3-bet ranges. Easy fold.

Minni’s SB 3-bet to 900 versus my MP open creates a relatively low SPR if I call (pot ~1900, effective ~4300), so I need to be disciplined postflop with one-pair hands—QJ top pair can be dominated by AQ/KQ/QK/JJ/QQ. That said, we have position, good immediate price (650 to win 1250), and Minni has shown willingness to 3-bet and later give up in prior hands, so folding is likely too tight. A 4-bet bluff isn’t attractive here because we’d risk ~1300–2000 to win 1250 and need a very high fold frequency, plus we’d be forced to fold to a shove. Best line is to call and realize equity in position, continuing mainly on strong flops (two pair/strong draws) and playing cautiously versus heavy pressure on marginal top pairs.

I 3‑bet preflop to isolate, so my range is strong — but the flop 6♥ 2♦ 2♥ completely misses my AdKh: I only have two overcards and a backdoor heart possibility. This paired, low flop actually helps a caller’s range (small pocket pairs, sets, 2x hands), and c‑betting OOP here is risky because I’ll frequently get called by better hands and can be put in a tough spot with a raise. With a 1,900 pot and 5,200 stack I prefer pot control and more information: I’ll check to see how FiveTwo acts. Plan: call a small-to-medium bet to realize equity and look to fold to a large raise; if he checks back I can continue on favorable turn cards (A/K/heart) or take a free card to a safer spot.

Minni was the preflop 3-bettor but checks this paired, low flop. That check very often indicates a lot of unpaired overcards (AK/AQ/KQ etc.) and some give-ups, while my hand (QJ) has two live overcards and benefits from taking the pot down immediately. I’ll stab small to apply pressure at low risk, deny equity, and keep my range from being too passive in position. If I face a check-raise at this low SPR, I’ll comfortably fold Q-high; if called, I’ll proceed cautiously and mostly continue only on good turns (Q/J) or favorable runouts.

I 3‑bet preflop to isolate, so my range is strong — but this flop (6h 2d 2h) is a low paired board that favors a caller’s range (small pocket pairs, 2x hands) and it misses my AdKh. I checked as planned to control the pot and get information. FiveTwo’s lead of 600 into a 2,500 pot is very small (~24%) — consistent with a probe bet/c-bet or thin-value from a small pair, but it can also be a light attempt to buy the pot cheaply. With two overcards and no real draw, the right line OOP is to realize equity and keep the pot manageable rather than turning this into a big bluff by raising. I’ll call the 600 to see a turn — I can continue on a favorable Ace/King/heart or fold to large aggression if he ramps it up. If he check‑backs I get a free turn; if he raises substantially (to the stated min‑raise threshold or much larger) I’ll strongly favor folding since this board and the action weight his range heavier than mine. Call 600 to realize equity and preserve options on the turn.

I 3-bet preflop to isolate with AdKh, missed the dry paired flop (6♥ 2♦ 2♥) and called FiveTwo's small probe-bet to realize equity. The turn (4♥) brings a third heart and gives me a 4-heart holding (Kh + 3 hearts on board) with two overcards, but I still have no made hand — only a one-card flush/overcard outs. Pot is 3,100 and my stack 4,600, so I must control the pot OOP. Betting here would bloat the pot with an inferior hand and commit a large portion of my stack against ranges (sets, 2x) that already dominate me; checking preserves my equity, allows me to see a cheap river, and keeps options open. I will check and plan to call reasonable river bets with my draw/overcards, fold to large polarized aggression, and re-evaluate if I hit a pair or the flush on the river.

Turn is a clear pot-control spot. After my flop stab gets called, this 6h-2d-2h-4h board leaves me with essentially just two overcards (6 outs) and no real draw (I don’t have a flush draw with only Jh). Minni’s double-check can still include slow-played overpairs (often with a heart) and some 6x/2x, and at this low SPR a turn barrel risks getting check-shoved and lighting chips on fire with near-zero equity. Checking back realizes my equity, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me potentially take a stab on many rivers if Minni checks again (or improve to a Q/J).

I never improved — AdKh on 6h 2d 2h 4h 5c is just Ace-high. FiveTwo’s line (call my 3-bet pre, a small 600 probe on the flop, then checking the turn) is consistent with small pocket pairs, single pair (6x/4x/5x) or a thin c-bet — many of which beat my Ace-high at showdown. Betting for value is a losing line (almost every value combo calls) and bluffing is unattractive: I don’t block enough of his calling combinations and I’ll face too many calls from small pairs. Pot control is correct — I’ll check and go to showdown; if he now leads a large bet I will fold rather than bloat the pot out of position.

River goes check to me in a 3-bet pot on 6h-2d-2h-4h-5c and I have essentially zero showdown (Q-high loses to all pairs and most A/K-high). Given Minni’s line (3-bet pre, check/call flop, then check-check), her range is heavily capped/medium-strength (a lot of AK/AQ/KQ, some 77–TT) and this river is a good pressure card for my range (I can credibly have 33 for a straight, 44/55/66 for boats/trips, 54s/65s two-pair+). So the best play is a polarized bluff at a size that makes it uncomfortable to peel with one-pair/ace-high but doesn’t risk my whole stack when called. If she has a real overpair and calls, so be it; I’m targeting folds from her large unpaired/medium pocket pair region.

I fold. I have AdKh and never improved — only Ace‑high (one heart blocker) on 6h 2d 2h 4h 5c. FiveTwo’s line (opened MP, called my 3‑bet, a small probe on the flop, passive turn and now a sizable 2400 river bet into a 5500 pot) is very value‑heavy (sets/paired hands like 22/66/44/5x/4x and thin value from 6x). Calling 2400 to win ~10,300 requires ~23% equity; my real showdown equity vs that range is well below breakeven. Raising to 4600 would be an all‑in bluff with little fold equity and poor EV since I don’t sufficiently block the villain’s value combos. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold to this large river bet.

UTG in a 6-handed game, Q3 suited is well below a profitable opening range. It’s frequently dominated when we make a queen, and when we make a small flush we can still be in reverse-implied-odds spots versus higher suited hands. With aggressive opponents behind (notably Elon/Flash) who can iso/3-bet, limping or opening creates avoidable high-variance, negative-EV situations. Best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger UTG holdings.

K6o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated potential against better kings. In middle position after UTG fold, with deep stacks and 6-handed play, it's a clear fold to avoid difficult postflop situations and wait for stronger holdings to maximize edge over 150 hands.

I am in the Cutoff with Pocket Sixes (6h 6d). The action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. My stack is deep (111 BBs), and I have position on the blinds. I have established a pattern of opening to 2.5 BBs (250) from this position in previous hands, so maintaining this sizing disguises my hand strength and balances my range. 66 is a vulnerable hand but strong enough to open late, aiming to take down the pot pre-flop or hit a set post-flop.

I’m on the button with 3♣9♣ — a weak but suited hand with good playability in position. Pro’s standard cutoff open to 250 is common and my call (250 ≈ 5.4% of my 4600) is cheap; calling preserves positional advantage and allows me to realize equity on flops where I can pick up a club/straight or bluff-catch. A raise here with 9♣3♣ is thin and will either commit me with a poor hand or get 3-bet by stronger holdings; I’ll fold to a sizable 3-bet preflop and proceed cautiously postflop if I miss (look to float/see turn or press when I pick up equity).

AhJh is a premium suited broadway hand. Pro opened to 250 and Minni called. In the SB, I have a strong hand that plays well both in 3-bet pots and multiway. I'll 3-bet to around 1000 to build the pot and potentially isolate, as this hand has good equity against opening ranges and plays well postflop with nut flush potential.

I am holding Ace-Jack suited in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a 6-max game. The action involves an open from the Cutoff, a call from the Button, and a 10BB squeeze from the Small Blind (Claude). Claude has a relatively short stack of 5350 (53.5 BBs). By 4-betting to 2500, I accomplish two goals: I likely fold out Pro and Minni, eliminating the disadvantage of playing a multi-way pot out of position, and I put Claude in a difficult position where he must either fold or commit his entire stack. Given Claude's previous active but somewhat foldable play (e.g., Hand #13 and Hand #17), a 4-bet represents extreme strength. If Claude shoves, I'll be getting nearly 3:1 odds to call, which A-Js often warrants against a 6-max 3-betting range. Taking control of the pot now is the most aggressive and professional way to leverage my stack size.

I opened to 2.5 BB with Pocket Sixes, faced a flat call from the Button, a 3-bet to 10 BB from the Small Blind, and now a cold 4-bet to 25 BB from the Big Blind. This is immense strength shown by the Big Blind (Flash). To call, I would need to put in 2250 chips. My implied odds are terrible because the effective stack with the 4-bettor is only ~7775, giving me roughly 3.5-to-1 implied odds, which is far below the required ~15-to-1 to profitably set mine. Additionally, the original 3-bettor (Claude) and the caller (Minni) still have to act behind me and could shove. Pocket Sixes are crushed by a cold 4-betting range (usually QQ+, AK). This is an easy fold.

I’m folding. I called the CO’s 250 open with 9♣3♣ intending to see a flop in position, but Claude’s 3-bet to 1000 and Flash’s subsequent 4‑bet to 2500 make this a very unforgiving spot. The pot is ~4000 and calling costs 2250 more (≈51.7% of my 4350 stack), giving me pot odds of only ~36%. 3♣9♣ has well under that equity against a typical 3‑bet/4‑bet range (and it’s worse multi‑way), so a call is a low-expected-value commitment. Raising/all‑in as a bluff would risk nearly my whole stack for little fold equity with two competent callers behind. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot on the button.

Facing a 4-bet from Flash after my 3-bet. Flash 4-bet to 2500, I need 1500 more to call. AhJh is a nice suited broadway hand but facing a 4-bet OOP, I'm likely dominated by AA, KK, QQ, AK, and crushed by JJ. If I call 1500, I'll have 2850 behind into a ~5500 pot (SPR ~0.5), essentially committing myself with a hand that's probably behind. 5-bet jamming is also bad as I'd only get called by better. Flash has shown aggression in previous hands. Against a standard 4-bet range, AhJh has insufficient equity to continue. Disciplined fold here.

Pocket Aces is the best starting hand in poker, especially UTG in a 6-handed tournament. With deep effective stacks and position out of position post-flop, I need to raise to build the pot for value, take initiative, and thin the field to reduce multiway pots where AA is vulnerable to sets. Standard UTG raise size around 3x the BB (300) given the history of similar raises (e.g., 250-300 frequently seen). This balances protection and value extraction while avoiding overcommitting early.

I hold 8s 9c (off-suit connectors) in Middle Position facing a raise to 300 from the UTG player (Elon), who is the chip leader. An off-suit 8-9 is a weak hand that performs poorly against a strong UTG opening range. Additionally, there are four players left to act behind me, increasing the risk of a squeeze or playing a multi-way pot out of position relative to the button. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding. Folding is the only prudent option.

Hand: J♦5♠ in cutoff facing an UTG open to 300. Elon (UTG) has shown frequent strength and large stacks behind (SB and BB) so calling or light 3‑betting here is too loose — J5o is easily dominated and performs poorly OOP multi‑way. My stack (~43bb) is worth preserving for better spots; a min‑raise to 500 or a call for 300 risks getting isolated by stronger ranges or squeezed by deeper stacks. Fold and wait for a cleaner opportunity in position.

T5o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. Even with button position, this hand doesn't play well enough to call 300. Elon raised from early position which typically indicates strength, and I'd be committing 6% of my stack with a marginal hand that has poor playability. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 6h. Elon raised to 300 from Under the Gun (UTG), which typically indicates a strong range. Q6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a chipleader's UTG open. The risk of being dominated is high, and I do not have the pot odds or hand strength to justify a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

UTG opened to 3x and it’s folded to us in the BB, so we’re heads-up versus a strong, positionally disciplined range. 7♠4♦ offsuit has very poor playability and reverse implied odds; it doesn’t make strong top pairs and can’t profitably continue on many flops. A light 3-bet bluff is also unattractive here (no blockers, vs UTG strength, deep enough stacks to get played back). Best EV is to release and preserve chips for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Seven offsuit (Qd 7s). This is a statistically weak hand that falls well outside a standard opening range for Under the Gun in a 6-max game. The hand has poor playability and is easily dominated by calling ranges. To preserve my stack and avoid marginal spots out of position, the correct strategic decision is to fold immediately.

I’m in middle position with A♣8♥ and about 43BB. This is a reasonable open from MP in a 6-max game — it plays well postflop and has good blocker value to A‑x hands. I want to take the initiative, avoid a multiway pot, and apply pressure to the cutoff/button/SB. A standard ~3x BB open (to 300 total) is the right sizing here: it keeps the pot manageable versus the big stacks (Flash/Elon) but gives me fold equity against the small stack cutoff and the blinds. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll give it up (A8o is often dominated), but I can continue versus a small cold‑3bet or call in position depending on the opponent and sizing. Raise to 300 to steal/define the pot.
63 suited is a very weak hand - not worth calling a raise with players behind. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Q5o. Minni raised to 300 from middle position. Q5o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a raise. Even with the advantage of position, calling here would be a mistake as I am likely dominated by Minni's raising range. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play in this spot.

Minni opens to 3bb from middle position and we’re in the small blind, out of position with T4o and the big blind still to act. This hand is dominated frequently, plays poorly postflop, and isn’t a good 3-bet candidate. Even with decent pot odds, realizing equity OOP multiway is tough, so we preserve stack and fold.

KJo is a profitable defend from the big blind against a middle position raise, as it has good equity, playability, and blocks top of Minni's range (KJ+, AKo). Minni has demonstrated loose raising tendencies in prior hands (e.g., Hand 10, 16, 22, 27 with small raises and folds behind), suggesting a wide range we crush or compete well against OOP. Deep effective stacks (40BB for Minni) favor realizing equity postflop rather than polarizing with a 3-bet, where we'd face calls/shoves awkwardly OOP. Pot is small (450), calling 200 more keeps it multiway-pot friendly if needed, but heads-up OOP is playable with this hand. Folding is -EV tight; raising risks bloating pot OOP without huge fold equity given opponent's aggression.

TPTK (KdJh on Kh9c2c) is very strong heads-up OOP vs Minni's loose MP open range, which includes Kx (we block Kd), Ax suited/club draws, broadways, mid-pairs. Board drawy (clubs, gutshots like J10/T8), so bet for value vs worse Kx/pairs/draws and protection. Checking risks free cards to draws or induces bluffs but pot-controls awkwardly OOP; betting builds pot when ahead (~70-80% equity vs range). Minni short (40BB), may call/shove lighter, but we crush calls. Size ~60% pot denies equity, extracts from floats. History shows Minni aggressive but folds to pressure (e.g., Hands 10,16). Deep stacks favor value-betting strong hands.

Heads-up vs Elon's BB after my MP open: flop K♥ 9♣ 2♣ and he bets 400 into a 1050 pot. I hold A♣8♥ — no pair but the Ace is a valuable blocker and I have very small backdoor club potential (need both turn+river clubs, ~4%). I also have ~12.4% chance to hit an Ace by the river. Folding here is too weak — my Ace-high is ahead of lots of bluffs/weak holdings and calling keeps the pot manageable. Raising to the min (800) as a semi-bluff is an option, but it’s risky: many Kx/paired hands will call or 3-bet, and I’d be committing too much with limited equity. Pot odds to call 400 into 1050 (~27.6%) plus my showdown/blocker value and turn improvement chances justify a call and a turn re-evaluation. Plan: call 400, see the turn, fold to big aggression if I don’t improve, but apply pressure or extract value if I pair my Ace or get a favorable runout.

TPTK (KdJh on Kh982c board) remains strong heads-up OOP vs Minni's wide MP opening range, which includes Kx (blocked), broadway, suited aces/clubs, pairs/draws. Turn 8c adds another club (board now three clubs: 9c,2c,8c), completing nut flush draws (A-Qc suited etc.) and adding straight draw outs (e.g., J10 for gutshot). However, Minni's flop call after my 400 value bet suggests she has showdown value or draws, but our hand crushes weaker Kx (KQ/KT/K9), pairs (99/88/22), missed draws, and floats. Pure flush combos are low-weight in her range (loose MP open ~15-20%: KQo+, AJo+, suited connectors, etc.); she often has overpairs vulnerable or stubborn draws. Checking risks giving free river cards to draws (10 outs for flush) or check-raises from monsters; betting extracts value from worse (~60-70% equity vs calling range), denies equity (~33% pot denial), and leverages fold equity given her 3650 stack (2.5x pot left, shove range polarized). History shows Minni calls light but folds to turn pressure (e.g., Hand 26 turn fold). Size 1000 (~2/3 pot) balances value/protection without overbetting OOP; larger risks fold out worse but invites shoves we snap. Deep stacks (mine) allow us to realize equity if called/shoved.

I hold A♣8♥ and I just paired my 8 on the turn (board 9♣ K♥ 2♣ 8♣). Elon bet 1,000 into a ~2,450 pot — calling 1,000 gives me ~29% pot odds (1,000 / (2,450+1,000) ≈ 29%). I have meaningful showdown value now (pair + an Ace blocker) and block some of Elon's best club/ace combos, while his line also includes a lot of Kx/medium pairs and bluffs from his wide BB calling range. Raising to the minimum (~2,000) would commit too many chips with a marginal hand and is unlikely to fold out enough better hands (and will get called or 3‑bet by the strong hands that dominate me such as Kx or completed flushes). Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and re‑evaluate the river (raise for value if I improve or fold to heavy continued aggression if I don't). Therefore I will call the 1,000.

River Td is relatively neutral: completes QJ straight (low density in Minni's MP raise + 2x OOP check-call range: ~1-4 suited combos like QJs/QJc), improves J10/QT/A10 to two-pair (unlikely passive line), pairs 10x (KJ blocks KT), but bricks most flushes (still only 3 clubs), gutshots (missed), and worse draws. Our TPTK (KdJh) crushes top of her value range (KQ/KT/K9/Kx flush draws, 99/TT/JJ/88/22, Ax high), which called flop/turn passively OOP on draw-heavy board. Flushes/sets/straights possible but check-call twice suggests capped (not raising monsters); history shows Minni calls light OOP but vulnerable (e.g., Hand 18 turn call then fold, Hand 26 turn fold, Hand 33 river fold). Equity vs her range ~60-70% (beats worse Kx/pairs > bluffs/draws). OOP first, value bet extracts max from stubborn worse hands; check risks free showdown or thin value bets we call. Size ~3/4 pot (2600) into 3450 denies equity, gets 1-2 streets value equiv from <KQ etc., commits her 2650 stack (near pot odds shove only nuts). +EV thin value line consistent with prior streets, exploits observed passive defense. Deep stack (16k) fine if snapped.

Pot is 6050, Elon bets 2600; calling 2600 commits essentially my whole stack (I’d be left with 50). Call odds = 2600 / (6050+2600) ≈ 30%. I hold A♣8♥ — second pair (8s) with the Ace club blocker. However the board (9♣ K♥ 2♣ 8♣ T♦) and Elon’s line (flop c-bet, larger turn bet after the club, and a substantial river bet) strongly favor value hands: completed club flushes (he could easily have two clubs and made a flush on the turn), Kx/top-two (K9, KT), sets (99,22, possibly 88), and even straights (QJ). My A♣ removes some club/A combos but leaves many two-club/value combos in his range. Against that value-heavy continuing range my pair of eights is behind far more than the ~30% equity needed to call. Raising all-in for 50 extra chips is an extremely thin bluff with little fold equity vs this line. Therefore the correct, lowest-variance play is to fold and preserve my remaining stack for better situations.

A♠9♠ in a 6-max game is strong enough to open from UTG — suited ace with good playability. My stack is ~26.5BB (2650), so I want to be proactive and build the pot with a standard open rather than limp (which invites multiway action and removes fold equity). I’ll raise to a 3x standard open (300) to take the initiative, narrow the field, and give myself favorable heads-up or positional scenarios to realize equity. If I face a substantial 3-bet or shove I’ll re-evaluate based on who it is and pot odds, but for now opening to 300 balances fold equity and pot control.

Q6 suited is a marginal hand facing a UTG raise. While it has some playability being suited, it's not strong enough to call 300 with several players behind who could squeeze. Minni's UTG raise suggests strength, and my hand doesn't play well multi-way or against a strong range. Easy fold.

Minni has raised to 300 from UTG. Minni is currently short-stacked with only about 23 big blinds. A raise from UTG usually signifies a strong range. My hand, Jack of Diamonds and Eight of Hearts (J8o), is weak and likely dominated by Minni's opening range. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me, including the button. Calling with such a marginal hand is not profitable in this position. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

Button vs UTG open from a short stack, A4o is not strong enough to happily take a flop multiway (dominated by better Ax) but it is a good candidate to 3-bet bluff because of the ace blocker and position. Minni has shown a willingness to open/fold to 3-bets earlier, and I’d like to deny the blinds an easy squeeze over my flat. I’m raising with the plan to fold if Minni jams (equity too low vs a shove range) and to proceed cautiously if called due to the low SPR.

Preflop in SB with Ac5d offsuit facing UTG open-raise to 300 by Minni (short stack 23.5BB) and button 3-bet to 900 by FiveTwo (85BB). Pot 1350, to call 850 (pot odds ~1.6:1, needing ~38% equity). A5o is speculative but offsuit, dominated by typical calling/3-betting ranges (AJs+, AQo+, TT+), poor postflop playability OOP vs two strong ranges potentially. FiveTwo aggressive historically but UTG open strengthens the spot. Deep effective stacks (my 199BB, FiveTwo 85BB); folding marginal hand preserves stack, avoids OOP multiway pots with dominated ace. Standard fold, seek better spots.

I hold [3d 2c] in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands. I am facing a raise from UTG and a 3-bet from the Button. The pot odds (requiring 800 to call into a pot of 1350) are insufficient for such a weak hand against two strong ranges, particularly out of position. Cold calling a 3-bet with 3-high unsuited is -EV. I must fold.

FiveTwo has 3-bet me on the button to 900 after my UTG open to 300. I have A♠9♠ and a 2350 stack (about 23.5BB). This hand is too good to just fold to a single 3-bet — the suited ace gives me blockers and decent equity — but it’s not strong enough to 4-bet-push or to make a thin isolated 4-bet (min-4-bet to 1500 would leave me with only ~850 behind and is too polarizing against a button 3-bet). Pot is 1350 and I need 600 more to call; calling gives me ~31% break-even equity (pot ~1950 after my call) which A9s often meets vs a typical 3-bet range. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves options postflop, and still leaves me ~1450 (≈14.5BB) to leverage if I hit. Plan: call now to see a flop; if I hit an Ace or a strong spade/connected board I can press, otherwise proceed cautiously OOP and fold to heavy turn/river aggression. Therefore I will call (total committed = 900).

I’m out of position with A♠9♠ on K♥ T♠ 4♦ — I only have Ace-high plus a backdoor spade draw and an Ace blocker, but I’m behind most realistic 3‑bettor value hands (Kx, TT, overpairs, AK). The pot (1,950) is already larger than my remaining stack (1,750), so pot‑control is critical: I don’t want to barrel off OOP with a marginal hand and commit my tournament life without improving. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize my (small) equity, and allows me to call small continuation bets while folding to large pressure. Plan: check — if Villain bets small (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 600–650) I’ll call to see a turn for my backdoors; if Villain fires a large bet or raises I will fold most of the time. If I pair my Ace or complete a spade on the turn I’ll transition to value/pressure accordingly.

We 3-bet pre and get a very low SPR pot versus a short stack. On K-T-4 rainbow we have bottom pair with an ace kicker: enough to deny equity and sometimes get folds from unpaired broadways (AQ/AJ/QJ that missed), but not strong enough to happily stack off versus a check-jam that will often be Kx/Tx or medium+ pocket pairs. Best is a small c-bet that pressures her overcards while keeping our loss minimal if she shoves and we have to fold.

FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop and now fires a very small c‑bet: 450 into a 2400 pot (~19% of the pot). I’m OOP with A♠9♠ on K♥ T♠ 4♦ — currently just Ace‑high with two backdoor routes (a double spade runner requirement to make a flush, and paired-A/9 outs). Calling 450 gives me 2850 to win for a 450 call, so I’m getting ~15.8% pot odds. My realistic equity to improve to a competitive hand by the river (pairing an A or 9, plus small added straight possibilities) is comfortably above that, and I also hold a useful Ace‑blocker to reduce some of Villain’s strong Ace combos. Raising OOP with a marginal hand would unnecessarily polarize and commit a large portion of my ~1750 stack; folding a hand this strong to such a small probe bet is too conservative. Plan: call now (keep pot controlled OOP), see the turn — if I pair or pickup substantial equity I’ll proceed aggressively, otherwise fold to significant turn/river pressure.

I have A♠9♠ on 4♦ K♥ T♠ 8♣ — right now I’m only Ace‑high. The flop gave only a backdoor spade draw and that is dead after the turn (8♣), so I have no meaningful draw and only thin pair‑outs (three A's + three 9's ≈ 13% to pair on the river). The pot is 2,850 and my remaining stack is 1,300 (~13 BB), so any bluff shove or large bet here commits a big chunk of my stack into a pot where Villain’s 3‑bet/c‑bet line will have many hands that will call profitably (Kx, TT, overpairs, broadways). I want to preserve fold equity and avoid getting called off by better hands: checking controls the pot OOP, lets me see Villain’s action, and keeps the option to call a small bet or fold to heavy pressure. If Villain checks I get to the river cheaply; if he bets small I can call one more street but I’ll fold to large aggression. For these reasons I check (amount = 0).

Turn brings the 8c, which doesn’t improve our A4 other than keeping our weak showdown hand (pair of 4s). Versus Minni’s check-call flop range in a low-SPR 3-bet pot, she has a lot of Kx/Tx and some sets, plus a few straight-draws. If we bet, her remaining stack is small enough that we frequently face a shove and get pushed into an awkward thin spot with bottom pair. Checking back realizes our equity (we can still spike an A or 4), keeps the pot controlled, and lets her bluff missed overcards on the river where we can make a more informed decision based on sizing/runout.

Board: 4♦ K♥ T♠ 8♣ 7♠; I hold A♠9♠ — I have no pair (Ace‑high) and the river 7♠ does not complete a five‑card spade flush (I only have four spades total). Villain (FiveTwo) was the preflop 3‑bettor, c‑bet small on the flop and then pot‑controlled with checks on turn/river — a line that contains a lot of value (Kx, Tx, overpairs, occasional sets) and is unlikely to fold a river shove frequently. Pot is ~2850 and my remaining stack is 1300: shoving or betting large here risks most of my stack into a spot with poor fold equity and little showdown improvement. A small thin bluff also has low expected value versus this opponent and line. Best play is to preserve chips and take Ace‑high to showdown — check and see if he bluffs/has a worse hand; avoid committing my tournament life with a naked Ace‑high.

Minni has checked the river in a low-SPR 3-bet pot after check-calling flop. Our hand is just bottom pair (4s) with an ace kicker, which has decent showdown value versus her missed overcards (AQ/AJ/QJ type hands) but is in terrible shape versus the bulk of her continuing range (Kx/Tx, pocket pairs 55–QQ, and occasional made straights like J9). Betting doesn’t get called by many worse hands, and any bet can induce a short-stack check-jam that forces us into an ugly thin call/fold spot. Checking back maximizes EV by realizing our showdown equity and avoiding paying off her made hands.

QJo from UTG is a reasonable opening hand in 6-max. It's on the borderline but playable. Standard open to 250 (2.5x). With 53BB effective, I can comfortably raise here and fold to 3-bets if needed. Minni is short-stacked which might invite squeeze plays, but I shouldn't let that deter me from playing a solid hand.

I am holding 4h 2c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Claude has raised from Under the Gun (UTG), suggesting a strong range. Playing this hand out of position against an early position raiser is mathematically unsound. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Claude opens UTG to 2.5bb in a 6-max configuration, which is still a relatively strong position/range. 9♣6♦ offsuit plays poorly (dominated, weak equity realization, tough postflop) and doesn’t make a good 3-bet bluff candidate versus UTG. With plenty of playable stack depth, the highest-EV choice is to pass and wait for better spots.

Q5o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit, poor playability, and dominated by most calling ranges. Facing a UTG raise from Claude, who has shown aggression in previous hands but typically with decent holdings, calling or raising is -EV. Position on button is nice, but this hand doesn't connect well postflop, especially multiway if blinds defend (Minni is short but could shove). Deep stack (19.9k), pot odds poor (250 to call into 400 pot pre, but implied odds low with this hand). Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 9s facing a UTG raise to 250 from Claude. The pot is 400, and I need to call 200. My hand (98o) is weak, especially against an Under-The-Gun opening range which is typically strong. Additionally, playing out of position is difficult, and the Big Blind (Minni) is short-stacked (12 BBs) and could easily shove, forcing me to fold and lose my investment. The expected value of calling here is negative. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I’m on the BB with 5♦3♠ and a 1,200 stack (≈12 BB). Claude opened UTG to 250 — an early-position raise representing a strong range. 5‑3 offsuit has very poor equity and plays terribly postflop against an UTG open; calling another 150 or risking an all‑in jam here is a marginal, high-variance play. With a short stack I should preserve chips for a better spot (or shove only with hands that have much better equity or fold equity), so I’ll fold.

I am in the Under The Gun (UTG) position with 3h 7s, which is an extremely weak hand. In a 6-handed game, UTG opening requirements are relatively tight, and 73-offsuit is well outside any reasonable opening range. There is no strategic value in playing this hand from early position.

K6o is too weak to open from middle position in a 6-max lineup with two players behind who have shown aggression (3-bets/isolations), and the blinds include a short SB who can reshove. Opening or limping risks getting 3-bet or playing dominated kings out of position without good playability. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for better spots.

Preflop in cutoff with two folds ahead (UTG and MP). Standard CO opening range in 6-max tournament includes suited broadways, strong aces, pairs, suited connectors, but J5o offsuit is too weak: poor high card strength, dominated often, limited straight potential with gappy cards, and offsuit removes flush draws. No need to limp (bad strategy). Raising light is possible in position but this hand has negative EV against typical calling/3bet ranges and shortstack SB shove range. Pot odds don't justify calling. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Opponents' histories show loose play but no need to force with trash.

I am on the Button with Q9o. The action is folded to me. While this is a standard position to steal the blinds, I need to consider the stack sizes and opponent tendencies. Minni in the Small Blind has a short stack of 1,150 chips (11.5 BB). In Hand #30, Minni 3-bet raised me when I opened KTh, forcing a fold. With her stack size now in 'push or fold' territory, there is a high risk she will jam all-in if I open. Q9o is a marginal hand that cannot call a shove and plays poorly against a calling range. Given the high likelihood of facing a re-raise from the aggressive short stack, and having plenty of chips (108 BB) to wait for better spots, the disciplined play is to fold.

I have A♥ K♥ in the small blind with the action folded to me. Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call; my stack is 1,150 (~11.5BB). With AK suited and only the big blind left to act, this is a clear shove spot — maximizes fold equity, isolates the BB, and avoids playing a big pot out of position with a short stack. A min-raise or just calling leaves me in tough postflop spots and invites 3-bets from deeper stacks. If called I have strong equity versus typical calling ranges (pocket pairs and broadways). All-in is the highest EV play here.

Minni raised to 1150 leaving only 50 chips behind - essentially an all-in. I'm getting pot odds of about 2.2:1 (calling 1050 to win 1250). 9To isn't great but against a desperation shove from a short stack, I have reasonable equity. Minni's range is likely very wide given they're essentially shoving their last chips. Easy call given the pot odds.

I have top pair (A♥) with the best kicker (K♥) but only 50 chips left after my large preflop commitment. The pot is 2,300, so a 50-chip bet is essentially a token — it won’t fold many worse A‑x or medium pairs and it doesn’t meaningfully protect against draws. Checking controls the pot size and keeps quieter hands and bluffs in; if Claude bets I can call my remaining 50 for very good odds, and if he checks behind I’m already in position to win at showdown as a favorite. With such a tiny residual stack, there’s no extra EV to be gained by a micro-bet, so I check.

I flopped middle pair with Td on a 5d-Ad-Th board. Minni checked with only 50 chips behind - they're essentially committed. I should bet to either take the pot now or get their last 50 chips in while I have a made hand. Any bet puts them all-in. I'll bet small to price them in or take it down.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♥K♥) and only 50 chips left. The pot is 2,400 and Claude’s flop bet is tiny (100), so calling 50 is an absolute bargain — tremendous pot odds to see showdown with the best hand versus most of his calling/continuation ranges (we beat weak aces, single pocket pairs, floats, etc.). I can’t meaningfully protect or extract more with a 50-chip stack; folding would be too nitty given the price. Call all-in for the 50 to realize equity and maximize EV.

We’re UTG 6-handed with 4♣2♣, a very weak suited wheel connector. From first position we’re likely to face multiple players behind who can isolate/3-bet, and when we do see a flop we’ll often be dominated when we make a pair and have poor playability out of position. With no special incentive to enter (and plenty of better steal/raise candidates), the highest-EV line is to pass and preserve stack for stronger spots.

53o is an extremely weak hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. In middle position pre-flop after UTG fold, facing standard blinds (pot 150, to call 100), folding is optimal to avoid playing a dominated hand out of position post-flop. No reason to limp or raise this trash; preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with 6d3d, a very weak suited one-gapper. While late position allows for wider opens, this hand is at the very bottom of the range and generally not profitable to open, even in an unopened pot. The Button has a short stack (24BB) and might shove, and the Big Blind covers me. There is no strategic merit in trying to steal the blinds with this specific holding given the risks. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I’m on the button with 7♣4♦ — a very weak, offsuit, poorly-connected hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop; min-raise is 200. With my stack (2,400 = 24bb) and two deep/aggro blinds (Flash has shown willingness to 3-bet/defend, Claude calls/plays back), raising to steal risks a call or a 3-bet that leaves me in a dominated, marginal postflop spot. Calling 100 is cheap but still doesn’t give me the ~40% equity I’d need to justify investing out of position against likely defending ranges. Preserve the stack for a better spot — fold preflop.

Folded to me in SB with T5o. Against just the BB (Flash), I have a weak hand but getting good pot odds (50 to win 150). Flash has shown aggression before. With this weak hand, I'll just complete to see a cheap flop rather than raise and potentially face a 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The action folded to the Small Blind, who just completed the blind by calling the remaining 50. Since I have the option to see a flop for free, checking is the most sensible play with a mediocre hand like J6o. There is no need to inflate the pot or risk a reraise by attempting a bluff here.

Flopped two pair (tens and fives) on 5d-Th-9s board. This is a strong hand that I need to bet for value. The board has straight draw potential (J8, 87, QJ) so I want to charge draws. Betting around 2/3 pot for value.

On a flop of 5-T-9, my Jack-Six offsuit has no made value and no significant draw. Claude completed the small blind pre-flop and is now leading for roughly two-thirds of the pot (135 into 200). Given the board texture connects reasonably well with a wide small blind completing range (hands like QJ, T8, 97, or even 5x), calling with just Jack-high and no real equity is a losing play. There is no reason to float or bluff here when I have no pieces of the board.

Pocket Jacks are a premium hand from UTG in 6-handed play. Standard strategy is to raise to isolate, build the pot, and narrow the field against speculative hands from later positions. With a deep stack of 199BB, I can afford aggression. Recent raise sizes in history are around 225-300, so raising to 300 (3x BB) is a balanced size that shows strength without overcommitting. Minni is short at 24BB, but others are deep, so this size discourages multi-way pots.

I am in Middle Position facing a raise from the Under-the-Gun player (Elon) to 300 (3BB). My hand, Queen-Three suited, is very weak and easily dominated by an UTG opening range, even from an aggressive player. Calling would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop, possibly out of position or dominated, and there are still four players left to act behind me who could squeeze or call. There is no strategic merit to calling or 3-betting this hand in this spot. The correct play is to fold.

Elon opened UTG to 300 — in 6-max that’s a relatively strong opening range. I have A♣T♥ in the cutoff which is a playable hand in position but is often dominated by UTG’s value hands (AK/AQ/ATx, TT+). With a 24bb stack I don’t want to commit with a marginal hand or make a tiny 3-bet that just invites callers behind. Calling keeps the pot manageable and gives me position post-flop to realize equity and extract value when I hit top pair or good draws. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold (ATo does poorly against polarized 3-bets), but I’ll continue aggressively on favorable flops (A high, good draws) and control the pot when I miss. Therefore I will call the 300 to play the hand in position.

Q2o is a weak hand. Facing a raise from Elon and a call from Minni, I don't have the equity or playability to profitably call here. Even with position on the button, Q2o is simply too weak to invest 300 chips (nearly 7% of my stack). Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 2d. Elon opened from UTG to 300, and Minni called from the Cutoff. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1 to call, 52 suited is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an UTG opening range. Additionally, Minni has a short stack, which limits the potential for implied odds. The best play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re getting a very good price in the BB: calling 200 more to play a pot that will be 950 (need ~17% equity). Even though 9♥4♥ is weak and we’ll be out of position, it’s suited (can realize equity via flushes/strong draws) and there’s a short-stacked caller (Minni) creating extra dead money and potential to win a decent pot when we connect hard. I’ll defend with a call and play largely fit-or-fold postflop, avoiding making thin top-pair continuations with this kicker profile.

We flop a very strong two-pair (9s3d4d with 9h4h) in a multiway pot. Leading is fine, but checking has higher EV here: (1) it lets UTG (the preflop raiser) continue with overpairs, 9x, diamond draws, and overcards that might fold to a donk; (2) it gives us a clean check-raise line to start building a pot while charging the many bad turn cards (diamonds, 5/6, broadway overcards); and (3) it keeps Minni’s short stack in with dominated hands/draws so we can potentially win their stack. Plan: check with intent to raise most reasonable bets for value/protection.

Pocket Jacks remain very strong as an overpair on a 9s-3d-4d flop, which is relatively dry but features a diamond flush draw and possible low straight draws/gutshots (e.g., 52, A5). FiveTwo checked from the BB, which is standard, and Minni flat-called pre-flop from CO with a short stack (21BB), suggesting a wide range including suited connectors, Ax, pairs, etc. No one has shown down nuts recently; opponents defend wide but fold to continued aggression often (e.g., Hand 1, 7, 13). As pre-flop raiser with positionally strong hand, c-bet for value against worse pairs/draws, protection vs. equity, and to deny free cards. Pot 950, betting ~2/3 pot (600) balances range, builds pot with equity advantage, and pressures Minni's short stack while keeping deep stacks involved if they continue. Folding equity high if they miss.

Elon opened UTG and has continued with a 600 c-bet into a 1550 pot on a very dry 9♠-3♦-4♦ board. I have A♣T♥ — no pair but the Ace blocks a lot of his Ax value combos and my hand has decent showdown value in position. Calling 600 gives me ~28% immediate pot odds and lets me use position to control the pot and make more informed decisions on the turn. Raising as a bluff would commit too much of my ~21bb stack and will often get called by better hands from an UTG opener; folding is too passive and wastes the equity of my Ace. Plan: call, see the turn — if I hit an Ace or villain shows weakness I can extract value; if villain barrels big and I don’t improve I’ll fold.

We have a very strong value hand (top+bottom two pair) on a wet, dynamic board in a multiway pot. Elon’s c-bet range here contains plenty of hands that will continue vs a raise (overpairs, 9x, diamond draws, combo draws), and Minni is short enough that we should happily raise to put them at risk immediately. Raising also denies equity to overcards/diamond/straight draws and sets up a low SPR on the turn so we can comfortably commit on most runouts. I’ll check-raise to a size that (1) effectively puts Minni all-in and (2) builds a pot vs Elon without folding out everything except nutted hands.

Facing a check-raise from FiveTwo in the BB after my standard c-bet and Minni's call on a 9s-3d-4d board. JJ remains an overpair with good equity against FiveTwo's check-raising range, which is polarized towards strong hands like sets (99, 44, 33), two-pair combos (94s, 43s), and flush draws/straight draws (Adx, 52d, A5d). Pure bluffs are possible given FiveTwo's aggressive history (frequent post-flop bets/raises in hands like 7,19,25,28,29,31,33), but less likely after Minni's flat-call, which blocks some draws/pairs and suggests she has something like a weak pair, Ax, or draw. Pot is 4950, calling 2200 more offers ~2.25:1 odds requiring ~31% equity; JJ likely has 40-50%+ vs. this range and crushes draws. Minni is short (1500 stack, ~900 left after her call) and will likely call any additional amount all-in regardless, building the pot further. Raising risks commitment vs. monsters while deep (190BB effective vs. FiveTwo's 78BB), but calling keeps pot odds favorable, controls pot size, and allows re-evaluation on turn (e.g., fold to further aggression if brick). Folding premium hand here leaks EV long-term in tournament with ICM considerations low at hand 41/150. Call to realize equity while maintaining fold equity on later streets.

Elon opened UTG and continued on the flop — a fairly strong range. I called the 600 c-bet to see a turn, but FiveTwo’s huge raise to 2800 and Elon’s call polarize the action toward very strong made hands (sets, 9x, maybe overpairs or strong diamond draws) or a heavy semi-bluff spectrum. My A♣T♥ is just Ace-high with no diamond backdoor and only blocks some Ax combos; versus two players who just committed large chips I’m almost certainly behind. Pot is ~7150 and calling 1500 would be an effective all‑in needing ~17% equity to break even; against the likely ranges my equity is well below that. I should preserve my ~15bb stack for a better spot — fold.

We have top+bottom two pair on a very dynamic board (two diamonds, and the 5 completes some straights like A2/76/62 while also adding more turn+river runouts that can kill action or beat us). After check-raising the flop and getting called, the SPR is ~1, so we should keep building the pot while denying equity to diamond/straight draws and charging 9x/overpairs that can still be in Elon’s flop-call range. Checking gives too many free cards and lets him realize equity with his draws.

I’ll bet a size that keeps worse hands calling but still meaningfully taxes draws and sets up a clean river shove.

Facing a 3200 donk-bet (~45% pot) on turn after flatting his large flop check-raise, with JJ as a strong overpair on 9s-3d-4d-5s board. Pot odds >4:1 require ~19% equity, easily met vs. polarized range (sets/two-pair ~15-20% equity; draws ~70%+). Turn completes straights (2345,3456) and adds spade draw, improving some combos (e.g., 25d to str+fd) but missing many fds. FiveTwo's aggression (hands 1,5,7,19,etc.) includes bluffs; donk suggests value or semi-bluff. Call realizes equity cheaply, controls pot vs. his 1400-behind stack; raise isolates vs monsters unnecessarily. Re-eval river.

River (9s-3d-4d-5s-6d) is one of the worst cards for our 9h4h: it completes all 2x and 7x straights and brings in the diamond flush. With only 4600 behind into 13550, a shove is unlikely to get called by worse (overpairs/weak 9x will often just check back or even fold to a jam) and will be called primarily by hands that now beat us. Our hand is strong enough to win at showdown versus the portion of Elon’s range that’s one-pair/9x and scared by the runout, so the highest-EV line is to check and evaluate if he bets (likely folding to big, value-heavy action).

Pocket Jacks remain a strong overpair on this 9s-3d-4d-5s-6d river board. While straights (23456 or 34567) are possible with holdings like 27o/s, 72o/s, or 7x connectors, FiveTwo's line—check-raise flop huge after Minni's call, turn donk-bet, river check—polarizes to nuts (sets, two-pair like 94/43/36, straights), bluffs (missed diamond draws like KdQd, spade draws, pure air from BB defense), or medium strength trapping. History shows FiveTwo aggressive (check-raises in Hands 1,29,33; bets value/bluffs), but folds to resistance (Hand 1 river fold). Minni's fold post-flop caps her range, blocking some draws. JJ crushes his medium value (99,TT,88,77, pairs), beats all bluffs/draws (~70%+ equity), and has ~20-30% vs pure nuts; overall ~45%+ equity vs range. Pot 13550, FiveTwo 4600 behind (78BB start but committed heavily). All-in value bet maximizes EV: extracts max from calling ranges (worse pairs/two-pair), denies equity to draws, high fold equity vs air. Checking realizes showdown value but misses bets from worse; shoving builds stack in tournament phase 41/150 with no ICM pressure yet. Balanced aggression exploits his wide BB defense.

We’re getting excellent pot odds: calling 4600 to win 22750 (need ~20% equity). Despite the river being terrible (completes diamonds + any 2x/7x straights), Elon’s line can still contain enough thin value/bluffs—overpairs without a diamond, some 9x, and occasional missed/non-nut holdings turned into a shove-to-us after we check—that folding top+bottom two pair for this price is too tight. We bluff-catch and accept we’ll be beat often, but not >80% of the time.

I am in UTG position (first to act) with Ten-Eight suited (Tc 8c). In a 6-handed game, this hand is considered too loose to open from early position. While it has some post-flop playability (flush and straight potential), it is easily dominated by better hands that opponents might call or raise with. Additionally, there is a short stack (Minni) with 15BB behind me who might shove, and deep-stacked aggressive players in the blinds. The standard and disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a stronger hand or better position.

I have 5♦9♠ with a 1,500 stack (15 BB) in middle position. The pot is only 150 and there are four players to act behind (Claude 44 BB, Flash 115 BB, FiveTwo 227 BB, Elon 89 BB), so my fold equity is very low — a shove will almost certainly be called by players with much stronger ranges and a small open-raise to 200 invites 3-bets that I can’t comfortably navigate. Limping/calling 100 into a likely multiway pot is poor with a weak, uncoordinated hand and out of position. Preserving my tournament life and waiting for a better shove/raise spot is the prudent play here.

K9o in cutoff with two folds in front. Decent hand for a late position steal attempt. With 44BB stack, I should open raise to 2.5x to apply pressure on the blinds and button. If called, I'll have position on the blinds.

I am on the button with Jack-Six suited, which is a marginal hand. Claude opened from the cutoff for 2.5x the big blind. While being in position is an advantage, J6s does not play well enough against a standard cutoff opening range to justify a call, especially with the chip leader (FiveTwo) and an active player (Elon) behind me in the blinds who may squeeze. Maintaining stack discipline is more important here than playing a speculative hand.

Claude is opening from the cutoff with a ~41bb stack and has shown a willingness to fold to pressure. Calling Q5s from the small blind is tough (dominated Qx, poor realization OOP, and we invite the BB in), so I prefer a polarized 3-bet approach: use my big stack to apply pressure and pick up the pot uncontested often. Q5s has some playability when called (suited, can make strong draws) and a small blocker effect to some Qx continues. If Claude 4-bets/jams, I’m folding—this is a fold-equity 3-bet, not a hand to stack off with versus a shortish 4-bet range.

43o is a complete trash hand with no playability. Facing a 3-bet from the SB after a CO open, the range is strong (pairs, broadways, suited connectors maybe). Calling OOP into a bloated pot with this hand has zero equity realization and bad implied odds. Squeezing is suicidal with no blockers and dominated by villain's range. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament with 89BB effective.

K9o facing a 3-bet from SB. FiveTwo has been active and 3-betting. My hand doesn't play well against a 3-bet range - often dominated by KQ, KJ, KT, or crushed by AA/KK/AK. With 41BB stack, calling puts me in tough spots postflop OOP in a bloated pot. Pot odds need ~34% equity but K9o struggles against typical 3-bet ranges. Better to fold and preserve stack.

UTG in a 6-handed game with a 15BB stack (1500). J♦7♥ is a weak, easily dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability out of position. There are five players to act behind including a very deep, aggressive Button (FiveTwo) and other players who have shown willingness to 3‑bet/raise — calling 100 invites a multiway pot with little fold equity and raising to 200 is too small to achieve meaningful folds. Shoving from UTG with J7o is marginal and will be called or crushed too often. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot or a stronger hand; fold here.
A3o in middle position is marginal but playable. With 41.5 BB stack and Minni already folded, I'll open raise to try to take down the blinds. If I face a 3-bet, I can fold. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am in the Cutoff with a small pocket pair (4s 4c). Claude, in Middle Position, has raised to 250. Claude's stack is relatively short at 3,900 chips. Typically, when set-mining, you want the effective stack to be at least 20 times the bet you are calling to ensure sufficient implied odds. Here, the effective stack is about 15 times the raise (3900 / 250 = 15.6). Additionally, I have an aggressive player (FiveTwo) on the Button and two other players behind me who could potentially squeeze. Given the stack sizes and the likelihood of being pushed off the hand or not getting paid off enough when I hit a set, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my 115BB stack.

Facing a middle-position open from a shorter stack, 5♣2♠ is too weak to defend profitably even with button position. Calling invites tough multiway dynamics vs the blinds with a dominated, low-playability hand, and 3-betting risks getting jammed on by Claude’s ~39bb stack and having to forfeit a large preflop investment. With plenty of stack and better spots to apply pressure, this is a disciplined fold.

K9o is a marginal offsuit hand from the small blind facing a middle position raise to 250 (2.5BB). Out of position postflop against Claude (39BB stack), it plays poorly. Better kings dominate it, and no strong equity or playability justifies calling or 3-betting. Pot odds are decent (400 pot, 200 to call), but implied odds are poor OOP with this hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots. History shows Claude raises frequently but defends; still, standard fold.

The opponent Claude opened from Middle Position (Hijack) to 250 (2.5 BB). I am in the Big Blind with K6o. Pot odds are 150 to call into 400, requiring roughly 27% equity. While K6o has enough raw equity against a typical opening range, it suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds out of position, especially against a mid-position open where the opponent's range is stronger than a button open. K6o is typically outside the profitable defense range vs a Hijack open. Folding is the prudent play to preserve the stack.
3d6s offsuit in UTG is a clear fold. Weak, disconnected, low cards with no playability from the worst position at the table.

87s is a strong speculative hand in a 6-max game, especially from middle position. It has excellent post-flop playability and potential to make straights and flushes. I will open with a standard 2.5x raise to take initiative and build a pot, while remaining mindful of the short stack (Minni) in the big blind who may look to shove.

Flash opens 2.5bb from middle and we’re in position with QJo, a hand that plays reasonably as a call but is awkward as a 3-bet since it’s often dominated when called and may face a 4-bet. Given the short BB (14bb) who can jam behind and create an unpleasant multiway all-in if we bloat the pot, I prefer keeping the pot small, realizing equity in position, and taking postflop lines based on texture and tendencies rather than turning this into a high-variance preflop spot.

94o is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card value, suitedness, or connectivity. Facing a middle position raise from Flash and a cutoff call from FiveTwo, the strength of their ranges makes this a clear fold. Even with button position, the pot odds (calling 250 into 650) don't justify entering with such trash, especially with SB and BB yet to act (BB Minni is short at 14BB and could shove wide). Preserve stack for better opportunities in a tournament setting where maximizing stack over 150 hands is key.

I am in the Small Blind with J7o, facing a raise from Flash (Middle) and a call from FiveTwo (Cutoff). The pot odds are roughly 23.5% (calling 200 into a total pot of 850), but my hand is very weak and easily dominated. Additionally, I would be playing out of position against two opponents, one of whom is the deep-stacked chip leader. The Big Blind is also short-stacked and could squeeze, putting me in a difficult spot. Given the poor playability of J7o multi-way and out of position, the correct strategic decision is to fold.

7♦3♥ is a very weak, offsuit, unconnected hand with almost no playability. I’m facing a raise to 250 and a caller, so it’s a multiway pot and I’ll be out of position — both factors drastically reduce my equity. Calling 150 into a 650 pot requires ~19% equity; 7-3o vs a raiser+caller rarely has that, so calling is a poor long-term play. I’m short-stacked (≈14 BB) but still have enough chips to wait for a much better shove/call spot (pocket pairs, aces, broadway hands, or good suited connectors). Raising or shoving with this holding is too risky — deeper stacks behind me will call/3-bet and I’ll be dominated. Fold and conserve the stack for a better opportunity.

On a board of Ac-Th-Kc, I have a flush draw with my 8c-7c. However, this board texture is extremely favorable for the pre-flop caller's range, as it contains three broadway cards. Many hands in FiveTwo's calling range (like QJ, AK, AQ, AJ, or KT) have connected strongly here. Since I am out of position and the board hits the opponent's range so well, a continuation bet is risky and likely to be called or raised. Checking allows me to see how FiveTwo reacts; I can potentially get a free card to my flush or call a reasonably sized bet to realize my equity.

We flop the nut straight (Broadway) on A♣ T♥ K♣. Flash’s check caps him a bit and we want to start building a pot while also charging hands with equity (especially club draws, Qx, Jx, two-pair/sets). A smaller value bet keeps dominated hands in and can induce raises/bluffs, while denying a free club/board-pairing card.

I have a flush draw with 8c7c on a board of Ac-Th-Kc. While the board is very broadway-heavy and likely connects well with FiveTwo's calling range, the bet size of 250 into a 650 pot (total pot 900) provides me with excellent pot odds. I need to call 250 to potentially win a total pot of 1150, which is roughly 21.7% equity required. With 9 outs to a flush (approximately 19% to hit on the turn) and significant implied odds if I hit, calling is the most profitable decision here. I will call and see how the turn develops.

The 4d on the turn is a complete blank and does not change the board texture significantly. I still hold a flush draw with nine outs to the nuts. Given that FiveTwo showed aggression on the flop by betting into me on a very broadway-heavy board, it is likely they have a strong piece of this board or a draw of their own. Leading here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands like an Ace or a King, and I could face a raise. Checking is the best option to control the pot size and hopefully see a cheap river card to realize my equity.

We still have the nuts (Broadway) on a very dynamic texture where Flash can have plenty of hands that will continue: Ax/Tx/Kx that don’t believe yet, two-pair/sets that may raise, and club draws that must be charged. After his flop check-call, his range is weighted to made hands and draws rather than air, so we should keep building the pot while denying a free club/board-pair river. A medium sizing targets calls from one-pair and draws without folding out everything but monsters, and it also sets up a comfortable river value bet.

I have an 8-high flush draw on a very coordinated broadway board (Ac-Th-Kc-4d). FiveTwo is showing significant strength by betting both the flop and the turn. The pot currently stands at 1800, and it costs 650 to call, meaning I need approximately 26.5% equity to make a profitable call. With 9 outs to the flush, I have a 19.5% chance to hit on the river. While I lack the immediate pot odds, the implied odds against FiveTwo's large stack are substantial if I hit my flush, as their range likely includes many strong top-pair hands, two pairs, or even a straight (QJ) that they will struggle to fold. Folding is too weak given the equity, and raising is unnecessary given the strength shown by the opponent. I will call and attempt to realize my equity on the river.

The river Ten of Diamonds completes the board, and I have missed my flush draw. My hand, 8-high, has no showdown value. Throughout the hand, FiveTwo has shown significant strength by betting on both the flop and the turn on a very broadway-heavy board. Given the board texture (Ac-Th-Kc-4d-Td), it is highly likely that FiveTwo holds at least a pair of Kings, Aces, or even a straight or trips. Betting as a bluff is extremely risky here, as FiveTwo is unlikely to fold any significant part of their range on this board, especially with their large stack. Checking is the most prudent move, allowing me to fold if they bet or simply reach showdown if they check behind (though I will almost certainly lose).
