River pairs the T, so our Broadway straight is no longer the absolute nuts (boats/quads exist), but it’s still a very strong value hand versus Flash’s likely check-call range (Ax, Kx, Tx, and some missed clubs). After check-calling flop/turn and checking river, he has plenty of hands that will pay off a reasonable value bet, while his strongest full houses may go for a check-raise. I’ll bet around half-pot to get called by one-pair/two-pair/trips, keep his bluff-catchers in, and avoid pricing myself into a huge check-raise (plan: call small/medium raises that can be Tx, fold to big polar raises/jams that are heavily boats).

I have 8-high and have missed my flush draw on a board that is extremely coordinated (Ac-Th-Kc-4d-Td). FiveTwo has bet on the flop, turn, and river, showing significant strength throughout the hand. Given the board texture, it is highly likely they have at least a pair, two pair, a straight (QJ), or have improved to trips with the river Ten. My hand has zero showdown value, and calling 1300 into a pot of 3750 with 8-high is a losing play. I must fold.

I am UTG with Ace-Five suited (Ac 5c). In a 6-max game, this is a standard opening hand despite the early position because it has excellent playability, containing both nut-flush potential and straight 'wheel' potential (A-2-3-4-5). Opening for 2.5x the big blind (250) is a standard raise size to thin the field and take the initiative. I am mindful of Minni in the Small Blind, who is quite short-stacked (13.5 BBs) and may shove, but A5s is strong enough to consider calling a short-stack jam or used as a 4-bet bluff if a larger stack re-raises me due to its high card blockers.

72o has terrible playability and equity versus an UTG open in a 6-max lineup. Calling creates a dominated, multiway-prone spot with little ability to realize equity postflop, and 3-betting this specific hand is an unnecessarily low-quality bluff given players behind and no strong blockers. With a healthy stack, best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates.

3s7h is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, no high cards, and offsuit. Facing an UTG raise to 2.5BB from Flash, who has been active in previous hands, folding is mandatory even from cutoff position. No fold equity worth bluffing with this, and calling would be spew with dominated equity and poor playability postflop. Preserve stack for premium spots in this tournament with 45/150 hands played.

I hold 8c4c on the Button facing a raise to 250 from Flash in UTG. A UTG opening range is typically strong, and while I have position, my hand is a weak suited two-gapper. Additionally, the Small Blind (Minni) has a short stack of ~13.5 BB, making them a prime candidate to shove over a flat call, which would force me to fold and lose chips. Given the poor playability of 84s against a strong range and the threat of a squeeze, the disciplined play is to fold.

I’m short-stacked (1,350 ≈ 13.5bb) so this is effectively a push/fold spot facing an UTG open to 250 (2.5bb). T9o is marginal but has usable equity and plays well all-in; calling 200 would leave me ~11.5bb out-of-position with poor postflop options, and a tiny 3-bet to 400 won’t generate meaningful fold equity or isolate. Shoving maximizes fold equity against the raiser and the big blind, simplifies the decision, and gives me a chance to double up if called. I’m shoving all-in for my stack.
96 offsuit is a very weak hand. Minni has 3-bet to 1350 and is essentially committed with only 50 chips behind. I'd need to call 1250 with a trash hand against a 3-bet. Clear fold.

I am currently UTG with Ace-Five suited (Ac 5c), a hand that sits at the lower end of a standard UTG opening range in 6-max play. I opened for 2.5x (250). Minni, in the Small Blind, has re-raised to 1350, leaving herself with only 50 chips behind. This is effectively an all-in for her 14 BB stack. To call, I must commit 1100 more into a current pot of 1700. This gives me pot odds of roughly 1.55 to 1, meaning I need approximately 39.2% equity to break even on a call. Against a standard 14 BB 3-betting range from the SB against an UTG opener (typically 88+, AJs+, AQo+), A5s only has about 30% equity. Even if I expand her range slightly due to her short stack, I am likely a significant underdog. Given the goal of maximizing my stack over 150 hands, taking a high-variance, negative-EV gamble for 11% of my stack is suboptimal. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

6-handed UTG with 5♦6♥ offsuit is too weak to open profitably against multiple players left to act, especially with several competent big stacks who can 3-bet or defend and put us in tough, low-EV spots. Our chip lead means we don’t need to force thin steals from early position; better to preserve initiative for stronger holdings and apply pressure in later positions vs the short stacks.

Js6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity, and no strong blockers or suited potential. In middle position pre-flop facing a standard big blind (100 to call), folding is optimal to avoid speculative plays out of position with a hand that rarely makes strong hands. Pot odds are poor (calling 100 into 150 gives ~2:1 but hand has low implied odds), and raising has no value. Preserving stack for better spots in tournament with 46/150 hands played.

I hold 8d 4c in the Cutoff position. This is an extremely weak, unsuited hand with poor playability. With three players yet to act, including a short stack on the button who might shove and a big blind with a large stack, there is no reason to get involved. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Stacks and situation: I’m on the button with 1,750 (≈17.5BB) facing SB and BB (Claude 4,150; Flash 10,075). Pot is 150, to call 100. Hand is K♥3♥ — suited king but a weak kicker. With ~17–18BB the game shifts toward push/fold. A small open invites 3-bets from deeper stacks and leaves me in difficult postflop spots out of position; limping/calling is too passive and concedes initiative. Shoving maximizes fold equity from the blinds, simplifies the decision tree, and gives me a reasonable chance to double up if called. I’ll shove all-in to either take the blinds or get it in with decent equity against calling ranges.
J2o is a weak hand. Minni raised big to 1750, and I'd need to commit 40% of my stack to call with a marginal offsuit hand out of position. Easy fold.

Minni has made a very large raise to 1750, which appears to be an all-in move given their remaining chips. To call, I need to commit 1650 more into a total pot of 3650, requiring approximately 45% equity. Ten-Trey offsuit (T3o) is a very weak hand that typically only has about 37% equity against a standard 50% shoving range. Even if Minni is shoving any two cards, I would only be a marginal favorite, but it is much more likely their range dominates me. With a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, there is no reason to take a high-variance, negative-expectation risk here.

Preflop from UTG with 6h9s offsuit, a marginal speculative hand at best. In early position with 6 players, this hand has poor playability, dominated equity against typical calling ranges, and low strength for value. Standard play is to fold to preserve stack for better opportunities in this tournament setting where position and hand strength matter greatly over 150 hands.

I am holding 3s 2h (32o) in middle position with roughly 106 BBs. This is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With four players left to act behind me, including a short stack in the cutoff who might shove light, there is absolutely no strategic merit in playing this hand. The correct play is a standard fold.

Hand: 6♠2♦ — essentially the worst starting hand. It’s folded to me in the cutoff with the button and both blinds to act; pot is 150 and calling costs 100. Calling here gives very poor equity (you need ~40%+ to justify a call into this pot) and will often be dominated or get multiway action against deeper stacks. Attempting a steal or a raise with this holding is weak — with 19 BB I should pick better spots to be aggressive. Preserve my shortish 1900 stack for a playable hand; fold.

On the button with 2s5c - a very weak offsuit hand. While I have position and it's folded to me, this hand has minimal playability. Both blinds have me significantly covered (Flash 10k, FiveTwo 24k). With only 41.5 BB, I should preserve chips for better spots rather than trying to steal with trash. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh 6c. It has folded around to me. The pot is 150 (50 from my small blind and 100 from the big blind). To call, it is 50 more. The Big Blind, FiveTwo, is the chip leader with a massive stack of 24,300. Qh 6c is a weak hand to play out of position against a large stack. However, since it is a blind versus blind situation and the pot odds for calling are 3:1 (50 to win 150), I could consider completing. But, given my position and the weakness of the hand, folding is a safe and standard play to preserve chips for better spots, especially against a loose/aggressive chip leader.

I am in UTG position with K2s (King-Deuce suited). In a 6-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open from early position. It is dominated by many calling ranges (other Kings, Ax hands) and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. With active players behind me, including a short stack who might shove, simply folding is the standard and most profitable play.

I have Qh8h in middle with a 1,900 (≈19bb) stack. This is a playable suited hand but not strong enough to open-raise into several deep, aggressive players behind (Cutoff, Button, SB) who will often 3-bet or call and put me to tough decisions. Folding is too tight and shoving/raising risks getting isolated and dominated. Calling the 100 lets me see a flop cheaply, preserves stack flexibility, and allows me to continue only when I hit a queen/heart or a favorable board; if I miss and face significant aggression I'll fold and conserve chips.

3s7s is a weak suited connector in the cutoff. While suited, this hand has poor high card value and limited straight potential. With one limper and players still to act behind me, this isn't a profitable spot to get involved. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the button with pocket Jacks, a premium starting hand. Minni has limped into the pot from middle position with a very short stack (1,800). Limping usually indicates a marginal hand or a desire to see a cheap flop. I need to raise here to build a pot, isolate the limper, and take control of the hand with my positional advantage. A standard raise to 4x the big blind (400) is appropriate to discourage the blinds from defending with wide ranges and to put pressure on Minni's remaining stack.

9c2d is far too weak to continue from the small blind versus a button isolate. Even getting decent immediate odds, we’ll be out of position, often dominated when we pair a 9, and the big blind can still come along or squeeze, making realization very poor. A bluff-squeeze here is unnecessary with this hand texture and likely to get called by Flash’s wide-but-sticky iso range and/or BB defending. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

73o is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em, completely uncoordinated, offsuit, low cards with minimal straight or flush potential. Facing a button raise to 400 after an early limp (Minni), with pot 650 and 300 to call from the BB. My stack is 8750 effective against Flash's 9625, but this hand has negligible equity against a button opening range (which is wide but still crushes 73o), poor implied odds due to lack of playability postflop, and I'm out of position. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no reason to speculate here.

I called the 100 to see a flop cheaply with Q♥8♥, but Flash’s button raise to 400 is significant aggression and leaves me out of position postflop. To call another 300 I’d need ~31.6% equity (300/(650+300)), which Q8s doesn’t reliably have versus a button’s opening/calling range. 3-betting or shoving is unattractive here — I lack the fold equity to make a shove profitable and will often be called by better hands, while a small reraise commits me to a tough out-of-position spot. Per my plan to conserve chips and avoid marginal confrontations with ~18bb, I’ll fold and preserve my stack, losing only the 100 already invested.

UTG with 5♣6♦ (off‑suit low connector) and a shortish stack (1,800 = 18BB). To call is 100 (1BB) or min‑raise to 200 (2BB), but this hand has poor playability out of position and is vulnerable to 3‑bets/squeezes from deep stacks behind (Button 24k, BB and CO ~10k). Limping or opening here will often result in a multiway pot or a costly confrontation OOP with little equity. With tournament life and better spots to attack, the +EV play is to fold and preserve the stack for a more favorable situation.

JTd is a solid suited connector in middle position. With Minni already folded, I should open raise to build the pot and potentially take it down. Standard raise to 2.5x BB (250) is appropriate here with this playable hand that has good post-flop potential.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Seven suited. Claude has raised to 250 from middle position. While this hand has some playability due to being suited, Q7s is a marginal hand to call a raise with, especially with the chip leader (FiveTwo) still to act on the button and two players in the blinds. Claude's range for raising in middle position likely dominates mine (AQ, KQ, QJ). I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Button vs Claude’s MP 2.5x open, K2o is too weak/dominated to flat profitably, but it’s a good candidate to 3-bet bluff because we block strong Kx (KK/AK) and we have position plus a big stack to pressure his 39bb stack. A raise also denies the blinds a cheap squeeze/call. Plan: take it down pre often; if Claude 4-bet jams, we comfortably fold; if he calls, we play in position with initiative and avoid stacking off with weak top pair.

J7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind. Facing a 3-bet from the button (FiveTwo, deep-stacked and aggressive) over Claude's open-raise, this is a clear fold. No fold equity for a squeeze, and calling OOP risks multiway pots with a marginal hand that rarely improves enough. Preserve stack for better spots.

I hold Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind, a premium hand. The action involves an MP open from Claude and a Button 3-bet from the chip leader, FiveTwo. With 100BB effective stacks against the 3-bettor, a cold 4-bet is a viable but high-variance option that isolates the hand but risks facing a 5-bet shove or playing a massive pot out of position. Cold-calling is a standard high-level play here to keep the 3-bettor's range wide, disguise the strength of my hand, and control the size of the pot while playing out of position. It also allows Claude (who is somewhat short-stacked) to come along with dominated hands or make a squeeze play that I can then react to. I choose to flat call the 750.

JTd is a solid suited connector that plays well in multiway pots. Getting 3.6:1 pot odds (1800:500) to call. FiveTwo 3-bet and Pro cold-called, so we're going multiway. With decent implied odds if I hit a straight or flush draw, and stack depth of ~3400 behind after calling, this is a reasonable call. The hand has good post-flop playability.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a dry rainbow board (Ad 4h 8s). We are 3-way with the pre-flop 3-bettor (FiveTwo) on the Button and the original raiser (Claude) in Middle Position. As the cold-caller in the Big Blind, checking to the aggressor is the standard play. Leading out (donk betting) would deny the aggressor the chance to c-bet with their bluffs or worse value hands (like KK, QQ, or worse Aces). I want to keep their ranges wide. My hand is strong enough to check-call effectively. Raising a c-bet would likely only isolate me against sets or chops, so I plan to check-call if faced with a standard bet.

I have JTd on A84 rainbow board with one diamond. I completely missed - no pair, no straight draw, just a weak backdoor flush draw. In a 3-bet pot multiway with an Ace on board, I have no reason to bet. FiveTwo (the 3-bettor) is yet to act and likely has strong hands in their range. Checking is mandatory here - I'll fold to any significant bet.

This is a 3-bet pot but it’s multiway and the flop is A-high (A♦8♠4♥), which heavily connects with both callers’ ranges (lots of Ax, some 88/44, and sticky pocket pairs). With K2o we have very little equity and a c-bet risks getting called frequently or facing a short-stack check-jam from Claude (and we can’t continue). Given the low fold equity multiway and the awkward SPR vs Claude, the higher-EV line is to check back and realize what little equity we have while keeping the pot smaller.

Pre-flop, I cold-called a 3-bet with AKo. The flop checked through on an A-4-8 rainbow board. This check-back by the 3-bettor (FiveTwo) suggests they either have showdown value they want to control the pot with (KK, QQ, JJ), a weak Ace, or they are trapping. The Turn is a Ten, which brings straight draws (QJ) and possible two-pair combos (AT) into play. Leading out (donking) into two opponents here is risky; it likely folds out the worse hands I beat (underpairs) and isolates me against better hands (sets, two pair). Checking is the superior option to control the pot size, induce bluffs from floating hands, or let opponents value-own themselves with worse hands like AJ or AQ. Given Claude's short stack, a check might also induce a shove from him with a draw or weaker Ace.

I turned second pair with JTd - the Ten hit giving me a pair. In a 3-bet pot multiway, I have showdown value but need to be cautious. The Ace on board is scary given FiveTwo's 3-bet range likely contains many Ax hands. Pro checked, and FiveTwo still behind me. Betting could invite a raise from FiveTwo who may be trapping with a strong Ace. Checking keeps pot small and lets me see a cheap showdown or evaluate if FiveTwo bets.

We have essentially zero equity here (K-high with no real draws) in a multiway 3-bet pot on an A-high board. Even though both players have checked twice, that’s fully consistent with a lot of Ax (standard check-to-the-3bettor) and with Claude’s short stack the risk of a check-jam over any stab is meaningful—forcing us to torch chips and fold. Since a turn bet is likely to get called/raised too often and we don’t have good barreling candidates on most rivers, the highest-EV/lowest-leak line is to continue checking back and take the free card, keeping the pot controlled and preserving the chance to win at showdown if they both have capped, non-A holdings.

Pre-flop, I cold-called a 3-bet with AsKd. The flop (Ad 4h 8s) and turn (Th) checked through, suggesting opponents are either pot-controlling marginal hands, trapping, or have air. The river is the Jack of Hearts (Jh), putting three hearts on the board and completing a broadway straight for any KQ. It also gives two pair to AJ and AT. Given the runout, my Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) has significantly lost relative strength. Betting here is thin because worse hands (like KK, QQ, or weak Aces) likely fold, while better hands (Two Pair, Sets, Straights, Flushes) will call or raise. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff or catch a small value bet from a worse hand, though my primary goal is to get to showdown cheaply given the dangerous board texture.

I rivered two pair (Jacks and Tens) on a board of 4h Ad 8s Th Jh. This is a strong hand. Pro checked, and FiveTwo is behind me. The 3-heart board is concerning for flush possibilities, but FiveTwo checked flop and turn which is unusual for a 3-bettor with a made hand. My two pair should be good often enough to value bet. Betting around half pot (~1100) extracts value from worse hands like AT, KT, or one pair Ace hands while keeping the bet size manageable if I face a raise from a flush.

We have K-high with no pair on A♦-high texture in a 3-bet pot that went multiway to the river. Claude is betting into two players (Pro still has to act behind us), which heavily suppresses bluff frequency—this is usually value (Ax, KQ for Broadway, two-pair/sets, or sometimes a heart flush). Our Kh blocks some of the natural bluff/value candidates (KQ and K♥X♥), making it even less likely he’s stabbing light.

We’re being laid a decent price (1100 into 3400), but our real equity vs a range that bets into two players here is far below what we need, and calling also reopens the door for Pro to call/raise, worsening our situation. Raising as a bluff has near-zero fold equity because Claude’s remaining stack is small and he’ll be priced in with any made hand. Best play is to preserve chips and fold.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) in a 3-bet pot that checked through on the Flop and Turn. The River (Jack of Hearts) is a scary card, completing a possible flush (hearts) and straight (KQ), as well as improving hands like AJ or AT to two pair. Claude leads out for 1100 into ~2300 (total pot 3400). I need roughly 24% equity to call. Claude's line of checking twice and then betting the river could represent a hand that improved (AJ, KQ, Flush), a trap, or a thin value bet with a hand like AQ, or potentially a bluff capitalizing on the weakness shown by the 3-bettor and myself. Given that I have the Ace of Spades (blocking the nut flush) and King of Diamonds (blocking KQ), and considering the pot odds and the weakness shown on previous streets, folding TPTK is too exploitable here. I beat AQ and any bluffs. It is a marginal spot given the board texture, but a call is mathematically justified.
QJo is a playable hand from UTG, though marginal. With 68BB effective stack and standard opening ranges, I'll open raise to 250 (2.5x BB). It's good enough to open but not strong enough to go bigger.

I am holding Queen-Five offsuit in middle position. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. Queen-Five is a very weak hand that is easily dominated and does not play well post-flop. There is no strategic reason to call or raise here, so the standard play is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Claude’s UTG open at 2.5x represents a relatively strong range 6-handed. Ts5c offsuit has poor playability (dominated, no suitedness, weak straight potential) and calling invites squeezes from Button/SB plus puts us in multiway spots where we realize equity poorly. A 3-bet bluff vs UTG is too thin with this combo and unnecessary given our comfortable stack. Best is to preserve chips and wait for stronger steal/3-bet candidates.

My hand is 6d3s, an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card value, straight potential, or suited connectivity. It's unplayable in most situations, especially facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has been active with raises but this position suggests strength or a decent playable hand. I'm on the button with good position, but the hand has zero equity against a typical opening range. Pot odds to call 250 into 400 are about 2:1, but my hand will be dominated postflop most of the time and requires perfect conditions to hit. With a deep stack relative to blinds (87BB), folding preserves chips for premium spots. No reason to speculate here; discipline is key to long-term stack maximization.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s6s facing a 2.5x raise from Claude in UTG. UTG opening ranges are typically strong. While my hand has some suited connector playability, playing out of position against a tight range is disadvantageous. Additionally, the Big Blind (Minni) has a short stack of 17BB, which is a prime size for a squeeze all-in. If I call and the BB shoves, I would have to fold, wasting chips. Given the positional disadvantage and the threat of a squeeze behind, folding is the prudent choice.

Utter garbage hand (8♦2♣) in the big blind facing an UTG open to 250. To call I must put in 150 into a 400 pot (needing ~27% equity) and play OOP against an UTG raising range that dominates and has far more equity than 8-2. Shoving as a bluff is an option with ~17BB, but Claude (UTG) has ~65BB and will call or reshove enough of his range that my fold equity is low — risking my tournament life for very poor equity. Preserve the stack for better spots (stronger hands or positional opportunities). Fold.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position with 8c 4s, which is an extremely weak hand. Standard strategy dictates folding such hands from early positions as they have very little equity and are difficult to play profitably post-flop, especially when out of position against several opponents.

A3o is too weak to open from middle position in a 6-max game with no antes: it’s frequently dominated when called, plays poorly postflop, and there are still four players behind who can 3-bet/squeeze. With a big stack I want to apply pressure in higher-EV spots (later position, better connectivity/suitedness) rather than take a thin steal that risks running into stronger Ax and pairs.

Preflop, folded to me in the cutoff with T9o, a playable hand from late position due to straight potential and good playability postflop. 6-handed, standard to open raise. Raise to 250, a standard sizing (2.5x BB) seen frequently in history. SB is short (~16BB), which increases fold equity, and I have position on blinds. My stack is ~87BB, comfortable for aggression. Folding too tight would miss value; limp bad.

I am on the Button with Ace-Deuce suited (A2s), facing a raise to 250 from Elon in the Cutoff. A2s is a strong candidate for a flat call in position due to its nut flush potential and wheel straight possibilities. We are effectively deep-stacked (over 80 BBs), which allows for implied odds play. While a 3-bet is a viable option to apply pressure, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, keep Elon's range wide, and use my position advantage post-flop. The short-stacked Small Blind is a concern for a squeeze, but I'm willing to evaluate that if it happens. Defending the button here is standard strategy.

I’m in the small blind with J♦9♠ (16.5 BB effective). Cutoff opened to 2.5bb and the button called, so this will be a multiway pot out of position. J9o has very poor post‑flop playability against an opener and a caller; calling 200 to play OOP vs deeper stacks gives weak equity and will frequently put too much of my short stack at risk. A min‑raise/squeeze won’t credibly fold either player and an all‑in shove is too thin vs callers who can comfortably call. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold and lose the small blind.

32 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even getting ~4.3:1 pot odds (150 to call into 650), this hand has terrible playability post-flop and will rarely make a hand strong enough to win at showdown. Easy fold.

Heads-up pot on flop 6s5d2s, a low, somewhat drawy board with two spades. I raised preflop from CO with T9o, a standard open, and Pro called from BTN with a wide range (suited connectors, Ax, small pairs, broadways). My hand has no pair, no straight draw (T9 needs 78 or JQ, not connecting here), no flush draw—pure air but live cards with backdoor potential. Acting first IP vs BTN caller. Pro's range hits this board decently (22-66, flush draws like AsXs, wheel draws like A4/A3/43 suited), but also misses wide (KQo+, higher broadways). As PFR, cbets have high fold equity here (~60-70% estimated) since board favors my range slightly (A-high, K-high, sets). Half-pot cbet sizes well for value/protection/mix, takes it down often or builds pot OOP when called. Checking risks giving free card to draws/improvers and looks weak, folding equity lower post-check bet. With deep stacks (~85BB), aggression key to realize equity. Bet 350 (~half pot) for balanced line.

I hold Ac2c on a 6s5d2s board, giving me bottom pair with the top kicker. Facing a standard continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650 from the Cutoff (Elon), I have sufficient equity to continue. My hand beats all of his bluffs (like KQ, KJ, QJ) and is ahead of flush draws. Folding bottom pair top kicker in position to a single bet would be too tight. Raising doesn't accomplish much as it likely folds out worse hands and gets called by better. Calling allows me to use my position to evaluate the turn, keeping his range wide.

Heads-up pot on turn board 6s5d2sQh after c-betting flop with T9o (complete air, no pair, no draws). As PFR OOP, flop c-bet was balanced for fold equity on drawy board where my range is perceived stronger (sets, two-pair, overpairs, flush draws). Pro's flat-call on flop polarizes their range: strong made hands (sets 22-55/66, two-pair combos) or strong draws (spade flush draws like AsKs/Axs, wheel straight draws A43s/34s), less likely medium strength due to board texture and my PFR range. Turn Qh is somewhat neutral: helps broadway ranges (KQ, AQ in my range), blocks some straight draws, but doesn't complete spades or wheels prominently. No improvement for my hand, still pure bluff. Double-barreling here risks getting called by the made hands that beat us and floating draws that hit, while fold equity decreases vs a caller on flop (Pro less likely to have total air). Stacks deep (~80BB effective), so checking preserves stack, allows pot control, induces bluffs from Pro's missed draws, and keeps us balanced (mixing checks behind cbets). If checked to and bet, easy fold; if checked through, cheap river with showdown value/live cards possible. Betting reduces EV as bluff catcher range is thin. Check is GTO-optimal line here.

I hold bottom pair with top kicker (Ac2c) on a board of 6s-5d-2s-Qh. After calling the flop c-bet, the opponent (Elon) has checked the turn. The Queen is a card that hits the pre-flop raiser's range well (AQ, KQ, QJs), so his check may indicate pot control with a marginal hand or giving up on a bluff, though a trap is possible. Betting here serves little purpose; better hands (like any pair > 2s) will call, and worse hands (missed broadways) will fold, meaning I cannot extract value or execute a profitable bluff. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot small, and induces potential bluffs on the river or gets to a cheap showdown.

River Td pairs my Ten, giving me top pair with a Nine kicker on a board of 6s5d2sQhTd. No flush completed (spades draw missed), no obvious straight (possible 78 for 5-6-7-8-9 or A43 wheel unlikely given ranges). As PFR who c-bet flop (balanced for fold equity/value) and checked turn (pot control, inducing from missed draws), Pro's checking behind turn polarizes to strong made hands (sets 22-66, 65 two-pair) or weak (missed spade/wheel draws, Ax medium pairs). My line looks strong, and Pro's range includes worse top pairs (AT/KT weaker kickers? KT has K, better; but Qx, Jx floats, 76-89 gutshots that pair T). Deep stacks (~80BB eff), pot 1350, value betting thin gets called by Qx, Ax, smaller pairs, while sets raise. Checking risks losing to bluffs/showdown but misses value from worse; bet ~60% pot sizes well for protection/value, fold equity on air, balanced overall strategy. Pro history shows calling flop/turn wide but folding rivers to aggression sometimes. Bet 850 to build pot/target medium strength.

I hold bottom pair (Ac2c) on a board of 6s-5d-2s-Qh-Td. My opponent (Elon, Cutoff) raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, checked the turn, and is now betting 850 into a pot of 1350 on the river. The runout (Queen turn, Ten river) is very bad for my hand as it connects heavily with a Cutoff opening range (hitting Qx, Tx, and broadway two-pairs). While I checked back the turn signaling weakness, which might induce bluffs, there are too many hands that beat me (any pair of 5s, 6s, Ts, Qs, or pocket pairs > 22). Even his 'bluffs' like missed straight draws or flush draws might have paired the Queen or Ten. Getting ~3.6:1 odds isn't enough to justify calling with what is effectively a bluff-catcher at the very bottom of my range. I likely have better hands to call with (like a 6 or a 5).

6-handed UTG, AJo is strong enough to open for value and to pressure the blinds. Our stack covers the table and there’s a very short stack on the button (16.5bb) who can reshove; opening keeps our range strong and allows us to realize equity while still being able to respond appropriately vs a jam. Standard sizing in this lineup has been ~2.5x, which applies enough pressure without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

Facing an early position raise from FiveTwo (UTG), who has been aggressive in previous hands but an UTG raise still represents a strong range. My hand J8o is offsuit, has poor post-flop playability, low equity against a typical UTG range (dominated by better Js, suited connectors, pairs, etc.), and there are multiple players yet to act including deep-stacked Pro, Minni on button, and blinds. Calling or raising commits chips to a marginal spot with position disadvantage. Best play is to fold and wait for better opportunities to build the stack, especially mid-tournament with a healthy 94.5BB stack.

I am in the Cutoff holding Q7o. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is also the chip leader, has opened to 250. An UTG opening range is typically strong, often containing hands that dominate Q7o (like AQ, KQ, QJs, 77+). Despite having position on the opener, my hand is too weak to call or 3-bet profitably, especially with the Button and Blinds still to act. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I’m on the button with Kc3c facing an UTG open to 250. Pot is 400 and I’d need to call 250 (leaving me ~1400, ~14bb). K3s is dominated by an UTG opening range and performs poorly multiway if the blinds come along — and a 3-bet/shove from my shallow-ish stack would be too loose given this holding. Fold preserves chips for a better, higher-equity spot on the button or in the blinds.

A6o in the SB facing a UTG raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. FiveTwo opened from UTG which typically represents a stronger range. A6o doesn't have great playability - it's dominated by better aces and doesn't make strong draws often. With 200 to call into a 400 pot, I'm getting 2:1 but being OOP against a UTG range makes this a fold.

I hold King-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind. FiveTwo, the table chip leader, has raised to 250 from UTG. Given the 6-max format and FiveTwo's frequent opening range, KQo is a very strong hand to defend with. The pot currently offers me odds of nearly 2.7 to 1 (150 to call into a total pot of 550). I will call to see a flop and close the pre-flop action.

On a dry, paired flop of 9h-4s-4d, I will follow the standard protocol of checking to the pre-flop aggressor. King-Queen offsuit has some showdown value and two overcards, but it is not strong enough to lead out into the UTG raiser. I'll check to see how FiveTwo acts and then decide whether to continue based on the bet sizing.

Heads-up in position after opening UTG, this paired low flop (9-4-4) is a good range-advantage spot to c-bet small. Flash’s BB defend contains a lot of unpaired overcards and middling hands that will fold to one bet, and our AJo benefits from immediate fold equity plus some denial vs hands like KQ/QJ/Tx. We don’t need a big size because the board is static and we’re not trying to build a large pot with a medium-strength hand; a small bet also keeps us from overexposing ourselves vs a check-raise (which we can comfortably fold to).

On a paired board of 9h-4s-4d, FiveTwo has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550. Holding King-Queen offsuit, I have two overcards and some backdoor possibilities. Given the excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 950, which is nearly 4.75 to 1) and the fact that this dry board may not have hit the UTG raiser's range particularly hard, I have sufficient equity to call and see a turn card. I will not raise here as I want to keep their bluffs in and minimize losses if they are holding an overpair or a nine.

On the turn, I have improved to top pair with a King kicker (Kd Qc) on a board of 9h-4s-4d-Qd. Given that FiveTwo was the pre-flop aggressor and continued on the flop, I will continue to check to them. This allows FiveTwo to continue bluffing with hands that missed or to bet with medium-strength hands like pocket pairs (JJ, TT) or a 9. If I lead out, I might fold out the bluffs I want to extract value from. I am comfortably ahead of most of their semi-bluffs and many of their value bets, though I must be wary of AQ or a slow-played trip 4s. I will check and evaluate based on FiveTwo's action.

Turn Qd is a meaningful card for BB’s flop-calling range (improves Qx, gives more credible top-pair continues), and our AJo has poor equity realization when called plus no real draws to barrel with. After a small flop c-bet gets called, the highest-EV line is to take the free card, keep the pot controlled, and potentially pick up an A/J on the river or induce bluffs from missed overcards that floated once.

I hold top pair, King kicker (Kd Qc) on a board of 9h-4s-4d-Qd-8h. The action went check-check on the turn after FiveTwo's flop continuation bet, which often indicates that the aggressor's range is capped or they are giving up with air. By checking the river, I am playing my hand as a bluff-catcher or a hand with high showdown value. If I bet, I risk folding out all the bluffs I want to extract value from, and I'll likely only be called by hands that beat me (like AQ or slow-played 4x) or a very thin range of worse hands. Checking allows FiveTwo to potentially fire a final bluff with missed overcards or check back with pocket pairs like JJ/TT or a 9, which I have beat. I will check and plan to call most standard-sized bets.

River is checked to us after we c-bet flop and checked back turn. With AcJd on 9-4-4-Q-8 we only have ace-high, but it still has real showdown value versus Flash’s missed overcards/floats (KJ/KTs/JT/AT-type hands) that called one small flop bet. If we bluff, we mostly fold out exactly those worse hands and get looked up by the hands that continued flop and now check river for pot control (9x, Qx, pocket pairs, occasional 4x), which is a bad value-to-risk trade. The river doesn’t complete an obvious draw that would make his range over-fold, so I’ll take the free showdown.

Pocket Kings is one of the best starting hands, especially UTG in a 6-handed game. Standard play is to raise for value and to thin the field, as KK performs well against a wide calling range but vulnerable to AA. Given blinds 50/100 and common raise sizes around 250 in the history, a 2.5-3x raise to 300 builds the pot without overcommitting deep stacks. This isolates short stacks like Minni (1650) who might shove light, while getting folds from weak hands. Position is early but hand is too strong to limp or fold.

I hold 6s 5c in Middle Position facing a raise to 300 from Elon in UTG. 65 off-suit is a very weak speculative hand that plays poorly against a strong UTG range and has negative expected value to call, especially with players left to act behind me. My stack is healthy at over 80 big blinds, so there is no need to get involved with marginal holdings in disadvantageous spots. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

Fold. Facing an UTG open to 300, 3♠4♥ is a very weak, easily dominated hand. I’m out of position with only 16.5bb and there are players behind (including a big blind with a huge stack) who can easily come over the top or call with stronger hands. Calling 300 or risking a shove here will put me in tough spots multiway or cracked by a better UTG range. Better to conserve chips and wait for a profitable spot in position or with a stronger holding.
52 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even with button position, calling a 3x raise with this garbage hand is clearly -EV. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, a strong hand in 6-max play. Elon (UTG) has raised to 300. From previous hands, Elon appears to be an active player who opens frequently from early positions (Hands #23, #35, #41). AJo is ahead of a wide opening range but can be difficult to play out of position if I just call. By 3-betting to 1050, I take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and isolate Elon if he chooses to call, while also discouraging the Big Blind from entering the pot.

Facing an UTG open and a fairly large SB 3-bet, J8o is too weak to continue from the BB. We’re dominated by both players’ continuing ranges, we’ll be out of position, and calling needs us to realize equity poorly in a likely multiway/pressure-heavy pot. A 4-bet bluff here is unnecessary spew given the stack-to-pot dynamics and the fact both villains can continue strongly. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Pocket Kings remains extremely strong facing a SB 3-bet. Small blind 3-bet ranges are polarized and wide: premiums like AA-QQ/AK, plus bluffs and suited hands due to position. KK crushes the QQ-JJ/AQ+ portion and is live vs AA. Folding is -EV disaster. Calling OOP risks postflop difficulties with ace-high boards common. 4-betting extracts value from worse hands, defines their range, builds pot OOP advantage with initiative. Sizing to ~2.7x the 3-bet (2800 total) is standard deep-stack: large enough to charge draws/bad calls but leaves room for postflop if they jam/shove (Flash has ~9700, can call). History shows Flash aggressive but folds to pressure (e.g., Hand 20). Maximizes EV long-term.

I am facing a 4-bet from Elon, who opened from UTG. While I initially 3-bet to isolate and take initiative with Ace-Jack offsuit, a 4-bet from an early position open typically represents a very strong range (JJ+, AK). AJo is significantly behind this range. Although Elon has shown aggression in previous hands, calling 1750 more to play out of position in a bloated pot with a dominated hand is a losing play in the long run. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Ts 2d), which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With 5 players left to act behind me, including the chip leader and a short stack, opening the pot here would be a significant strategic error. My stack is healthy at over 80 big blinds, so there is no pressure to make loose plays. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

7♣5♦ is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and I’m out of position with two players (cutoff, button) and the blinds still to act. My stack is only ~16.5bb (1650), so calling 100 or making a tiny open to 200 would either put me into a multiway pot where I’m likely dominated or invite a shove/squeeze from deeper stacks. I’ll conserve chips for better spots or a shove from late position — fold this marginal hand.
Ah7h in the cutoff is a solid opening hand. Suited ace with decent playability. Two players already folded, I have position on the blinds. Standard open raise to 250.

I am on the Button with 4d 2h, which is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. Claude has raised to 2.5x the big blind from the Cutoff. Given my hand strength and the fact that I still have two players behind me in the blinds, there is no strategic value in calling or raising. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

AdKd is a premium hand and far ahead of Claude’s CO opening range. From the small blind I don’t want to just call and invite the BB in; I want to build a pot for value, deny equity, and set up a low SPR where AK plays very well (and I’m happy to continue versus a 4-bet/jam from Claude’s stack depth). A standard SB vs CO 3-bet sizing accomplishes this.

9c5s is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a standard open from Claude in CO (loose position) followed by a large 3-bet to 1000 (10BB) from FiveTwo in SB, who has shown aggression in prior hands but likely has strength here given the sizing and position. Pot odds are about 1.5:1 (1350 pot, 900 to call), but equity realization OOP is abysmal against a strong 3-bet range. No blockers or speculative value justifies calling or raising. Folding preserves my 105BB stack for better spots in this multi-way tournament.

FiveTwo 3-bet to 1000 from SB after my open. I have Ah7h - a suited ace with position postflop. Pot is 1350, need to call 750 (getting ~1.8:1). With position against a big stack (22k), suited ace has good implied odds and playability. A7s is a reasonable defend here - I can play postflop well with the nut flush draw potential. Folding is too tight, 4-betting would be overplaying it.

3-bet pot with a low SPR (~2.7 effective) and we flop top pair/top kicker on Kh-3d-6h. This board is good for our 3-betting range and we want to start building a stack-off line versus Claude’s continuing range (KQ/KJ, QQ-JJ/TT, 66/33 occasionally, and heart draws). A medium c-bet gets value and denies equity while keeping worse hands in, and it also sets up a clean turn shove size if called.

I flopped the nut flush draw with Ah7h on Kh3d6h. I have 9 outs to the nuts. Getting 2900:800 (3.6:1) pot odds, and with ~35% equity to make the flush by river, this is an easy call. Position gives me good control on future streets. Raising as a semi-bluff is an option but with my stack size (5800), I'd be essentially committing. Better to call and re-evaluate turn.

Turn SPR is now very low (Claude has ~3200 behind into a 3700 pot), and we still have top pair/top kicker. The Td adds some extra pair+draw and two-pair possibilities, but Claude’s continuing range after calling the 3-bet pre and flop c-bet is still heavy on Kx (KQ/KJ/KT), medium pairs (QQ-JJ/99) and heart/diamond draws. With this SPR, the cleanest and highest-EV line is to jam: we maximize value versus worse Kx and draws, deny equity to hands like QJh/QJdd/heart draws, and avoid awkward river decisions. Holding the Ad also blocks the nut diamond draws, making it less likely we’re running into the strongest combo draws when called.

I have the nut flush draw (Ah7h) with 9 outs, needing to hit on the river. Pot is 6900, need 3200 to call. Getting ~3.2:1 odds but I need ~4.5:1 for a pure draw (9/46 = ~20%). If I call, I'll have only 1800 left - not enough for meaningful implied odds. EV math: calling has EV of ~3820 vs keeping 5000 by folding. FiveTwo's large bets indicate strength (likely Kx, set, or two pair). With insufficient pot odds, limited implied odds due to shallow remaining stack, and villain showing strength, this is a disciplined fold.

I’m UTG with pocket threes (3c3s) and a 1,650 stack (~16.5 BB). Limping here is weak — players behind are deep and aggressive so I’ll either want fold equity or a clean heads‑up set‑mining spot. I’m not yet in pure shove/fold range, so I’ll open to a standard size to take the initiative, narrow the field, and preserve chips if I miss. A raise to 2.5x BB (250 total) is a normal sizing at this table: it gives fold equity vs the blinds and keeps implied odds vs deeper callers if I hit a set. If I face a large 3‑bet/shove I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy 3‑bets), but for now open-raise to 250.

A4o is a weak hand to defend against an UTG raise, even from a short-stacked opponent. With players behind me who could squeeze, and poor postflop playability with this hand, folding is the correct play here.

I am holding Queen-Four offsuit in the Cutoff position. Minni, who is currently the short stack at the table with only 14 Big Blinds, has opened with a raise to 2.5BB from UTG. While Minni's short stack might suggest she is widening her range, Queen-Four is still a very weak hand that lacks both high-card strength and connectivity. Calling or 3-betting here would be a mistake, especially with active players behind me like the big stack FiveTwo on the button. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chips.

A5o is too marginal to start inflating the pot versus an UTG open (even from a short 14bb stack) where we’re frequently dominated when called/jammed on. On the Button we can profitably flat to realize our equity and keep Minni’s range wide, while also keeping our risk low if the SB/BB squeeze (we can comfortably fold and lose only 250). If it goes heads-up, we’ll have position with a manageable SPR versus a short stack and can apply pressure on favorable textures.

2d8h is one of the worst possible hands in poker: offsuit, disconnected low cards, no straight or flush potential. Facing an UTG open-raise from Minni (short stack ~14BB, wide range possible but still strong) and a button call from deep-stacked FiveTwo, my equity is minimal against their ranges. In SB, calling 200 into a 650 pot offers poor implied odds and post-flop playability. Folding preserves my 10k stack for better spots in this multi-table dynamic.

I am in the Big Blind with [8d 9s] facing a raise from Minni (UTG) and a call from FiveTwo (Button). The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a pot of 650, roughly 18.75%), but several factors suggest a fold. First, Minni is playing a short stack (1400 chips, ~14BB) and raising from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range (likely overpairs or strong Aces) that dominates my pair outs. Second, my hand is offsuit, significantly reducing its playability compared to 98s. Third, being out of position in a multi-way pot against a short-stack aggressor limits my ability to realize equity; I will often be forced to fold on the flop if I don't hit a monster (two pair or better), and the implied odds to chase those monsters are capped by Minni's small remaining stack. Even with good direct odds, the equity realization and reverse implied odds make this a losing play over time.

I raised preflop to set-mine but missed hard. Flop 8c Jc 9c is extremely hostile for my 3♣3♠: three clubs on board means villain (in position and a preflop caller) can easily have a made flush or very strong club-based draws. I only have 2 outs to hit a set and 9 clubs to hit a flush (roughly ~43% to improve to a set or a flush by the river), but that overstates my true winning chances because villain may already hold a flush. With ~14 BB effective I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a weak pair, so I’ll control the pot and preserve chips. Check and see how the button reacts — call reasonable small bets to chase with the right pot odds, fold to large/commitment bets.

Minni is very short (SPR < 2) and checked on a monotone flop where many UTG hands without a club will be uncomfortable continuing. Although we have essentially no immediate equity (no club, no meaningful draw), we have strong range advantage/credibility on this texture because our Button flatting range contains many suited hands with clubs that already have a flush. A small stab needs very little fold equity to show an immediate profit, and we can comfortably bet/fold versus a check-jam from a short stack.

I opened with 3♣3♠ and missed this very club-heavy flop (J♣9♣8♣). Facing a small 200 bet into 850, calling is the right play: I have 2 outs to a set and 9 clubs remaining for a possible flush — ~11 total outs -> ~41–42% to improve by the river, while my break-even equity to call 200 is only ~19%. Many club outs may be dominated if Villain already has clubs, so I won’t bloat the pot or bluff-raise out of position with just a weak pair; instead I’ll call cheaply to see the turn and re-evaluate (fold to large turn aggression, proceed cautiously if a club or a 3 appears).

Turn is a blank (K♥) that doesn’t meaningfully improve my 3♣3♠ — I only have two outs to a set and nine clubs to hit a river club (11 outs total, ~11/46 ≈ 24% to improve), and many of those club outs are effectively dead if BTN already has a club flush. I’m out of position, the button is deep-stacked and wide/aggressive (flop raised), and my stack is now shallow relative to the pot (~1,200 chips). Betting here would bloat the pot with very weak equity and little fold‑equity; a shove/bluff is unlikely to fold enough of BTN’s calling range and would be high variance. I’ll control the pot and check to the river. Plan: fold to large river aggression unless I actually hit a 3 or a clean club that meaningfully changes my read; if the river is a club/3, re-evaluate for value or a call depending on sizing and villain’s line.

Minni is extremely short with only ~750 behind into a 1050 pot (SPR < 1). After check-calling the small flop stab on a monotone board, her range contains some flushes/two-club hands, but also a lot of one-club hands and non-club pairs that are now in a miserable spot facing an all-in. Shoving maximizes fold equity and cleanly denies her realizing river equity; if called we’re usually crushed (no club), but we only need ~42% folds for the jam to be profitable given the pot size, which is very plausible versus her capped/checking range.

Board is extremely club‑heavy (J♣9♣8♣) and BTN has been aggressive (flop raise, turn jam/large raise). I have 3♣3♠ — only 2 tens to make a set and 9 clubs to make a flush = 11 outs total (~11/46 ≈ 24% to improve on the river). Calling 750 into a 1,800 pot would require ~29.4% equity to break even (750/(1800+750)), so the raw odds are against me. Worse, many of those club outs are likely dominated (if Villain already holds a club flush, a river club often gives them a higher flush), so my true live equity vs Villain’s raising range is well below the break‑even point. Calling would commit the majority of my 1,200 stack (leaving ~450) and put me in a very marginal, high‑variance spot; shoving as a bluff is unlikely to fold enough of Villain’s range and would be overly risky. I planned to fold to large turn aggression earlier — that remains the correct play. Fold and preserve chips for a better spot.
5c7h is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. This is a clear fold - no playability from early position with this hand.

Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand from middle position in a six-max tournament format. While opening is sometimes acceptable to steal blinds, several factors favor a fold here. Firstly, FiveTwo (the chip leader) is sitting in the Cutoff and has shown a high level of aggression, making it likely I will face a 3-bet or a difficult post-flop situation. Secondly, Minni in the Big Blind is very short-stacked (11 BBs) and may shove a wide range to survive; calling an 1100-chip shove with A7o is not ideal. Given my healthy stack of nearly 100 BBs, I choose to fold and wait for better spots.

6-handed and folded to us in the cutoff, we should be opening fairly wide to attack the blinds. K6o isn’t a premium, but it’s good enough as a steal candidate, especially with a very short-stacked BB (11bb) who will often fold; when they shove, we can comfortably fold without risking much. Use a modest open size to keep risk down vs a jam/3-bet while still applying pressure.

AQo is a strong hand on the button facing a cutoff raise from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in recent hands (e.g., Hand 55 raise, Hand 54 big bets, Hand 52). Position is excellent, allowing postflop control. 3-betting for value and isolation, as FiveTwo's range is likely wide (suited connectors, broadways, etc.). Minni short in BB may shove but we have fold equity and position. Size to 800 (3.2x the raise) is standard for value while maintaining fold equity against calling range. Pot will be ~1200 post-3bet, good for our stack depth.

I am holding Qh 6s in the Small Blind. The action has been heavy before me: FiveTwo raised to 250 from the Cutoff, and Elon 3-bet to 800 from the Button. My hand is very weak (Q6 offsuit) and performs poorly against 3-bet ranges. Additionally, I would be playing out of position against two active players if I called. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a weak holding. This is a standard fold.

Fold. I’m 11bb with T9o in the big blind facing a 3-bet to 800 from the button with the original raiser still in. The pot is 1,200 and calling 700 would leave me ~400 (≈4bb) behind, effectively committing most of my stack to a multiway pot out of position with a marginal, often-dominated holding. Jam to 1,100 is an option but versus two opponents my fold equity is low and T9o has poor equity against the 3-bettor’s value-heavy range. Preserving my stack for a clearer double-up/shove spot is higher EV than calling or risking an all-in here, so I fold.

We opened light from the CO, but Elon’s button 3-bet to 800 will contain a lot of hands that dominate K6o (better kings, broadways, strong suited aces). Even though the direct price isn’t terrible, we’ll realize equity poorly being out of position and often making second-best pairs. K6o is also a poor 4-bet bluff candidate (weak blocker value, likely to get peeled/jammed by this stack depth). Best EV is to cut losses and continue stealing in better spots.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position and have been dealt 7s 2c, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Given the poor hand strength and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against five other players, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. Folding is the only sensible move.

We’re in middle position with T5o, a dominated, low-equity hand that plays poorly multiway and has very limited postflop realization, especially when we get called by better tens/fives or suited connectors. With several players still to act (cutoff/button plus blinds), the likelihood of facing a raise or playing a tough pot out of position is high. Our stack edge is best leveraged with hands that can comfortably open and continue; this isn’t one of them. Preserve chips and wait for better steal/pressure spots versus the short stacks.

Preflop in cutoff with K5o after two folds (UTG and MP). Unopened pot, good position to steal blinds. SB has only ~10BB (1050), likely to fold or shove light. K-high has decent blocker and postflop playability in position. Standard open-raise size of 3x BB. Raising to 300 applies pressure and builds pot with fold equity against blinds.

I am holding 4c 3h on the Button, which is a very weak hand (unsuited low connectors). The player in the Cutoff (Elon) has raised to 300. Given the poor playability of my hand and the strength shown by the raise, calling or raising is not profitable. The standard and most EV+ play is to fold.

I’m short-stacked (1050 = ~10.5bb) in the SB facing a 3bb CO open to 300 with the Button folded. 3♠5♠ is marginal, but at ~10bb this is a push/fold spot — calling (putting 300 total) leaves me ~8bb with almost no fold equity and a difficult postflop life; a small reraise won’t generate meaningful fold equity either. Shoving maximizes fold equity to pick up the current 450 pot and keeps the decision simple. I accept that I’ll be called by some hands from the BB or opener and often be dominated, but given the stacks and position an all‑in shove is the highest EV play here.

KQs is a strong hand. Minni 3-bet to 1050 but only has 50 chips left - essentially all-in. Elon opened to 300 and may come along. KQs plays well multiway and has great equity against typical 3-bet ranges. With 1450 in the pot and 950 to call, I'm getting decent odds. Calling is the right play - raising would only fold out Elon's weaker hands while keeping his stronger range.

Preflop with K5o in CO after opening to 300. Facing 3bet to 1050 (~10BB shove) from short-stacked SB Minni (effective 11BB pre) and flat call from BB Claude (~40BB). Pot odds to call 750 into 2400 are ~3.2:1 (24% equity), improving slightly if Minni shoves remaining 50. Minni's shortstack shove range is wide (pairs, Ax, broadway, connectors), where K5o has ~35-40% equity due to K blocker. However, Claude's cold-call range after 3bet is much stronger (TT+, AQ+, AK suited etc.), where K5o <30% equity and often dominated. Multiway equity drops further (~25-30% combined). Postflop: Acting last but weak king-high/5 kicker plays poorly 3way, vulnerable to domination, few strong draws. Deep stack (105BB), no need to realize equity in marginal spot. Discipline: Fold light opens to 3bet + call, preserve stack for premium situations.

I have 3♠5♠ on a J♣ Q♠ 2♥ flop — no pair and only a runner‑runner spade/straight possibility. The pot is huge (~2400) while I’m effectively crippled with only 50 chips left after my preflop action. Betting 50 into this pot is non‑credible and simply invites a shove or a raise from Claude (who called my preflop shove and therefore has a strong, connected calling range: Qx/Jx/pairs/broadways). I’ll check to see a free turn and avoid risking my tiny stack with essentially no showdown value; I’ll fold to any meaningful pressure unless the turn gives me real equity (pair or obvious double‑up card).

Flopped top pair with second-best kicker (KQ) on Q-J-2 board. Minni only has 50 chips left and checked. I should bet for value - this is a strong hand. A bet around half pot (~1200) extracts value and puts Minni all-in for their remaining 50.

I’m the short stack (50 left) after my big preflop shove; on the Jc Qs 2h flop Claude has bet 1,200 into a ~3,600 pot and I face a 50-chip decision (my last chips). My hand (3s5s) has almost no showdown value — only backdoor spade/runner-runner straight possibilities — but calling 50 is essentially free: pot after my call would be ~3,650 so my break‑even equity is ~50/3,650 ≈ 1.37%. Even against a strong continuing range (Qx/Jx/pairs) I have a non‑zero chance to hit, and the cost to try to double up is trivial relative to the pot. I can’t credibly raise and folding preserves a tiny stack but gives up positive EV. Therefore I will call my last 50 chips (all‑in) and hope to hit turn/river to double up.

AQ suited is a premium hand from UTG in 5-handed play. It plays well postflop with position potential and suited connectors value. Standard raise size preflop is 3x the BB to 300, building the pot while maintaining fold equity against the field. Opponents have shown aggression in history, but this hand warrants a raise. Pot is 150 (blinds), min raise 200, but 300 is appropriate sizing for value and isolation.

I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit (T9o) in Middle Position (UTG+1) facing a 3bb open raise from the Under-The-Gun player (Elon). T9o is a weak hand that is often dominated by an UTG opening range (which typically includes AT, KT, QT, JJ-99). Despite having position on the raiser, the hand lacks the playability and equity to justify a call, and it is not a suitable candidate for a 3-bet bluff in this configuration. Folding is the standard and correct decision to preserve chips.

78 suited on the button is a good speculative hand with position. Elon raised from UTG to 300. I have direct position and can play this profitably with implied odds if I hit straights, flushes, or two pair. Calling is standard here - suited connectors play well in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit (Q8o). Elon raised from UTG and Claude called on the Button. Q8o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position and multi-way. Despite the pot odds, calling here often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am likely dominated. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

Even getting ~3.75:1 immediate odds (200 to win 750), 23o has extremely poor playability and equity realization multiway, especially out of position versus an UTG 3x open and a button flat (ranges that dominate us and put us in many reverse-implied-odds spots). With shallow-to-mid opponent stacks, implied odds are limited and we’ll be forced to fold most flops. Best is to preserve stack and avoid spew.

Preflop: Raised AQ suited UTG for standard value and isolation. Flop: 7s Kd Ad rainbow-ish but two diamonds. Hold AcQc for top pair top kicker (TPTK), nuts blocker with Ace. Strong value hand heads-up vs button caller. Board draw-heavy (diamond flush draw, gutshot straight draws like 10-J). First to act OOP, must bet for value/protection vs worse Ax (AJ, AT), Kx, draws. Claude's button call range includes Ax suited, KQ, KJ, suited connectors/draws. Bet ~2/3 pot (500) to charge draws, build pot, deny equity. Avoid check to induce bluffs/allow free cards. Stack depths allow aggression (my 10k, his 6k).

I flopped bottom pair (7s) on an A-K-7 board with two diamonds. Elon opened UTG and c-bet 500 - this board hits his UTG range extremely hard (AK, AA, KK, AQ, AJ all connect). My pair of 7s is very weak here. I have no flush draw (wrong suits) and no real straight draw. Against his range, I'm crushed. Easy fold.

Current table is 5-handed, so UTG corresponds to the Hijack position. The blinds (FiveTwo and Elon) are deep-stacked and have shown significant aggression recently. QJo is a marginal opening hand from the Hijack, often dominated by calling ranges (AQ, KQ, AJ) or forced to fold to 3-bets which are frequent at this table. Given my stack depth (approx. 80BB) and the active players behind, preserving chips and avoiding a reverse-implied odds situation with a easily dominated offsuit broadway hand is the prudent play. I will fold.
8-3 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. In UTG+1 position with 4 players behind, this is a clear fold. No playability, terrible equity, and poor position.

I am on the button with Ten-Five suited. While the button is the ideal position to attempt a steal, this specific hand is quite weak and sits well outside a standard opening range, even in a steal situation. Looking at the blinds, FiveTwo (SB) has a massive stack and has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands, and Elon (BB) is also active and willing to contest pots. Attempting to steal with T5s into two active players, one of whom has me significantly covered, is a low-EV play. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

Folded to me in the small blind, so this is a classic blind-steal spot. Even with a marginal offsuit Q2, raising puts immediate pressure on Elon’s big blind range, wins the pot often, and gives me initiative when called. I’ll use a small-but-standard SB open size to keep my risk low and my range wide; if Elon 3-bets with meaningful sizing, I can comfortably fold this dominated hand.

Preflop in BB facing a raise to 250 from SB after three folds. Pot is 350, calling 150 more gives excellent implied odds (over 3:1). K5o is a decent defending hand vs a likely wide SB stealing range, as it blocks kings, has good playability, and decent equity against worse hands. FiveTwo has been aggressive historically but from SB this is standard wide. Postflop, I'll have position heads-up. Folding is too tight given odds; raising risks playing a marginal hand OOP against their deep stack. Call to realize equity and control pot.

Heads-up SB vs BB on a low paired flop (6c-4c-6h) with Q2o and no club/backdoor equity, my hand is close to pure air. While a small c-bet can work on paired boards, Elon has shown a willingness to apply pressure and can check-raise this texture with a lot of 6x, 4x, and club draws, putting me in a bad spot with no equity. Checking keeps the pot small, protects my checking range, and lets me realize my (limited) showdown value / pick better bluff turns (A/K/Q, etc.) rather than donating vs aggression.

Heads-up on a paired 6-6-4 rainbow-ish board (two clubs) with Ks5c. I flopped a strong club flush draw (9 clean outs) plus king overcard (blocks their Kx), giving ~35-40% equity vs typical SB calling range. FiveTwo checked first in as aggressor, representing weakness or traps, but their history shows aggression; however, check suggests missed flop (Ax, broadway, pocket pairs below 6). Betting semi-bluffs: high fold equity vs air/draws, value from worse Kx/pairs/draws, denies equity to their floats/OESD. Checking risks giving free card to overcards/completes and pot control suboptimal with initiative. Size 350 (~70% pot) balances value/protection/fold equity without overbetting paired board. Deep stacks allow fold equity realization.

We opened SB very wide and checked a paired flop specifically because Q2o has essentially no equity here (no clubs, no meaningful backdoors) and doesn’t make good bluffing candidates. When Elon stabs 350 into 500, our hand is at the very bottom of our range: calling would be a pure float with poor turn playability out of position, and raising would be an unbacked bluff without blockers to his continuing range (6x/4x, club draws, overcards that won’t fold). We can defend this spot with better overcards, backdoor draws, and some traps; Q2o should just be released.
AKo is a premium hand in UTG. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB (250) to build the pot and define my hand strength. With 61 BBs effective, this is a straightforward open-raise spot.

Claude has raised to 250 from UTG. Holding Ace-Deuce offsuit in UTG+1, I am facing a raise from the earliest position at the table. A2o is a marginal hand with poor playability post-flop and is significantly behind a standard UTG opening range. With three players yet to act, including an aggressive chip leader on the button, calling or 3-betting would likely put me in a difficult situation. Therefore, the most disciplined strategy is to fold and wait for a stronger hand.

Claude’s UTG open is fairly standard (2.5bb) and we do have position, but Jh4h is simply too dominated to profitably continue. Calling invites squeezes from the blinds and, when we do see a flop, we make a lot of weak Jx that are crushed by Claude’s range. This isn’t a good candidate to 3-bet bluff either due to poor playability when called. Preserve chips and wait for better spots.

Preflop in SB facing UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) from Claude with a 58BB stack. My hand K5o is weak and offsuit, dominated by typical UTG raising range (AQo+, KQo+, pairs 88+). Poor playability postflop. Pot odds are about 3:1 but equity too low (~20-30% vs range). No reason to call, defend, or 3bet. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. BB (Pro, 79BB) acts behind but doesn't change decision.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4d 3s, a very weak off-suit connector. The player in UTG (Claude) has raised to 2.5bb. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win 400), my hand plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position against a strong UTG opening range. The equity of 43o is often dominated, and it will be difficult to realize that equity. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve chips.

I am UTG with Ace-Ten suited (AhTh), which is a very strong hand in a 5-handed game. My stack is healthy at nearly 100 big blinds. I will open with a standard raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that helps define my hand and puts pressure on the remaining players, especially with a massive stack like FiveTwo sitting directly to my left.

Flash opens UTG to 3bb in a 5-handed game, which still represents a reasonably strong/wide-but-not-loose range. 7c6d is offsuit, plays poorly multiway (and we still have Button/SB/BB behind), and lacks the suited equity to realize well versus an UTG range. With a big stack advantage, there’s no need to defend marginal offsuit connectors here or turn them into a low-quality 3-bet bluff. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

On the button with 7d9d, suited connectors have excellent playability and potential to make strong hands post-flop. Flash's UTG raise to 300 suggests a strong range, but my position allows me to realize equity effectively, especially deep-stacked (112BB). Pot odds are favorable (calling 300 into 450, ~1.5:1 direct, better implied/multiway). SB/BB still to act, but calling keeps the pot multiway-friendly for speculative hands. Folding too tight, 3-betting risks vs tight range without great blockers. Prefer calling to control pot size and exploit position.

I am holding 2d 3h in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. Facing a raise from UTG (Flash) to 300 and a call from the Button (Elon), I am getting pot odds of roughly 3 to 1 (calling 250 to win a pot that will be around 1000). However, 32o has very poor equity multi-way and plays terribly out of position. I will be sandwiched between the opener, the caller, and potentially the Big Blind. There is no strategic justification for entering this pot. I must fold.

I have 56o in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300 with a call from the button. I need 200 to call into a 750 pot (3.75:1 odds). While the price is decent, 56 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position in a multiway pot. UTG raises typically indicate strength, and I'll be playing OOP against two opponents. The hand lacks the connectivity of suited cards and is too speculative here. Folding is the disciplined play.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a dry board (Ad 5s 8h). As the pre-flop aggressor, I should follow through with a continuation bet to get value from weaker Aces, middle pairs, and potential floats. Elon's button call range likely includes many hands I currently beat. A bet of roughly 47% pot (350) keeps the pot manageable while extracting value.

Heads-up on the flop with 7d9d on Ad5s8h. This gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or T for the nuts or near-nuts straight, 8 clean outs ~16% equity baseline, plus backdoor flush draw and two live cards for overcards). Flash's UTG raise pre-flop suggests a strong range: pairs TT+, AKo/AQs+, suited broadways. The ace-high flop hits his range well (Ax, sets unlikely), but his small 1/3-pot c-bet (350 into 1100) is standard continuation, possibly weak Ax, or probing. Excellent pot odds (3.1:1 direct, better implied deep-stacked at ~100BB effective vs his 90BB). Position on button is huge for realizing equity on later streets. Folding is too tight given playability. Raising as semi-bluff risks bloating pot OOP against strong range without fold equity certainty (small bet size suggests he may continue). Calling is optimal: keeps pot manageable, disguises hand strength, allows turn control, and exploits position to extract value if we hit or he checks weak. Plan: call flop, evaluate turn (if check, can bet for value/thin value or check back; if bet, can raise if hits or fold showdowns). Consistent with pre-flop speculative call strategy.

On the turn 4h, I have top pair with a Ten kicker and have picked up the nut flush draw. The board is relatively dry, although the 4h brings some straight possibilities for a hand like 76s. Elon's call on the flop suggests he likely has an Ace, a middle pair, or a draw. I want to continue betting for value against weaker Aces (A9-A2) and to build the pot in case I hit my flush on the river. A bet of 850 (approximately 58% of the pot) is large enough to extract value and charge any potential draws while keeping the initiative in the hand.

Heads-up on turn with 7d9d on Ad5s8h4h board. Retain open-ended straight draw (6 or T for nuts/strong straights: 45678 or 6789T, 8 outs ~32% to hit river). Backdoor diamond flush draw (Ad on board) and 9 overcard add equity (~35-40% vs typical UTG range). Flash's UTG preflop raise, small flop c-bet (weak Ax/continuation?), larger turn bet (850 into 1450, ~58% pot) after 4h (brings heart draws, possible floats/polarization). Excellent pot odds (850 to win 3150, ~21% required; implied odds strong deep ~100BB effective, position). Folding suboptimal after correct speculative calls pre/flop. Raising semi-bluff risks vs strong range (Ax/sets/draws won't fold easily OOP), bloats pot OOP for him. Calling optimal: controls pot, disguises strength, exploits position on river (can value bet hits, check-fold misses vs checks, bluff weak rivers). Consistent with GTO speculative play: realize equity multi-street with position/playability.

I have successfully hit the nut flush on the river with the Ten of Hearts and Ace of Hearts on a board containing three hearts (8h, 4h, 9h). Throughout the hand, I have been the aggressor, and Elon has called both my flop and turn bets, which strongly suggests he holds a hand with showdown value like a pair of Aces, two pair, or a set. Now that the flush has arrived, I must bet for value. A bet of 2,200 (approximately 70% of the pot) is a sizable value bet that targets Elon's likely range of top pairs or better. Given Elon's sticky nature in previous hands, he is likely to call with any strong Ace or two-pair combination, and I want to maximize the pot before the hand ends.

Facing a 2200 bet into 5350 (~73% pot) on the river with 7d9d, pairing the 9h for middle pair (9s with 7 kicker). Board Ad5s8h4h9h features a completed nut flush draw (hearts), possible straights (5-6-7-8-9, 4-5-6-7-8 indirectly), and an Ace dominating unpaired hands. Flash's UTG preflop raise followed by three OOP bets of increasing size (flop 350 small c-bet, turn 850, river 2200 large) on a draw-heavy board indicates a strong polarized range: Ax (top pair/good kicker), sets/two-pair, flushes, or value straights. Bluffing unlikely from UTG with this line. Previous calls flop/turn justified by ~32% equity from OESD + backdoors and position, but river equity now ~15-25% vs his range (loses to Ax+, flushes, straights; ties some bluffs unlikely). Pot odds 5350:2200 (~2.4:1) require ~29% equity to call profitably, which we lack even with implied odds diminished (his stack ~6k after bet, river). Raising offers low fold equity vs premium range OOP. Folding preserves 9700 stack (97BB) for superior spots in deep tournament, adhering to GTO speculative play: realize equity while drawing, fold thin value on scary runouts.

5-handed but still first to act with no antes, so stealing doesn’t pay much and we’ll face too many calls/isolations from position. T7s is playable but not strong enough to open UTG into several shorter stacks who can 3-bet/jam and force us to burn chips. Best is to pass and wait for higher-equity opens/spots where our chip-lead pressure is cleaner.

K3 offsuit is a very weak hand with dominated king and poor playability. Positioned UTG+1 in a 5-handed game after UTG fold, but still early. Pot is 150 with blinds posted, to call 100 for a limp, but limping weak offsuit hands leads to difficult out-of-position spots postflop. Standard play is to fold and wait for better opportunities to build the stack.

I am on the Button with T9o (Nine of Clubs, Ten of Spades). It is folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, T9o is at the very bottom of the opening range. The Big Blind (Flash) is the current chip leader with over 110 BB and has demonstrated aggressive play, including 3-betting. Opening a marginal offsuit hand into an aggressive big stack is a high-variance play that often leads to folding to a 3-bet or playing a difficult post-flop spot. Given my comfortable stack depth of ~78 BB, I do not need to force steals with marginal hands. I choose to fold and wait for a better spot or a less aggressive configuration.

Heads up against the BB with Q4o. This is a marginal hand but completing for 50 to see a flop in position to the remaining players (though OOP to Flash) is reasonable. Q4o isn't strong enough to raise for value, and folding is too tight heads up. I'll complete and see a flop cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce of hearts. The Small Blind, Claude, has simply completed the bet. Since I am already in the hand and there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check to realize my equity for free. Raising with such a weak hand out of position is unnecessary.

Missed this flop completely with Q4o - no pair, no draws. Board is Js 5c Ad which hits many hands in Flash's BB range (Ax, Jx). Checking is best - no need to bluff into a board that favors their range, and I can see a free turn.
