I have Ten-high on a board with an Ace and a Jack, providing me with no pair and no real draws. Claude checked from the Small Blind after completing the pre-flop bet. There is no reason to bluff into a tiny pot with zero equity, so I will check behind and take the free card.

Turn brought me a queen, giving me second pair. Flash checked behind on the flop, suggesting they likely don't have a strong ace or jack. With middle pair now, I should lead for thin value and protection against draws (KT now has a gutshot, backdoor flush draws improved). A small bet extracts value from weaker holdings and defines their range.

Claude has led out for a small bet on the turn after the Queen of Diamonds appeared. My hand, Ten-Deuce of hearts, is extremely weak, holding only Ten-high and a gutshot straight draw to a King. The board is now very coordinated (Ace-Jack-Queen), making it highly probable that Claude has hit a pair or better, or at the very least, holds a hand with much better showdown value than mine. In a small pot where I have very little equity and no real reason to bluff, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

K4o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and kicker. UTG in a 5-handed tournament, folding is standard to avoid domination by better kings or tough post-flop decisions OOP. Deep stack (97BB), no need to risk with weak holdings; wait for premium hands to build stack.

I hold Jd 2s in UTG+1 (Cutoff in 5-handed). This is a statistically very weak hand (off-suit, unconnected, low kicker) with no playability. With three players left to act behind me, including the Button, entering the pot would be a losing play. The standard action is to fold immediately.
QJo on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a standard steal spot. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to try to take down the blinds or play in position postflop if called.

I am in the small blind with 5h3h. Claude, on the button, has raised to 250. While 53s has some speculative value, calling out of position against a button raise is generally a losing play, especially with the chip leader (FiveTwo) still to act in the big blind. The pot odds (200 to call a 400 pot) aren't sufficient to justify playing this weak hand out of position for the rest of the hand.

Claude is opening fairly often on the button and we’re getting a good price (150 to win 400), so Q8o is within a reasonable 5-handed big blind defend. A 3-bet would bloat the pot out of position with a dominated, low playability hand and risks facing a shove from Claude’s 60bb stack, so we take the higher-EV, lower-variance defend and realize equity postflop.

Heads-up BB vs BTN on A♥T♥3♣ with Q♣8♥ (no heart, no pair, no meaningful straight draw). This board favors the preflop raiser’s range (lots of Ax), and we have very little equity/ability to continue versus even a small c-bet. Leading would mostly just burn chips into a range that can call/raise comfortably. Best is to check and keep the pot small, planning to fold to most bets and look for a better turn to stab if Claude checks back.

I have QJo on an A-T-3 board with two hearts. I have a gutshot straight draw (K gives me the nut straight) and a backdoor flush draw with the Jh. This is an Ace-high board which favors my preflop raising range. A small c-bet of around half pot makes sense to apply pressure and potentially take down the pot, while also building it with my equity.

We’re BB vs BTN on A♥T♥3♣ with Q♣8♥ and face a small c-bet (275 into 825). Even getting ~25% pot odds, this hand has very low equity versus Claude’s range (lots of Ax, Tx, heart combos) and poor realization: no pair, no real straight draw, and only a very thin backdoor heart possibility. Calling just invites us to hemorrhage chips OOP on most turns, and a check-raise bluff is unattractive here because our blockers are weak and this board strongly favors the preflop raiser. Best is to cut losses and fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Spades and Eight of Hearts (T8o). In a 5-handed game, UTG range should still be reasonably strong. T8o is a weak unsuited hand with poor playability and connectivity. It is well below the standard opening range for early position. With a healthy stack of 78 BB, there is no need to make loose speculative plays from out of position. The correct and standard play is to fold.
AKo is a premium hand. UTG+1 position with Pro folded. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to build the pot and narrow the field.

I am holding Ace of Clubs and Deuce of Diamonds on the button. Claude, in UTG+1, has opened with a raise to 250. While I have position, Ace-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly against a standard opening range from an early/middle position. Calling would likely put me in a situation where I am dominated or playing a weak hand out of position post-flop if the blinds decide to enter. Given my healthy stack and the early position of the raiser, folding is the most prudent play to wait for a more advantageous situation.

Claude is opening from early position in a 5-handed configuration and has a ~63bb stack, so his range is reasonably strong. Js4c is offsuit, highly dominated, and plays very poorly out of position from the small blind (reverse implied odds, tough to realize equity, and we risk getting squeezed/playing multiway vs BB). It’s not a good candidate to defend or 3-bet bluff (weak blockers and poor postflop playability). Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG+1 position, with TJo offsuit in the big blind. This is a marginal hand with poor post-flop playability out of position. Claude's range from early position is strong, including pairs, broadway hands, and suited connectors. While pot odds are favorable (calling 150 into 400, about 3.7:1), the implied odds are limited due to offsuit nature, no suited potential, and being OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots, avoiding tough post-flop decisions in a tournament with 150 hands total and my stack at 9600 (96BB). History shows Claude raises frequently but this spot is standard fold.
92 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially from UTG where I need to play tight since I'll be out of position. Easy fold.

I am in UTG+1 with 6s3s. This is a very weak hand, even with its suitedness. From an early position, it should almost never be played. I have a healthy stack and don't need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands.

3-handed with two folds to me on the Button, ranges should open very wide and I can apply chip-leader pressure on the blinds. Even with 72o (bottom of range), the immediate steal is profitable often enough, and being in position lets me realize equity and win pots with initiative on many flops. I’ll use the minimum open to risk less versus a 3-bet/jam while still threatening the blinds’ ranges.

Preflop in SB with Ad6c facing a button min-raise to 200 after two folds. FiveTwo on button has a wide range. A6o has some equity (~38-42% vs wide BTN open) and blocks strong Ax hands, with good pot odds (150 to call into 350 pot, ~30% needed). However, postflop OOP vs aggressive button player like FiveTwo is tough; A6o gets dominated by better aces, has poor playability if misses. BB Pro still to act and may defend wide. Standard GTO favors folding marginal offsuit aces OOP in this multiway spot potential. Prioritize good postflop situations deepstacked mid-tournament to maximize stack long-term. Opponent tendencies show FiveTwo steals often but bets streets aggressively when checked to.

I am in the Big Blind facing a min-raise to 200 from the Button (FiveTwo). The Small Blind has folded. The pot currently contains 350 chips (50 SB + 100 BB + 200 Button bet). I need to call 100 more chips to see the flop. This offers me pot odds of 3.5 to 1, meaning I need approximately 22% equity to call profitably. My hand, King-Three offsuit (K3o), has roughly 40-46% equity against a typical wide Button opening range. Despite being out of position, the price is too good to fold a hand with a King blocker. I will call and evaluate the flop, likely check-folding if I don't improve.

I defended the Big Blind with K3o against a Button min-raise. The flop comes 9d-9h-7s. This texture misses my hand completely, leaving me with just King-high and no draws. The board is paired, which often leads to the pre-flop raiser c-betting or checking back. Being out of position with a weak hand, leading (donk betting) is not profitable, and I cannot withstand significant pressure. My best course of action is to check to the aggressor. If they bet, I will likely fold; if they check, I will try to realize equity on the turn for free.

We flop a marginal two-pair (9s and 7s) but with the absolute worst kicker, and BB’s defend range contains a lot of 7x that has us outkicked, plus all the 9x/trips and boats that can punish a c-bet. This paired, fairly static board doesn’t need much protection, and betting mostly folds out hands we already beat (random overcards) while getting called/raised by the part of the range that dominates us. In position, the highest-EV line is pot control: check back, realize our showdown value, and be prepared to bluff-catch or value-bet thinly on safe runouts if BB shows weakness again.

The turn brings the 7h, making the board [9d 9h 7s 7h]. This double-paired board is interesting because it counterfeits any pocket pair lower than 7s (22-66). My hand is essentially Nines and Sevens with a King kicker (9-9-7-7-K). This means I am currently beating all lower pocket pairs (22-66) and all unpaired hands lower than King-high (Qx, Jx, Tx). I only lose to Ace-high, pocket pairs 88+, or a 9/7. The opponent checked back the flop, suggesting they likely don't have a strong hand (like a 9) or a strong overpair, though they could have Ace-high. Betting here has little value as worse hands fold and better hands call. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply with what is likely the best hand or to induce a bluff from a worse hand. I will check.

We improved to a very strong full house (7s full of 9s) on a board where BB can easily have plenty of 7x and some pocket pairs/overcards that will still pay something off, but we’re not nutted because any 9x has us crushed (9s full / quads with 99). After BB checks twice, the best line is a small value bet that keeps worse hands in and also controls the size so we’re not forced into stacking off versus a polarized raise. If raised, we can mostly continue by calling and re-evaluating river since our hand is strong and we also have outs to quads on a 7 river.

The board is double-paired (9-9-7-7), which significantly alters hand values. My King-high is effectively Two Pair (Nines and Sevens) with a King kicker. This hand is ahead of all pocket pairs from 22-66 (since the 7s on board are higher) and all unpaired hands lower than King-high (Qx, Jx, etc.). I only lose to Ace-high, pairs 88+, or a 9/7. The opponent checked back the flop, suggesting weakness or a trap, but often capping their range. Their small turn bet of 200 into 450 (less than half pot) offers favorable odds (requiring ~23% equity). Given the Button's wide range, they likely have many counterfeit low pairs or random broadway hands that they are turning into a bluff or betting for thin protection. My King kicker is strong enough to bluff-catch this small sizing.

On a double-paired board of [9d 9h 7s 7h Ts], my hand is effectively Two Pair (Nines and Sevens) with a King kicker. I currently beat all counterfeited pocket pairs (22-66) and unpaired hands lower than King-high (Qx, Jx). However, I lose to any Ace-high, any Ten (which pairs the river), and pocket pairs 88+. The opponent checked the flop and bet small on the turn, a line often indicative of marginal showdown value or a cheap bluff. Betting the river serves no purpose as worse hands will fold and better hands will call. Checking is the dominant strategy here: it allows me to realize my showdown equity if the opponent checks back (winning against their counterfeited low pairs or lower high cards) and avoids inflating the pot with a marginal hand. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold, but checking gives the best chance to win the pot for free.

We river a strong but non-nut full house (77799). Pro’s line (check flop, check/call small turn, check river) contains lots of medium strength bluff-catchers like Tx, pocket pairs, and random overcards that may pay off a small bet, while the hands that beat us (any 9x, TT, T7, 97/9T) are likely to check-raise for value. Best EV is a thin value bet with a controlled size that can still get called by worse, and we can comfortably bet/fold versus a raise because bluffs are extremely unlikely on this double-paired board.

Sizing: ~35% pot to target crying calls without bloating the pot versus the top of Pro’s range.

On the river, the board is [9d 9h 7s 7h Ts], giving me Two Pair (Nines and Sevens) with a King kicker. My hand beats any pocket pair 66 or lower (which play the board with a Ten kicker) and any unpaired hand lower than a King. However, I lose to any Ace-high, any Ten (which now pairs or makes a full house), and any pair 88 or higher. The opponent's line of checking the flop and betting small on both the turn and river is highly indicative of thin value, likely from a weak Ace or a rivered Ten. A small bet of 300 into 850 typically targets King-high or chopped pots and suggests they want a call. While the pot odds are attractive (requiring only ~21% equity), the opponent's range is heavily weighted towards Ax hands that dominate me. Since I would have plenty of Ax in my own range to call with, I can comfortably fold my King-high here.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position with 4-3 offsuit. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker and should never be played from early position, as there are four players left to act behind me who could easily have superior hands. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack.

5-handed after UTG folds, AKo is a clear top-of-range open from UTG+1. Raising builds the pot for value against dominated Ax/Kx, denies equity to the blinds, and gives us initiative versus the shorter stacks behind (Button/SB/BB). I’ll use a standard live sizing that has been common in this game to 3x to pressure the blinds while still allowing calls from worse hands and keeping our range uncapped versus any 3-bets.

Facing a raise to 300 from FiveTwo in UTG+1 after UTG fold, with J9s on the button. This is a strong speculative hand with position: suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Pot odds are favorable (calling 300 into 450, about 1.5:1 direct but implied odds strong deepstacked). FiveTwo has been aggressive historically but my position allows me to realize equity well postflop, whether heads-up or multiway if blinds call. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks too much with a bluff-heavy range here vs an early raiser. Calling to play postflop in position maximizes EV.

I hold 2d 7d in the Small Blind. The action began with a raise to 300 (3bb) from the chip leader FiveTwo, followed by a call from the Button. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 250 to win a pot that will be 1000), 7-2 suited is arguably the worst hand in poker due to poor playability and domination issues. Furthermore, I would be playing out of position against two active opponents, including the massive chip leader. The risk of losing a larger pot due to reverse implied odds outweighs the cheap price to call. Folding is the only correct decision here.

I have QJo in the BB facing a raise to 300 and a call. Pot is 750 and I need 200 to call - getting nearly 4:1 pot odds. QJo plays well multiway and has good implied odds. Squeezing here would be marginal since QJo doesn't play great as a 3-bet bluff against a raiser and caller. Easy call to see a flop.

Board is monotone spades (4s 6s 8s). I hold the Js giving me a flush draw to the second nuts. Checking is best here - I don't want to lead into opponents who may already have a flush. If they bet, I can call or raise as a semi-bluff with my strong draw. If it checks through, I get a free card to improve.

Monotone flop (4s6s8s) multiway is a very poor texture for c-betting with AdKc and no spade: our equity is low, our fold equity drops significantly 3-handed (both caller ranges contain plenty of suited spades), and betting exposes us to awkward raises where we can’t profitably continue. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our overcard equity, and lets us respond to sizing (we can still call some small bets in position versus missed spade/one-spade floats, or fold to pressure).

Flop is 4s6s8s, a highly coordinated monotone board favoring spades. I hold 9sJh, giving me a strong flush draw (four spades, with 9s providing a decent flush blocker/high card) and overcard equity with the Jack. No made hand, but 12 outs to flush (~47% equity if called) plus straight draw potential (e.g., 5/7/T for various straights/combos). Preflop: FiveTwo raised UTG+1, I called button, Claude defended BB multiway. Both checked to me in position. FiveTwo's check could be weakness (missed flop, trapping) or trap with strong spade hand. Claude likely weak. This is a prime semi-bluff spot: massive equity, fold equity vs. opponents' weak ranges (non-spade holdings that missed), position to realize equity. Checking risks giving free card to draws/improves; betting builds pot, denies equity, takes initiative. Sizing ~2/3 pot (650) balances value (weak flushes/pairs), bluffs/draws. Deep stacks support aggression to maximize EV long-term.

I have the Js for the second nut flush draw on this monotone board. Pot is 1600 and I need 650 to call - getting ~2.5:1 odds. With 9 outs (~35% by river) and strong implied odds when I hit the second nuts, this is a clear call. Raising as a semi-bluff is possible but risky with FiveTwo still to act and Elon showing strength. Calling keeps the pot manageable while giving me excellent odds to realize my equity.

Monotone flop (4s6s8s) is extremely unfavorable for AdKc with zero spades, especially multiway. Elon’s bet into two players and Claude’s call heavily weight both ranges toward at least one spade (often a made flush or strong draw), which crushes our equity; even pairing A/K is frequently not enough. With poor implied odds and very low fold equity versus two opponents, continuing is -EV. Preserve stack and fold.

The 3s completes my flush - I now have the Jack-high flush (second nuts, only losing to As). With four spades on board, anyone with a single spade has a flush. Elon showed strength on the flop. Checking here makes sense to: 1) Induce a bet from Elon if he has a smaller flush or is bluffing, 2) Control the pot in case he has the As. If he bets, I can raise for value. If he checks, I can bet river.

Turn board brings the 3s, completing four spades on board, giving me the nut flush with 9s (highest spade, board 3s4s6s8s). On flop (three spades), I had strong flush draw + Jack overcard, semi-bluffed 650, Claude called (likely holding a spade draw, weak pair, or lower flush draw), FiveTwo folded. Claude checks turn again after flush completes, representing possible weakness (worse flush like 2s-7s/Ts, or non-spade hand hoping to check down). Nuts on monotone board with possible straight draws dead, massive value range: worse flushes, sets unlikely to call pre but possible. Pot 2250, deep effective stacks (me 8600, Claude 5750). Bet ~3/4 pot (1700) for value, gets called by worse flushes/draws improves, builds pot, denies equity if he has air. Checking risks free river or check-behind value loss. Position allows control.

I have the Jack-high flush - second nuts on this 4-spade board. Elon bet 650 on flop and now 1700 on turn, showing clear strength. He likely has a flush or is semi-bluffing. With only the As beating me, I should raise for value. Many worse flushes (any spade below J) will call. Min raise to 3400 gets value while not over-committing if he has the nuts.

Turn pairs the board with a low spade, completing the nut flush for me holding 9s (highest spade on board: 3s<4s<6s<8s<9s). Previous semi-bluff on flop with strong draw hit the nuts. Claude's BB defense pre, check-call flop on monotone spade board, check-raise turn after flush completes indicates likely a made flush (medium spade like 2s5s7s etc.) or monster (higher spade/SF like 57s for SF). Range analysis: preflop wide but spade-heavy post-flop callers; high spades (T+/s) possible but less dense, SF exact combos low (~4 for 57s etc.). Pot 7350, facing +1700 (4.3:1 odds), trivial. Call sees river but Claude acts first (~2350 left), risks check-check losing value or his bet I raise. Raising all-in (6900 total) extracts max value from worse flushes (bulk of range) as he gets ~5:1 to call 5200 more into ~13k pot, commits his short stack now, consistent with aggressive style, no fold equity needed vs value range, prevents river redraws/redos (e.g., high spade river upgrades board flush potentially). EV-maximizing jam in position deepstack tournament.

I have the Jack-high flush (second nuts) on a 4-spade board. Elon's 3-bet to 6900 puts me all-in for my remaining 2350. The pot is 12550 + my 2350 = 14900, giving me over 6:1 odds. I only lose to exactly As. Even if Elon has the nut flush sometimes, I cannot fold the second nuts getting these odds. I'm effectively pot committed with a monster hand.

5-handed, A6o is near the bottom of an UTG opening range but still profitable at some frequency, especially with our large stack and multiple shorter stacks behind. Raising applies immediate pressure to the blinds and lets us win the pot uncontested often; it also keeps the SPR manageable if called. I’ll use a standard small open to avoid bloating the pot out of position, and I’m prepared to fold to significant 3-bets from the deeper stacks while reassessing if the short stack shoves.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position against an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands. My stack is 28.5BB, but calling or raising here commits too much equity to a marginal hand with dominated king risk and few strong draws. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this multi-way potential pot.

I hold 9s 5c on the Button. The player in UTG (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 250 (2.5BB). My hand is 9-high offsuit, which is extremely weak and far below the threshold for calling or 3-betting against an early position open. I have no equity to defend here.

JQo in the SB facing a UTG raise to 250. This is a playable hand but not strong enough to 3-bet against UTG range. Calling 200 to see a flop is reasonable - I have decent equity and can play fit-or-fold postflop. Folding is too tight with this hand.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit. FiveTwo raised from UTG to 250, and Claude called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me only 150 more to call, giving me pot odds of 4:1. KJo is a strong enough broadway hand to defend the big blind at this price. While playing out of position against an UTG raiser requires caution, closing the action and seeing a flop with these odds is the most standard and profitable play. I will call and evaluate the strength of my hand based on the flop texture.

I flopped top pair with Jd on Jc7c2c, but this is a scary monotone club board and I have no clubs. Any opponent with a single club has a flush draw, and two clubs = made flush. With two opponents behind me (FiveTwo the preflop raiser and Flash), betting risks getting raised and being in a tough spot. Checking to see what they do is the prudent play - I can call reasonable bets with my top pair but fold to heavy aggression indicating a made flush.

I have flopped top pair with King kicker, which is a strong starting point. However, the board is monotone with three clubs, and I do not hold a club myself. This makes my hand quite vulnerable to anyone holding a single high club or a made flush. Since the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) is yet to act behind me, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to see how the aggressor continues and gauge the strength of my opponents' hands without inflating the pot on a dangerous texture. If FiveTwo bets, I will likely call to see a turn, depending on the sizing, but I want to avoid building a massive pot without a redraw to a flush.

Monotone flop (Jc-7c-2c) in a 3-way pot: as the UTG raiser I still have a meaningful range advantage (overpairs, strong Jx, and nut/strong club combos), and both blinds checking suggests they miss a lot of the time. With Ad6c I have ace-high plus a one-card flush draw, so a small c-bet can win the pot immediately versus all the no-club air and some marginal hands, while also setting a price to realize equity when called. I’ll keep it small to reduce check-raise pain multiway and because this board is very static; if I face a big check-raise I can comfortably let it go with this non-nut draw.

I have top pair Jd plus the Qc giving me the second nut flush draw on this monotone board. FiveTwo's 250 c-bet is fairly small. I'm getting 4:1 pot odds with substantial equity - either my top pair is good or I can improve to a queen-high flush. This is an easy call. Raising risks building a big pot when I could be behind a made flush.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker, which is a strong hand, but the monotone board of three clubs makes it extremely vulnerable. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 250 into a 750 pot, and Claude has called from the small blind. I am getting 5:1 pot odds (250 to win a current pot of 1250) to see a turn card. While I do not hold a club to redraw to a flush, top pair is too strong to fold given these favorable odds and the relatively small bet size. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn. If a fourth club appears or if there is heavy betting on later streets, I will proceed with extreme caution, but for now, the price is right to continue.

Turn brings the As on a monotone club board. I have top pair (Jd) and the Qc for a second-nut flush draw. Action is on me with two players behind. Betting here is risky - if someone has a made flush or the Ac/Kc, I could face a raise. My hand has decent showdown value and I have 9 outs to improve to a flush. Checking keeps the pot controlled while giving me a free card opportunity if it checks through, or allowing me to evaluate if FiveTwo continues betting.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a significant scare card that likely improves the range of the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) or even Claude. My hand, which was top pair on the flop, is now second pair on a monotone board with an overcard. Since I do not hold a club to redraw to a flush, my hand has lost much of its relative strength. Checking is the most logical action to keep the pot small, exercise pot control, and see how the original aggressor reacts to the Ace.

Turn A♠ is a great card for our UTG range and specifically improves us to top pair while keeping a club (6c) as backup equity. After both blinds check, this is a good spot to apply a second barrel: we can fold out a lot of flop-calls that were one-club overcards / weak Jx / 7x that now hate the Ace, and we also deny a free river to hands holding a single club that can outdraw us. I’ll keep the sizing modest to reduce the pain versus a check-raise from a made flush (which we can comfortably fold to), while still charging draws and getting thin value when called by worse.

I have top pair (Jd) plus the Qc for second nut flush draw. FiveTwo bets 600 into 1500 (pot is now 2100). Getting 3.5:1 pot odds with 9 outs to the flush plus showdown value with top pair. If FiveTwo has a made flush, I still have outs unless they hold the Ac. The small bet sizing could be a value bet, but could also be a probe with Ac or continuation from a strong non-flush hand. Easy call with my equity - folding is way too tight and raising risks getting jammed on by a better flush.

I am currently holding second pair (Jacks) with a King kicker. The board is quite dangerous with three clubs (monotone) and an Ace appearing on the turn, which is a significant overcard to my Jacks. However, I am getting very favorable pot odds of nearly 5:1 to call (600 to win a pot that will be 3,150). FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet about 44% of the pot, and Claude has called. While it's possible one of them has an Ace or a flush, my hand still has showdown value against bluffs, smaller pairs, or draws. Given the price, folding second pair here would be too tight. I will call the turn bet and re-evaluate on the river, though I intend to fold to high aggression if the board texture worsens or if FiveTwo continues to fire large on the river.

River 4d is a blank - no flush completed. I have top pair (Jd) with Qc blocker. FiveTwo bet flop and turn but checked river, suggesting either a missed draw or pot control. Flash also checked. My top pair has good showdown value but betting risks only getting called/raised by better hands (made flushes). The Qc blocks the second nut flush. Checking to showdown is optimal - I can beat missed draws and weaker Jx hands while avoiding a check-raise from a slow-played flush.

On the river, the board texture remains dangerous with three clubs and an Ace. I am holding second pair (Jacks) with a King kicker. Since Claude checked, and FiveTwo (the original aggressor) is still behind me, checking is the most prudent action. Betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me (Aces, flushes, or sets) and wouldn't force any better hands to fold. I will check to see if I can reach a cheap showdown or evaluate a potential bet from FiveTwo.

River goes check/check to us in a 3-way pot on Jc-7c-2c-As-4d. With Ad6c we have top pair but a weak kicker, and both blinds have called twice on a monotone flop/turn line where their continuing ranges are heavily weighted toward made flushes (two clubs), Jx with a club, sets, and some sticky Jx/7x/pocket pairs. Betting now is thin at best and risks getting called (or check-raised) primarily by hands that beat us, while many worse hands will either fold or only call at very low frequency multiway after two barrels. We have solid showdown value and already extracted value on earlier streets, so the best play is to take the free showdown.

UTG in 5-handed play with QJs suited, a strong broadway suited hand with good playability. Stack of 28.5BB is playable but not deep; raising takes initiative, builds pot with strong hand, and denies equity to limpers. Standard open size around 3x BB (300) given the small blind limp dynamic (pot 150 suggests possible limp, but as first to act effectively). Folding too tight, limping invites multiway pot OOP poorly. Raise to 300 to isolate or build.

I hold 4d 7s in UTG+1, facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Elon). My hand is extremely weak (7-high, unsuited, disconnected) and performs poorly against an UTG opening range. With players left to act behind me and no potential for a profitable post-flop scenario, the correct decision is to fold immediately.

ATo on the button facing a UTG raise from short-stacked Elon (2550 remaining). This is a solid hand in position. I could 3-bet to isolate but that would likely commit Elon with his short stack. Calling keeps my range wide and I have position to outplay postflop. Two players still to act behind but they'd need strong hands to squeeze.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce suited. Elon, who is relatively short-stacked at 25.5 BB, has raised from UTG to 300. Claude has called on the button. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 250 to win a pot that would be 1000), K2s is a very weak hand to play out of position against a UTG opening range. A King-high flop would often leave me dominated by hands like AK, KQ, or KJ. Given my current stack of 10,025, there is no need to risk chips in a multi-way pot with a marginal hand in the worst possible position.

KTs suited is strong enough to play aggressively from the BB, especially versus an UTG open from a shorter stack and a loose-ish BTN flat that caps Claude’s range. There’s a lot of dead money (750) and a squeeze pressures Claude to fold many marginal calls while also often isolating Elon. I’ll size so that (1) folds win the pot immediately, and (2) if Elon jams his remaining ~25bb, the price to call is good with KTs suited.

Facing a large 3-bet to 1300 (13x BB, 4.3x my raise) from the deep-stacked BB after my UTG open and button flat-call. QJd suited is playable preflop but marginal OOP vs 3-bet range, which includes premiums (AA/JJ+, AK/AQ), broadways, and suited connectors from BB position. My 25.5BB stack makes calling 1000 (pot odds ~2:1) risky: commits 40% of stack, leaves 12.5BB effective, OOP postflop in bloated pot (~3k+), with Claude acting behind who can squeeze/flat. Shoving 2550 risks stack vs villain's wide calling range (where QJs has ~38-42% equity but low fold equity given deep stacks and history of FiveTwo's aggression/value). Folding preserves 22.5BB for better spots; QJs not premium enough to continue in this dynamic. Tournament phase favors patience with short-ish stack.

FiveTwo squeezed to 1300 after my flat call. ATo is a marginal hand against a 3-bet from a big stack. I have to put in 1000 more into a 1950 pot. While I have position, ATo doesn't play well against a 3-bet range - I'll often be dominated by AQ/AK or flipping with pairs. FiveTwo has been aggressive and has a huge stack to apply pressure postflop. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in UTG position with Qd8c (Q8 offsuit). This is a weak hand that is easily dominated by many hands in opponents' ranges (KQ, QJ, AT+, pairs). Even in a 5-handed game, opening from UTG with Q8o is generally a losing play due to position disadvantage and hand weakness. My stack is healthy (73.75 BB), so I have no pressure to make desperate moves. The disciplined and correct action is to fold.
J9 suited in UTG+1. Marginal hand but has playability with the suited diamonds giving flush potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x to build pot if I connect and fold out some weak hands.

Claude has raised from early-middle position (UTG+1 in a 5-handed game). My hand, Ten-Nine offsuit, is marginal and often dominated by an opener's range. Furthermore, the Big Blind (Elon) is very short-stacked with only 24.5 big blinds remaining and is likely to shove over a call, putting me in a difficult spot. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have more equity or a better position to steal.

Claude is opening from early position to 2.5x, and we’ll be out of position the entire hand from the small blind. Q3s is too weak and too easily dominated (especially when we make a queen), and flatting from the SB creates poor realization and reverse implied odds. A 3-bet bluff here is also low quality versus an early-position range and with a short-stacked BB who can jam over the top. Best play is to conserve chips and fold.

Preflop in the big blind facing an early position raise to 250 from Claude (UTG+1) after UTG folded and button/SB folded behind. Pot is 400, calling 150 more gives about 2.7:1 odds, but Q9o is offsuit, gapped, and queen-high with poor playability. Claude's EP range is strong (pairs 88+, AJo+, ATs+, KQs, etc.), where Q9o has poor equity (~30-35% at best, often dominated by AQ/AK/KQ). No strong blockers or suited/connectors for 3-bet bluff. My stack is 2450 (~24BB), so no need to defend wide; fold preserves stack for better positions/spots. Standard fold.

J2 suited is too weak to open from UTG. Even with the suited hearts, the hand lacks playability - huge gap between cards, terrible kicker if I hit a jack, and I'm in the worst position at the table. Easy fold.

I am in the UTG+1 position with Ace-Seven offsuit. The player in UTG has folded. While Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand, in a 5-handed game, it's strong enough to open for a raise to take control of the pot and potentially pick up the blinds and ante. My stack is around 100 big blinds, providing enough flexibility to play this hand. A standard 2.5x open (250 chips) is a solid play here.

Flash is opening from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game, so his range is wider than full-ring but still contains a lot of hands that dominate J6o (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, bigger pairs, strong aces). J6o plays poorly postflop even in position due to reverse implied odds, and we also have a short stack in the SB (Elon) who can jam 24bb and force us to abandon our 250-chip call. With no strong blockers or suitedness to realize equity well, this is a clean fold.

Q8o is a marginal offsuit hand with mediocre high card strength and poor connectivity or suitedness. Facing an early-position raise from Flash (UTG+1), who has shown aggression in prior hands, the standard villain range is strong (pairs, broadways, suited connectors). Defending from SB OOP requires a hand with good equity and playability, which Q8o lacks—it's often dominated and awkward postflop. Pot odds are decent (400:200 or ~3:1), but implied odds are poor OOP vs raiser likely to c-bet. With 24BB stack, better to wait for stronger hands to 3bet/shove or defend profitably rather than spew chips in a multiway pot potential (Pro BB yet to act). Folding preserves stack for superior spots in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with J4o. Facing a 2.5x raise from Flash in UTG+1. The pot odds are roughly 27% (150 to call into a total pot of 550), but my hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position. My equity realization with J4o is low, making this a negative EV call despite the cheap price. Folding is the standard play to preserve chips.

I have been dealt pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in poker. Being under the gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game, my primary goal is to build a pot while ensuring I don't let too many players in for cheap. A standard raise of 3x the big blind is consistent with my previous playstyle (like in Hand #61) and should ideally induce action from the larger stacks or the short stack on the button.

Flash’s UTG open to 3x in a 5-handed game is still a fairly strong range, and we’re going to be out of position postflop with a dominated, offsuit 8-6 that makes too many second-best pairs. Calling is -EV and 3-bet bluffing here is unnecessary given the poor playability and the risk of getting played back at (especially with a short stack on the Button). Best decision is to simply release and preserve stack for better spots.

Facing a standard UTG raise to 3BB from Flash, who has shown aggression in history. 67s is a speculative suited connector with good playability in position on the button. With 24BB effective stack, calling preserves fold equity postflop and takes advantage of implied odds if it hits big multiway, as blinds may call. Folding is too tight here; 3-betting or shoving is overplay given range strength of UTG open. Pot odds are decent (~2.5:1), and position allows controlling pot postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Tc 7h, facing a raise from UTG to 300 and a call from the Button. Although the pot odds are roughly 3:1, T7o is a very weak hand that plays poorly multi-way and out of position. It is dominated by the ranges of both opponents. The prudent play is to fold and preserve chips.

I have Q8o in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300 and a button call. Pot is 750 and I need 200 to call, getting 3.75:1 odds. Q8o is marginal but I'm closing the action with decent pot odds. My hand has enough equity (~30-35%) against their ranges to make this a profitable call. I'll see a flop.

I completely whiffed this flop with Q8o on 2c 4h 5d. No pair, no draws, nothing. I'm first to act and should check to see what the preflop aggressor does. If Flash or Elon bet, I'll fold - I have no equity worth defending.

I have pocket Aces, the best possible overpair on this low board. While the 2-4-5 flop is somewhat coordinated and offers straight possibilities for hands like A3 or 36, it is unlikely my opponents have those given the pre-flop action. I need to bet for value and protection against overcards or gutshots. Claude has checked to me. Elon is very short-stacked with only 2100 chips remaining; a bet here puts significant pressure on him and builds the pot against Claude's range which likely contains mid-pairs or high-card floats. A bet of 600 into the 950 pot is a standard sizing to extract value while maintaining control of the hand.

Flop is 2c4h5d, giving me an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: 3 or 8) with 6s7s, plus backdoor spade flush draw and two overcards. Flash's UTG raise and continuation bet into a multiway pot suggests a strong range (overpairs, Ax, sets like 22/44/55, two-pair combos), but the low connected board hits speculative hands. Claude's check indicates weakness. Pot odds are favorable: 600 to call into 1550 (~2.58:1, requiring ~28% equity, but OESD has ~32% vs. typical C-bet range). Position on button allows control; calling keeps the pot multiway, realizes equity cheaply, and sets up turns where I hit (32% by river) or can bluff/rep with initiative. Raising (min 1200) risks isolation vs. Flash's strong range without fold equity (he bets big), and shoving overcommits 2100 stack (effective 24BB pre, now shallower). Folding squanders equity. Call preserves stack flexibility in tournament.

I have Q8o with no pair and no draw on 2c 4h 5d. Flash bet 600 and Elon called. I need 600 to call into a 2150 pot (3.6:1 odds, need ~22% equity). With just two overcards against two opponents who've shown interest, I have roughly 12% equity at best. Even if I hit a Q or 8, the low connected board means straights are possible. Easy fold.

The turn 3h has completed a straight for my pocket Aces (A-2-3-4-5). While any 6 in Elon's range would create a higher straight (6-high), the board texture also makes it extremely likely that he holds an Ace for the same straight, or a set/two-pair that he will not fold given his short stack. With only 1500 chips remaining and a pot of 2150, Elon is heavily pot-committed. I am betting his entire remaining stack of 1500 to maximize value from his Ax hands and lower made hands, while accepting the risk of a 6-high straight.

Turn 3h completes the nut straight (3h-4h-5d-6s-7s). Flash's large turn bet after UTG raise and flop c-bet into a connected low board suggests strong value (sets like 22/44/55, two-pair 24/25/45, overpairs, combo draws) or a semibluff (heart flush draw with overcard, gutshots). Board brings heart flush draw (3h4h) and low straight draws. With the absolute nuts and only 1500 behind (all-in call), shoving maximizes EV by getting value from his entire continuing range while denying equity to draws. Pot odds irrelevant with nuts (~100% equity vs. most hands). Can't raise (stack < min raise size). Folding insane. Call all-in commits the stack profitably.

5-handed UTG is still the worst position postflop, and Q3s is too weak/too often dominated when we get action (especially versus Elton’s shorter stack reshove range and wider flats from the blinds). As the big stack we can apply pressure with better suited/connectors and broadways; here the EV of stealing 150 doesn’t justify the reverse-implied-odds and multiway/OOP problems.

9s6s is a suited connector with some playability in multiway pots due to flush and straight potential, but from UTG+1 (early position with 5 players), it's too weak to open limp or raise. I'm OOP postflop against button, SB, and BB, who may have wide ranges. My stack is 51.5BB, not desperate for action. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities, avoiding tough spots OOP with marginal equity.

I am on the Button with 5s 7h, a very weak off-suit hand. The action has folded to me. With approximately 72 big blinds, I have a healthy stack and do not need to take unnecessary risks by stealing with trash hands. 75o has very poor playability post-flop if called. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from any position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

J7o in the small blind with everyone folding to me. This is a marginal hand but at only 50 more to call into a 150 pot, I'm getting 3:1 odds. Raising to steal is an option but J7o plays poorly when called and I'll be out of position. Completing is the most reasonable play - cheap look at a flop where I could hit a pair or straight draw.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Five offsuit. The Small Blind (Claude) has limped in. In a limped pot, checking is a standard and low-risk play with a hand like A5o, which has decent showdown value and can flop top pair or a straight draw, but isn't necessarily strong enough to justify building a large pot out of position or inducing a re-raise from a potentially trapping range. I will check and see the flop for free.

Flopped top pair with J7o on Jc5d6h. Flash checked to me. This is a clear value bet spot - I'm ahead of most of Flash's limping range. Board has some straight draw potential (47, 78) so I want to charge draws. Betting around 60% pot.

In a blind-vs-blind limped pot, my pair of fives with an Ace kicker is quite strong. Claude's lead for roughly 60% of the pot could represent a Jack, a smaller pair, or a straight draw (like 78 or 47), but it could also be a simple stab at a small pot. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands (Jacks or higher pairs) and fold out his bluffs. By calling, I keep his range wide, control the size of the pot, and can evaluate the turn based on his subsequent action and the board texture.

Still have top pair with J7 on Jc5d6hTd. The turn Td adds some straight potential (89 now has a straight) and creates a backdoor flush draw. Flash called the flop, could have a jack, pair of 5s/6s, or draws. The T could have helped them if they have T-high or 89. I should continue betting for value against worse jacks and middle pairs, but keep the bet size moderate given my weak kicker. Around 55-60% pot.

In a limped pot between the blinds, ranges are extremely wide. Claude has led out on both the flop (J-5-6) and the turn (10). My pair of fives with an Ace kicker is currently a weak bluff-catcher, but the pot odds are relatively favorable (calling 275 to win a total pot of 1000, requiring 27.5% equity). Claude could be betting with various straight draws (78, 89, 47) or semi-bluffs that I currently beat. While the Ten is a better card for his range than mine, I will call one more street to see the river, as folding bottom pair immediately to a second barrel in this specific positional battle can be overly exploitable.

River 2d completes the diamond flush draw. I have top pair weak kicker (J7) after betting flop and turn for value. Flash has called twice, suggesting they have some piece of the board - could be a jack (potentially better kicker), a ten, or now possibly a flush. The diamond completing is concerning. With my weak kicker and the flush potential, I should check to control the pot. If checked through I likely win, and if Flash bets I can make a decision based on sizing - probably call a small bet but fold to large aggression.

On this river, my pair of fives has modest showdown value in a limped blind-vs-blind pot. Claude bet both the flop and turn before checking the river. This check often indicates either a missed draw or a medium-strength hand (like a Ten or a Six) that is looking to reach showdown cheaply. Betting here would likely only result in being called by better hands (Jacks, Tens, or better pairs) and folding out hands that I already beat. By checking back, I can win against Claude's missed straight draws or air without risking more chips against his potential bluff-catchers.

UTG in 5-handed play with K2o, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by any other king. No position advantage, and my stack of 51.5BB doesn't require speculative limping or raising. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase.

I am in UTG+1 with 9s3s, a very weak suited hand with poor playability and low equity. With Elon folding ahead of me, I am effectively opening the pot from early position. Opening ranges here should be tight, and 93s is far outside the playable range. With a stack of 72 BBs, there is no need to get involved with trash hands. Folding is the only standard and profitable play.

K8o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and a decent king-high hand. Will raise to 2.5x to try to take down the blinds or play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand to defend against a standard Button open, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. The most profitable play in the long run is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Button opens to 2.5x and we’re getting an excellent price in the BB (150 to win 400). Even though J6o is dominated and plays poorly out of position, our very deep stack and the wide Button opening range make this a standard defend at this price. A 3-bet bluff doesn’t perform well here (we get called by better and fold out worse), so we keep the pot controlled and realize equity with a call.

This A♣T♣2♠ flop smashes the Button’s preflop raising range (lots of Ax, broadways) and our J♣6♦ has very little immediate equity: no pair, no real straight draw, only a backdoor club draw plus a marginal overcard to the T. Leading into the preflop aggressor here doesn’t accomplish much—we’ll mostly get called by better and fold out hands we already beat, while inflating a pot out of position with a weak holding. Standard is to check range and respond based on sizing (continue only versus small c-bets with our backdoor equity, otherwise fold).

Flop is A-T-2 with two clubs. I completely whiffed but this is a good c-bet board - Ace-high favors my preflop raising range. FiveTwo checked to me. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should get folds from unpaired hands and small pairs. If I get called or raised, I can give up.

Claude’s 200 c-bet into 750 is a very small, range-y sizing, so we’re getting an excellent price to continue. Jc6d has little immediate equity, but we do have a backdoor club draw (with the Jc) plus some thin improvement cards (a J to pick up a pair) and, importantly, the ability to realize positionless equity and potentially contest later streets when he checks back or on favorable turns (clubs / some broadway texture shifts). Raising here on an A-high board that favors the Button’s range will run into too many continues (Ax, Tx, strong club hands), so the higher-EV line is to peel the small bet and proceed cautiously versus bigger turn barrels.

Turn brings the 3rd club, giving us a real flush draw with J♣6♦, but it’s a non-nutted draw (many Button floats/opens contain K♣/Q♣). Out of position, leading (donk) would often just build a pot where we get called by better made hands/stronger club holdings, and we don’t want to get blown off our equity by a raise.

Checking keeps our range protected (we can have plenty of flushes here) and lets us realize equity for free when Claude checks back. If he barrels, we can continue versus reasonable sizing with our draw and use the J♣ blocker to consider more aggressive lines versus small, range-y bets.

Turn brought the 9c, putting three clubs on board. I have the 8c giving me a flush draw, but it's only 8-high - a very weak flush if I hit. FiveTwo has been check-calling, likely indicating they have something (pair, draw, or possibly a made flush). Betting here is risky - if I'm called or raised, I'm often dominated. Best to check behind and take a free card to see if I can hit my flush on the river, then proceed cautiously even if I do since an 8-high flush can easily be beaten.

River is a brick after turn went check-check on the 3rd club. With J♣6♦ we have essentially no showdown value (J-high loses to most of Claude’s checked-back range like KQ/QJ/any pair), but we do have a relevant blocker (J♣) to his strongest flushes. Claude’s small flop c-bet followed by a turn check on a scary texture is consistent with a lot of Ax without a club, Tx, underpairs, and give-ups—hands that are put under real pressure by a polarized river lead. Our BB defend range credibly contains many two-club combos that make a flush on the turn and can check it to the raiser. So the highest-EV play is to turn our missed draw into a bluff with a near-pot sizing to maximize fold equity versus his marginal made hands.

River brought 7d - no fourth club. I only have the 8c, so with just 3 clubs on board I don't have a flush. I have K-high with no pair. FiveTwo check-called flop, checked turn, now leads river for 900 into 950. This betting line (passive then aggressive on river) typically represents value - likely an Ace, a Ten, or a flush with any two clubs. K-high beats nothing but bluffs, and I'm getting 2:1 odds needing to be right 33% of the time. FiveTwo's line doesn't look like a bluff - easy fold.

I am in UTG position with 7c 2c, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite being suited, the low ranks and gap make it very unlikely to win at showdown without significant improvement, and playing it out of position against four opponents is highly -EV. With a healthy stack of over 70 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with trash hands. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

Ah5h is a suited ace in UTG+1. Decent playable hand with flush potential. Pro folded, so I'm first to act effectively. Standard 2.5x open to 250 is appropriate - suited aces play well postflop and I have position on the blinds.

I am holding Q4o on the button. Claude raised from UTG+1. Q4o is a very weak hand and does not justify a call or a 3-bet against an open raise, even from the button. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening from early position in a 5-handed game, and J8o from the small blind is too dominated and plays poorly out of position. Calling gives BB a great price to come along, creating a multiway pot where we’ll struggle to realize equity. A light 3-bet isn’t ideal here either (no blockers, and BB has ~50bb so can defend/4-bet), so the lowest-variance, highest-EV line with our stack lead is to just let it go.

Preflop in BB facing an early position raise to 250 from Claude after UTG fold and SB fold. Pot is 400, to call 150 more (total 250 committed). 6d8d is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Pot odds are favorable (~3:1 effective). Heads-up OOP vs Claude's strong EP range (pairs, broadway, suited aces), but equity is decent (~35-40% vs typical EP open). Good implied odds if we hit, and stack depth allows (50BB). Folding is too tight in BB defense; 3-betting as bluff is possible but risky OOP without great fold equity history. Call to realize equity and see flop.

Heads-up on the flop OOP vs Claude's UTG+1 raising range (tight: pairs 88+, broadway, suited broadways/AKs). Board 3d-5d-9h is draw-heavy with two diamonds and gutshot possibilities. Holding 6d8d gives me a strong combo draw: 9 outs to the nut flush draw + 4 outs to the 5-6-7-8-9 straight (gutshot to 7), total ~15 outs for ~32% equity on flop alone, rising to over 50% by river. No showdown value currently, but massive potential. First to act, leading out (donk bet) with strong draws is standard modern play to deny Claude's equity (e.g., overpairs, top pair), build the pot when we hit/improve, and leverage fold equity against his capped range (few sets, no two-pair likely). He may have 9x, 5x, 3x but rarely. Half-pot bet sizes well for getting called by weaker hands/draws while protecting. Pot 550, bet 300 (roughly half-pot). Stack depth (49BB eff) allows aggression without overcommitting. Folding/checking too passive; checking risks free card or tough spot if he bets big.

I flopped middle pair (5s) with ace kicker on 3d9h5d. Elon's 300 bet is about 55% pot. I have a decent made hand and backdoor nut flush draw in hearts. Calling is correct - folding is too weak with this holding, and raising only gets called by better hands. I have good showdown value and implied odds if I hit my ace or flush.

Turn Jd completes my nut advantage flush (Jd-8d-6d-5d-3d), a very strong hand on this diamond-heavy board. Heads-up OOP vs Claude's UTG raising range, which called my flop donk bet of 300 into 550 (pot now 1150). His range likely includes overpairs (TT+, AQ+), top pair 9x (esp. Ad9d, Kd9d), sets (99, 55), and missed draws that called. No immediate straight on board, but flush is live threat. First to act with monster: betting for value is standard to extract from overpairs, weaker 9x/top pair, sets, and any stubborn draws. Checking risks giving free card to overcards or inducing bluff-raise, but primarily misses value. Pot bet (1150) too large given stack depths (my eff ~46BB, his deeper); 3/4 pot (800-900) balances value/protection while leaving room for river aggression if he floats/calls. History shows Claude calls flops but folds turns/rivers to pressure (e.g., Hand 13, 54). Bet 850 to target his medium strength.

The Jd brings a third diamond to the board, completing potential flush draws. I have just a pair of 5s with ace kicker - no diamonds. Elon is continuing to bet aggressively (850 into 1150). My hand is now very weak: I can't beat any made flush, any jack, or even likely a 9. The three-diamond board significantly devalues my holding, and I have no diamond draw to improve. Against Elon's continued aggression on this texture, folding is correct.
Pocket Kings in UTG - premium hand. Standard open raise to 250-300 for value. Want to build the pot while not scaring everyone away.

K2o is a very weak hand to play against an under-the-gun raise. My position is not great, and the hand lacks any significant post-flop potential or showdown value against a typical UTG opening range. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

A5s on the button is a strong candidate to 3-bet versus an UTG open: it blocks AA/AK/AQ, has good playability in position, and can make wheel straights/flushes when called. With our deep stack we can apply pressure to Claude’s opening range and often win the pot preflop, while still having position and equity when he continues. I’ll use a standard in-position sizing that discourages the blinds from coming along but keeps his continuing range wide enough to make the bluff profitable.

T9 suited is a speculative hand with good playability multiway due to straight and flush potential. However, facing an UTG raise from Claude (tight position, strong range) followed by a button 3-bet from the deep-stacked aggressive FiveTwo, who frequently 3-bets wide to isolate. I'm in SB (OOP postflop), stack 5700 (~57BB), pot odds to call 850 into 1325 are ~1.56:1 (needing ~39% equity heads-up, worse multiway with BB to act). Equity likely insufficient vs combined ranges, and postflop dynamics poor OOP vs aggression. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with J6 offsuit. The action involves a UTG raise to 2.75bb and a Button 3-bet to 9bb. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two potentially strong ranges. Facing a 3-bet cold, I have no pot odds or equity to justify continuing. This is a standard fold.

I have KK - second best starting hand. FiveTwo 3-bet to 900 after my open. This is a clear 4-bet spot. KK is too strong to just call and let them see a cheap flop. A 4-bet to around 2.5x their raise builds the pot with my premium hand and narrows their range. If they 5-bet all-in, I'm calling with Kings.

Claude’s 4-bet sizing (to 2400) gives us a very good price in position: we’re calling 1500 to win 3450, and we have a suited wheel ace that can flop nut flush draws/wheel draws and occasionally make strong hands that can stack his remaining 6900. With effective stacks only ~93bb and an SPR around 1.4 after calling, a 5-bet shove risks a lot for a relatively small pot and likely doesn’t generate enough fold equity versus a UTG 4-bet range that can call profitably. Calling keeps his bluffs in, lets us realize equity in position, and we can comfortably fold most flops we miss while jamming/calling on our strongest draws/values.

Excellent flop for my Kings. The Tc 2d Ts board is very favorable - FiveTwo is unlikely to have a ten after just calling my 4-bet (they would 5-bet TT). Their range is likely QQ, JJ, AK, maybe AQs. I'm crushing this range. With 9300 behind into a 4950 pot, I want to bet for value and set up getting stacks in. A c-bet around 2000-2500 lets me get value from underpairs and AK while setting up a turn shove.

We’re in a 4-bet pot versus an UTG range that’s heavily weighted to big pairs (and some AK). On Tc-2d-Ts our As5s is basically pure air: no meaningful draw (only a thin backdoor spade flush) and very few clean ways to improve (an Ace helps vs KK–JJ but is dead vs AA and still loses to some AK lines). 

With Claude betting 2200 and only ~4900 behind, the SPR is so low that if we call, we’re very often facing a turn jam where we can’t profitably continue without improving. A bluff-raise/shove also won’t work well because his range is strong and he’s getting excellent pot odds to continue with overpairs. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.

I am holding Ten-Four offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and high probability of being dominated. In a five-handed game, standard strategy dictates folding such marginal hands from early position to avoid getting into difficult spots post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We’re 5-handed but still effectively early position (UTG+1) and Q6 offsuit is too weak/dominated to profitably open or limp. Opening invites tough spots versus the blinds and gives Elon (short on the button) an easy reshove target; calling off would be bad and folding to a jam burns chips. Best EV is to pass and preserve our stack for better steal spots (CO/Button) and stronger holdings.

7s2c is one of the worst possible hands in Hold'em, with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Even on the button with two folds ahead, the standard play is to fold pre-flop. Raising as a steal is too loose here because 72o has abysmal equity against calling ranges from blinds (which include pairs, suited cards, broadways), and post-flop playability is nonexistent. SB (Pro) and BB (Claude) have deep stacks and have shown aggression in history. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are poor for this trash. Folding preserves stack for better spots; tournament phase is early (hand 76/150), no need to gamble.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold Q8o, which is a marginal but playable hand in a Blind vs Blind scenario. Given the stack depths (I have ~70BB, BB covers me) and being out of position, raising creates a bloated pot where I am at a disadvantage. However, folding is too tight given the 3:1 pot odds to complete. Limping (calling) allows me to see a flop cheaply and realize equity without exposing myself to large losses against the chip leader.

I'm in the BB with Ac5d facing a limp from Pro in the SB. Everyone else folded. I have the option to check and see a free flop, or raise to put pressure on Pro's limp. A5o is a decent hand but not strong enough to build a big pot. However, raising for value/isolation against a limper is standard. I'll raise to around 3x to put some pressure on Pro's weak limping range.

I limped from the Small Blind with Q8o, hoping to see a cheap flop. However, the Big Blind (Claude) has raised to 300. I am out of position against the chip leader who has shown aggression. Although the pot odds are roughly 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), Q8o plays poorly out of position in a raised pot and is often dominated. The reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make calling -EV. Folding is the disciplined decision here.

5-handed UTG is effectively a middle/late position spot, so we should open a reasonably wide range. 44 is strong enough to raise for both value and initiative: we can often win the blinds uncontested, and when called we have good implied odds to flop a set against opponents who can pay off (especially Claude’s 143bb stack). With several medium/shorter stacks behind, keeping the open size around 2.5bb applies pressure without inflating the pot too much if we face a 3-bet; we can comfortably fold to significant 3-bet aggression and proceed on favorable flops when called.

Qѕ7♥ is a weak offsuit hand with little connectivity or high card strength. Facing an UTG raise to 250 from FiveTwo's large stack (25k+), their range is strong (pairs, broadways, suited connectors). Calling OOP commits 250 into a 650 pot (~38% pot odds) but with poor equity, playability, and reverse position. Raising is suicidal with this hand. Stack at 57BB allows patience; fold to preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the Button with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). The Under-The-Gun (UTG) player, who is also the chip leader, has opened for a raise to 250 (2.5 BB). K9o is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard UTG opening range (which includes AK, KQ, KJ, KTs, and better pairs). Despite having position, calling here is unprofitable in the long run due to reverse implied odds and the strength of the opponent's range. 3-betting is too risky against the chip leader with this specific holding. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Premium hand KK in small blind facing a UTG raise to 250. This is an easy 3-bet for value. I want to build the pot and isolate. Standard sizing would be around 3x the open, so 750-850. Going with 850 to get value from hands like AK, QQ, JJ while pricing out speculative hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The action involves an UTG raise from FiveTwo to 250 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Claude) to 850. J6o is a very weak hand that does not play well in 3-bet pots, especially when the original raiser is still left to act and has a significant stack advantage. Calling 750 more into a 1200 pot with this hand is a losing play long-term. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Claude’s small blind 3-bet to 8.5bb gives us a very attractive price and, importantly, we’ll have position postflop. With 44 we’re not strong enough to 4-bet for value, and turning it into a bluff 4-bet isn’t great versus a SB 3-betting range that can continue often enough (plus we’d bloat a pot where we rarely realize equity unimproved). Calling 600 to win a pot that will be ~1800 gives immediate odds, and the effective stack (~12.65k behind after he 3-bets) provides solid implied odds to set-mine with an SPR around 7. Plan: proceed mostly fit-or-fold (continue strongly on sets; otherwise be disciplined vs c-bets on unfavorable textures, with occasional floats on very favorable low/dry boards where his range is capped/overcards-heavy).

I have KK as an overpair on 5c 8h Th. Good flop for my hand - no overcards. Board has a heart flush draw and some straight possibilities. FiveTwo flatted my 3-bet, could have TT/88/55 that hit sets, JJ/QQ that I beat, suited connectors, or broadways. I should c-bet for value and protection. Betting ~60% pot (around 1100) to extract value from pairs/draws while pricing out speculative hands.

We missed our set and have an underpair (44) on a fairly dynamic T-high, two-heart board that favors the 3-bettor’s range (overpairs, Tx, strong broadways). Claude’s small c-bet gives a close price, but our hand has very poor realization: many turns (A/K/Q/J/9/heart) either improve his overcards/draws or increase his barreling frequency, and we’re often forced to fold. With only two outs to a set and no real draw, this is a disciplined fit-or-fold spot—release and preserve stack for better runouts.

J♣8♥ is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity from UTG in a 5-handed tournament. Early position requires a tight range to avoid tough post-flop spots OOP with marginal holdings. Folding preserves my 57BB stack for better opportunities in this deep phase (hand 78/150). Opponents' stacks and histories show aggression, but this spot is standard fold.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively Hijack in 5-handed play) with Q8o. This is a weak hand that falls outside of a standard opening range from this position. With 70 big blinds, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better opportunities. The hand plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated. Folding is the standard and correct play.

On the button with 3d5d, a suited connector with some potential. Two players have folded, leaving only the blinds. This is a standard button steal spot - I have position and a hand with some playability if called. Raising to 250 to try to take down the blinds. If 3-bet, I can easily fold.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. Claude has raised from the Button to 250. While K7o has some equity against a wide Button opening range, playing it out of position against an aggressive opponent like Claude is difficult. Additionally, FiveTwo is in the Big Blind with a massive stack and a high likelihood of squeezing or making the post-flop play even more complicated. My current strategy is to maintain my stack for better opportunities where I have positional advantage or a stronger starting hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Claude is opening the button to 2.5bb and we’re getting decent immediate odds (150 to win 400), but J4o has very poor playability and realization out of position. When we make a jack we’re often dominated, and the hand doesn’t have suited/connected properties to compensate. A light 3-bet bluff is possible with our stack leverage, but J4o is too weak to choose as a bluff candidate versus a competent opponent who can defend/call in position. Best EV is to avoid marginal OOP spots and fold.

I am in UTG position with 4c3c, a low suited connector. With 5 players at the table and approximately 70 big blinds, this hand is too weak to open profitably from early position. The risk of being dominated or playing a bloated pot out of position against aggressive opponents (like the chip leader in the SB) outweighs the speculative value. The standard play is to fold.
Q3 suited is too weak to open from early position. Even with the flush potential, this hand doesn't have enough equity to profitably raise here. Folding is the correct play.

The action has folded to me on the button. While 8-7 offsuit is a marginal hand, it is a standard opening candidate for a steal attempt in a 5-handed game from the button. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to put pressure on FiveTwo and Elon in the blinds. This allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or play the hand with positional advantage if called.

Facing a button open, we’ll play a mostly 3-bet-or-fold strategy from the small blind because calling puts us out of position for the whole hand and often invites the BB in. Jh5d is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated top-pair potential, and not a great 3-bet bluff candidate (no suitedness/connectivity and only a mediocre blocker). Even with decent pot odds, the positional disadvantage and multiway/squeeze risk make this a clear negative-EV defend.

Facing a raise from the button with 10-4 offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a wide button opening range. My stack is 56BB, but folding trash here is standard to wait for better spots in a tournament setting with 71 hands remaining.
J9 suited in UTG is a marginal open, but the suited connectivity gives it decent playability. I'll make a standard raise to 250. If I face a 3-bet, I can fold without losing much.

I am holding T9o in UTG+1. Claude opened from UTG with a 2.5x raise. While T9o can be a playable hand in some late-position scenarios, calling an UTG raise from a five-handed table with this hand is generally losing in the long run, especially with a large stack like FiveTwo on the button and two medium stacks in the blinds who might squeeze. I will preserve my stack of 72 BB and wait for a more advantageous spot.

Claude’s UTG open in a 5-handed game is still a fairly strong range, and Q5o is dominated too often (Qx and better broadways) with poor postflop playability. Calling to “take a flop” invites tough multiway spots vs the blinds and puts us in reverse-implied-odds situations. A 3-bet bluff isn’t attractive here either given Claude has shown willingness to continue/4-bet in prior hands. Best EV is to release and preserve position/stack for better steal and 3-bet spots.

Preflop from Small Blind facing a UTG raise to 250 (2.5x BB) from Claude, a competent opponent. Holding Ad2s (offsuit ace with poor kicker), which has poor playability postflop out of position. Range for UTG open is strong (high pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc.), and my hand is dominated by most aces in that range and plays poorly OOP. Pot is 400, calling 200 gives ~3:1 odds, but implied odds are weak due to reverse domination and multiway potential with BB to act. With 55BB effective stack, no need to speculate; fold to preserve stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind facing a standard 2.5x open from UTG (Claude), who has been opening very frequently. Despite his wide range, Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very poor hand with low playability and reverse implied odds, especially out of position. The pot odds of roughly 27% are insufficient given that my equity realization will be low with this hand. It is a trivial fold.

In a 5-handed game, UTG is equivalent to the Hijack position in a full-ring game. Ace-Eight offsuit is a marginal but standard opening hand in this position. I have a healthy stack of 72.25 BBs, and while there are two large stacks to my left (FiveTwo and Claude), a standard open to 2.5x the big blind is appropriate to take control of the pot. If I face a 3-bet from the deep stacks, I can comfortably fold without compromising my tournament position, as A8o does not play well against a strong 3-betting range.

Facing an UTG open from Flash in a 5-handed lineup, 5♦3♦ is near the bottom of playable suited hands. It doesn’t block strong continues, performs poorly versus a tight UTG range, and calling invites squeezes from the players behind (especially with a shorter button stack who may jam/squeeze). With little upside and high reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs, the highest-EV choice is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

Preflop on the button facing a raise to 250 from Flash in UTG with K9o. UTG range is strong (typically 10-15%: pairs 77+, AJs+, KQs, AJo+, KQo etc.). K9o has a king blocker but is offsuit and dominated by better kings like AKo, KQo, KJs. Equity against this range is around 28-32%, but realizing it OOP for Flash while IP is tough postflop due to reverse implied odds and multiway potential with blinds behind. My stack is 55BB, effective stacks allow play, but folding marginal hands preserves chips for superior positions/spots. No read on Flash loosening up UTG from history; he raises standard sizes but gets action sometimes. Better to wait for stronger hands from position to maximize stack growth.

I hold AsJh in the Small Blind facing a raise to 250 from Flash in UTG. Flash has been active and raising frequently, suggesting a wider opening range than a typical tight UTG player, which increases the value of my AJo. However, playing out of position is a disadvantage. 3-betting would inflate the pot and isolate me against a stronger range, making post-flop play difficult if called. Calling allows me to realize my equity with good pot odds (getting 2:1 on the call) while keeping the opponent's range wide and the pot manageable. I will flat call.

K6s in BB facing a raise to 250 with one caller. I need to call 150 into a 600 pot (getting 4:1 odds). This is a reasonable spot to defend with a suited king getting such good pot odds. I can play postflop in position vs Flash and the hand has some playability.

On a monotone flop of 5h3hQh, I have the AsJh, giving me a strong flush draw (Jack-high) and an overcard. As the first to act in a multi-way pot (SB vs BB vs UTG), leading out is generally suboptimal because the board texture heavily favors ranges that contain flushes or nut flush draws (Ah/Kh), which the UTG opener or BB defender could easily have. Checking allows me to see the action from the pre-flop raiser and the BB. If they bet, I have sufficient equity to call and see a turn, but I don't want to bloat the pot out of position without a made hand.

I have K6s (spades) on a monotone heart board (5h 3h Qh). I have zero equity here - no pair, no hearts, no draws. Betting would be lighting money on fire since anyone with a heart or a queen isn't folding. Check and give up on this hand.

The flop came monotone with three hearts, and I do not have a heart in my hand. This is a very dangerous board texture for my Ace-Eight offsuit. Since I have no flush draw and only overcards, I have very little equity here against two opponents. Checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot size and see if the turn provides a safer card or if the betting action gives me more information about my opponents' hands.

On the turn, the board is 5h 3h Qh Kd. I hold As Jh, giving me a Jack-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (a Ten makes a straight). The pot is 750. The flop checked through, which usually indicates weakness, but the King on the turn favors the UTG opener's range (containing AK, KQ, KJ). As I am out of position against two opponents, leading out is risky because I might get raised by a made flush or a strong King, forcing me to fold my equity. Checking allows me to potentially see a free river or call a reasonable bet given my strong draws. My Ace-high also has some minimal showdown value if the river bricks and it checks down.

Turn brought Kd giving me top pair with a weak kicker on a monotone heart board. While I've improved, this board is extremely dangerous - any single heart gives someone a flush. Betting for value is problematic because: (1) flush hands will call/raise, (2) hands I beat like weak queens might fold. Checking for pot control makes sense - if someone bets, I'll have a decision, but I shouldn't build a pot on this scary texture with just one pair.

The turn card is the King of diamonds, adding another overcard to the board. Both the Small Blind (Pro) and Big Blind (Claude) have checked to me for a second time. While their repeated checking suggests weakness, the monotone heart texture makes this a dangerous board to bluff on, as any opponent with a single heart may be reluctant to fold. My Ace-high with an Eight of hearts blocker has some marginal showdown value and a very small chance to improve to the best hand on the river. Checking back allows me to see a free river card and keep the pot small, which is consistent with a pot-control strategy when holding a hand with limited equity and some showdown value.

I hold AsJh on a board of 5h 3h Qh Kd Qs. The board contains three hearts, but I only hold the Jh, meaning I missed my flush draw. I also missed my straight draw. My final hand is a Pair of Queens with an Ace-King-Jack kicker (playing the board's pair of Queens and King, plus my Ace). 

Given the action went check-check on both the flop and turn, my opponents likely do not have strong flushes or strong Kings. However, my hand (One Pair of Queens with top kicker) loses to any pocket pair 66 or higher (which would make Two Pair), any Kx, any 5x, or any 3x.
