I essentially only beat pure bluffs or weaker Ace-high hands. 

Betting serves little purpose as better hands (Two Pair, Trips, Flushes) will call or raise, and worse hands (busted draws) will fold. Checking gives me the best chance to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate if an opponent bets. Being out of position against two players, one of whom was the pre-flop raiser (UTG), caution is warranted.

River: Board is 5h 3h Qh Kd Qs. I have K6s giving me two pair (Kings and Queens). Everyone checked through on a monotone heart flop which is suspicious - either draws or trapping. But the board paired and no more hearts came. The fact everyone checked all streets suggests weak holdings or busted draws. My Kings up is likely best here. I should bet for thin value - around 350 to get called by worse kings, weaker queens, or medium pairs. Keep it small since Flash still acts behind me.

The board is 5h 3h Qh Kd Qs, with three hearts on the flop. After checking through the flop and turn, the Big Blind (Claude) leads for 350 into a 750 pot. My Ace-high (Ac 8h) has very little showdown value in a three-way pot, especially after both opponents have had multiple opportunities to act. While the 8h is a minor blocker to the flush, Claude's lead on the river after checks on previous streets often indicates a marginal made hand (like a King or a small pocket pair) or a slow-played Queen that has now improved to trips. Given that I have to beat both Claude's betting range and the Small Blind (Pro) who is still to act behind me, calling with only Ace-high is a losing proposition. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

The board runs out 5h 3h Qh Kd Qs. I hold As Jh. The board pairs Queens on the river. My hand strength is effectively the board's Pair of Queens with an Ace-King-Five kicker (using the board's K and 5 plus my Ace). I lose to any hand with a Pair (pocket 44+), any King, any Queen, any Five, any Three, or any Flush. Claude checked the flop and turn but leads out for 350 on the river after Flash folded. Betting into two opponents (before Flash folded) after checking two streets usually indicates thin value (like a 5x, 3x, or weak King) or a trapped monster, rather than a pure bluff. Since I only beat pure air or counterfeited pocket deuces, and getting ~24% pot odds isn't enough to justify calling with just Ace-high/Board-pair in this spot, I will fold.

5-handed UTG we’re effectively opening from early position and will be out of position versus most of the table. K3 suited is too dominated (especially by Kx opens/calls and 3-bets) and doesn’t realize equity well OOP, while the steal value isn’t great given multiple competent stacks behind who can 3-bet or defend. With a large stack, we can pass on marginal opens and preserve EV for better spots.

65dd is a speculative suited connector with straight and flush potential, but in UTG+1 position with multiple players behind (Pro on button, Claude in SB with deep stack, Flash in BB), it's too weak to open-raise or call the BB. Folding early position marginal hands preserves stack for better spots in this multi-table tournament phase. Implied odds insufficient OOP against unknown ranges.

Folded to me on the Button with JTo. This is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds. My hand is strong enough to play in position if called, and raising puts pressure on the SB and BB to defend. A standard size of 2.5x BB (250) is appropriate given the stack depths and table dynamics.

Holding As9c in the SB facing a button open from Pro. A9o is a solid hand to 3-bet here vs a likely wide button opening range. I have good stack depth at 160bb and this hand plays well as a 3-bet - blocks AA/AK combos and can flop well. 3-betting to around 850 puts pressure on Pro and takes initiative.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Claude) after a Button open (Pro), my Ace-Nine offsuit is in a difficult position. While the 3-betting range in a 5-handed game can be wide, A9o is often dominated by Claude's value range (AJ+, 99+) and doesn't play well against his aggressive tendencies post-flop. Calling would put me in a bloated pot, and 4-betting as a bluff is risky against a player who has shown high activity and aggression recently. Given my stack size of nearly 69bb, I prefer to find a better spot than defending with a marginal hand against a 3-bet.

I opened to 250 with JTo on the Button, which is standard. Claude in the Small Blind has 3-bet to 850. The price to call is 600 chips into a pot that will be around 1800, requiring about 33% equity. JTo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a reasonable 3-betting range (AT, AJ, KJ, QJ, KQ, TT+). While I have position, the reverse implied odds are significant if I hit top pair. Claude has shown aggression previously (e.g., Hand 77 with K9), but calling a 3-bet with offsuit broadway connectors is generally a losing play at this stack depth (approx. 65 BBs). I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand UTG in a 5-handed game. Standard play is to raise for value and to thin the field, as JJ performs well against a wide calling range but is vulnerable to AA/KK/QQ. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is a balanced size based on game dynamics and previous actions like Hand 41 where I raised JJ to 300 preflop. This builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against speculative hands. My stack of 5550 is deep enough to play postflop if needed.

I am holding 4c 3h in UTG+1 facing an UTG raise to 300 (3BB) from Elon. This hand is extremely weak (unsuited low connectors) and falls well outside of any reasonable calling or 3-betting range, especially against an early position open. My stack is 6425 (64BB), which is healthy, so there is no need to make speculative plays with garbage hands. The correct play is to fold immediately.

KTd on the button facing a 3x UTG open from Elon. This is a solid suited broadway hand with excellent position. Elon is relatively short-stacked at 5250. Calling is standard here - good implied odds with position and a hand that can make strong top pairs and flushes. Could 3-bet but flat calling keeps the pot manageable and retains position advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Elon raised from UTG and Claude called on the button. Q5o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot. The correct play here is to fold and wait for a better spot, as defending the small blind with this holding against an UTG raise is not profitable.

We’re getting a very good price in the big blind: 200 more to call with 7d6d in a pot that’s already 750, and we’ll go multiway with a hand that realizes equity well (suited connector, can make strong disguised hands). A squeeze 3-bet would bloat the pot out of position versus the button and risks running into a short-stack jam from Elon, reducing our implied odds and forcing us into tougher decisions with a marginal holding. Calling keeps ranges wide, preserves stack leverage, and takes the +EV pot odds.

Flop is 7s-6h-7c and we hold 7d6d, giving us sevens full of sixes (the effective nuts here since quads aren’t possible with the last 7d in our hand). With no flush draw and very little need to protect, the best way to maximize value multiway is to keep ranges wide and let the preflop raiser (Elon) or the button (Claude) continuation-bet/stab. Checkingaising immediately risks folding out overcards and medium pairs that might otherwise put money in. Plan: check with intent to check-raise a reasonable sizing versus a bet.

Pocket Jacks remain very strong on this flop of 7s-6h-7c. I have an overpair to the paired board, which hits some calling ranges for trips (77), but mostly weaker pairs, Ax high, or draws (straight draws like 45, 58, 89 for gutshots; no strong flush draw). FiveTwo's check from BB suggests weakness in a wide defending range. As preflop raiser UTG, a continuation bet is standard for value against Ax, KQ, lower pairs, and to charge draws/protect my hand. Claude on button can call wide but will fold worse often. Betting 2/3 pot (650) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity and keeping my range balanced. My stack depth (5250) supports postflop play.

I have KTd on a 7s 6h 7c paired board - complete air with just two overcards. Elon c-bet 650 into a pot of 950, which is a sizeable bet. I have no pair, no draw, and the board texture favors hands that connected (pocket pairs, 7x). Calling with just overcards on a paired board is not profitable, especially with FiveTwo still to act behind me. Easy fold.

We have sevens full of sixes on 7s-6h-7c with 7d6d, effectively the nuts versus Elon (only 77/66 beats us and both are impossible with the board + our hand). After Claude folds, we’re heads-up vs a short stack (Elon ~3950 behind after betting 650). The goal is to get his stack in while keeping his range as wide as possible (overpairs, 6x, 7x, and even some stabs). A small check-raise is best: it looks bluffy on a paired board, keeps in weaker hands, and also sets up an easy turn jam/induce shove with a low SPR. If he jams over, we snap-call.

Facing a check-raise from FiveTwo in the BB on a paired 7s-6h-7c flop after my continuation bet as UTG raiser. JJ is an overpair but the board heavily favors BB's defending range, which includes sets (77), two-pair (76), and possibly stronger overpairs (QQ+), trap hands, or combo draws. FiveTwo's history shows aggressive play with value hands, often check-raising or betting strong holdings (e.g., Hand 1 flop bet, Hand 41 massive bets on low board). My UTG raising range is strong, so their check-raise polarizes to nuts or bluffs, but bluffs less likely given position and board texture. Pot odds are ~4.5:1 (2900/650), requiring ~18% equity, but against value-heavy range, equity is <20% (crushed by 77/76, drawing thin vs overpairs). Effective stack 4600 is deep enough post-call (~3950 left into 3550 pot), but reverse implied odds are poor if behind. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 83/150 hands played; discipline > spew. Previous flop bet was standard, but fold to resistance.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with pocket sixes (6s, 6c). With a stack of over 64 big blinds, this hand is strong enough to open-raise for value and set-mining potential, as well as to steal the blinds. In a 5-handed game, UTG is effectively the Hijack position, where opening pairs 66+ is standard. My standard opening size in this tournament has been 2.5bb (250 chips), which balances my range and denies equity to random hands in the blinds. If I face significant aggression (like a large 3-bet), I can easily fold without losing much.
A4o is a marginal hand facing a UTG raise with 3 players behind me who could squeeze. This hand plays poorly multiway and is dominated by better aces. Easy fold in this spot.

I am on the button with Qc 4c. Pro has opened for 2.5x from UTG. While the hand is suited and I have positional advantage, Q4s is too weak to call a raise from early position, especially with a chip leader in the small blind and a short stack in the big blind who might shove. UTG ranges are typically strong, and my hand is likely dominated by Pro's range (QJ+, Ax, pocket pairs). Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Pro’s UTG open (5-handed) is still relatively strong, and K3o plays poorly out of position from the small blind with the big blind left to act. Calling needs decent equity and realizability, but we’re often dominated when we make a king and we invite the BB to come along, lowering our EV. A 3-bet bluff isn’t a great candidate with K3o (weak playability when called). Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Preflop in the big blind facing an UTG raise to 250 from Pro (6175 stack). Pot is 400, to call 150. My hand Jd3d is suited but weak with a low kicker and poor connectivity. UTG ranges are strong: pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways, some suited connectors. Equity vs that range is around 30%, but postflop OOP with shallow stacks (45bb), implied odds are limited, and playability is poor. Standard fold to avoid tough spots.
KTs is a solid UTG opening hand - suited broadway with good playability. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am in UTG+1 holding Jack-Ten offsuit. Claude has opened from under-the-gun for 2.5x the big blind. Claude has been playing very aggressively recently, showing a willingness to 3-bet and 4-bet frequently (as seen in Hands #75, #77, and #82). While JTo is a decent hand in short-handed play, it is often dominated by an UTG opening range which typically includes AJ, KJ, QJ, and pocket pairs. Calling here might also invite a squeeze from the button (FiveTwo, who has a massive stack) or the blinds. Given my stack size of 68BB and the tournament progress, I prefer to find better spots rather than playing a marginal hand out of position against an aggressive chip leader.

Claude opens from UTG 5-handed to 2.5bb; even if his range is somewhat active, 83o is near the bottom with poor playability, dominated top-pair potential, and little ability to realize equity postflop (plus blinds still to act). Not a good candidate to defend or 3-bet bluff here.

8c5s is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has a strong range, from out of position in the SB. Pot odds to call 200 into 400 are marginal at best (~3:1), but equity against Claude's UTG opening range is abysmal, likely <30%. No reason to speculate postflop OOP with this hand. Pro acts behind, could squeeze. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 2h, a very weak off-suit hand. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG. Although I only need to call 150 chips into a pot of 400 (getting decent pot odds), J2o plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive opener. The likelihood of realizing my equity is low, and I am dominated by most of Claude's range. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

Holding 75s UTG in a 5-handed game (effectively the Hijack position) is generally too marginal for an open, especially with two significantly larger and aggressive stacks (FiveTwo and Claude) still to act behind me. To preserve my stack and wait for higher-equity opportunities, I will fold.

92o from UTG+1 (5-handed) is far too weak to enter: it makes dominated one-pair hands, has poor playability, and performs terribly versus any 3-bet/jam pressure—especially with a short button stack who can reshove wide. No need to defend 1 BB here in a high-EV spot; preserve range strength and avoid negative implied odds.

Folded to me on the button with Qd6s, a speculative hand with queen blocker and straight potential. Button position is ideal for stealing blinds in an unopened pot. Pot odds favorable with 150 in pot for a raise. Standard play is to raise wide here to apply pressure on SB and BB, who will fold often. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is a good size to balance value and bluffs while maintaining fold equity. If called or re-raised, I have position to play post-flop effectively. Folding too tight; limping too passive.

I am in the Small Blind holding Qh 4d. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300 (3bb). My hand is Queen-Four offsuit, which is a very weak holding. Being out of position against the Button with a hand that has poor playability and is easily dominated is a losing proposition in the long run. The pot odds do not justify a call given the reverse implied odds and lack of equity realization. A 3-bet bluff is not suitable with this specific hand. The standard and correct play is to fold.

T9o in the BB facing a button raise of 300. Getting 2.25:1 odds to call 200 into a 450 pot. This is a standard defend with a playable suited connector-type hand. Elon is short-stacked (4150) and likely opening wide from the button. T9o has good playability postflop with straight potential. Calling is the correct play here.

Flopped middle pair (tens) on Th Qs Qc board. The paired queens are concerning - if Elon has a queen I'm crushed. My T9 is a medium-strength hand that plays well as a check. I can call a reasonable bet or check back if he checks. No need to build the pot with second pair weak kicker on a paired board.

Preflop raise from button with Qd6s was standard for fold equity and position. Flop Th Qs Qc gives me top set (trip queens with Qd), an extremely strong hand heads-up against Claude who defended wide from BB and checked. Paired board reduces straight draw possibilities (mainly J9), no obvious flush draws. Claude's check suggests weakness: air, marginal Ax/Tx, or weaker Qx/pairs. Value bet essential to extract from his calling range (worse Qx like QJ/QT, two-pair like QT/TQ unlikely, AT/AQ sets). Pot 650, bet ~2/3 pot (450) balances value/protection, induces calls from ~20-30% equity hands while denying equity to draws. Position allows control if check-raised. Deep stacks but my 4150 effective vs his 16000; build pot without overcommitting.

I have middle pair (tens) on a Th Qs Qc board. Elon c-bet 450 into 650. Getting about 2.4:1 odds. On this paired board, Elon could be c-betting with many unpaired hands (AK, AJ, etc.) or bluffing. If he has a Q or overpair I'm crushed, but second pair has decent showdown value here. Folding is too weak, raising accomplishes nothing (only called by better). Call and reassess turn.

Turn brought a blank 5c. I have middle pair (tens) on a QQT5 board. With To Call at 0, I'm first to act. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value - I only get called by better (Qx, overpairs). Checking to control pot size and see what Elon does is optimal. If he bets big, I can fold; if he checks back, I can try to get to showdown cheaply.

On the turn, the board is Th Qs Qc 5c. I hold Qd6s for top set of queens, the stone cold nuts given the paired queens on board—no higher full houses or quads likely without QQ. Claude checked flop after wide BB defense, called my 450 value bet into ~650 pot, now checks again on turn. This range is weak: AxTx, pocket pairs below Q, Qx worse than mine (QJ/QT), marginal two-pair like QT or Q5 unlikely. Turn 5c is relatively blank—completes no strong straights (possible J9 or KJ but narrow), adds club flush draw but board already has two clubs, so he likely wasn't drawing hard. His double check suggests no strong hand, but he'll call with top pair, overpairs, or draws. With pot 1550 and my 3700 stack (effective), a bet of ~2/3 pot (1050) extracts max value from his calling range (~25-35% equity hands), builds pot to ~3600, leaving me ~2650 behind for river flexibility. Overbet shove risks folding out one-street value; check risks free card to draws/improves. Position and history (Claude calls stations but folds to aggression) favor firing big value here to maximize stack in tournament mid-stages.

Facing 1050 into 1550 on QQT5 board with T9 (middle pair). Elon has bet both streets with increasing sizing. If I call, pot is 3650 and he has 2650 behind - essentially committing me. On a paired board, his double barrel suggests strength (Qx, overpairs). My T9 has showdown value but calling turn + likely river jam is too expensive with second pair. The 2/3 pot sizing on turn indicates he wants value or is setting up a shove. Folding is correct - I'm only beating bluffs and can't profitably call off against his value-heavy range.

5-handed UTG with AJs is a clear value open. As the chip leader, I want to apply steady pressure on the shorter stacks in the blinds/button while keeping my risk controlled versus a reshove. A 2.5bb open builds the pot, denies equity to random overcards/connectors, and still leaves me room to respond appropriately if a short stack jams.

72o is one of the worst possible starting hands with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. FiveTwo's UTG raise to 250 indicates a strong range (top 10-15% of hands). Calling or raising would be a massive mistake given my position (UTG+1), stack depth (52.5BB), and lack of playability. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this tournament phase where survival and selective aggression matter more than speculative plays.

I am holding J6 offsuit on the Button facing a raise from FiveTwo in UTG. J6o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is dominated by most of the opening range from UTG. Given my stack size of roughly 64 BBs, there is no need to get involved in a pot with marginal holdings against the chip leader. The standard and correct play here is to fold.

8c4c is a weak suited hand. Facing a UTG raise from FiveTwo (who has shown to be active), I'm out of position with a marginal holding. While suited connectors/gappers can be playable, 84s is too weak to defend here - poor high card value and the gap makes straight potential limited. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5s 3s. FiveTwo opened from UTG for 250. The Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This provides me with pot odds of approximately 3.67 to 1. While 53s is a weak hand and I will be out of position, the price is attractive enough to defend the big blind against a single raise, especially with a suited hand that has some playability on various flop textures.

I am in the Big Blind with bottom pair (threes) on a board of Qd-3d-Tc. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, FiveTwo, who raised from UTG. This board texture favors an UTG opening range, as it contains several broadway cards. My bottom pair is very weak and vulnerable. My standard play here is to check to the aggressor and evaluate their action. I do not have enough equity to lead out or check-raise.

Heads-up versus the BB after he checks, this Q♦T♣3♦ flop is fairly good for my UTG range (lots of strong Qx, overpairs, and broadways) even though it’s dynamic with draws. With A♣J♣ I have a gutshot to Broadway (K), two overcards to many BB holdings, and a backdoor club flush. A small c-bet pressures his wide defending range, denies equity to hands like random overcards/weak pairs, and sets up profitable barrels on K/A/club turns while keeping my risk controlled versus a check-raise (where I can comfortably fold).

I am in the Big Blind with bottom pair (threes) on a board of Qd-3d-Tc. FiveTwo, who raised pre-flop from UTG, has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550. This bet size (roughly 36% of the pot) gives me pot odds of 3.75 to 1, meaning I only need about 21% equity to make a profitable call. Although my hand is weak and the board favors the UTG range, folding bottom pair to such a small bet on the flop would be playing too exploitably. I will call to see a turn card, hoping to improve to trips or two-pair, or to see if the action slows down on a safe turn card.

I have improved to two pair (fives and threes) on the turn, but the board now shows three diamonds, making a flush possible. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo). Given the board texture and the potential for my opponent to have a flush or a higher two-pair/set (like QQ or TT from an UTG range), I will check to see how the opponent reacts and potentially control the size of the pot.

Turn brings the 5♦ putting a third diamond on board. With A♣J♣ I’m still just Ace-high with a gutshot to K, but this is a strong barreling card for my UTG range: I credibly have big one-pair hands with a diamond (AQ/KQ with a diamond, overpairs with a diamond) and strong diamond combos, while many of BB’s flop check-calls without a diamond (random Tx/3x/medium pairs/air) are now under significant pressure.

I want to keep the sizing small because (1) Flash is short and can check-jam, and I’m not interested in donating a big bet with only a gutshot, and (2) I still generate good fold equity on this texture even with a smaller bet. Plan: bet small and comfortably fold versus a raise/jam; if called, mostly shut down unless I improve or get a very favorable river/line.

I have improved to two pair (fives and threes) on the turn, which is a strong holding, but the presence of three diamonds on the board is a major concern as it makes any flush possible. FiveTwo, opening from UTG, has a range that includes high-value diamonds, sets (QQ, TT, 55, 33), and strong top-pair hands like AQ or KQ. However, FiveTwo's bet size on the turn (350 into 950) is relatively small, providing me with excellent pot odds of approximately 4.7 to 1. Given these odds, folding two pair is too conservative, especially since I have four outs to improve to a full house if I am currently behind a flush or a better two pair. I will call the bet to see the river and evaluate the final board texture, as raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

On the river, the board has paired with a 3h, making my final hand a full house (threes full of fives). This is a massive improvement and beats all flushes and straights. The only hands that beat me are pocket Queens (QQ) or pocket Tens (TT), which are possible but less likely than a flush or a single Queen in FiveTwo's UTG range. Since I have been check-calling the whole way, and the board has now paired (scaring off potential flushes from betting), I will lead out with a value bet. A bet of 1100 into a 1650 pot is large enough to get value from flushes or trips while looking like a potential bluff or missed draw to a suspicious opponent.

River pairs the 3 and Flash now leads 1100 into 2750 (~40% pot) after calling flop and turn on a board that went 2♦→3♦. Our AcJc is pure ace-high with no diamond, no pair, and no relevant blocker to his strongest value (flushes) or even many of his natural value bets (Qx/Tx/3x that improved to trips). Given he continued on the diamond turn, his range is heavily weighted toward hands that can comfortably call there (a diamond, a queen, or a made hand), and this sizing is consistent with value/protection. With essentially zero showdown value and no good bluff-catching properties, calling is -EV, and raising as a bluff is unlikely to generate enough folds versus a turn-calling range.

Best play is to let it go and preserve stack/ICM leverage versus the shorter stacks.

UTG position with J3 offsuit, a very weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Standard play is to fold preflop to avoid spewing chips early in the tournament. Multiplayer field, no position advantage, better to wait for premium hands to maximize stack long-term.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed) with Jd Th. The stack sizes behind me (Button and Blinds) are deep, particularly FiveTwo with 250 BBs and Claude with 155 BBs. With no antes in play, the incentive to steal the blinds is reduced compared to an ante game. JTo is a marginal hand that is often dominated by calling ranges (AJ, KJ, QJ, AT) and plays poorly against 3-bets, which are a real risk given the deep stacks and aggressive players behind. I previously raised this exact hand in Hand #82 and was 3-bet by Claude. Given the lack of dead money (antes) and the positional disadvantage against the deep-stacked Button, folding is the prudent, lower-variance play.

8h4s is a very weak hand. While I'm on the button with position, this hand is at the bottom of any reasonable stealing range. FiveTwo in the BB has a massive 25k stack and can defend wide. Not worth risking chips with such poor equity if called.

With the action folded to me in the Small Blind, I am holding T7 offsuit, which is a weak hand. While there is a potential to steal the blinds, FiveTwo (the Big Blind) is the chip leader with a massive stack and has shown willingness to defend and play aggressively. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand with a marginal holding against the chip leader is not an ideal situation for building my stack. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game with [Ad 3d]. While A3s is a playable hand and often included in GTO opening ranges from the Hijack (which corresponds to UTG 5-handed), it is on the lower end of the spectrum. Given the active and deep-stacked opponents behind me (specifically Claude and FiveTwo), and the fact that I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand if called, the conservative and lower-variance play is to fold. I have a healthy stack of ~64BB and can wait for better spots or better position to open speculative hands.
9h4s is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity. From UTG+1 (early position), this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such a weak holding.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the strongest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My position on the button is ideal. With both early players folded, the action is on me to build value against the blinds. A standard raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is designed to extract value from FiveTwo (the chip leader) or Elon (who is relatively short-stacked) while keeping their calling ranges wide enough to generate post-flop profit.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb button open with 64o and a short-stacked big blind behind who can squeeze/jam (especially if we 3-bet). Calling is unattractive because we’ll be out of position in a bloated pot with a dominated, low-equity hand; 3-bet bluffing is also poor given the risk of the BB reshove and the hand’s lack of playability. Best is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 (3x). My hand Qh6d is offsuit, poor playability, dominated by any Qx, and low equity against a wide button range which includes many better hands. Pot odds are 450 in pot, calling 200 into 650 (~23% needed), but equity vs typical button open (~30-40% hands) is around 25-30% at best, with bad postflop prospects due to reverse implied odds and poor suitedness/connectivity. Stacks are deep-ish (my 51bb), but folding trash preserves stack for better spots. No history suggesting Flash is super wide here. Standard fold.
8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position where I need to play tight. This hand has poor playability - not suited, poorly connected, no high cards. Easy fold.

A6o is a weak hand to open from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. While it has an Ace blocker, it plays poorly against 3-bets or when called by the chip leader (FiveTwo) on the button. Folding is the most disciplined choice here to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position.

We’re first in on the button with two blinds behind. Even though 7♥6♠ is offsuit and not a premium, the button is the best stealing spot: we have maximum positional advantage and strong fold equity versus two relatively short stacks who will often just fold their marginal holdings. A small open keeps risk low, pressures the blinds’ ranges, and lets us realize equity in position when called.

AKo is a premium hand, top of my 3-bet shoving range from SB. Button raiser after two folds likely has a wide range including suited connectors, Ax, broadways. 3-betting for value, isolation from BB, and fold equity. My stack is 51BB, effective with opponent. Standard sizing around 3.5x the open raise (250 * 3.5 ≈ 875), rounding to 900 to build pot while leaving room postflop. Calling is too passive OOP; folding is too tight.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo facing a Button open to 2.5bb and a Small Blind 3-bet to 9bb. This is a classic 'squeeze' scenario or a value 3-bet from the SB. My hand, Queen-Jack offsuit, is easily dominated by the ranges of both the opener and especially the 3-bettor (hands like AQ, KQ, AJ, JJ+). Additionally, the original raiser (FiveTwo) has a massive stack and is yet to act, creating a risk of a 4-bet. The pot odds of roughly 39% are not sufficient to justify a call with a hand that is likely dominated and often plays poorly in 3-bet pots. Preserving my 63bb stack is better than entering a high-variance spot with a marginal hand.

Elon’s small-blind 3-bet to 900 creates a low SPR if we call (effective ~42bb; after calling pot ~1900 and Elon has ~3350 behind). 7♥6♠ offsuit is too weak and poorly realized in 3-bet pots—dominated frequently and with limited playability when we miss. A 4-bet bluff would need a very high fold rate and risks running into a shove range that we can’t continue against. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold this marginal opener versus the 3-bet.

I am in the Under The Gun (UTG) position with a very weak hand, 3d 4c. This hand has almost no equity and is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Playing it from early position would be highly unprofitable as I have the entire table left to act behind me. Therefore, the most optimal and standard play is to fold.

5-handed and after UTG folds, we’re effectively in the cutoff with position on the blinds and only the button left to act. J8o is loose but playable here, especially with my big stack: I can apply pressure to the blinds (and a shorter-stacked button) and often win preflop or take advantage of position postflop. Limping gives up initiative and invites multiway pots; folding is a bit too tight given the table size. I’ll use a standard small open size to keep risk down while still pressuring the blinds.

Preflop on the button with A5o (offsuit), facing a raise to 250 (2.5BB) from FiveTwo in early position (UTG+1). My stack is 5500 (55BB), effective with FiveTwo's massive 24650 stack. A5o is a marginal but playable hand here: ace high with wheel straight potential (A-2-3-4-5), good blocker to AA/AK, and excellent postflop playability due to position. Pot is 400, calling 250 offers ~1.6:1 direct odds, plus strong implied odds heads-up or multiway. FiveTwo has raised frequently in history (e.g., Hands 7,14,15,19,25,28,31,33, etc.), often with speculative or strong hands, but button defense range includes A5o vs EP openers in modern play. SB (Pro) and BB (Claude) behind, but their stacks (6k/15k) and tendencies don't scream frequent squeezes. Folding is too tight; 3-betting polarizes too much with this hand (not great blocker/setup). Calling to realize equity in position and control pot postflop, aiming to stack build over remaining hands.

I hold AJo in the Small Blind. FiveTwo (UTG+1) is the active chip leader and raised to 250, followed by a flat call from Elon on the Button. While AJo is a strong candidate for a squeeze play (3-betting to ~1000-1100) to punish the wide opener and capped caller, FiveTwo's massive stack and frequent activity make him dangerous to engage in a bloated pot out of position without a premium hand. The price to call is very cheap (200 chips to win a pot of 650+), offering excellent direct pot odds. Calling keeps the variance lower and allows me to see a flop cheaply, although I must be cautious playing multi-way out of position. I will flat call to realize my equity.

I have T2o in the BB - a weak hand. However, I'm getting good pot odds: 150 to call into 850 (5.6:1). Three players already in. While T2o is marginal, the price is cheap and I might flop top pair occasionally. Calling to see a flop multiway.

I hold Ad Js on a Kh Qc 9s flop in a 4-way pot from the Small Blind. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and an overcard. The board texture is extremely wet and interacts heavily with the opponents' ranges (sets, two pairs, made straights with JT, strong top pairs). Being out of position against three players, leading out (donk betting) would be unwise as I have little fold equity and my hand is currently weak. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser and other players in position to see what develops. I am hoping to see a free turn card or face a very small bet, but I am prepared to fold to significant aggression given the coordinated nature of the board.

I have T2o on a KQ9 board with a gutshot straight draw (need a J). This board hits many hands in the preflop raiser's range. With just 4 outs to a straight and no pair, I should check and take a free card. No value in betting here.

Multiway on a very connected K-Q-9 flop, our J8 has only a gutshot to the T (plus thin backdoor club potential) and no showdown value. A c-bet will get called a lot by Kx/Qx, strong draws (JT, T8), and pairs, and we risk getting check-raised off our equity on a board that favors the callers’ continuing ranges. Taking the free card keeps the pot controlled against shorter stacks and lets us realize equity and potentially bluff/value bet on favorable turns (T, A, club, J/8 for added equity).

Multiway pot (4 players) on a dry but coordinated K-Q-9 rainbow flop, holding A-5 offsuit for pure ace-high, no pair, no straight or flush draw. All opponents checked to me in position. FiveTwo (original raiser) checking suggests possible trap or weak range, but board hits typical raising range hard (KQ, K9, Q9, sets). Betting air multiway risks getting raised by monsters or called by medium strength, poor EV with no fold equity guarantee. Ace-high has thin showdown value but loses to most pairs. Checking preserves stack (55BB effective), controls pot, realizes equity if ahead cheaply, and keeps options open on turn (e.g., if low card comes for wheel gutshot). Position allows seeing free cards or acting last later. Consistent with preflop plan of speculative call for postflop playability.

On the Turn, the board is [Kh Qc 9s 5c]. The action checked around on the Flop, suggesting no one has a monster or everyone is exercising pot control. I hold [Js Ad], giving me Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to a Ten. Being out of position in a 4-way pot, leading out is risky as better hands (pairs) will call and worse hands will fold. The 5c brings a flush draw, making semi-bluffing less attractive. My goal is to realize my equity (hit a Ten on the river) as cheaply as possible. I will check and evaluate if there is any betting behind.

I have T2o on KQ9-5 board with just a gutshot straight draw (need J). Turn 5c doesn't help. With no pair and only 4 outs, and 3 opponents in the pot, there's no value in betting. Check to see a free river.

We’re 4‑way on a K-Q-9 board where the callers’ ranges connect heavily (Kx/Qx/9x, JT, pair+draws). After checking flop, our J8 has essentially just a gutshot to the T (4 outs) and almost no showdown value, but a turn stab into multiple opponents is still likely to get looked up too often, and we risk getting check-raised off our equity. With the pot still small and everyone checked, the higher-EV line is to take the free river: realize our straight equity and keep the option to value bet if we hit a T or consider a bluff on a particularly good river card if it checks through again.

On the turn, the 5c pairs my 5d, giving me bottom pair (55) with Ah kicker in a 4-way pot of 1000. The board Kh-Qc-9s-5c is coordinated with straight draws (J10, 10-8, J8) and a completed club flush draw. All opponents checked flop and turn, suggesting caution or weakness, but FiveTwo's preflop raise range includes Kx, Qx, 99, Ax suited, etc., that could have value. My hand has showdown value vs bluffs/draws and thin value vs weaker Ax, overcards, or missed draws. Position allows betting for value/protection multiway without overcommitting my 5250 stack (5.25k effective). Checking risks giving free cards to draws or letting villains realize equity cheaply. Half-pot bet (500) targets calls from worse (A-high, gutshots, weak pairs) while denying equity, building pot with ~2:1 implied odds if called. Consistent with preflop speculative call plan; exploits passive lines post-turn card.

On the Turn, I face a 500 chip bet from Elon (Button) into a pot of 1000 after it was checked to him. I hold AsJs on a [Kh Qc 9s 5c] board, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (requiring a Ten) and one overcard. My direct pot odds are 3:1, requiring ~25% equity to call profitably. My gutshot provides 4 outs (~9% equity), and my Ace outs are thin and potentially dominated or not clean, totaling at best ~15% equity. Furthermore, I am out of position in the Small Blind and have two players (Claude and FiveTwo) left to act behind me who could raise or call. Without sufficient equity or position, calling is negative EV.

I have T2o on KQ9-5 with just a gutshot straight draw (need J for 4 outs). Elon bet 500 into 1000 pot. I'm getting 3:1 odds but need about 11% equity to call - with only ~8% to hit my gutshot, this is a losing call. No showdown value with T-high. Easy fold.

Elon’s 1/3-pot stab (500 into 1500) comes after a 4-way check-through flop on a K-Q-9 texture that heavily favors the callers’ continuing ranges. With Jc8h we have only a bare gutshot to the T (4 outs ≈ 8–9% on the river) and no flush draw. Calling 500 to win 2500 (20% required) is clearly -EV, and the implied odds are poor/reverse-implied because a T can still be dominated by JT/AJ and won’t always get paid. A bluff-raise would need very high fold equity, but his turn bet into multiple checked players is weighted toward Kx/Qx/strong draws that won’t fold often, and his ~47bb stack makes him more likely to continue. Best play is to let it go and preserve stack for better spots.

3h4s is an offsuit, disconnected low hand with poor high-card value and limited playability. From UTG (even 5-handed), opening or limping invites isolation from the blinds and plays poorly in bloated pots out of position. With a comfortable stack advantage, there’s no need to take thin, low-EV spots—better to preserve chips for higher-leverage steals with hands that can realize equity postflop.

Preflop, UTG+1 with A8o (Ah8c). FiveTwo folded UTG. Facing a small bet/to-call of 100 into 150 pot (great odds but multiway OOP risky). A8o is a strong enough hand for an early position raise: Ace-high blocker, straight potential, dominates weaker aces/limpers. Raising isolates potential limper(s), builds pot with strong hand, and takes initiative. Effective stacks ~60BB, no issue. Min raise 200 too small; size to 400 (4x BB + dead money) standard to discourage weak calls/multiway. Folding weak, calling OOP bad.

I hold 3d 6c on the Button, which is a very weak hand (unconnected, low, offsuit). Elon has raised to 400 from UTG+1, indicating a potentially strong range. Calling 400 chips (roughly 6.5% of my stack) with this hand is not profitable. There is no strategic merit in floating or 3-betting this specific hand against an early position open. The correct play is to fold immediately.

5c4c is a low suited connector facing an EP raise from Elon. While suited connectors can hit big, I'm out of position in the SB, and 54s is at the bottom of playable suited connectors. Elon's raise from UTG+1 suggests a stronger range. With my healthy stack of 15200, I don't need to take marginal spots OOP. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Ten suited, which is a strong hand in a 5-handed game. Elon raised to 400 (4x) from the Cutoff position. While his sizing is slightly larger than usual, my hand is too strong to fold and has great playability post-flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here because out-of-position 3-betting against a 4x open might bloat the pot unnecessarily and force me to fold to a shove, whereas calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity with a hand that flops broadway and flush draws well.

On this flop of Qh-Ts-6h, I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker. This is a very standard check-calling hand. Leading out would likely only get called by better hands (Queens) or draws that have significant equity against me. By checking, I allow Elon to continuation bet with his air or marginal holdings. Given the pre-flop action where he raised 4x, his range is somewhat polarized, but this board texture favors his range. I will check with the intention of calling a standard-sized bet to see a turn card.

Heads-up on flop Q♦ T♠ 6♥ after my UTG+1 preflop raise to 400, which Flash defended from BB. Board is drawy with straight possibilities (J9, K9, 98, J8), but rainbow suits limit flush draws. My A♥8♣ is pure ace-high bluff-catcher but represents a strong range including top pair (AQ, KQ, AJ), overpairs, sets (QQ/TT/66), and broadways. Flash checking first suggests weakness, likely floating or weak pairs/draws. As preflop aggressor with position, standard continuation bet to deny equity to his calling range (which includes more speculative hands) and take down the pot frequently. Small-medium sizing ~60% pot pressures floats without overcommitting my 58BB stack. Pot control via checking possible but betting maximizes fold equity and EV here.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker on a board of Queen-Ten-Six. Elon's bet of 500 into a pot of 850 is a standard continuation bet. My hand is far too strong to fold here, as it beats all of his air, underpairs, and straight draws like J9 or KJ. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Queens or better) and force out his bluffs. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play to keep his range wide and see a turn card, where I can reassess based on the board texture and his further action.

On the turn 8h, the board texture is becoming increasingly coordinated. My middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker remains a solid medium-strength hand, but it is not strong enough to lead out for value. By checking, I maintain pot control and allow Elon to continue his aggression with bluffs or semi-bluffs (like AK, AJ, or straight draws like J9). If he checks back, I am happy to see a free river and potentially realize my equity or reach a cheap showdown. If he bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but given the pot odds and the fact that I still have a gutshot straight draw (Jack) and middle pair, I am likely to call one more bet to see a river.

Heads-up on turn board 6♥ Q♦ T♠ 8♥ after my preflop raise and flop c-bet of 500 into ~1100 pot, called by BB Flash who checks again. Improved from ace-high to middle pair 8s with A kicker (beats weaker pairs like 86, T6, naked Q/T worse than AQ/AT). Board drawy with OESD possibilities (J9, 97) and now open-ended flush draw (hearts). Flash's twice-checking range likely weak pairs (99-77), draws (J9s, h-suited connectors), missed floats. As preflop aggressor with position, betting thin value + protection denies equity to ~30% of his range while extracting from worse made hands/draws. Pot 1850, effective ~5k; 1300 (~70% pot) balances value/fold equity without overbetting, preserves stack for river shoves if raised. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or induces bluffs but realizes equity too passively mid-late tournament when stack-building needed. History shows Flash calls light but folds to continued aggression often (e.g., Hand 1, 21). Bet to maximize EV.

On the turn 8h, the board texture has become more coordinated, but my middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker remains a solid bluff-catcher against Elon's aggressive range. Elon's bet of 1300 into a pot of 1850 is a strong barrel, but given his history of high aggression and multi-street betting (as seen in hands #36 and #41), he could easily be firing with semi-bluffs like KJ, AJ, or heart draws. I am getting approximately 2.4:1 on a call (needing about 29% equity). With a gutshot to a straight (Jacks), outs to trips (Tens), and potentially two pair (Kings), I have enough equity and showdown value to justify calling one more street. Folding here would be too exploitable against an aggressive opponent, and raising would only isolate me against hands that have me beat, such as Qx or sets.

On the river 2s, the board is 6h-Qd-Ts-8h-2s. I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker. This is a classic medium-strength hand that functions best as a bluff-catcher. The river is a complete brick, meaning no new draws arrived and the draws that were present (hearts, KJ, AJ) missed, with the exception of J9 which arrived on the turn. I am out of position against Elon, who has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. Leading into him here makes little sense as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands and might be forced to fold to a shove. By checking, I allow Elon the opportunity to check behind with his marginal hands (like a weaker Ten or a small pair) or to fire a third barrel with his missed draws (AK, AJ, KJ, or heart draws). My plan is to check and evaluate his action, though given the pot size and his remaining stack, a check-call is the most likely path if he bets, as my hand is near the top of my range for checking this line.

River board completes no obvious new draws (2s is a low brick, hearts flush draw missed as no third heart, spade flush incomplete). Heads-up pot 4450 after Flash calls flop and turn bets from BB, checking again shows likely capped range: weaker pairs (77-99, low Qx/Tx like QJ-Q9, T9), missed draws (J9, 97s, heart floats like AhXh, KhJh), bluffs. My A♥8♣ hit middle pair + A kicker on turn, beating all those weaker made hands and pure air. As consistent aggressor from my strong preflop range (top pairs, sets, overpairs), thin value bet extracts from worse while denying showdown to misses. Position allows control; pot ~1x stack (4050), so large sizing ~60% pot (2700) pressures calls/folds from better (unlikely post two calls) without overcommitting fully (leaves ~1350). Checking risks free showdown but misses value from calling range (~20-30% equity realization). History shows Flash defends wide but vulnerable to pressure (e.g., folds to river agg in similar spots). Bet to build stack late tournament.

On the river 2s, the board completes as 6h-Qd-Ts-8h-2s. This is a pure brick card that doesn't change the strength of any hands except potentially a very unlikely pocket deuces. I have middle pair (Tens) with the best possible kicker (King). Elon's line of 400 pre-flop, followed by a 500 flop bet, 1300 turn bet, and 2700 river bet represents significant strength, likely a Queen, a set, or a straight (J9). However, Elon has demonstrated a high level of aggression throughout the tournament (Hands #36, #41, #61), often firing multiple barrels. Many semi-bluffing hands in his range missed, such as KJ, AJ, AK, and various heart draws. I am getting roughly 2.65:1 odds on the call, meaning I only need to be right about 27% of the time. Given the board texture and Elon's tendency to over-bluff or barrel wide, my hand is a strong candidate for a bluff-catch. Folding would be too exploitable given my history of folding to his river aggression (Hand #61).

UTG with 13.5BB effective stack in a 5-handed tournament spot (hand 93/150). AJoffsuit is a premium hand here, top ~10% and strong blockers. Short stack dynamics shift to push/fold strategy per Nash equilibria; AJ is a clear shove from UTG 5-handed at ~13BB to maximize fold equity against Pro (next to act, 60BB) and blinds who fold wide. Small raise risks multiway pots or being squeezed; limp is weak. Shove builds huge pot if called (good equity ~35%+ vs calling range AQo+/TT+), or steals blinds to ladder stack. History shows aggressive play, opponents capable of folding.

I hold Ten of Clubs and Five of Hearts, which is a statistically weak hand (T5 offsuit). Faced with an UTG raise to 1350 from Elon (who appears to be all-in given his stack is listed as 0), I am getting pot odds that do not justify calling with this holding. My hand has poor equity against a typical UTG shoving range, and there are still three players left to act behind me who could wake up with a strong hand. Committing over 20% of my stack with T5o is extremely negative EV. Therefore, the only correct play is to fold.

Elon has made a very large open raise to 1350 (13.5BB). Q6o is a weak hand that doesn't play well against a tight raising range. Even with button position, calling this large raise with Q6o is -EV. Easy fold.

Facing a raise to 13.5 big blinds from UTG by an aggressive player like Elon, King-Eight offsuit is a clear fold from the Small Blind. K8o is significantly behind a standard UTG opening range and playing out of position for over 10% of my stack with a marginal hand is not conducive to maximizing my stack size in this tournament.

We’re in the big blind with 6♦3♦ facing a very large UTG open to 1350 (13.5bb) after everyone folds. This sizing heavily weights toward strong hands and leaves us poor implied odds with a dominated, low suited hand. Calling 1250 more would be -EV, and 3-betting is lighting money on fire versus an UTG range at this size. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with 7c2c, which is a very weak starting hand (often considered one of the worst). Despite being suited, it has poor playability and connectivity. With a 60BB stack and the entire table yet to act, there is no justification for opening the pot or limping. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips for better spots.
5d 9h is a very weak offsuit hand in UTG+1. No playability, easy fold from early position.

I am on the button with 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I have positional advantage, the big blind (Elon) is very short-stacked with only 1,400 chips (14 BBs), making him highly likely to shove over a steal attempt. Additionally, the small blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folds to us in the SB, so this is a prime steal spot heads-up. Even with a marginal hand like T7o, we should be opening very wide from the small blind, especially versus a very short-stacked BB (14bb) where a raise applies maximum pressure, often wins immediately, and denies equity when he just calls. Using a standard small open keeps our risk low while still setting up good pot odds if he jams wide.

Short stack with 14BB in BB facing a min-raise from the SB, who has been aggressive in history. J9s suited connectors have excellent playability, straight/flush potential, and ~35-40% equity vs a typical SB stealing range. Calling 150 leaves me with 12.5BB effective postflop OOP, which is awkward and low EV due to difficult SPR situations. Shoving all-in maximizes fold equity against a wide range while denying equity realization if called. FiveTwo's massive stack allows a call but doesn't change that this is a high-EV spot to jam given position and hand strength. Folding is too tight preflop.

We opened very wide from the SB and the BB has effectively jammed to 1400, giving us strong pot odds: calling 1150 to win 2800 total means we only need ~41% equity. Given Elon’s recent short-stack shove behavior and typical blind-vs-blind reshove ranges being quite wide (many Ax, Kx, broadways, suited hands, and pairs), T7o is near the bottom but still has enough raw equity often enough to justify the call with these odds and our large stack. Taking the spot to potentially bust the short stack is also valuable.

Pot is already 2800 and Elon has only 100 behind, so the remaining SPR is effectively ~0. Any bet we make just forces in his last 100 with essentially no fold equity (he’ll be getting massive odds), and we don’t have a hand that benefits from “protection” on this 5h8h3h board (we’re just T-high with no heart). Checking is best to (1) let him punt his last 100 when he decides to bluff, and (2) avoid turning our hand into a needless bet when we’re calling any tiny jam anyway due to pot odds.

Facing a monotone heart flop 5h-8h-3h, holding Jc9c (jack-high, no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw). FiveTwo (SB, huge stack) checked OOP after calling my preflop short-stack shove. Pot is 2800, my remaining stack is 100 (tiny 3.6% of pot). Pure air but amazing pot odds if called. Villain's check could represent weakness, missed draws, or monster trap, but fold equity exists against bluffs or weak pairs/draws wary of the board. Checking risks losing to any showdown value villain has and no further action. Shoving maximizes fold equity and realizes equity if called (live cards like Ax, broadways). With near-all-in effective stack and tournament goal to build stack, aggression is +EV here despite thin equity.

Elon has effectively put his last 100 chips in on the flop. We’re calling 100 to win a pot of 2900, needing only ~3.3% equity. Even with pure T-high and no heart on a monotone board, we still have far more than that (we can pair a T or 7 by the river, and sometimes even win against complete air that missed). There’s no strategic value in folding for 100 here; we just take the price and try to realize our equity to finish the side/all-in pot.
AJo is a strong opening hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. Standard play is to open raise here. I'll raise to 2.5x BB (250) as I've been doing throughout this tournament.

I am holding 7-5 offsuit in UTG+1. Claude raised to 250 from UTG. 7-5 offsuit is a very weak hand and does not justify a call or a 3-bet against an early position opening range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play in this situation.

Claude’s 2.5x UTG open is likely fairly wide 5-handed, and on the button I can realize equity well with T9o at a good price. I don’t want to 3-bet here because the blinds are short (especially Elon/Pro) and can jam over the top, forcing me to fold and lighting chips on fire; plus T9o plays better as a position call than as a bloated-pot bluff versus an opponent capable of 4-betting. I’ll flat and play postflop in position, folding if a blind squeezes all-in.

Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250 (2.5BB) and a button call from the deep-stacked FiveTwo, my J3s suited connector is too weak to call 200 more from the SB out of position. Pot odds are ~3.25:1 (~23% equity needed immediately), but multiway OOP with Pro yet to act, post-flop playability is poor against likely strong ranges. With ~30BB stack, better to fold and wait for stronger spots to maximize tournament life and stack growth.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc9s facing a standard 2.5x open from UTG (Claude) and a flat call from the Button (FiveTwo). The pot is currently 650, and I need to call 150 more to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of 150/800 = 18.75%. Although J9o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position against two players, the price is extremely efficient. I do not need to win the hand often to break even on this call. I will call to close the action and evaluate the flop, playing straightforwardly fit-or-fold.

I have flopped middle pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker on a board of 9h-4h-Tc. While this is a reasonable hand, I am out of position in a multi-way pot against the UTG opener (Claude) and the Button caller (FiveTwo). The board texture is wet with straight and flush draws available. Leading out (donk betting) would be unwise as I would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, and I would deny myself the opportunity to control the pot size. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If there is a bet, I will evaluate based on the sizing and the action of the other player, but for now, checking allows me to see how the opponents proceed without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I have AhJd on 9h4hTc. I flopped the nut flush draw with Ah plus two overcards and a gutshot to the nuts (Q gives me broadway). This is a strong semi-bluff spot. Pro checked, so I should c-bet to build the pot with my draws and potentially take it down now. Betting around 50% pot.

We flop top two pair (T9) on a fairly dynamic board with a heart flush draw and multiple straight/backdoor possibilities. Claude’s small 1/3-pot c-bet multiway is consistent with a wide range (overpairs, Tx, 9x, heart draws, some bluffs), and there’s a player behind who can realize equity cheaply if I just call. Raising here builds a pot against worse made hands and charges the many draws while still keeping in hands like overpairs/Tx/strong draws given the small initial sizing. I’ll use a value/protection raise that’s not so huge that it folds out everything worse but makes continuing with draws expensive.

I hold Middle Pair (Jc9s) on a wet board of 9h-4h-Tc. After checking, the UTG player c-bet 400, and the Button raised significantly to 1600. This raise represents a very strong range, likely including sets (44, 99), two pair (T9s), or powerful combo draws. My middle pair is well behind the value range of the raiser and is currently drawing dead or very thin against sets and two pairs. Furthermore, the original bettor (UTG) is still behind me and could hold an overpair or top pair. Calling 1600 represents a significant portion of my remaining stack (~27%) with a marginal hand in a multi-way spot facing heavy aggression. The prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I have Ah for nut flush draw (9 outs) + gutshot to broadway (3 more outs with Q) + overcard outs. That's roughly 12-15 outs giving me ~45-50% equity. I need to call 1200 into a pot of 2800, requiring only ~30% equity. This is a clear call with my draw. Shoving could be considered but calling keeps my options open and I have good implied odds if I hit.

The 4d paired the board on the turn. I still have the nut flush draw (9 outs) and gutshot broadway (3 additional queens). That's ~12 outs giving me roughly 24% equity on the river. FiveTwo check-raised me big on the flop, showing strength - likely a set, two pair, or strong draw. Now I can check for a free card. No need to bet into someone who showed aggression - if they have a set, the board pairing could have given them a full house. I'll take the free card and hope to hit my flush or straight on the river.

Claude’s flop c-bet/call vs my raise keeps in a lot of one-pair hands (overpairs, Tx, some 9x) plus heart/straight draws. The turn pairing the 4 changes some combos (A4s/54s improve to trips), but it also gives him incentive to check a wide range, and there are still plenty of rivers that either beat me (hearts/overcards completing straights) or kill my action. With T9 I’m still ahead of most of his continuing range, so I want to bet for value while charging draws, but not so big that I polarize myself into “4x or air” and fold out the exact overpairs/Tx I’m targeting. A solid ~65% pot size sets up a clean river shove on many runouts and keeps his bluff-raise frequency in check.

I have Ah for the nut flush draw (9 outs = ~19% equity). Pot is 6600, need to call 2600 - requiring ~28% equity for direct odds. I'm not getting the right price. The board is now paired (4-4), meaning if FiveTwo has a set of 9s or Ts, they could fill up and beat my flush even if I hit. FiveTwo's aggressive line (check-raise flop, bet turn) suggests a strong made hand. Implied odds exist but are diminished by the paired board risk. At hand 95/150 with a decent stack, I shouldn't chase a draw at bad odds when villain is showing significant strength.

I am dealt 8-3 offsuit in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and is virtually unplayable, especially from early position with the entire table yet to act. Given the stack sizes and the presence of aggressive players behind me, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand. Folding is the standard and most prudent play.

We’re 5-handed with a premium hand (QQ) in an early position after UTG folds. This is a clear value open: we want to build the pot vs dominated pairs/broadways and also apply pressure to the shorter stacks behind (notably the Button). A standard sizing keeps our range balanced and invites worse hands to continue while still denying cheap realization. I’ll open to 3x.

My hand is 3d5c, a very weak suited connector with poor playability and low equity against an early position raise from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands. From the button with only ~30BB effective stack (2950), calling risks too much of my stack into a pot where SB and BB can still act, potentially isolating or facing a 3-bet. Pot odds are not sufficient (calling 300 into 450), and folding preserves stack for better spots in late tournament phase (hand 96/150) to maximize EV.

I hold [5s Td] in the Small Blind. FiveTwo has opened to 300 (3BB) from UTG+1. My hand (Ten-Five offsuit) is extremely weak with poor playability and equity against an opening range. Furthermore, I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a large stack aggressor. The pot odds do not justify a call with this specific holding. This is a trivial fold.

5-9 offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the BB. FiveTwo has been active and I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. Even getting 2.25:1 pot odds, this hand plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have good implied odds. Easy fold.

5-handed and first in, QJh is a strong suited broadway that plays very well in raised pots (good connectivity, strong top-pair potential, and can make nut/near-nut draws). Limping invites multiway action and reduces fold equity; folding is too tight at this depth. I’ll open-raise to pressure the blinds and isolate the shorter stacks, using a solid but not bloated sizing that still discourages loose calls.

7c6d is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and no high card strength. Facing an UTG raise to 250 (2.5x BB) from deep-stacked FiveTwo (25k stack), who plays aggressively but has a strong early position range here. Pot 400, to call 250 is ~20% pot odds, but equity vs their range is low (~25-30% max), and postflop OOP in early position with 29.5BB stack. Tournament stage (hand 97/150), preserve stack for better spots closer to bubble/pay jumps. Standard fold to avoid spew.

I am on the Button with K2s facing a standard 2.5x open from the UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader. K2s is a weak suited King that is easily dominated by a UTG opening range (which likely includes AK, KQ, KJ, KTs). Although I have position, calling invites the blinds to enter, creating a multi-way pot where my hand plays poorly. 3-betting is risky against the chip leader who can easily call or 4-bet. Conserving my 58BB stack for better opportunities is the prudent play.

6h8s is a weak hand in the small blind facing an UTG raise with Flash still to act behind me. This hand lacks the strength to call profitably out of position, especially against an early position raiser. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Ten offsuit. The chip leader, FiveTwo, has opened from UTG with a standard 2.5x raise to 250. With 150 more to call into a pot of 400, I am getting approximately 2.6:1 odds, which is more than enough to defend this hand in a 5-handed environment. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, QTo has decent playability and equity against a wide opening range. I will call to see the flop.

The flop is 8c 5s 8d, giving me overcards on a paired board. In a heads-up pot where I called from the Big Blind, checking to the original raiser (FiveTwo) is standard procedure. My hand has some showdown value against complete bluffs and can potentially improve to a strong top pair or trip eights on later streets. I will check and see how FiveTwo responds, as this board likely missed most of their opening range as well.

Paired, dry flop (8-5-8 rainbow) heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range: we have lots of strong 8x/overpairs while BB has many unpaired hands that hate continuing. With QhJh we have two overcards plus some backdoor potential, and we benefit a lot from immediate fold equity. I’ll make a small, range-style c-bet to fold out random overcards, small pairs, and weak floats while keeping the pot controlled if called.

FiveTwo's small continuation bet of 200 into 550 (roughly 36% pot) on a dry, paired board of 8-5-8 is a standard move for the pre-flop aggressor to take the pot down immediately. My hand, Queen-Ten offsuit, provides two overcards and some showdown value if the board runs out in my favor. I am receiving approximately 3.75:1 odds on a call, requiring only about 21% equity to continue profitably. Given that this board likely missed a significant portion of FiveTwo's UTG opening range and my hand retains equity against his bluffs and some of his lower-value holdings, calling to see a turn is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play. Raising would unnecessarily bloat the pot against the chip leader, and folding is too tight given the price.

The turn card is a Queen, giving me top pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker on a paired board (8-8-5-Q). This is a significantly improved hand. I previously called a small continuation bet on the flop with overcards. Now that I have hit my hand, my goal is to maximize value without over-committing against the tournament chip leader, FiveTwo. Checking is the most strategic move here for several reasons: it allows FiveTwo to continue bluffing with hands that missed the board (like AK or AJ), it provides pot control if FiveTwo is holding a better Queen (like AQ or KQ) or an Eight, and it disguises the strength of my hand. If FiveTwo checks back, I can safely evaluate the river. If he bets, I have a clear call given the strength of my top pair.

Turn gives us top pair (Q) on a paired board (8-5-8-Q). After Flash calls the small flop c-bet, their range still contains a lot of underpairs (66–TT), some 5x, and a portion of floats/broadways that now picked up a queen or will fold. We have a clear range advantage as the preflop raiser and want to (1) get value from worse one-pair hands/underpairs, (2) deny equity to hands like AK/AJ/KJ/JT, while (3) keeping the pot controlled so we’re not bloating it into the part of Flash’s range that is 8x. A small, pressure/value bet accomplishes this and allows a disciplined fold if we face a raise.

On the turn, I've improved to top pair with a Queen. The board is paired with eights (8-5-8-Q), which means any eight in my opponent's range has me crushed. However, FiveTwo's bet of 350 into a 950 pot is relatively small (roughly 37% pot), which often indicates a continuation bet with a wide range, including air, smaller pocket pairs, or a Queen with a weaker kicker. Raising here would likely only be called by hands that beat me (like an 8, AQ, or KQ) and would force out the bluffs I want him to continue with. By calling, I maintain pot control against the chip leader and keep his bluffing range wide for the river. I am receiving nearly 3.7:1 odds, making this a clear call to see the final card.

The river card is an Ace, which is a very coordinated card for my opponent FiveTwo's UTG opening range (containing hands like AK, AQ, AJ, and AT). My pair of Queens, which was top pair on the turn, has now been demoted to second pair. There is no value in betting here, as I would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten (Aces, better Queens, or an Eight). Checking is the best option to realize my showdown value. If FiveTwo checks behind, I may win against smaller pocket pairs or missed draws. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the size of the bet relative to the pot and the likelihood of a bluff.

River brings the Ace, which is a key range-shifter: it improves a chunk of Flash’s flop/turn calling range (Ax floats, A5) into a better pair than our queens, while the hands we’d like to get value from (underpairs 66–TT, most 5x) are now more likely to bluff-catch less or just fold to a bet because the Ace is a scary overcard. If we bet, we risk getting called mostly by hands that beat us (Ax) or chopping with Qx, and we open ourselves up to a check-raise from trips/full houses (8x/55). With solid showdown value (QQ88A), checking back realizes equity and avoids turning a medium-strength hand into a thin/reverse-value bet.

Pocket sixes is a premium hand from UTG, roughly top 6% of hands. With a 30BB stack in a tournament, we raise for value to build the pot, gain fold equity against loose callers, and play post-flop with initiative. Standard open-raise size is 3x the BB (300 total) given no prior action. Limping risks multi-way pots where sets are vulnerable; folding is too tight. Opponents like FiveTwo show aggression but calling tendencies, so raising thins the field. Effective SPR will be manageable.

I am holding T9o in UTG+1 facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from the UTG player (Elon). T9o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by an UTG opening range. Additionally, I have players left to act behind me, and calling would put me in a vulnerable position. With a stack of roughly 58BB, there is no incentive to get involved with a marginal offsuit connector against an early position raise. Folding is the standard and correct play.
Q6o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise from Elon. Even with button position, this hand plays poorly post-flop and has little equity against a typical UTG opening range. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Elon, who is relatively short-stacked (2,650 chips), has opened from UTG for 300. While the price to call is only 250 more, K8o is a weak hand to play out of position against a UTG opening range. Additionally, FiveTwo in the Big Blind is the chip leader and has been very active; calling here invites a squeeze or puts me in a difficult spot post-flop with a dominated hand. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament (hand 98/150), I will prioritize preservation and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Elon is opening UTG 5-handed with only ~26.5bb, so his range is relatively strong and he can respond aggressively (including jamming) versus a 3-bet. Tc3o is too weak and dominated to defend profitably here despite the decent price (200 to win 450), and it has poor playability postflop. Best is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with 5d 7s, which is an extremely weak hand (unsuited one-gapper, low rank). With 57 BBs, I have no reason to get involved with trash hands from early position. The standard play is to fold immediately.
Q3o is a weak offsuit hand in UTG+1 position. This is not a hand worth opening from early position - it plays poorly postflop and has limited value. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Q6o. While the button is a prime stealing position, Q6o is at the very bottom of any reasonable opening range. Looking at the players in the blinds, Elon is short-stacked with 3,000 chips (30 BBs) and has shown a tendency for aggressive play, making him likely to shove over a button raise. FiveTwo in the small blind has a massive chip lead and is playing very active. Given the risk of a 3-bet or a shove from the blinds, folding this weak hand is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folded to me in the SB heads-up versus a short-stacked BB (30bb). 7♢6♣ has reasonable playability, but raising risks inducing a profitable jam from Elon that I can’t call with this hand. Completing keeps the pot small, realizes equity, and lets me outplay postflop without committing to a big preflop decision.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind limp from FiveTwo, who has a massive stack. My hand is 3h4c, a very weak offsuit connector with minimal playability. Raising would require bluffing with a hand that performs poorly postflop, and with only 30BB effective stack, I prefer to check and realize my equity on the flop for free. Position allows control postflop, and the pot odds are excellent (already invested 100, seeing flop heads-up). Folding isn't necessary since it's free. Standard check to induce bluffs or hit improvement.

Limped pot, heads-up, and this K-J-7 rainbow board is high-card heavy: BB has lots of air/overcards that will fold, while my 7x can get thin value/protection versus random hands and worse pairs. I also don’t want to build a big pot versus a short stack and then face an awkward jam; by using the minimum stab I can bet-fold comfortably if Elon shoves, while still picking up the pot a lot and denying equity when called.

Facing a flop bet of 100 into 300 from FiveTwo, who limped preflop from SB and has a massive stack (250x BB). My 3h4c has zero equity: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot would need 5, but board is K-J-7 rainbow-ish with one heart), no flush draw. Board favors big card hands like Kx, QJ, suited connectors that hit. FiveTwo's small bet could be weak (probe) or thin value, but calling OOP with trash commits 10% of my 30BB stack into a pot where I have no fold equity later and poor playability. Raising is a pure bluff with a hand that blocks nothing useful and performs terribly if called. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in late tournament with 52 hands left; limped into a favorable board for villain's range.
7c5d is a weak offsuit hand from UTG position. This is not in my opening range - it's not suited, poorly connected, and has no high card value. Easy fold from early position.

I am dealt [2h, 3h] in UTG+1. In a 5-handed game, this is one of the weakest possible hands to play. Even though it is suited, its high-card value is non-existent, and it realizes equity poorly. With several players left to act behind me, including a short stack in Elon and a massive stack in FiveTwo, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folded to us on the button with a big stack: this is a profitable steal spot versus blinds, even with a marginal holding like 65o, because we have position and strong immediate fold equity. I’ll use a small-but-standard open to pressure the blinds while keeping my risk controlled; if either blind comes back with significant aggression (3-bet or shove, especially from the short SB), this hand is an easy fold.

Facing a button raise from aggressive deep-stacked FiveTwo (251BB), with my 98s suited connectors in SB (30BB effective). Early position folds increase steal likelihood. 98s has excellent playability for straights/flushes, good blockers, and is a prime 3-bet bluff candidate OOP for fold equity (~50-60% vs wide BTN range). Calling 200 OOP risks multiway pot with Pro BB behind; folding too tight late tournament. 3-bet to 800 (3.2x) balances value bluffs, applies pressure on deep stack. If called/4-bet, good equity; if folds, build stack with 50 hands left.

I am holding 2d 5h in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I am facing a raise from the Button (FiveTwo) and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Elon). Cold calling a 3-bet with 5-high offsuit is negative EV. The stack depth (57 BB) does not justify getting involved in a bloated pot with trash cards out of position. The only correct move is to fold.

We opened very light (65o) on the button as a steal. The small blind has now 3-bet to 800 with only 2,200 total, creating a very low SPR if we continue. Calling 550 gives us position but we’ll often be forced into committing on the flop with a weak hand versus a range that is typically pairs/aces/broadways, and 4-bet shoving won’t generate much fold equity because Elon would be calling only 1,400 more into a pot giving great odds. This is exactly the type of ‘short-stack 3-bet aggression’ spot where we should just abandon the steal and preserve stack.

I am holding Ten of Clubs and Trey of Diamonds in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated by almost any range my opponents will be playing. In a five-handed game, T3o is an easy fold from UTG to preserve my stack for better spots.

5-handed but still effectively early position (UTG+1) with three players left to act including the Button. Td4d is too weak to open-raise profitably from here and doesn’t realize equity well multiway; limping just invites isolation and puts us in tough spots out of position. With deep stack, we don’t need to take thin steals with this holding. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

On the button with J6 suited after two folds, this is a standard raising spot.
