Excellent position to realize equity postflop, potential for straights/flushes, and good fold equity against blinds who may not defend wide. Raising to 2.5x the BB (250 total) balances stealing blinds and playing a playable hand in position. My 33BB stack supports this aggression without overcommitting.

I hold 9s 3h in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (trash). Facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Elon), my equity is poor, and I would be playing out of position. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding. A 3-bet bluff is not suitable here given the hand strength and my stack depth. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips.

75o is a weak hand. Even getting 2.67:1 pot odds from the BB, this hand plays poorly postflop and has limited potential. Elon's short stack makes it harder to realize equity. Folding is the disciplined play.

5-handed from UTG we should play a fairly tight range. Tc4c is too weak and dominated when we make a ten, and it has poor realization multiway against the blinds’ defending ranges. With a very short stack behind (Elon 3500) who can reshove, opening this hand creates unnecessary variance and puts us in awkward spots out of position. Best is to preserve our strong chip position and fold.

J7o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability, especially from early position (UTG+1). Facing a to-call of 100 into a 150 pot, the pot odds aren't compelling enough to speculate with such a weak range. Opponents have deep stacks, and folding preserves my 35BB stack for better spots in a tournament with 48 hands left. Standard fold to avoid tough postflop situations out of position.

I am on the Button with 8d 3s, which is an extremely weak hand (offsuit, disconnected, low rank). Although I have position, opening with such poor holdings against two active blinds with large stacks (Claude and Flash) is negative EV. There is no strategic merit in trying to steal here given the hand quality. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

T5o is a weak hand, but folded to me in the SB heads up against the BB. I can either fold for 50 chips, complete, or raise to steal. Completing is cheap at 50 more chips and lets me see a flop. With this marginal hand, completing is reasonable - no need to bloat the pot with a raise or give up my small blind entirely.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 2c. The action has folded to the Small Blind (Claude), who simply completed. With a very weak hand like 9-2 offsuit, there is no value in raising. I will take the free flop by checking.

I have T5o on a Jc4hQs board - completely missed. I only have ten-high and a gutshot straight draw (need a K). Flash checked, giving me a free opportunity. With such weak holdings, there's no reason to bluff here. I'll check and see a free turn.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 2c in a limped pot. The flop is Jc 4h Qs, which does not hit my range at all. Claude (Small Blind) checked. Given that I have no equity and no showdown value, but it is a limped pot and I am offered a free look at the turn, I will check behind. There is no reason to bluff into a dry board with zero backup when I can see another card for free.

I hit bottom pair with my 5 on the turn. Flash has checked twice showing weakness. With a small pot of 200, a small bet around half pot could get value from worse or take it down. If Flash check-raises, I can fold. Worth betting for thin value/protection against overcards.

I have 9-high with no draws on a board that I haven't connected with at all. Claude has bet into me on the turn after a checked flop. While the bet is small (100 into a 300 pot), I have no equity to continue and no reason to try to float or bluff with zero backup. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

UTG with 5h8d offsuit, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and no suited potential. Standard play from early position is an immediate fold to avoid playing out of position post-flop with such a weak range. My stack is 35BB, and there are deep-stacked opponents behind; limping or raising would be exploitable and -EV. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this late stage (hand 103/150).

I am in UTG+1 with Qd 8c, which is a weak off-suit hand. With three players yet to act, including the deep-stacked chip leader in the Big Blind, opening this hand is a losing play. It is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop out of position or even in position against calling ranges. My stack of 56 BB allows me to wait for better spots. The correct action is to fold.

As3h on the button with both UTG and UTG+1 folded. This is a good stealing opportunity with an ace-high hand. I'll make a standard button raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds. Against just Flash and FiveTwo in the blinds, this hand plays well as a steal attempt.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-3 suited. While this hand has some speculative post-flop value, I am facing a raise from Claude on the Button. If I call, I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against the Button and potentially the Big Blind (FiveTwo), who is the current chip leader and has been quite active. Given that I have a healthy stack of 125 big blinds and there are more favorable spots to exploit, folding this weak suited gapper is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary chip loss out of position.

Claude is opening the button to 2.5x and we’re getting a very good price in the big blind (150 to win 400). Even with T6o, versus a wide BTN opening range we have enough raw equity and can realize it reasonably often by playing straightforward postflop (check most flops, continue on favorable textures/pairs, avoid bloating pots when dominated). This hand isn’t a good 3-bet candidate out of position due to poor playability and domination risk, so we take the cheap defend.

Flop Qh-8s-3s heavily favors the BTN raiser’s range (lots of Qx, overpairs, strong broadways), while we have no pair and no meaningful immediate draw (only a very thin backdoor spade possibility). Leading here is unnecessary and risks building a pot out of position with a low-equity hand. Best is to check our range, let Claude continuation-bet his value/bluffs, and respond based on sizing and future runouts.

I flopped bottom pair (3s) with an ace kicker and a backdoor spade draw. FiveTwo checked to me. This is a standard c-bet spot - I have the initiative, position, and some equity. A small c-bet of about 50% pot should either take it down or get value from worse/draws.

We’re facing a small c-bet, but with T6o on Qh-8s-3s we have no pair, no gutshot, and not even a backdoor flush (no spade). Our equity versus BTN’s c-betting range is too low to justify continuing, and out of position our ability to realize equity or generate profitable bluffs on later streets is poor on this texture that strongly favors his range. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Deuce suited (Ts 2s). This is a very weak hand with poor playability and connectivity. Being first to act with 4 opponents behind makes playing this hand extremely risky and unprofitable in the long run. My stack is roughly 56 BBs, so I have plenty of time to wait for better opportunities. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
J4s is a weak hand from UTG+1 position. While suited, the hand has poor playability with a large gap between cards. With 4 players still to act behind me, this is a clear fold in early position.

I am on the button with 6d 2d, a weak suited hand. My stack is 12,500, which is healthy, but this hand has very little equity against typical opening ranges. However, everyone has folded to me on the button. While I could attempt a steal, the small blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader with nearly 25k and has been quite active/aggressive. The big blind (Elon) is very short-stacked with only 3,400, meaning he is likely to defend wide or jam over any raise. Given the high probability of being called or shoved on by the blinds and the overall weakness of 62s, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.

Folded to me in the SB versus a short BB (34bb). AhTh is a strong suited ace that benefits from taking initiative and building a pot against many worse aces/broadways the BB will defend or even jam. I’ll open-raise rather than complete to deny equity, put pressure on the BB’s wide range, and set up an easy decision versus a shove (we’ll often have sufficient equity to call).

Pocket eights is a premium hand in the big blind facing an isolation raise from the small blind, who often has a wide range here including many hands worse than 88, Ax, broadway, suited connectors. My stack is 34BB, providing fold equity with a 3-bet while committing reasonably if called. SB has a massive stack (24k+), but history shows FiveTwo raises frequently but folds to resistance sometimes (e.g., multiple folds to 3-bets or post-flop aggression). 3-betting to 900 (3x the raise) builds a pot when ahead, denies equity to worse hands, and positions me well for post-flop play if called. Calling risks playing out of position multi-street OOP with a vulnerable pair, and folding is too tight given equity and pot odds (3:1). Shoving is unnecessary overplay with fold equity already strong.

Elon is very short (2600) and has 3-bet to 900, leaving only 1700 behind. With that stack depth, a shove from me won’t generate much fold equity because he’ll be getting excellent pot odds (needs ~33% to call) and will likely continue very wide. By flat-calling, I take a good immediate price (600 to win 1800 = 33%) with a strong, playable suited ace, keep in any bluffs/overplays, and importantly retain the option to fold on the worst flops instead of committing preflop at a higher breakeven equity. SPR will be <1, so the postflop plan is straightforward versus a jam.

We’re heads-up vs a 17bb 3-bettor with an SPR < 1, and this K-high flop connects strongly with Elon’s short-stack 3-bet range (AK/KQ, overpairs, some Kx). With AhTh we currently have mostly Ace-high with only backdoor hearts and limited clean outs when he’s on a king. Shoving or betting just commits money with essentially no fold equity because he’s priced in; the profitable plan is to check and respond: fold to a jam (we won’t have the ~33% equity often enough), and if he checks back we realize our equity and can improve on later streets.

Pocket 88 remains a strong overpair to the 9 and 3 on this K-9-3 flop with two diamonds. As the preflop 3-bettor, my range is perceived as strong (AK, KK, QQ-JJ, etc.), and FiveTwo's OOP call preflop and check here suggest possible medium strength like Kx without diamond, Ax floats, flush draws (Adx, QdJd etc.), or smaller pairs. 88 has good equity (~45-55% vs calling range) and blocks his sets. With SPR ~1:1 (1700 left into 1800 pot), checking risks free cards to 6 over cards, diamond flush draws, or straight draws turning good. Betting 2/3 pot (1200) extracts value from worse (KJ, QJ, TT-77, draws), protects against draws, and leverages fold equity against air/floats given his history of folding to aggression postflop (e.g., Hands 1,5,19,25,44,55,73,87). If he check-raises, we can reassess but likely ahead; flat call, barrel turn. Short stack (26BB) demands aggression to build without spew. Pot committed anyway on later streets.

Elon’s short-stack 3-bet range can still contain plenty of non-made hands (AQ/AJ/QJ/JT-type) that will c-bet this K-high board, and even versus his value hands we’re not drawing dead: we can improve with an Ace (sometimes clean vs KQ/QQ+), a Ten for second pair vs pure overcards, and we retain backdoor hearts. With the pot at 3000 and 1200 to call, we’re getting about 28–29% pot odds, which is realistic against his overall betting range. Raising/shoving doesn’t generate fold equity versus a short stack who’s already put money in and will call off wide, so the best line is to call and reassess turns (continuing on improving cards, and often folding on bricks if he jams and our equity is clearly insufficient).

Turn brings the 3rd diamond, which is a very good card for Elon’s c-bet/short-stack range (it adds equity to his overcards with a diamond and gives him more semi-bluff candidates), while it does almost nothing for AhTh (no diamond, no pair, no meaningful draw). With Elon only 1400 behind into a 4200 pot, any lead from us has essentially zero fold equity—he’s priced in to call off extremely wide—so betting would mostly just burn chips with ace-high. Best is to check and realize whatever equity we have; if Elon shoves, we can fold because our outs are too thin/dirty versus his Kx/pairs/diamond hands.

Pocket 88 remains an overpair on this Kd-9d-3h-4d board, ahead of most of FiveTwo's check-call range (KQ-KJ, Ax suited diamonds that didn't hit flush, 77-TT, combo draws like T5dd or 52dd that bricked straight). The turn 4d completes diamond flushes (AdXd, QdJd, etc.), but my 8d blocks some (e.g., A8d, 98d unlikely), and FiveTwo's double check suggests weakness or trap rather than monster (sets/flushes might check-raise flop). With SPR ~0.33 (1400 into 4200), checking risks giving free river cards to overcards (Q,J,T,A,K missed draws) or villain improving; pot control unnecessary OOP with thin value. Shoving 1400 (~1/3 pot) extracts max value from worse (top pair no diamond, stubborn draws), leverages fold equity against floats/bluffs given FiveTwo's fold history to aggression (e.g., Hands 1,7,13,19,21,25,44,55,59,65,73,87), and denies equity. Called by better ~20-30% time (flush/set), but equity realization strong shortstacked; checking worse as villain reps strength OOP. Tournament late (hand 104/150), stack ~14BB demands aggression to ladder up vs deep SB.

Elon is effectively all-in for 1400 into a 5600 pot, so calling 1400 wins a final pot of 7000 and requires only ~20% equity.

Even though this turn (3rd diamond) is bad for AhTh (no pair, no diamond, no real draw), a short-stack 3-bettor can still jam this sizing with a meaningful chunk of hands that we currently beat: AQ/AJ-type overcards (often without a diamond), and some one-diamond semibluffs that want maximum fold equity while denying us a free river. Against those, we’re ahead right now and realize very high equity. When he’s value-heavy (Kx/overpairs/two-diamond flushes), our equity is poor, but the price is good enough that we don’t need him to be bluffing very often to justify a call.

Given his aggressive tendencies and the very favorable pot odds, calling is the best chip-EV decision.

I am holding 93 offsuit in the Under the Gun (UTG) position in a 4-handed game. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the fact that there are three players left to act, including the chip leader FiveTwo on the button, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

With Flash folded, this is a button steal spot versus blinds. Even though K2o is weak, position plus significant stack leverage (I cover both blinds) makes an open profitable: we win the pot immediately a good % of the time and can realize equity in position when called. I’ll use a standard small-to-medium open size to apply pressure without risking much if either blind 3-bets (easy fold versus significant aggression, especially from the shorter SB).

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 6h, a very weak off-suit hand. Facing a standard 2.5bb open-raise from the Button (FiveTwo), who has a large stack and position on me. Playing this hand out of position is expected to be negative EV. With a stack of roughly 56 big blinds, I have plenty of time to wait for better spots. Folding is the only reasonable option here.

J7o in the BB facing a button min-raise from FiveTwo. Getting 400:150 or ~2.67:1 pot odds. FiveTwo has been active throughout the tournament with wide opening ranges from late position. J7o is marginal but the pot odds justify a call to see a flop rather than folding or 3-betting with this hand. Will play carefully post-flop given position disadvantage.

Flop: Qd 8d Td - three diamonds. I have Jh 7d, giving me a flush draw with the 7d plus a gutshot straight draw (need a 9 for Q-J-T-9-8). That's roughly 12 outs. However, my flush would be very weak (7-high flush). I'm out of position against FiveTwo who raised preflop. Best play is to check and see what FiveTwo does - I can call a reasonable bet with my draws, or take a free card if checked behind.

Monotone Qd-8d-Td is a very high-connectivity flop that interacts strongly with BB’s defending range (lots of suited hands/diamonds, Qx/Tx/8x, and combo hands). With Kh2s and no diamond, I have essentially no equity and a c-bet will get called (or check-raised) too often to be profitable. In position, the best EV line is to check back, keep the pot small, and look for better turn cards to represent (paired board, A/K/J non-diamond) or to pick up real equity.

The turn brought the 9d, putting four diamonds on board. I have 7d giving me a 7-high flush. I also have a Jack-high straight (Q-J-T-9-8). However, my flush is extremely weak - any diamond higher than 7 (Ad, Kd, Jd, 6d-no wait, even 6d loses to my 7d) beats me. Actually Jd or higher beats me. Given FiveTwo's wide preflop range from button, there's a significant chance they hold a diamond. Betting for value would be risky as I'd only get called by better hands. Checking to control pot size and potentially induce a bluff or see a cheap showdown is optimal.

Turn brings the 4th diamond, making this an excellent scare card to attack Claude’s range after he checks twice. With Kh2s and no diamond I have essentially zero showdown value, so checking back just resigns the pot a lot. As the preflop raiser on the button, I credibly represent having a diamond (AdX/KdX, suited broadways) and can fold out a large portion of his non-diamond holdings (one-pair Qx/Tx/8x, some Jx, random floats). I’ll use a medium sizing that pressures those hands while keeping risk controlled and folding comfortably if check-raised.

I have 7d giving me a made flush (Qd-Td-9d-8d-7d), plus the Jack-high straight. The flush is the better hand but it's weak - any opponent with Ad, Kd, or Jd has me beat. FiveTwo's small bet of 325 into 550 (59% pot) gives me ~2.7:1 odds. Given the small sizing, calling is correct here - I have a made hand but it's too weak to raise for value, and folding a flush is too tight. I'll call and re-evaluate on the river.

River 5c is a blank. I have 7-high flush (Q-T-9-8-7 diamonds) which beats all non-flush hands but loses to any higher diamond (Jd, Kd, Ad). FiveTwo checked to me. Betting for value is risky - I only get called by better flushes. Checking back secures showdown value against straights, two pairs, and smaller flushes. No need to turn my made hand into a bluff or bloat the pot against a likely better flush.

Claude’s turn call on the 4-diamond board heavily weights him toward having at least one diamond (i.e., a made flush), and the river 5c doesn’t change the texture in a way that meaningfully increases my fold equity. With Kh2s I have no diamond blockers (Ad/Kd/Jd), so this is one of the worst hands to try to blast off with—he can show up with plenty of flushes and will call too often. Best EV is to give up and take whatever tiny chance K-high is good versus the rare non-diamond peel.

4-handed UTG (first to act) still needs a reasonably strong opening range, especially with two players in the blinds who can 3-bet and a short stack on the button who can reshove. T5o is too weak and dominated to open or limp profitably; it makes poor top pairs and has limited playability. Preserve stack and wait for better pressure spots versus the shorter stacks.

With 4 players remaining, play loosens up significantly. Position is key. I am on the Button with A5o. It is folded to me. My stack is roughly 56 BB, which is healthy but the shortest at the table. A5o is a standard opening hand from the Button in a short-handed game due to the Ace blocker and position. I will raise to 2.5x BB to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds.

J9o in the small blind facing a button open of 250. This is a marginal hand but playable given the price (200 to call into a 400 pot). I'm getting decent odds and J9 can flop well with straights and pairs. Folding is too tight here, and 3-betting OOP with this hand isn't ideal. Calling to see a flop is the best play.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a 4-handed game. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Claude) has called. I only need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 750. Given the excellent pot odds and the strength of my hand relative to a typical button opening range and a small blind flatting range, calling is a highly profitable play that closes pre-flop action.

Flopped a gutshot straight draw (need a T) with J9o on Q83 two-spade board. No flush draw, no pair. Checking is standard here - I have some equity with the gutshot but not enough to bet for value, and this isn't a great bluffing spot. Will check and see what develops.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on a 3s-Qs-8d board. This is a very strong hand in a three-way pot. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is on the button, and the small blind (Claude) has already checked. In this situation, the standard play is to check to the raiser. This allows Pro to make a continuation bet with his entire range, including bluffs and semi-bluffs (like spade draws), which keeps the pot growing while I am likely ahead. If I bet now, I might force out the hands I dominate or his bluffs. I will check and plan to evaluate my next move based on Pro's action.

I hold Ah 5s on a board of 3s Qs 8d. It is a multi-way pot with 3 players. Both opponents checked to me. I have Ace-high showdown value but no immediate draw (only backdoor spades and backdoor straight). The board is wet with a flush draw present. Betting into two opponents on this texture with just Ace-high is risky as I am likely to be called by any pair (Qx, 8x, 3x) or flush draw, and folding out better hands is unlikely. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card, and keep the pot small. It is the standard play for a marginal made hand/showdown value in position multi-way.

Turn pairs the board with 8h. I still only have a gutshot straight draw (need a T). With two opponents who could easily have a queen or an 8, betting here as a bluff is risky. Checking to see a free river is the best play - if I hit my straight I can extract value, if not I minimize losses.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a board that has now paired the eights. The flop was checked through by all three players, which suggests that the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) likely does not have a monster hand. Claude's checks indicate a marginal hand or a draw. Since I likely have the best hand, I will lead out on the turn to extract value from weaker Queens (like QJ or QT), pocket pairs, or draws (specifically the spade flush draw). Checking again risks giving a free card to a hand that could overtake me on the river. A bet of roughly half the pot (400 chips) is a standard value bet in this situation.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 8h, making it 3s Qs 8d 8h. The Big Blind (Flash) leads out for 400 into a pot of 750 after the flop checked through. This lead often indicates a made hand (trips with an 8, a Queen) or a semi-bluff with a draw. With Ah 5s, I only have Ace-high. I am behind any pair (3x, Qx, 8x, pocket pairs) and currently losing to all value hands. While I might be ahead of some pure bluffs or draws, the presence of the Small Blind (Claude) yet to act behind me makes floating risky. I have the 5s which blocks some spade flush draw combinations Flash could be bluffing with, slightly increasing the likelihood they have value. Given I am drawing very thin against value and face a bet into multiple opponents, folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack.

Flash bets 400 into a pot of 750 on a paired board. I only have a gutshot straight draw (need a T for J-high straight). Getting about 2.9:1 odds but only have ~8% equity. Need about 25% equity to call profitably. Clear fold - not enough odds to chase the gutshot, and Flash likely has a queen or better.

Current hand is 107/150, 4-handed play. I am in UTG (also acts as Cutoff in 4-handed) with a stack of 53BB. My hand is Qc9s (off-suit). While ranges widen in 4-handed games, Q9o is still a marginal hand to open from first position. It is easily dominated by hands like KQ, AQ, QJ, A9, and plays poorly post-flop against calling ranges. Furthermore, the Big Blind has a massive stack (277BB) and can apply significant pressure. It is safer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot.
Q9o on the button after UTG folds. This is a standard open from the button - decent hand with position. I'll raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds or play postflop with position.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of Fives (5s 5h). The Button (Claude) has opened for 250. This is a standard opening range from the Button. While I could call to set-mine, 3-betting out of the SB is a stronger play to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop, especially against a wide Button opening range. If called, I have a clear plan on most flops, and if the Big Blind (FiveTwo) gets involved, I can evaluate based on their action. I'll raise to 850 to exert pressure and define the hand.

We’re facing a BTN open and an SB 3-bet, and we’ll be out of position against at least the 3-bettor. 8♦3♦ is too weak to defend here: the direct price (750 to win 1950-ish) requires far more equity than we’ll have versus a typical SB 3-betting range, and the hand has poor playability/dominance issues when we make a pair. A 4-bet bluff would need an unrealistically high fold rate and exposes us to an easy shove from Flash’s stack size. With a large stack, the best EV/variance tradeoff is to simply let it go.

Q9o facing a 3-bet from the SB. While I have position and decent pot odds (600 to win 1200), Q9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range. Flash's 3-bet likely includes strong aces, pairs, and broadway hands that dominate Q9. Folding is the disciplined play here rather than calling with a dominated hand.

J4 suited from UTG is too weak to open. While suited, the hand has poor playability with a weak kicker and gap. From early position with 3 players to act behind me, I should fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with K3o. While it is a 4-handed game and button opening ranges are typically wide, K3o is at the very bottom of that range. FiveTwo in the Small Blind is the chip leader and has shown aggression, and Pro in the Big Blind is short-stacked, making them more likely to defend or shove. To preserve my stack for better opportunities in the final 42 hands of the tournament, I will fold this hand.

Folded to me in the SB versus a shorter-stacked BB. With AhAs I want to start building a pot immediately and play for stacks; raising also denies BB a free flop with two cards and keeps my range strong in this spot. A solid SB open size should get called by many worse hands and can induce a reshove from BB’s 52bb stack that I happily snap.

I am in the Big Blind with T2o facing a large raise to 450 (4.5BB) from the Small Blind. The large sizing gives me poor pot odds (requiring ~39% equity) compared to a standard raise. T2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Against a massive chip leader who is applying pressure, there is no strategic merit in defending this hand. I fold.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. While the ranges should be wider than in a full-ring game, 9-8 offsuit is still significantly below the standard opening threshold. With a stack of over 130 big blinds and 41 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to play such a weak hand from out of position, especially with a chip leader like FiveTwo sitting on the button. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Button vs blinds, Ks6o is a profitable open in 4-handed play: we have position, strong steal incentive, and we cover both blinds (especially the short SB), creating good fold equity and postflop leverage. Limping gives the BB a free realize and invites multiway pots where K6o performs poorly. I’ll raise small to keep risk controlled while still pressuring their ranges; if we face a strong 3-bet we can comfortably fold.

I hold a low pocket pair (4c 4s) in the Small Blind. The Button (FiveTwo), who has a large stack and likely a wide opening range, has raised to 2.5bb. Folding is too tight given the pair's potential. 3-betting is risky as it inflates the pot out of position with a hand that plays poorly post-flop unless it hits a set. Calling is the standard play here: it gives me a good price (pot odds) to see a flop, hoping to hit a set (set mining). My stack of ~52bb allows sufficient implied odds for set mining against the deep-stacked opener.

AJc is a premium hand in the BB. FiveTwo raised to 250 from button and Pro flatted from SB. With suited broadway cards, I should 3-bet here to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop. A 3-bet to around 850 (roughly 3.4x the open) is standard sizing to isolate and punish the button's wide opening range.

We opened K6o as a wide button steal, but the BB’s 3-bet to 850 with the SB already calling is a classic squeeze spot that heavily weights Claude toward strong, high-card hands and pairs. K6o performs very poorly versus that range (domination issues, weak playability), and calling also risks Pro’s short stack back-jamming, putting us in an awkward spot with a marginal hand. With plenty of stack and better steal spots available, the highest-EV play is to release and preserve our edge.

I previously flatted the button raise to set-mine, but the Big Blind has squeezed to 850. The initial raiser (FiveTwo) has folded. I now face a call of 600 more into a pot of 1350. While I have decent direct pot odds (~31%), pocket fours play very poorly post-flop out of position in a 3-bet pot unless I hit a set. To profitably set-mine, I typically need effective stacks to be 15-20 times the calling amount to justify the times I miss. Here, my remaining stack is ~4375, which is only about 7.3x the calling amount (4375/600). This ratio is far too low to justify calling solely for set value, and I cannot effectively realize my equity without improving. Shoving 50bb with 44 is too loose against a squeeze range. The disciplined play is to fold.

4-handed from UTG, pocket 4s is comfortably inside an opening range. Raising rather than limping keeps initiative, attacks the blinds, and lets us potentially win preflop or play a smaller SPR pot with position disadvantage minimized by initiative. I’ll use a standard 2.5x sizing to apply pressure and reduce multiway flops while not risking too much against a 3-bet.

I am on the Button with 8c 4c facing a raise to 250 from the UTG player (FiveTwo). My stack is roughly 50 big blinds. While suited gappers have some potential, 8-4 suited is generally too weak to profitably call an UTG open, even in position. The risk of domination or missing the flop completely is high, and calling invites the blinds to squeeze. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I have QQ in the small blind - a premium hand. FiveTwo opened to 250 from UTG. With pocket queens, I should 3-bet for value. FiveTwo has been active and has a big stack. A 3-bet to around 850 is appropriate to build the pot while giving them a chance to make a mistake by calling or 4-betting lighter.

In Hand #110, I am in the Big Blind with King-Six suited (Ks 6s). The action opened with FiveTwo raising to 250 from UTG, followed by Claude 3-betting to 850 from the Small Blind. K6s is a speculative hand that performs poorly in 3-bet pots, especially when squeezed between an UTG opener and a Small Blind 3-bettor. Claude's 3-bet range from the SB into an UTG open is likely very strong (JJ+, AK, AQs), and I am dominated by many of those hands. Furthermore, the original raiser (FiveTwo) is still to act and could 4-bet. To minimize risk and preserve my stack for better spots, I will fold.

We’re heads-up versus Claude’s small-blind 3-bet and, importantly, we’ll have position postflop (SB acts first). With 44, a 4-bet would mostly just fold out the hands we want to keep in (AQ/KQ-type, suited broadways) and isolate ourselves against a stronger continue range; we also can’t profitably continue versus a 5-bet shove. Calling 600 more is cheap relative to the pot and gives excellent implied odds to flop a set (effective stack vs Claude is ~12.4k behind, >20x the call). In position we can also realize equity well and navigate on favorable textures. So we take the +EV call.

I flopped top set with QQQ on Qs9cTs. This is an excellent result but the board is coordinated with straight draws (KJ, J8) and spade flush draws. I need to bet for value and charge draws. Betting around 60-70% pot makes sense to build the pot while protecting my hand.

We have 44 on a Q-T-9 two-spade flop in a 3-bet pot. This board heavily favors Claude’s 3-bet range (overpairs, AQ/KQ, strong broadways), while our hand is essentially an underpair with no meaningful straight/overcard equity—just two outs to a set. Calling 1100 to win 4000 requires ~27.5% equity, and even as a set-mine the remaining effective stack (~10.2k) doesn’t give anywhere close to the implied odds we’d need versus a competent opponent on this coordinated texture. Raising as a bluff is also poor since 44 blocks nothing and has no backup equity. Best play is to fold and preserve stack.

Current stack is approximately 50BB, which is healthy but the smallest at the table. Playing 4-handed, UTG is effectively the Cutoff. K5s is a marginal opening hand from this position. While it can be opened in an aggressive game, the presence of three deep-stacked opponents (Claude on BTN, Flash in SB, FiveTwo in BB) who have shown aggression (3-betting) makes this a high-variance spot. If I open and face a 3-bet, I must fold. If I get called, I am often dominated or playing a weak kicker out of position against the Button. Given the stack dynamics and the marginal nature of the hand, I will fold to preserve chips for better opportunities.
J8o on the button after UTG folds. Marginal hand but excellent position. This is a standard button steal spot - I can apply pressure to the blinds with position. A raise to 250 should work here.

I am holding 8d 2h in the Small Blind. This is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised from the Button, which is a standard steal position, but my hand has almost no playability post-flop and I will be out of position. There is no reason to invest any chips with such a low-equity hand. I will fold.

Button opens to 2.5x and I’m in the BB with T4o. Although the direct price (150 more) is tempting, this hand has very poor playability and realization postflop (dominated top-pair risk, no suitedness, weak connectivity). It’s also not a good candidate for a 3-bet bluff (no strong blockers, too low equity when called). Best EV is to release and wait for a better defend/3-bet spot.

A4o from UTG in a 4-handed game. This is borderline - normally I'd fold A4o from UTG, but 4-handed play requires wider opening ranges. With only 3 opponents, ace-high has decent showdown value. I'll make a standard open to 250 to maintain table presence and potentially steal blinds, especially with Pro being short-stacked in the BB.

I am on the button with J9 suited, a hand that plays well in multi-way pots or in position. Claude, who has been quite active, opened to 2.5x from UTG. Given my position and the playability of my hand, a call is standard here to see a flop. FiveTwo in the SB has a massive stack and Pro in the BB is short-stacked, so I need to be aware of potential squeezes, but calling allows me to realize my equity in position.

We’re in the SB out of position versus an UTG open and a BTN flat, which is a great spot to squeeze rather than play a multiway pot with capped ranges behind us. A♣5♣ is an ideal 3-bet candidate: it blocks strong Ax (reducing 4-bet frequency), has good playability when called (suited ace, wheel/straight potential), and benefits a lot from taking down the dead money (pot is already 650). I’ll size up to apply pressure to both ranges and discourage the BB from coming along cheaply, while still keeping it a clear 3-bet/fold versus strong 4-bet ranges.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 8h. Facing a UTG open, a Button call, and a large squeeze to 1400 (14BB) from the Small Blind. This is a very strong line from the SB. My hand, King-Eight suited, is easily dominated by the SB's squeezing range (likely AK, AQ, KQ, pairs). Additionally, the original raiser (UTG) and the caller (BTN) still have to act behind me, creating a risk of a 4-bet. Calling would cost me nearly 30% of my remaining stack, which is too high a price for a marginal speculative hand in this spot. The disciplined play is to fold.

A4o facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo who has shown aggression throughout. I'm out of position with a weak ace that will be dominated by most 3-bet ranges (AK, AQ, AJ, pairs). Flash still behind could squeeze. Pot odds require ~36% equity which A4o doesn't have vs a reasonable 3-bet range. Clear fold - this hand doesn't play well OOP against aggression.

I am on the button with J9 hearts. Claude opened from UTG for 250, and I called. FiveTwo, the chip leader in the small blind, has now squeezed to 1400. This is a very large 3-bet (5.6x the initial raise). While J9s has good playability in position, calling an additional 1150 (nearly 10% of my stack) against a big stack who is showing significant strength or putting on maximum pressure is not ideal. My hand is likely dominated by a standard 3-betting range, and I would be playing for a high percentage of my stack with a speculative hand. Folding preserves my stack to find better spots.

In a 4-handed game, King-Queen offsuit is a strong starting hand that warrants an open-raise from any position. I am first to act and will set a standard opening size of 2.5x the big blind to 250. This allows me to take control of the pot while still being able to evaluate if I face resistance from the aggressive chip leader (FiveTwo) or the big blind (Claude).

Flash opens UTG to 2.5x in a 4-handed game; even if his range is wider than full-ring, 4♣2♥ offsuit is at the very bottom with terrible playability and reverse implied odds. Calling 250 just to realize equity in position isn’t profitable, and 3-bet bluffing with this hand is too low-equity and risks running into stronger continues while also inviting squeeze/jam dynamics from the blinds. Best EV is to conserve chips and wait for better spots.

I hold Qc7h in the Small Blind facing an UTG raise from Flash. Q7o is a weak hand, significantly dominated by a typical UTG opening range (which includes AQ, KQ, pairs, suited broadways). Being out of position against an UTG raiser with a hand that has poor playability and reverse implied odds makes calling a losing proposition. With 48 BBs, I should preserve my stack for better spots or steal opportunities rather than defending loosely. The correct play is to fold.
A4o in BB facing UTG raise to 250. Getting good pot odds (150 to win 400 = ~2.7:1). Ace-high has some showdown value and can flop top pair. Not strong enough to 3-bet but worth a call at this price.

Completely missed this flop with Ac4d on 6d8cQs. I have just ace high with no draws - no flush draw, no straight draw. Standard check here and likely fold to any bet. No reason to bluff into the preflop aggressor with this board texture.

I have flopped top pair with a strong king kicker on a relatively dry board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should place a continuation bet here to extract value from weaker hands in Claude's range, such as middle pairs, pocket pairs, or straight draws like 97 or JT. A bet size of roughly 60% of the pot (350 into 550) is appropriate to build the pot while not over-committing against a potential check-raise.

Completely whiffed this flop with Ac4d. I have just ace-high with no draws on 6d8cQs. Flash c-bet 350 into 500, which is a standard continuation bet. My hand has no equity development potential and ace-high is unlikely to be good by showdown. Easy fold.

8♢3♥ offsuit is near the bottom of any 4-handed UTG opening range and plays very poorly postflop, especially out of position and deep vs SB/BB. The 150-chip dead money isn’t worth exposing our big stack to 3-bets/pressure and tough postflop spots with a dominated, disconnected hand. Preserve stack and wait for better steal candidates.

I am on the Button with A6o. The UTG player has folded. This is a standard spot to attempt to steal the blinds. I have the best position, an Ace blocker, and a stack depth (48 BB) that allows for normal play. My recent play has been relatively tight, which may increase the fold equity of my raise. A raise to 2.5x BB (250) is consistent with my previous sizing and puts pressure on the blinds to have a playable hand. If 3-bet, I can easily fold. If called, I have position post-flop.
4h9c is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing a button raise. Poor playability, no position postflop, and would need to call 200 into a 400 pot. Easy fold.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Pro has raised from the button to 250. Although the price to call is relatively low (150 chips to win a total pot of 400), Queen-Deuce offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very poor post-flop playability, especially out of position. Given that we are 114 hands into a 150-hand tournament and my stack is healthy, I see no reason to defend with such a marginal hand against a button open.

I am in the UTG position (4-handed, effectively Cutoff) with 7c8c (suited connector). While this hand is often an open in 4-handed play, I am the shortest stack at the table (approx 50BB) facing three significantly larger stacks (130BB, 140BB, 270BB). The Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has shown aggression, making it risky to open marginal hands that might face 3-bets or floating. Folding 78s reduces variance and preserves my stack for hands with better high-card value or implied odds in better position.
On the button with 73o - one of the worst hands in poker. While I have position, this hand has virtually no playability post-flop. Not worth a steal attempt with such a weak holding. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. It has folded to me. In a four-handed game, an Ace in the Small Blind is a very strong hand to open with when folded to. FiveTwo is the chip leader in the Big Blind and has shown a willingness to play pots, but A4o is well ahead of his random range. I will raise to 300 to either take the blinds now or set up a favorable post-flop situation where I have the betting lead.

Flash is opening from the small blind in a 4-handed game, which should be a very wide range. From the big blind we’re getting a good price (200 to win 600 after calling), and while T4o is a weak holding, it has enough equity versus a wide SB opening range to defend at least sometimes. As chip leader, I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand via a 3-bet, so I’ll take the lower-variance defend and play postflop.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have flopped Ace high with a backdoor nut flush draw on a board of Queen-Jack-Six. This board texture is quite coordinated, but it also favors my perceived range of high cards. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain initiative and attempt to take the pot down immediately. If FiveTwo, who is the chip leader and quite active, raises, I can comfortably fold. If he calls, I have the overcard Ace and the Ace of clubs to potentially improve on the turn.

Flop is QJ6 with two clubs; with T4o (one small club) we have no pair and no real straight draw—only a weak backdoor flush and some thin overcard/runout equity. Flash’s SB opening range is wide, but this is a strong c-bet board for him (many Qx/Jx, broadways, good club draws), and being out of position I’ll face a lot of turn pressure and poor realizability. Even getting 300 to win 1200 (25% pot odds), my realized equity is likely below that versus his betting range, so the best stack-preserving, high-EV line is to release now rather than float and bleed on later streets.

J7o from UTG in a 4-handed game is a weak holding. It's easily dominated and plays poorly postflop. With a solid stack of ~147BB, I don't need to force action with marginal hands from early position. Standard fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine suited, which is a very strong opening hand in a 4-handed game. I have a healthy stack of 134.5 big blinds. Claude has folded. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the small blind (FiveTwo) and the short-stacked big blind (Pro).

Flash is opening very wide from the button 4-handed, but Q5o performs poorly out of position: it’s frequently dominated when we make a queen, has weak straight/flush potential, and calling invites the BB to realize equity and/or squeeze. A light 3-bet bluff here is also low quality with this offsuit, low kicker hand. Preserving stack and waiting for better SB defend candidates is higher EV.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Flash). This is a strong starting hand that is well ahead of a typical wide Button opening range. While a 3-bet is an option to take the initiative, it risks isolating me against better Aces or pairs and bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Calling allows me to keep Flash's range wide, including dominated hands like weaker Aces and Broadways. Given the stack depth (~48bb) and positional disadvantage, defending by calling is the standard and lower-variance play.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against the pre-flop raiser (Button) on a Kd-4h-Qc flop. This board texture heavily favors the aggressor's range, which contains many Broadway combos (AK, KQ, QJ, KJ). My hand (Ah Td) has Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Jack). Leading out here is generally a mistake as it folds out worse hands and gets called by better ones. The standard play is to check and evaluate the opponent's action. If they bet, I will consider the sizing, but for now, checking allows me to potentially see a free turn or induce a bluff.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Nine suited and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes King-Queen-Four, which hits my range as the pre-flop aggressor significantly better than the Big Blind's defending range. Pro has checked to me. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (roughly 46% of the pot) to take advantage of my range lead and attempt to end the hand immediately. If Pro calls, I will re-evaluate based on the turn card, but on this board, I expect to take it down often.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit on a King-Queen-Four board. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (need a Jack) and an Ace overcard. Crucially, I also hold the Ace of Hearts, giving me the nut backdoor flush draw on a board with one heart (4h). Facing a bet of 300 into 650 (less than half pot), the price is reasonable. My combination of equity (gutshot + overcard) and playability on future streets (picking up a flush draw or hitting the straight) makes this a call. Folding would be too weak given the backdoor potential and the small sizing from the Button.

I am in the Big Blind with AhTd on a board of Kd-4h-Qc-4d. I defended the pre-flop raise and called a small continuation bet on the flop with my gutshot straight draw (needs a Jack) and overcard. The turn card is the 4d, pairing the board. This card is generally neutral but increases the likelihood of chops or counterfeiting two-pair hands. Out of position, leading out (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much as I have little fold equity against a King or Queen, and I don't want to build a pot with just a draw. My standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If they check back, I realize my equity for free. If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, as I still retain my straight outs and Ace outs.

The turn pairs the board with a Four. Pro has checked for a second time. While my Ace-high has some showdown value, Pro's call on the flop (a board with two high cards) suggests he likely has a piece of the board, such as a King, Queen, or a draw like Jack-Ten. By checking back, I control the pot size and take a free card to potentially improve to top pair or win at showdown if he was on a draw. Given Pro's relatively short stack, a second barrel here is unlikely to push him off a made hand and might invite a check-raise that forces me to fold my equity.

I hold AhTd on a board of Kd-4h-Qc-4d-5d. My final hand is a pair of 4s with an Ace-King-Queen kicker (effectively Ace-high showdown value). The river brings a third diamond, completing potential flushes. Flash showed strength on the flop but weakness by checking back the turn, suggesting a capped range of marginal made hands or missed draws. Betting here achieves little, as worse hands (missed straight draws) will fold, and better hands (any pocket pair, Kx, Qx, or flush) will likely call or raise. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value against his missed draws or weaker Ax hands without risking more chips. If he bets, I can comfortably fold.

I have Ace-high with showdown value. Pro called my flop continuation bet on a board with two overcards (King and Queen), suggesting he has at least a piece of the board or a strong draw like Jack-Ten. Since the turn and river both went check-check after his flop call, I am likely either beat by a marginal made hand (like a Queen or a small pair) or winning against a missed straight draw. Betting here as a bluff is risky because Pro is unlikely to fold a King or a Queen, and my Ace-high is strong enough to beat his missed draws. I'll take the free showdown.

I am dealt King-Queen offsuit in the first position (UTG) of a 4-handed game. This is a very strong hand in a short-handed format and is a mandatory open. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that I have used in recent hands to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds and the button. Given the stack depths, particularly the small blind being relatively short at 50BB, KQo plays well as an aggressive opening hand.

Button vs UTG open, 9-4 offsuit is too weak to defend profitably. It has poor playability, no relevant blockers for a 3-bet bluff, and calling also exposes us to a high-frequency squeeze/jam from the short-stacked SB. Best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better spots in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 9h, a very weak offsuit hand. The player in the Under-The-Gun position (Flash) has raised to 300 chips (3 BB). Given my poor holdings, being out of position, and facing an early position raise which typically indicates strength, I have insufficient equity and playability to continue. The correct strategic decision is to fold immediately to preserve my stack of roughly 50 BB.

T7s in BB facing UTG raise to 300. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds but T7s plays poorly out of position against an UTG range which is typically tighter. While suited connectors have some playability, this hand is too weak to defend profitably here.

4-handed UTG, 6♠3♣ offsuit is at the very bottom of playable holdings: poor high-card value, dominated when we make a pair, and limited ability to realize equity out of position versus 3 players. With Pro very short (4,950), there’s additional incentive to avoid thin, high-variance spots and let others clash while we preserve our big stack. We’ll wait for better steal candidates (suited/connectors, better blockers) to apply pressure.

I am on the Button with 4s 2s, a very weak suited hand. Although I am in position to steal, my stack is the shortest at the table (approx 50 BBs) while the blinds (Claude and Flash) have significantly larger stacks (145+ BBs) and can easily defend or 3-bet light. 42s is generally a fold in this spot in standard ranges, especially given the stack depth disparity. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q4o is a weak hand, but heads-up against the BB with 3:1 pot odds (50 to call into 150), I can complete cheaply. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand, and folding seems too tight given the price. I'll limp and see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The Small Blind (Claude) has just completed the bet, meaning it is a limped pot. While an Ace is relatively strong in a heads-up situation against a Small Blind's limping range, A3o is still a marginal hand that doesn't play particularly well against a calling range if I raise. Checking here allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small, which is a prudent strategy with 32 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack.

I flopped middle pair (queens) on a K-A-Q two-club board. My kicker is terrible (4c) and this board hits many hands in BB's range. Better queens, any king or ace, and club draws all beat or have good equity against me. Betting would only get called by better hands. Best to check and control pot size, then decide based on Flash's action.

On the flop of Kd-Ac-Qc, I have top pair with a weak kicker. The pot was limped pre-flop between me (Big Blind) and Claude (Small Blind). Claude checked the flop. This board is extremely coordinated and hits a broad range of hands, but in a limped pot, it's less likely either of us has a monster like AK or AQ, though Claude could easily have a King or a Queen. Checking behind is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and see if the turn changes the board texture, as my hand has some showdown value but cannot withstand significant pressure.

I have third pair (queens) with a 4 kicker on AKQ7 board. Flash has checked both flop and turn, suggesting weakness. However, my hand is still vulnerable - any Ax, Kx, or better queen beats me. The 7s doesn't change the dynamic. With club draws still possible and my weak kicker, I should check to control pot size and get to showdown cheaply. Betting only gets called by better hands.

On the turn, the board is Kd-Ac-Qc-7s. I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (Trey) is very weak. The board is extremely coordinated with three broadway cards, making it likely that if my opponent has a piece of the board, they might have a better kicker or a two-pair/straight. Since the pot was limped and has remained small with checks on the flop and now the turn from Claude, I will check behind to continue controlling the pot size and reach showdown cheaply with my marginal strength hand.

River completed the club flush. I have third pair (Qs) with a weak kicker on AKQ78 board with three clubs. Flash has checked every street showing weakness. My hand beats air/missed draws but loses to any flush, Ax, Kx, or better Qx. Betting only gets called by better hands. Check to showdown.

On the river, the board is Kd-Ac-Qc-7s-8c. I have top pair with an Ace and a weak kicker (3). The pot was limped pre-flop and both Claude and I have checked on every street (flop, turn, and now river). The board now has three clubs, making a flush possible. Given that the pot is very small (200) and Claude has shown no interest in betting, I have significant showdown value. Betting for value is risky because my kicker is the lowest possible and any hand that calls me might either beat me with a better Ace or be a King/Queen that is hesitant to call anyway. Checking back ensures I win the pot if I have the best hand without risking a check-raise on this coordinated board.

Current tournament state: Hand 119/150, 4 players left. I am the short stack (49.5 BBs) with ~5k chips, while opponents have 13k-26k. I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed) holding A6o. While an Ace is decent in short-handed play, A6o is at the bottom of the opening range from the Cutoff, especially without antes in the pot to improve the risk/reward ratio. Being the short stack, I want to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots out of position against larger stacks who can apply pressure or have me dominated. The risk of getting 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position with a weak kicker outweighs the potential blind steal value here. A disciplined fold is the best play to preserve the stack for better opportunities.
I have QQ on the button - a premium hand. Pro folded, leaving the blinds. I should raise for value. Standard raise to 250-300 is appropriate.

I am holding King-Jack of diamonds in the small blind, which is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. Claude has raised from the button, a position from which he likely has a wide range. I want to 3-bet here for a few reasons: to extract value from weaker hands in his opening range, to take initiative while out of position, and to discourage the big blind (FiveTwo), who has a massive stack, from entering the pot with a wide range of speculative hands. A raise to 975 is a standard 3-bet size from the small blind against a button open.

We’re in the BB with 74o facing a BTN open and an SB 3-bet to 975. This hand has terrible playability, no relevant blockers, and we’ll be out of position in a bloated pot against a strong/condensed range (and potentially still multiway if Claude continues). Calling 8.75bb more is -EV, and turning 74o into a 4-bet bluff is also poor due to lack of blockers and the deep stacks increasing postflop punishment when called. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

I have QQ on the button facing a 3-bet from Flash in the SB. QQ is a premium hand that plays well as a 4-bet. Flash could be 3-betting light against my button open. With effective stacks around 127bb, I should 4-bet for value to around 2.5x the 3-bet. If Flash 5-bets, I'm committed to call with QQ.

Claude has 4-bet to 2500 after my 3-bet. This is the exact same dynamic and sizing as Hand #17, where I folded A5s to his 4-bet. While King-Jack of diamonds is a strong starting hand, it is significantly behind a typical 4-betting range (QQ+, AK, and occasionally bluffs like Axs). Calling 1525 more out of position with a hand that is likely dominated by his value range (AK, AJ, KQ) is a losing play in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack and look for a better spot, as I still have a healthy stack and roughly 30 hands remaining in the tournament.
K4o from UTG is too weak to open. Even 4-handed, this hand has poor playability and will often be dominated by hands that call or 3-bet. Folding is the correct play.

On the button with Q8o, I am in a standard opening position in a 4-handed game. However, looking at the table dynamics, FiveTwo (Small Blind) is the chip leader and has been playing very aggressively, frequently 3-betting or calling raises. Pro (Big Blind) is the short stack with about 48 BBs and might be looking for a spot to shove or defend light. While Q8o is often an open in GTO button ranges, against an aggressive big stack in the blinds, it's a marginal hand that often leads to difficult post-flop spots or being forced to fold to a 3-bet. With 30 hands remaining and my stack being relatively healthy but below average, I choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand to engage with the chip leader.

Folded to me in the small blind versus the short-stacked big blind. Even with 3♣2♥, this is a good spot to apply pressure and pick up the dead money: I’m risking a small amount relative to my stack to win the pot immediately, and I can often take it down with a continuation bet when called. If Pro responds with a shove, I can comfortably fold this hand and preserve my stack.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, facing a standard 2.5x open from the Small Blind, JTo is a strong enough hand to defend. I am getting favorable pot odds (need to call 150 to win a pot that will be 500, requiring 30% equity) and will have position on the aggressor post-flop. Folding would be too tight given the wide range the SB should be opening. A 3-bet is possible but calling is the standard low-variance play to realize equity in position.

Blind-vs-blind I opened extremely wide, so I still want a c-bet at decent frequency to take down the pot immediately. This 9-7-4 board is more connected and BB hits it fairly often with pairs and straighty hands, so I don’t want to bet large with total air. A small c-bet pressures his overcards/whiffs and denies equity while keeping my risk low versus his short stack. If Pro check-jams, I simply fold (I’ll have essentially no equity and terrible pot odds).

I am in position (Big Blind) against the Small Blind on a [4h 9d 7c] board. I hold JTo, which gives me two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). The opponent bets 175 into a pot of 500, offering me very favorable pot odds of roughly 20.5%. My hand has significant equity against a wide blind-vs-blind range; an 8 gives me the nuts, and hitting a Jack or Ten likely gives me the best hand. Given the small bet size, my equity, and position, folding is too tight. Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily when I have reasonable equity to realize. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play.

Turn pairs the 7, which is generally better for BB’s defending range, but Pro’s line (call pre, call small flop bet) still contains plenty of one-and-done floats/overcards and marginal pocket pairs that don’t love facing a second barrel—especially given his short stack and ICM-ish pressure with 4 left. I have essentially no showdown value with 3c2h, so checking just concedes the pot too often and invites him to realize equity or bluff rivers. A small, range-driven turn stab keeps my risk low while needing very little fold equity (betting ~30% pot) and still credibly represents overpairs/9x/7x from my wide SB open. If he check-jams, I snap-fold.

I am in the Big Blind facing a second barrel from the Small Blind on a [4h 9d 7c 7h] board. The opponent's bet sizing is extremely small (250 into 850, roughly 23% pot), offering me pot odds of about 5.4 to 1 (requiring ~18.5% equity). I hold JTo, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (any 8 makes a straight) and two overcards (Jack and Ten). Against a wide Blind vs Blind range, my overcards often serve as live outs, and the straight draw is strong. The small bet size typically indicates a marginal hand, a blocker bet, or a weak draw, rather than a polarized nut hand. Given the excellent price and position, folding is too tight, and raising might fold out the hands I dominate or isolate me against trips. Calling allows me to realize my equity cheaply and re-evaluate on the river.

River is 4h-9d-7c-7h-7s and I have pure air (my best hand is just the board 7-7-7-9-4). After Pro check-calls flop and turn, his range is heavily weighted toward hands that now make a full house or better: any pocket pair, any 9x/4x, and of course any 7x (quads). There just aren’t enough “nothing” hands left that will fold often enough to justify a bluff, and with him being the short stack he’s also more likely to look me up rather than overfold. Checking concedes, but it avoids burning chips into a range that is very strong on this runout.

The river brings a third Seven, making the board [4h 9d 7c 7h 7s]. This creates a very dangerous board texture where any pocket pair makes a Full House, and any 9 or 4 also makes a Full House. Even if the opponent has just high cards (A, K, Q), they out-kick my Jack. I only beat missed straight draws (like 56, 68) or lower high cards playing the board. Given the opponent's weak small barrel line followed by a check, they likely have marginal showdown value that beats me (like a 4, 9, or Ax) or complete air. Betting achieves nothing as better hands call and worse hands fold. I will check back to take the free showdown, hoping to win against missed draws.

I am holding 5-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Acting first (UTG) in a 4-handed game, there is no justification for entering the pot with this hand. I will fold to preserve my stack for more profitable situations as we approach the end of the 150-hand session.

Folds to us on the button with Q9o. In 4-handed play this is comfortably within a profitable button opening range: we have position, decent high-card equity, and good fold equity versus the blinds. Pro is shorter and likely to defend more straightforwardly; Claude can 3-bet, but we can simply fold to significant pressure given Q9o’s reverse-implied-odds when deep. I’ll use a small-but-standard steal size to risk little while still applying pressure.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9c 3h, which is a very weak hand (9-3 offsuit). The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Calling would put me out of position with a garbage hand against an aggressive opponent, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary and risky given the hand strength. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.
8-2 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even with pot odds of 150 to win 400, this hand plays poorly post-flop and has very little equity against a button raise range. Easy fold.

4-handed UTG is relatively wide; pocket 4s is a profitable open both as a steal and with reasonable equity when called. With a large chip lead and a short stack on the Button, I want to apply pressure and take initiative rather than limp and invite multiway play. A standard 2.5x open keeps my range strong, builds the pot when I flop a set, and still allows me to fold versus heavy 3-bet pressure if needed.

I am on the Button with 7d Th, facing an UTG raise from the chip leader (FiveTwo). T7o is a very weak hand that plays poorly against an UTG opening range, which is typically strong. Despite having position, the hand has little potential to make strong hands and is easily dominated. With 55 BBs, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot against the chip leader. Folding is the standard and correct play.

82 offsuit is a trash hand. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo who has been fairly active but still opens from early position with reasonable hands. I'm out of position with one of the worst hands in poker. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who has a massive chip lead, has raised to 250. This is a 2.5x open. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win 400), Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against the chip leader. Given that I have a healthy stack of over 120 big blinds and there are only 28 hands left in the tournament, there is no reason to take a marginal or negative expected value spot with such a poor starting hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in UTG position with [2h, 4s], which is one of the worst starting hands in poker. With 55 BBs, I have a healthy stack but am the shortest at the table, so chip preservation is relevant, and there is absolutely no incentive to open this trash hand from early position against deep-stacked opponents. The only logical play is to fold immediately.
73 offsuit is a very weak hand, even on the button. While stealing the blinds is appealing, FiveTwo has a massive stack and has shown aggression with 3-bets. This hand is too weak to profitably open.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Both the UTG and Button players have folded, leaving the action to me and the Big Blind (FiveTwo). In a blind-versus-blind situation, any Ace is a relatively strong hand. FiveTwo is the current chip leader and has shown significant aggression in recent hands. By raising, I can take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. If called, I have the advantage of holding an Ace, though I will be playing the rest of the hand out of position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to test the Big Blind's defense.

Heads-up blind-vs-blind after folds: Flash is opening from the small blind with a wide range, even if the 3x sizing is on the larger side. We’re getting 200 to win 400 immediate (need ~33% equity) and 87o has enough raw equity/playability to defend, especially with our deep stack. I don’t want to 3-bet this offsuit combo (poor realization when called/4-bet), so the best line is to take the price and see a flop.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop contains a King, which fits well within my perceived range. Ace-high has some showdown value but is unlikely to win if the hand goes to check-down. I also hold the Ace of clubs, providing a backdoor nut flush draw. A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard play here to maintain initiative and potentially take down the pot immediately, as many of FiveTwo's calling hands pre-flop might have missed this board or will struggle to continue against a bet.

We flop second pair (8x) on a board where SB can c-bet extremely wide in blind-vs-blind pots. The sizing is small (300 into 900), giving us great immediate odds (need ~25% equity) and allowing us to keep in his bluffs and worse hands (random floats, club draws, gutshots like QJ/J9, some worse 8x). Raising mostly isolates us versus stronger Kx/Tx and better draws, while folding is far too tight for this price. Best line is to defend with a call and re-evaluate on the turn based on sizing and card texture.

The turn card (Jack of Spades) makes the board very coordinated, completing potential straights and improving many hands in FiveTwo's calling range. My Ace-high has lost significant value and I have no direct draw to a nut hand. Betting again into the chip leader on such a wet board is high-risk with low expected fold equity. Checking is the best option to see how the opponent reacts and potentially reach a cheap showdown or fold if facing significant aggression.

Turn goes check from Flash after a very small flop c-bet. On this K-T-8-J texture he can be giving up a lot (missed overs/air), or checking some medium-strength hands/draws. With second pair + a small gutshot (9) we benefit a lot from denying a free river to club draws/Q9-type hands, while still getting thin value from worse 8x, Tx, and floats that continue. I’ll keep the sizing modest to avoid inflating the pot versus his Kx/straights and to make it easy to fold if he springs a big check-raise.

The turn card, the Jack of Spades, makes the board extremely coordinated (K-J-T-8). This card likely improves FiveTwo's range, which includes many combinations of pairs and straight draws that called the flop. My Ace-high has no showdown value and no remaining draws to improve. Although the bet size of 450 into a 1200 pot is relatively small, calling out of position with a weak hand against the chip leader on such a wet board is a losing play. I will fold and preserve my stack.
4-handed play, UTG with A3o. While A3o is marginal from UTG in a full ring game, it's a standard open in short-handed play. I'll raise to 2.5x to steal blinds or build a pot with ace-high equity.

Claude has opened from UTG (effectively the cutoff in 4-handed play) for 2.5x. My hand, Ten-Four suited, is quite weak even in a short-handed game. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, calling here would risk being squeezed by the large stack in the Small Blind or shoved on by the short-stacked Big Blind. The hand has poor playability and high reverse implied odds if I hit a Ten. Given the tournament stage and my current stack, it is best to wait for a stronger opening or a better semi-bluffing candidate.

KsTs plays well as a suited broadway with good blocker value (K) and strong postflop playability, but calling from the SB invites the short BB in and leaves us out of position without initiative. With our big stack and Claude’s frequent opens, a 3-bet leverages fold equity, denies the BB a cheap realization, and lets us win the pot preflop a lot while still having playable equity when called. If we face a 4-bet from UTG we can comfortably fold this hand and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Kd Jc. Claude opened from UTG and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind. This action indicates strong ranges from both opponents, particularly the SB 3-betting an UTG open. My hand, KJo, is easily dominated by hands like AK, KQ, AJ, JJ+ in their ranges. Calling would require committing about 15% of my stack (800 chips) which is too expensive for a marginal hand in this spot, especially given I am the shortest stack at the table. I should fold and preserve my chips.

A3o is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet, especially out of position. FiveTwo has been active with 3-bets. I'm dominated by better aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) and at a significant disadvantage against pocket pairs. While I'm getting ~2:1 odds, the positional disadvantage and weak holdings make this a clear fold.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Jack offsuit (Ah Jc) is a very strong hand to open from under-the-gun (UTG). I have a healthy stack of 12,050 chips (120.5 big blinds). I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds and the button.

4-handed on the button we can defend fairly wide, but Q6o is near the bottom of playable holdings versus an UTG open. It’s frequently dominated when we make a queen, has poor playability, and calling also gives the short-stacked SB a good squeeze/jam spot that we can’t profitably continue against. A bluff 3-bet is possible in theory, but Q6o is a poor candidate (limited blockers and terrible equity when called). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I hold 8c 4h in the Small Blind, facing a 3BB raise from Flash in UTG. This is a very weak unsuited hand (84o) with poor playability and equity against an UTG opening range. Being out of position and facing strength, I am not getting the right price or implied odds to continue. The correct play is to fold immediately.
9-5 offsuit is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. I'm out of position with poor pot odds (200 to win 450) and no implied odds given my hand's weakness. Easy fold.

4-handed we can open fairly wide from UTG/CO, but 4h2h is the very bottom of the suited range and performs poorly when called/3-bet. Both blinds (Claude/Flash) have shown willingness to fight (3-bets and pressure postflop), which reduces our steal EV and increases the chance we have to fold to a 3-bet after investing chips. With a big stack relative to one short stack (Pro), there’s no need to punt marginal opens—preserve stack and pick better spots to apply pressure.

Current hand is 126/150. I am on the Button with As8h, a respectable starting hand for a steal attempt in a 4-handed game, especially when folded to. My stack is roughly 54BB. Opening from the button allows me to potentially take down the blinds (1.5BB) uncontested or play the hand in position post-flop. A8o is well within the standard button opening range (top ~40-50% of hands). A standard open size of 2.5BB (250 chips) is appropriate to apply pressure while not committing too many chips if 3-bet.

Q9o in the SB facing a button raise. Pro is on the button with a short stack (52 BBs), making this likely a steal attempt. Q9o is marginal but playable against a wide button range. Calling is reasonable to see a flop, though we're OOP. Flash in BB could squeeze. With 150 BBs deep, I can afford to defend here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The Button raised to 250 and the Small Blind called. Although I am getting good pot odds to call (150 to call into a pot of 600), the poor playability and lack of connectivity of this hand, combined with being out of position for the rest of the hand, makes folding the best long-term decision. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Missed the flop completely with Q9o on K33. No pair, no draw. Against Pro's button opening range, this board hits him reasonably well (any King, pocket pairs). With a short-stacked opponent who could easily have better, checking is standard. If he bets, I'll likely fold as I have no equity worth defending.

The board K-3-3 is very dry. As the pre-flop raiser on the Button, I have a range advantage, but my specific hand (As8h) has marginal showdown value. Betting here would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (like Q-high or J-high) and get called by hands that beat me (any King, pocket pairs). Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size against a larger stack, and potentially induce bluffs or call down later streets if the board runs out safely. It is a standard pot control line with a weak Ace on a paired board.

Turn brings 4c, board is 3h Kc 3s 4c. I have Q9o - complete air with no pair and no draw. Pro checked behind on the flop, showing weakness (likely doesn't have a King). I could bluff here representing strength, but a small stab risks chips with zero equity. If he has any pocket pair he'll call. Better to check and potentially take a stab on the river if he checks again, or just give up. No need to build the pot with nothing.

I hold Ace-high (As8h) on a dry, paired board of 3h-Kc-3s-4c. My opponent, Claude, has checked to me twice (flop and turn), indicating weakness and a lack of a King. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, my hand has showdown value that I want to protect. Betting here serves little purpose: it folds out worse hands (like Q-high) that I am already beating, and likely only gets called by hands that beat me (like pocket pairs 55-99). Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially induce a bluff on the river, or take a cheap showdown. I will check and re-evaluate on the river.

River brings the Ts on a 3h Kc 3s 4c Ts board. I have Q9o - queen high, no pair. Pro checked behind on both flop and turn, showing significant weakness (likely doesn't have a King). However, bluffing here accomplishes little - my bet would only fold out hands I beat (pure air) while getting called by any pocket pair (55-99, JJ-QQ). I have some slim showdown value with Q-high against missed Ax hands. Classic way-ahead/way-behind spot - checking is optimal.

The board is 3h-Kc-3s-4c-Ts. I hold As8h, giving me a pair of Threes with an Ace-King-Ten kicker. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. Claude checks to me again on the river. His line is consistent with a weak hand, such as a small pocket pair, a weak Ace, or missed high cards. Betting here has negative expectation because better hands (like small pocket pairs or a rivered Ten) will likely call, while worse hands (like Q-high or worse Aces) will fold. I have sufficient showdown value to win against his bluffs or missed draws. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity without risking more chips in a small pot.

I am in UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed play) with QJo. While this hand is often an open in late position, I am the shortest stack at the table (though still deep with ~58BB) facing three opponents who cover me significantly, including a massive chip leader in the Big Blind. QJo is a hand that can easily get dominated (by AQ, KQ, AJ) and plays poorly out of position if called by the Button or blinds. Given the stack dynamics and the risk of bleeding chips in a marginal spot out of position, I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities to enter the pot.
