8s4h is a very weak offsuit hand. Even on the button with a steal opportunity, this hand has poor playability. FiveTwo in the BB has a large stack (27k) and has been active defending/3-betting. The risk-reward isn't favorable here - I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

In a blind-vs-blind situation with a pocket pair (55), the standard play is to raise. This allows me to take the initiative against the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately. Pocket pairs have significant value in heads-up play, even if they are vulnerable to overcards on the flop. A 3x raise is a standard opening size in this position.

SB open to 3x in a 4-handed game is likely fairly wide. From the BB we’re getting 2:1 immediate odds (call 200 to win 400), so we only need ~33% equity and Th7d has enough against a typical SB opening range. A 3-bet would inflate the pot out of position with a dominated, low-playability offsuit hand, so the best EV line is to defend via call and realize equity postflop.

The flop of 6s-2h-8c is relatively favorable for my pocket fives, as there is only one overcard (the eight) on a very dry board. Since I held the lead pre-flop, a continuation bet is standard here to protect my hand against overcards and to extract value from weaker hands in the Big Blind's range. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a balanced sizing that allows me to define where I am in the hand without committing too many chips if FiveTwo shows significant strength.

Flash’s 1/3-pot c-bet (300 into 600) is consistent with a very wide continuation range from the SB in a 4-handed spot. We’re getting 3:1 immediate odds (call 300 to win 1200), so we only need ~25% equity. Th7d has a gutshot to the 9 plus some additional ways to improve (T/7 pairs) and, importantly, this board (6-2-8 rainbow) hits the BB defending range reasonably well, giving us opportunities to win future streets when he slows down. A check-raise bluff here doesn’t have great blockers/equity and risks getting jammed on by his stronger continues, so the best EV is to defend via call and realize equity/keep his range wide.

On the turn, the board is 6s-2h-8c-9c. My pocket fives are now essentially fourth pair on a board that is becoming quite coordinated. Pre-flop, I raised and FiveTwo called. On the flop, I continuation bet and FiveTwo called again. This suggests FiveTwo has a piece of the board or a draw. The nine is a scare card that potentially improves FiveTwo's range if they are holding middle connectors or have paired up. Since I am out of position against the chip leader, I will check here to keep the pot small and see how they respond. Betting again risks getting raised or called by a range that almost entirely beats me, whereas checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or catch a bluff if they bet small.

Turn completes our hidden straight (6-7-8-9-T), which is likely the best straight available on this texture. After SB c-bets flop and then checks turn, his range is heavily weighted toward one-pair hands (8x/9x/6x), some slowplays (sets/two pair), and a meaningful chunk of club draws that want a cheap river. We should bet for (1) value vs his pairs and (2) protection/denial vs clubs and pair+draw combos, while also starting to build a pot that can win a bigger stack if he has a strong made hand. A larger sizing than standard is justified because draws exist and our hand is very strong but not invulnerable (club rivers/board pairs).

FiveTwo has made a massive overbet on the turn, betting 2100 into a 1200 pot. This move by the chip leader is extremely polarizing and puts a high price on continuing. My pocket fives are now fourth pair on a board of 6-2-8-9, and I am losing to almost any piece of the board FiveTwo might have called the flop with (8x, 6x, 9x, or 77). Additionally, the turn 9 is a card that coordinates the board significantly, making straights like 7-10 or 7-5 possible. While FiveTwo is known to be aggressive, calling such a large bet with a weak underpair is not a profitable long-term strategy, especially since I would likely face another large bet on the river. Preserving my stack of 11,500 for better spots is the more prudent move here.

A8o in UTG at a 4-handed table is a reasonable opening hand. With fewer players, hand values increase and I should be opening wider. Standard raise to 2.5x to take down the blinds or build a pot with position on the blinds if called.

I am on the button with 3d 4s, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude raised from UTG. Given the hand's lack of equity and playability, the standard play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing Claude’s UTG 2.5x open from the small blind, 9♣6♦ is too weak to defend out of position versus a likely tighter UTG range. Calling gives a decent immediate price, but we’ll realize equity poorly OOP and can get put in tough spots (especially if Pro comes along or squeezes short). A light 3-bet isn’t attractive either since Claude has shown willingness to fight back with 4-bets, and this hand has poor playability and blocker value. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold AhKd (Ace-King offsuit) in the Big Blind, facing a standard open raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. My stack is 56.75 BBs. Claude has shown a tendency to open wide and has even 4-bet light (all-in with A6o in Hand #119) but also folds to 3-bets (Hand #124). AKo is a premium hand that plays well as a 3-bet for value, especially when out of position, to build the pot against dominated hands like AQ/AJ/KQ or lower pairs. Calling is passive and allows the opponent to realize equity cheaply while leaving me out of position without initiative. A standard 3-bet size here is about 3.5x the open, around 850-900. I will raise to 850 to extract value and define the range.

Facing a 3-bet from Pro in the BB. A8o is a marginal hand that doesn't play well OOP against a 3-bet range. Pro's 3-bet to 850 represents strength, and even if he's 3-betting light, I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand with a hand that can easily be dominated. The 600 to call into 1150 gives decent odds, but playing A8o OOP vs aggression is a losing proposition long-term. Disciplined fold here.

I am dealt pocket fours (4s 4h) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. Pocket pairs carry significant value, though low pairs like fours primarily benefit from flopping a set. In a 4-handed environment, this hand is strong enough to open for a raise rather than limping. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot, potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a profitable situation if I hit a set on the flop. Given the stack sizes, I have enough chips (115 BB) to play post-flop comfortably.

4-handed, Flash’s UTG open is likely quite wide and we have position plus an ace blocker. A6o isn’t great as a flat because it can get dominated and invites the blinds in, but it works well as a light 3-bet to isolate, deny equity, and take initiative vs a frequent opener. If Flash 4-bets (especially large/jam), we comfortably fold; if called, we can apply pressure in position on many boards with range advantage and strong blocker effects on A-high textures.

I am holding 7s 6h in the Small Blind. The action has been heavy before me: Flash raised to 300 from UTG, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 950 from the Button. Facing a raise and a re-raise, my off-suit connector is far too weak to continue. I am out of position against two players who have shown strength. Calling would cost a significant portion of my stack (900 chips out of 6025) with a very low probability of winning. Conserving chips for a better spot is the clear choice.
3d5c is a very weak offsuit hand. FiveTwo 3-bet to 950 showing strength. I'm out of position with a garbage hand that has minimal equity against a 3-betting range. Clear fold.

I opened with a raise to 300 using pocket fours (4s 4h), and FiveTwo on the button has 3-bet to 950. The blinds have folded, so it's back to me. I need to call 650 more into a pot that will be 2,050. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.1:1. While pocket fours are vulnerable post-flop, especially out of position, the stack depths (112 BB effective) provide sufficient implied odds for set mining. FiveTwo has shown a tendency to be aggressive and play for large pots, increasing the likelihood that I will get paid off if I hit a set. I will call to see a flop, looking primarily for a four, and will likely fold to significant aggression if the board is unfavorable and I miss my set.

On an Ace-high paired board (Ad As 2d) as the caller of a pre-flop 3-bet, I am out of position and the board significantly favors the range of the 3-bettor, FiveTwo. My pocket fours are now a very marginal hand, only beating potential bluffs or a lower pair like treys. There is no reason to lead into the aggressor here. Checking is the standard play to see how they react. I will look to fold if faced with significant aggression, as it's highly likely they have an Ace or a superior pocket pair.

We 3-bet pre and get called, then on Ad As 2d we hold Ah6d for trips with only one ace (Ac) left in the deck, so villain has very few Ax combos that beat us. This is a great spot to start extracting value from pocket pairs, diamond floats, and stubborn broadways while keeping their range wide. I’ll use a small range-style c-bet to get called often and avoid blowing them off their marginal continues.

On an Ace-high paired board (Ad As 2d) in a 3-bet pot, the advantage lies heavily with the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo. My pocket fours are now effectively a very weak bluff-catcher. While FiveTwo's bet of 550 is small (roughly 21% of the pot) and offers good pot odds to call, playing this hand out of position against an aggressive chip leader is dangerous. Most of the hands FiveTwo 3-bets with (Ax, pocket pairs 55+, and high broadway cards) are either crushing me or have significant equity to over-draw me on the turn and river. Since my hand has very little potential to improve and I am likely to face further aggression on later streets, I choose to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

4-handed ranges widen a lot and Q4s is a reasonable open as a steal/initiative hand with decent playability (suited, can make strong flushes and some top-pair boards). With our big stack we can pressure the shorter stacks in the blinds/button; if we face a 3-bet or shove, we can comfortably fold and lose a small, controlled amount. I’ll use a standard sizing that still keeps the pot manageable.

I am on the Button with Ks5s facing a raise to 250 from the UTG chip leader (FiveTwo). Although I have position, K5s is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a UTG opening range (better Kings, pairs). Calling could also invite squeezes from the blinds who cover me. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament (Hand 130/150), preserving my 60bb stack for a better spot is the disciplined play. Folding is the standard action here against an early position open.

A5o in the SB facing a UTG raise of 250. Getting 2:1 pot odds to call 200 more. A5o is marginal but has some playability - can make top pair or wheel straights. FiveTwo has been active. Calling to see a flop is reasonable here, though I'm out of position. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but worth seeing a flop at this price.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The UTG player (FiveTwo) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I need to call 150 chips to see the flop in a pot that will be 750. These are excellent pot odds (4:1). While K3o is a weak hand to play out of position, the price is low enough to justify a call to see if I can hit a favorable flop, especially in a 4-handed game where King-high has some value. I will proceed with caution post-flop.
Missed flop completely - just ace high with a backdoor wheel draw. Board is 3d7dTh which connects better with FiveTwo's opening range. No pair, no real draws. Check and evaluate what others do.

I have bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker on a board of 10-7-3 with two diamonds. In a multi-way pot against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) and the Small Blind (Claude), bottom pair is a marginal hand. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the action develops. Claude has already checked, and I want to see if FiveTwo continues their aggression. I am looking for a cheap showdown or an improvement on the turn.

We’re the preflop raiser and both blinds checked to us, so we have the range advantage (overpairs, Tx, strong broadways) and can profitably apply pressure. Even though it’s multiway on a somewhat connected/diamond board, a small c-bet targets the widest part of their ranges (overcards, weak pairs, hands that missed) and risks little relative to the pot. With Q4s we have minimal equity, so taking the immediate fold equity line is higher EV than checking and facing turn stabs.

I have ace high with no pair, no flush draw (no diamonds), and no straight draw on a 3d7dTh board. FiveTwo's c-bet of 250 into 750 is small but I have zero made hand value and minimal draws. Even with 5:1 pot odds, my ~12% chance to hit an ace or 5 doesn't justify calling, especially with Flash still to act behind. Clear fold.

I have bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker on a 10-7-3 board. The pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) has bet 250 into a pot of 750, giving me 4:1 pot odds to call. Since Claude has folded, I am now heads-up and closing the action on this street. A 1/3 pot bet from FiveTwo could be a standard continuation bet with a wide range of hands, including overcards that I currently beat. Given the favorable price and the potential to improve to two pair or trips, I will call this bet to see a turn card, but I will remain cautious if the betting continues.

I have bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker on a board that is becoming more coordinated (3-7-10-9). The 9h is a bad card for my hand as it connects with many hands in FiveTwo's range, potentially completing straights or making two-pair combinations. As I am out of position and my hand is extremely marginal, I will check to see how the pre-flop aggressor responds. If they bet significantly, I will likely fold, as my pair of threes has very little showdown value against a range that continues on this turn.

Turn goes heads-up vs Flash after he called a small flop c-bet on T73 (two diamonds). The 9h is a very connecting card that improves a lot of his flop-calling range (9x picks up a pair, 98/J8/86 pick up straight equity, pairs+draws continue), while our Q4s has essentially no equity and no relevant blockers. Firing again would need a high fold frequency and also exposes us to check-raises from his draws, turning this into a low-EV spew spot. With a big stack, the disciplined line is to check back, keep the pot manageable, and only consider bluffing certain rivers if checked to (or simply fold to river aggression).

On the river, the board has become extremely coordinated (3-7-10-9-J), making many straights possible (any 8 or Q-8). My bottom pair of threes with a King kicker has almost no showdown value against FiveTwo's range, which likely contains many overcards, pairs that improved (like a Jack or Nine), or even pocket pairs that were already ahead. Since the turn checked through, FiveTwo might have a marginal hand or a missed draw. However, betting into the pre-flop aggressor with such a weak hand is not profitable, as I am unlikely to fold out better hands. I will check and hope to reach a cheap showdown, though I expect to fold if FiveTwo makes a significant bet.

River goes check to us in a spot where our Q-high has essentially no showdown value versus Flash’s flop-calling range (lots of Tx/7x/9x and some Jx that now improved). The J♠ is a very good pressure card for our UTG range: it completes credible value like KQ/Q8 and we can also have slow-played sets/two-pair lines after checking turn. After Flash checks river, his range is capped toward bluff-catchers and missed draws; applying a polarized sizing gives us the best chance to fold out his medium pairs (7x/9x and some weak Tx) that would snap off smaller bets. If he check-jams, we comfortably fold and preserve our large stack.

On the river, the board is highly coordinated (3-7-10-9-J), meaning any 8 or Q-8 has a straight. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back the turn and has now fired an over-bet (1500 into a 1250 pot). This action is highly polarizing, usually representing either a very strong hand (like a straight, a set, or two-pair) that checked the turn for pot control/trapping, or a complete bluff. My hand is only bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker. Even if FiveTwo is bluffing with missed diamonds or overcards like AK/AQ, I am at the very bottom of my calling range and there are too many combinations in FiveTwo's range that naturally beat a pair of threes. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG (first to act in 4-handed play) with 9d Tc. My stack is roughly 60 BBs. T9o is a marginal hand that is generally considered too weak to open from the first position, even in a short-handed game, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop out of position if called by the button or blinds. With the chip leader in the Big Blind likely to defend wide, opening here has negative expectation. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with 2s6s - a very weak suited gapper. While I have position, this hand is at the bottom of any reasonable stealing range. FiveTwo has a massive stack (29.6k) in the BB and could easily defend or 3-bet light. The risk/reward of stealing with such a weak hand isn't favorable. With 20 hands left, I'll preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with JcTh, a decent speculative hand in a 4-handed game. The pot has been folded around to me. Claude (Button) and Pro (UTG) have already folded. I only need to call 50 more to see a flop against FiveTwo in the Big Blind. While raising is an option to steal the blinds, FiveTwo has been quite active and has a massive stack (nearly 30k), making them less likely to fold to a standard blind steal. Given the stack depths and the fact that I'm out of position, calling the 50 to see a flop is a low-cost way to potentially hit a strong hand or a good draw. Raising might invite a 3-bet from the chip leader, which I'd rather avoid with this specific holding.

Small blind has limped, and we’re heads-up with position postflop (BB acts last). A7o is well ahead of a typical SB limp range and plays best as the aggressor. Raising also denies equity to random suited/connected hands and lets us win the pot outright a good amount while applying ICM/stack pressure as the big stack. I’ll use a straightforward iso size that’s big enough to punish the limp but not so big that we bloat the pot unnecessarily.

FiveTwo has raised to 450 from the Big Blind after I completed from the Small Blind. Jack-Ten offsuit is a solid hand in a 4-handed context and has reasonable playability. I am getting roughly 1.5-to-1 odds to call the raise. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the chip leader, folding would be too tight given the hand strength and the relatively small price to see a flop. I will call and see if I can connect with the board.

I've missed the flop completely. The board is K-7-7, which is quite dry. I'm out of position against the chip leader, FiveTwo, who raised pre-flop. My hand, Jack-high, has very little showdown value and no draws. I'll check to see if FiveTwo bets. If they do, I'll likely fold unless it's a very unusual sizing, as I have no equity here.

We flop trips with top kicker on a dry, paired board (K77). Flash’s limp/call range contains plenty of Kx, small/mid pairs, and random floats that can continue versus a small c-bet. Betting also denies a free turn that could pair the K and sometimes turn our hand into a worse full house versus Kx. I’ll use a small, range-friendly sizing to keep his weaker continues in and start building the pot.

I have completely missed the flop of Kh-7c-7d with my Jc-Th. FiveTwo, the chip leader and pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 300 into a 900 pot. This is a very standard sizing on a paired board and could represent a King, a Seven, or just a routine bluff with two overcards. However, I have no piece of this board, no draw, and only Jack-high. Continuing out of position against a player with a massive stack would be a mistake. I will fold and preserve my chips for a better opportunity.

Q7o is a weak hand from UTG position. This hand is easily dominated and doesn't play well postflop. With a healthy stack of 142 BB, there's no need to open marginal hands from early position. Standard fold.

I am on the button with 82o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While I have positional advantage, the small blind (FiveTwo) has a massive stack and has been playing aggressively, and the big blind (Pro) has a medium stack. There is no reason to risk chips with such a low-equity hand when I can wait for a better spot to preserve my stack.

Folds to me in the small blind, so this is effectively a heads-up steal spot versus the big blind. With a big chip lead over Pro’s ~59bb stack, applying pressure preflop has good immediate EV by picking up the dead blinds often, and it keeps my range uncapped compared to limping too much. 6♣4♥ isn’t a premium, but it has decent playability when called (can make straights/two-pair) and benefits from fold equity. I’ll use a small but standard SB open size to deny some equity and avoid giving Pro an easy check option; if faced with a 3-bet, I’ll comfortably fold this hand.

I am in the Big Blind with [6d Tc], a very weak offsuit hand. The Small Blind (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader, has raised to 2.5 BBs. While I am in position and getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 350, requiring ~30% equity), T6o plays poorly post-flop, suffering from reverse implied odds and domination issues. As the tournament short stack, I need to preserve my stack and avoid marginal spots where the chip leader can apply maximum pressure to deny my equity. Folding is the prudent long-term decision here.

I am in the UTG position with 2d 4h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of 9600 chips (96 big blinds) and 18 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot from early position with such a hand. I will fold and wait for better spots.

4-handed and folded to us on the Button, K8o is comfortably within a profitable opening range. With position and a big stack versus blinds that can be pressured, I prefer raising over limping to take initiative, win blinds often, and play a pot in position when called. Sizing to 250 (2.5x) is standard at 50/100 and puts immediate pressure on the SB/BB without overinflating the pot with a medium-strength hand.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kd Ks) in the Small Blind, the second-best starting hand. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader and playing very aggressively/widely, has opened to 250. I must 3-bet here to extract value from their wide range and to isolate them, avoiding a multi-way pot out of position. A standard 3-bet size from the Small Blind against a Button open is around 3.5x to 4x. I will raise to 900. This size denies equity to the Big Blind and builds a pot while I have a premium hand.

K6o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Pro raised to 900 over FiveTwo's open, indicating strength. K6o is dominated by most 3-betting ranges (AK, KQ, KJ, big pairs). With 800 to call into a 1250 pot, the pot odds don't justify calling with such a weak offsuit king. Easy fold.

Facing a sizable SB 3-bet to 900 (relative to my 2.5x open) with K8o, this is a dominated, low-realization hand. Calling creates a low SPR pot where I’ll be forced into tough spots and often have to fold postflop, and when I do make a pair it’s frequently dominated by Pro’s value-heavy 3-bet range. A 4-bet bluff would need an unrealistic fold frequency given his large sizing and 50bb stack. Best play is to relinquish the 250 and keep my stack for better steal/pressure spots.

4-handed we should open fairly wide from UTG, especially with a big stack edge. Td8h is not a premium, but it has decent playability and benefits from fold equity versus the blinds/button. I’ll use a small standard open to apply pressure while keeping the risk low; if I face a sizable 3-bet (especially from Claude/Flash), I can comfortably fold and preserve my chip lead.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) on the Button, a premium hand. The UTG player (FiveTwo) opened to 250. This is the exact same dynamic as the previous hand (#133) where I 3-bet to 900 and FiveTwo folded. While varying my play by flat-calling is an option to trap or keep the opponent's range wide, 3-betting is generally superior to build the pot for value, isolate the opener, and reduce the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) which benefits an overpair. Since I just 3-bet the previous hand, an immediate repeat aggression might induce a light 4-bet or a loose call from the large-stacked opener who might suspect I am making a play. I will raise to 850 (roughly 3.4x the open) to extract value and define ranges.

KQo facing a 3-bet from Pro after UTG open. This is a strong hand but in a tough spot OOP. KQo plays well postflop and has good equity against 3-bet ranges. Folding seems too tight, 4-betting would be turning this into a bluff since I'm OOP and could face 5-bet. Calling to see a flop is the best line - I can make strong top pairs and potentially outplay postflop.

I am holding Jack-Trey suited in the Big Blind. UTG opened, Button 3-bet to 850, and the Small Blind cold-called. While I have decent pot odds to call (750 into a 2050 pot), the strength of my hand is very low relative to the action. I am likely dominated by any Jack in their ranges, and playing this hand out of position against three opponents in a 3-bet pot is a high-variance, negative expected value play. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, folding is the most disciplined and correct choice.

We’re facing a 3-bet from Pro (who is relatively shorter) plus a cold-call from Claude, and we’d have to continue out of position in a bloated pot with T8o. Even though the direct price (600 into ~2050) looks tempting, our equity realization is poor multiway: we’re often dominated when we make a pair, and we don’t have suitedness/connectivity strong enough to justify taking a tough OOP spot. With a large stack advantage, there’s no need to defend this hand here; folding preserves our leverage for better +EV spots.

Flop is Ah Ts As - paired aces. I have KQ high which completely missed. Pro 3-bet preflop so his range is strong - likely has many aces (AK, AQ, AJ), pocket pairs, and some broadways. On this board, betting accomplishes nothing - I can't get value and won't fold out better hands. Checking is standard here. If Pro checks back I might have the best hand against lower pocket pairs. If he bets, I'll need to fold as I have no pair and no draws.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) on an A-A-T board. The pot is 2050 and effective stacks are around 5400 (SPR ~2.6). I 3-bet preflop and was cold-called by the Small Blind. The SB's cold-calling range typically consists of pocket pairs (TT-QQ), strong broadways, and occasionally trapped aces (AQ, AK). 

This is a classic 'way ahead / way behind' scenario. I am well ahead of pairs like QQ and JJ, but drawing to 2 outs against any Ax or TT. Because the board is paired with Aces, it is difficult to get value from worse hands if I bet, as underpairs will be wary, and better hands (Ax) will never fold. Additionally, betting opens me up to a check-raise which would put me in a miserable spot. 

Checking back controls the pot size, disguises my hand strength, and may induce bluffs on later streets or allow me to extract value from underpairs on the turn/river when they feel safer. I block AK heavily, reducing the combos of strong Aces, but the risk of an Ace in the SB flatting range is real enough to warrant caution.

Board now shows three aces (Ah As Ad Ts). With trip aces on board, my KQ plays as AAA-KQ. Pro's 3-bet range is concerning - if he has any pocket pair, he has a full house. If he has an ace, he has quads. My KQ kickers only beat unpaired broadway hands with worse kickers. Betting accomplishes nothing - I can't fold out full houses or quads, and I might get raised off the best hand against random broadways. Checking maintains pot control and lets me potentially get to showdown cheaply.

I hold Pocket Kings on a board of [Ah Ts As Ad]. The board now contains three Aces. My hand strength is Aces full of Kings (A-A-A-K-K), which is an incredibly strong hand. I beat any full house made with pocket pairs like QQ (A-A-A-Q-Q) or TT (A-A-A-T-T), and I crush any non-paired hands. I only lose to the case Ace (Ac), which would give the opponent Quads. Given the preflop action (SB cold-called my 3-bet), his range is heavily weighted towards pocket pairs (JJ-QQ) which are now drawing dead to me but have showdown value. Betting here likely folds out those worse hands as they will fear the Ace. Checking back disguises my strength, avoids opening myself up to a check-raise from the rare Ace, and gives the opponent a chance to either bluff the river or value-bet a worse full house (like QQ) on the river thinking I have a missed high card hand. I will check back to extract maximum value on the river.

River 4h completes the board: Ah Ts As Ad 4h. Three aces on board means everyone has trip aces minimum. My KQ gives me AAA-K-Q kickers. Pro 3-bet preflop, so his range is weighted toward pocket pairs (KK-JJ which make full houses), aces (which make quads), and TT/44 (full houses). The only hands I beat are unpaired broadways with worse kickers (KJ, QJ, JT). Given Pro's 3-bet range is heavily weighted toward hands that beat me (pairs=boats, aces=quads), betting for thin value is too risky. I'll check - if he checks back I often win with my kickers, if he bets I can fold knowing his range crushes mine here.

I hold Pocket Kings on a final board of Ah-Ts-As-Ad-4h, giving me Aces full of Kings (A-A-A-K-K). I am only beaten by the single remaining Ace (Case Ace) which would make Quads. My opponent, playing from the Small Blind, cold-called my 3-bet pre-flop, putting him on a range heavily weighted toward pocket pairs (JJ-QQ) and perhaps some broadway hands. He has checked to me on all three streets. While a value bet could target hands like QQ or JJ, those hands might fold fearing I hold the Ace (Quads) or Kings, given my pre-flop strength and the scary board texture. Additionally, betting opens the door to a disastrous scenario if he holds the Case Ace. Following my previous assessment to control the pot and realize my equity against his underpairs, checking back guarantees the win against his likely holdings (JJ-QQ) without risking a fold from worse or a cooler against the nuts.

I am in UTG position with 7c Td, which is a very weak off-suit disconnected hand. Even in a 4-handed game, this hand is far below the opening threshold, especially with 75 BBs where I can afford to wait for better spots. The stack sizes of opponents, particularly the massive stack in the Big Blind, discourage loose opens. The correct play is to fold immediately.

J9o on the button is a solid hand to open. Pro folded UTG, so I'm first to act. With position and only the blinds behind, this is a standard steal spot. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to try to take down the blinds or play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 6c. The button (Claude) has raised to 250. While 96 suited is a playable hand in some contexts, calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand against the original raiser and potentially the Big Blind (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and very active. With only 15 hands remaining in the tournament and a solid stack, I prefer to avoid marginal spots where I am out of position. Folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities.

Button open is likely wide 4-handed, and we’re getting a great price (150 to win 400). K4s has decent equity and playability (suits/board coverage) to defend the big blind, while 3-betting would bloat the pot OOP with a hand that can be dominated when called/4-bet. Best is to call and realize equity, using our big stack advantage postflop.

We flop trips on a paired king board (KK3) with K4, which is strong but not invulnerable: any better Kx (A/Q/J/T kicker) has us outkicked, and 33/K3 already has a full house. Versus a wide BTN open 4-handed, Claude will c-bet this texture at a high frequency with lots of air and small pairs, so leading would often fold out bluffs and mostly continue only stronger hands. Checking keeps his range wide, lets him bet his bluffs, and allows us to control pot size OOP while still having strong equity plus outs to improve to a boat/quads (K/3/4).

Flop is 3d Ks Kc - a paired king board that's very dry. As the preflop aggressor with position, this is a textbook c-bet spot. FiveTwo's BB calling range mostly misses this board. My J9 has no showdown value but I can represent strength. A small c-bet of ~40% pot should take this down often enough to be profitable.

We have trips (KKK) with a weak kicker, so the hand is strong but not a great candidate to build a huge pot OOP versus a BTN range that contains plenty of better Kx (AK/KQ/KJ/KT) and some 3x/full houses. Claude’s small c-bet on a paired board is likely a high-frequency range bet, so raising mostly folds out his air and continues his stronger/value-heavy range, while calling keeps bluffs and marginal hands in, controls pot size, and preserves our ability to improve to a boat/quads on later streets.

Turn 2h is a brick on K-K-3 and doesn’t meaningfully change ranges. With K4 we have trips but a weak kicker, so building a big pot OOP is risky against a BTN range that contains plenty of better Kx (AK/KQ/KJ/KT) and some full houses (33/K3). Claude’s flop sizing looks like a high-frequency range c-bet; checking again keeps his air and thin-value hands (A-high, small pairs) betting, lets us realize our equity and boat/quads outs, and preserves pot control. If we lead, we often fold out the hands we want to keep bluffing and mostly get action from stronger parts of his range.

Turn is 2h, a blank. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet but checked again. They could have a small pair, a float, or occasionally a king. With J-high I have no showdown value. I can either give up and check, or continue barreling to fold out floats and small pairs. Given the dry board texture and that many hands that called the flop still can't beat K-high, I'll fire another barrel around 55% pot to maintain pressure and fold out hands like 44-QQ or floats.

We have trips on K-K-3-2 with K4, which is strong enough to continue but our kicker is weak, so raising would mostly isolate ourselves versus better Kx (AK/KQ/KJ/KT) and occasional full houses (33/K3), while folding out the bluffs and thin-value hands that make this line profitable. Claude’s small flop c-bet and turn barrel can still contain a lot of air and medium strength that will keep betting, and we’re getting a good price (550 to win 2100). Best is to call, keep his range wide, and re-evaluate on the river based on sizing/runout.

River improves us from trips to a very strong full house: KKK44. The only realistic hand that beats us is pocket KK (quads), which is a tiny part of a BTN opening range, and this runout gives Claude plenty of hands that can pay (any Kx for trips, any 3x/4x for an underfull, and some sticky pairs that may bluff-catch). If we check, Claude can often check back air/marginal hands and we lose value, so leading is best. I’ll use a solid value size (~75% pot) that gets called by Kx/boats without pricing out too much of his range. If raised large, I can strongly consider just calling (keeping in rare bluffs), as we’re essentially at the top of our range but don’t want to stack off unnecessarily versus the only real value threat (KK).

FiveTwo check-called flop and turn, then leads river for 1600 into a 2050 pot. This is a strong line - after passively calling my barrels, they're now betting into the aggressor. This polarizes their range to either value (Kx, full house, slow-played pair) or a bluff. With J-high I have zero showdown value and lose to any pair. Bluff-raising would be extremely risky against this line from a player who's shown they want to put money in. The smart play is to fold and save chips.

J7s from UTG is too weak to open, even 4-handed. It's a marginal suited one-gapper that plays poorly from early position against 3 remaining players, including FiveTwo's massive stack who has been aggressive. Fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with Queen-Seven offsuit, this is a marginal hand that is generally at the bottom of a standard opening range in a 4-handed game. Considering the current tournament standings, FiveTwo (the chip leader) is in the small blind and has been very active, and Pro (the short stack) is in the big blind and likely to defend aggressively to stay alive. Entering a pot with a weak hand like Q7o out of position against the chip leader or into a potential shove from the short stack is not ideal. I will fold and wait for a higher-equity opportunity to improve my position as we approach the end of the tournament.

Folds to us in the small blind heads-up versus a shorter big blind. J5o isn’t a great limping hand because it makes dominated top pairs and gives BB a free look; as the bigger stack we should apply pressure and take initiative with a wide stealing range. I’ll open small (2.5x) to target immediate folds and keep the risk low—if BB shoves, this hand is an easy fold.

The opponent in the Small Blind (FiveTwo) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. I am in the Big Blind with [4h Kd]. In a blind-vs-blind scenario, ranges are typically quite wide. My hand, K4 offsuit, has reasonable equity against a wide opening range. Furthermore, I have position on the opener post-flop, and the pot odds are favorable (calling 150 to win a pot that will be 500, requiring ~30% equity). Given my stack depth (over 70 BBs), calling is a standard defense to realize equity in position.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on Q-J-6 rainbow, I have a medium-strength made hand (2nd pair) that benefits from denying equity to overcards/gutters (AT/KT/T9, etc.) and can still get calls from worse pairs (6x, pocket pairs like 77-TT) and some floats. I’ll use a small c-bet to keep the pot manageable and simplify decisions; if Pro check-raises, my kicker is too weak to continue and I can comfortably fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K4 offsuit. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) raised pre-flop, and I defended. The flop comes Q-J-6 rainbow. This texture connects well with the Small Blind's opening range, which likely contains many broadway cards. FiveTwo continues with a bet of 175 into a pot of 500. I have only King-high with no immediate straight or flush draws. My hand has very low playability on future streets, and I am drawing thin against any pair. Although the price is cheap, floating here with K-high and a weak kicker on a board that favors the aggressor is not profitable. I will fold.

I am UTG with 8s 4c, which is a very weak hand. Given the short-handed nature of the table (4 players), I should still play conservatively with junk hands from early position to protect my stack as the tournament nears its end. Fold is the standard and correct play here.

4-handed and folded to us on the Button, KTo is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ random holdings and plays well in position. With significant stack advantage over both blinds, an open-raise leverages fold equity and keeps us as the aggressor, while still being a size that we can comfortably fold to a large 3-bet if needed. I’ll use a standard 2.5x to pressure the blinds and build a pot when called.

In the Small Blind with pocket Sevens (7d 7h), facing a 2.5x open from the aggressive chip leader on the Button. With a stack of ~72BB, this is a bit too deep to shove effectively, and a 3-bet bloats the pot out of position with a hand that is vulnerable to overcards. Calling is the standard play here to set-mine (excellent implied odds given effective stacks) and play a controlled pot against a wide button range. I will call the additional 200 chips.

KQo is a strong hand in the BB facing a button raise and SB call. With 150 to call into a 600 pot, I have excellent pot odds, but 3-betting for value is the better play here. KQo plays well as a 3-bet against button opening ranges, and I can isolate or build a pot with a premium holding. Raising to 850 is a standard 3-bet size.

Claude’s 3-bet to 850 is relatively small versus my BTN open and the pot already contains Pro’s cold-call, so this can be a squeeze with a fairly wide range. With KTo 4-handed I’m still high enough in my opening range to defend, and being in position lets me realize equity and control pot size postflop (I can fold to heavy pressure on bad textures where I’m dominated). A 4-bet with KTo is possible but unnecessarily inflates variance; calling keeps weaker parts of Claude’s squeeze range in and preserves my positional edge.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Sevens (7d 7h). Facing a squeeze to 850 from the Big Blind (Claude) and a call from the Button (FiveTwo), the pot is currently 1950. I have already contributed 250 chips, so it costs me 600 more to call. This gives me direct pot odds of approximately 23.5% (600/2550). While my raw equity against a 3-bet range and a calling range is reasonable (approx 33%), my main goal is to set-mine. My remaining stack of roughly 7,000 gives me implied odds of over 11:1 (Stack/Call = ~11.6), which is acceptable for set mining in a multi-way pot, especially since the SPR will be low enough (~2.7) to easily get stacks in if I hit. Being out of position is a disadvantage, but my play is straightforward: fit-or-fold. The price is too good to pass up for a chance to triple up.

I am out of position in a 3-way squeezed pot with pocket Sevens on a Jack-high board (5h 3c Jc). The SPR is low (~2.5). My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards and stronger pairs in the opponents' ranges (especially the BB squeezer who likely has AJ+ or higher pairs). Leading out (donking) makes no sense as I fold out worse hands and get called by better. Checking is the standard line to the pre-flop aggressor. If facing a bet, I will likely have to fold given the multi-way action and board texture, as my hand is unlikely to improve significantly.

I 3-bet preflop with KQo and got called by two players. The flop 5h3cJc completely missed me - I have just two overcards with no club for the flush draw. Against two opponents on a coordinated board with a flush draw, c-betting with zero made hand equity is burning money. Check and see a turn, potentially hitting a K or Q.

3-bet pot goes 3-ways and both opponents check on J♣-5♥-3♣. K♠T♣ has no made hand, but we do have two overcards plus a backdoor club draw, and position. Claude (preflop aggressor) checking multiway caps him a bit (more give-ups/overcards), and Pro’s cold-call range contains hands we can fold out with a cheap stab (AQ/AK without clubs, some mid pairs that don’t love this texture). A small continuation stab needs a low fold frequency to show immediate profit and keeps our risk controlled versus Pro’s potential check-jam or Claude’s check-raise (we can comfortably fold to aggression).

I entered this pot primarily to set-mine against the 3-bet, getting excellent implied odds. The flop [5h 3c Jc] did not give me a set. While I have an overpair to the 3 and 5, the presence of the Jack and the action suggest caution. The Button (chip leader) bet 700 into a pot of 2550 (approx 27%) into two opponents, one of whom is the pre-flop 3-bettor. Although the sizing is small and tempting (offering ~21% pot odds), I am sandwiched. The BB (Claude), who 3-bet pre-flop, is still left to act behind me. He could easily have a better pair (88-AA, AJ) or check-raise. My hand (77) is vulnerable to overcards and draws, and I am drawing to 2 outs against any Jack or higher pair. Sticking to the 'fit-or-fold' plan in this 3-bet multi-way pot out of position is the disciplined play to preserve my healthy 63BB stack.

FiveTwo bets 700 into 2550 (~27% pot) after I checked. I have KQo with no pair, no draw. I'm getting 4.6:1 pot odds needing ~18% equity. With just 6 overcard outs (~13% on turn), I'm not getting the right price. FiveTwo likely has a jack, set, or club draw. Folding is correct - calling here with air to chase overcards is -EV, especially against an aggressive opponent who could fire again on the turn.

4-handed we can open fairly wide, but T5o from UTG is near the bottom of the range and plays poorly (dominated by most Tx/5x that continue) and will often realize equity badly out of position versus a Button flat or a blind defend/3-bet. With a large chip lead there’s no need to take thin, high-variance spots here—better to preserve stack and apply pressure with hands that have better playability/blockers.

I am on the Button with K3o. FiveTwo folded. While the Button is a great position to steal blinds, K3o is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability and significant reverse implied odds due to the weak kicker. My stack is 63.75 BBs, so I am not desperate. The blinds (Claude and Flash) have deeper stacks and can defend or 3-bet, putting me in a difficult spot. Standard strategy dictates folding K3o here to wait for a better opportunity.

J4o is a weak hand. Heads up against the BB, I could complete for 50 more, but J4o plays poorly post-flop and has very limited equity. Folding saves chips for better spots - 12 hands left in tournament.

Current hand is QJs (Queen of Hearts, Jack of Hearts) in the UTG position (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play). My stack is 6,375 (64 BB). This is a strong suited broadway hand that falls well within a standard opening range for 4-handed play. I want to build the pot and take the initiative rather than limping. A standard open raise size in this tournament has been 2.5x (250). I will raise to 250 to value my hand and deny equity to random holdings.

J7o is a weak hand. Pro raised from UTG which typically represents a strong range. Even with position on the button, J7o doesn't play well enough to profitably call here - I'll often be dominated by better Jacks and the hand has poor playability postflop. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. Pro, the UTG player, has raised to 250. Pro's stack is relatively short at 6125. While K7o has some high card value, it is a weak hand to play out of position against a UTG raise. Calling puts me in a spot where I will likely be out of position for the rest of the hand with a dominated King or a weak kicker. Folding is the most prudent play here to preserve my stack of 9450 as we approach the end of the tournament.

We’re getting a very good price in the big blind: 150 to win 400 (~27% required). In 4-handed play Pro’s UTG open should be fairly wide, and even 95o has enough raw equity to defend profitably. A 3-bet bluff here isn’t attractive because 95o plays poorly when called and we’d be building a bigger OOP pot versus an opener with decent stack depth. Best is to defend and realize our pot odds, then proceed cautiously postflop.

Pro’s flop check on 3c-8c-8h caps him away from a lot of strong value, while my BB defend range contains plenty of 8x (and some 3x) that can credibly lead. With 9h5d I have essentially no showdown value and poor realization if I check back and let Pro realize with his overcards. A small stab targets his unpaired broadways/A-high, denies equity, and keeps my risk low; if I face a check-raise I can comfortably fold this hand.

I hold QJs on a 3c 8c 8h board. The board is paired and connects reasonably well with a Big Blind defense range (which includes many 8x, 3x, and flush draws). The opponent, who is the chip leader, leads out (donk bets) for 200 into a 550 pot. While the price is cheap, I have only two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. I have no immediate pair or strong draw. Floating with Queen-high against an active chip leader on a paired board is risky and can lead to difficult spots on later streets. Preserving my 60+ BB stack for better opportunities is the prudent play.
72o is the worst starting hand in poker. UTG position with 4 players, no reason to play this. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with Ten-Nine offsuit in a 4-handed game. Claude has folded. Ten-Nine is a standard opening hand from the button to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind, which is consistent with the standard opening size seen in this tournament.

We’re facing a BTN open to 250 with 8♠4♦ offsuit from the small blind. Even getting a seemingly decent price, this hand has very poor playability out of position, is easily dominated when we make a pair, and performs badly in multiway pots if the BB comes along. A light 3-bet squeeze is possible given our big stack, but 84o is too low-equity and too hard to realize; it also risks getting flatted in position or facing a shove from the shorter BB. Best EV is to release and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 2.5BB open raise from Flash on the Button. My hand is Q7o (Queen-Seven offsuit). The pot odds are approximately 27% (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550). While Q7o has roughly 45% raw equity against a wide button opening range, I will be playing out of position post-flop, which significantly hinders equity realization. Furthermore, Q7o suffers from reverse implied odds as it is dominated by many better Queens (KQ, QJ, QT, Q9) in the opponent's range. Against a 2.5x raise, standard strategy suggests folding Q7o and defending with slightly stronger hands (e.g., Q9o+, Q5s+). With only 10 hands left in the tournament, preserving chips is preferable to engaging in a marginally -EV or neutral EV spot out of position.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in the first position to act (UTG) in a four-handed game. With only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack of 96 big blinds, there is no reason to play this hand or attempt a high-risk bluff from this position. The standard and most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

4-handed on the Button after UTG folds, this is a prime steal spot. A4o is comfortably within a wide BTN opening range: it has an Ace blocker (reduces frequency of strong 3-bets), can make top pair and some wheel potential, and plays fine in position versus blinds. With a big stack versus two shorter stacks, applying pressure and accumulating blinds is high EV. I’ll use a standard small open that still pressures the blinds without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a possible 3-bet.

I hold King-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and aggressive, has raised to 250. My hand is strong (top ~10%) and dominates much of a standard Button opening range. However, I am out of position and have the shortest stack (though still deep at ~60BB). A 3-bet here is a viable option for value, but it risks bloating the pot out of position or facing a 4-bet that would force me to fold a good hand. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide (including hands I dominate like KJo, QJo, weaker Kx/Qx) and see a flop with a manageable Pot-to-Stack ratio. I will call and look to play post-flop.

8s5s in the BB facing a min-raise and a call. Getting 4:1 pot odds (600 pot, 150 to call) with a suited connector. While 85s is marginal, closing the action with good pot odds and some implied odds if I hit makes this a profitable defend. Can flop straights, flushes, or two pair.

On a Flop of Jh 5c 5d, I hold King-Queen high. This is a fairly dry, paired board. Being out of position in a 3-way pot, I have no reason to lead out (donk bet). My hand has decent showdown value against air but is behind any pair. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) and see what happens. I will check and evaluate based on their actions.

I flopped trip fives with 85s - a monster hand! The board is Jh 5c 5d. Pro has checked, and I'm in great shape to trap. FiveTwo was the preflop raiser and likely has broadway cards or a pocket pair. If they have Jx, they'll probably c-bet. Checking to induce a bet from FiveTwo is optimal here - I can then either call to keep them in or check-raise depending on sizing. No need to bet and potentially scare everyone away with trips.

We’re 3‑way on a paired board (J55) with A4o and no real draw. In a multiway pot, a flop c-bet gets called by almost all better hands (any Jx, any pocket pair, any 5x) and mainly folds out the hands we already beat (random broadways/air), so betting doesn’t accomplish much and risks getting check-raised by trips/strong Jx. Checking back keeps the pot small, realizes our ace-high showdown value, and lets us take a stab on many turns if they check again.

On the Turn, the board is [Jh 5c 5d 9d]. I have picked up significant equity with the [Qs Kd]: I now have the second-nut flush draw, a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), and two overcards. Despite having 15+ outs, playing out of position in a multi-way pot suggests caution. The flop checked through, indicating likely weakness from opponents, but leading out risks a raise that could force me to fold my equity or commit too many chips with just a draw. Checking gives me the option to see a free river or call a bet with excellent pot odds given my strong draw.

I have trip fives - a monster hand. Everyone checked the flop, so no one showed strength. On the turn with 9d adding a diamond draw, I should lead out for value rather than risk another check-through. Pro checked, and FiveTwo (the preflop raiser) might have Jx, a pocket pair, or even a diamond draw they'd call with. Betting around 60% pot to extract value from Jx hands or draws while not scaring them away.

Claude’s 450 lead into 1200 after a flop check-through looks like a classic probe: it can be thin value (9x, small pocket pairs) but also a decent amount of air trying to fold out two overcards. Folding A-high is likely a bit too tight given the price (need ~27% equity) and our positional advantage. Raising as a bluff doesn’t make much sense multiway because any Jx/9x/pair continues and Pro can wake up behind. Best is to call and re-evaluate rivers: fold to significant further aggression, but if it checks through we can often win at showdown vs missed stabs and sometimes consider a small river bet if both show weakness.

I hold [Qs Kd] on a [Jh 5c 5d 9d] board. I checked the turn, the Big Blind bet 450 into the 750 pot, and the Button called. The pot is now 1650, and I need to call 450 to close the action. This gives me pot odds of 450/2100 ≈ 21.4%. I have a King-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), giving me roughly 12 clean outs, plus potential outs with my King and Queen overcards. My equity is likely around 25-30%, making a call clearly profitable. As the short stack late in the tournament (Hand 141/150), preserving chips while taking +EV spots is crucial. A raise is too risky given the stack depths and multi-way action, but folding is incorrect given the price.

On the River, the board is [Jh 5c 5d 9d Js]. I hold [Qs Kd], giving me Two Pair (Jacks and Fives) with a King kicker. The double-paired board counterfeits any pair of 9s (or lower pocket pairs like 88) that opponents might hold, meaning I now beat hands like T9 or 88 which might have called the turn. I lose to any Ace-high, any 5 (Full House), or any Jack (Quads/Full House). Since the pot is multi-way and Claude showed strength on the Turn, betting achieves little value as better hands will call and worse hands (missed draws) will fold. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value against missed draws or counterfeited pairs without risking more chips.

I have fives full of jacks (55JJ). However, the river Jack is concerning - anyone with a Jack now has jacks full of fives which beats me. FiveTwo raised preflop and could easily have AJ/KJ/QJ/JT. Pro could also have Jx. I should make a small value bet to get called by pocket pairs (66-TT, QQ+) or 9x, but keep it modest since I'm often beat if raised. Around 1/3 pot seems right for thin value.

We’re bluff-catching with pure A-high in a multiway spot, and that’s the key problem: we don’t just need Claude to be bluffing often enough, we also need Pro (who checked) to not have a pair that can overcall and beat us. Claude’s line (check flop, small probe turn, small value/blocker bet river) is very consistent with thin value like 9x or some small pair, and the small sizing also encourages Pro to peel with any pair. With no relevant blockers to Claude’s likely value range and very poor realization when Pro can still call, the 700 price isn’t actually “cheap” in EV terms. Best play is to let it go and preserve stack for higher-leverage spots late in the set.

I hold [Qs Kd] on a final board of [Jh 5c 5d 9d Js]. My hand is effectively Two Pair (Jacks and Fives) with a King kicker. The board is double-paired, meaning any Ace, any 9, any 5, any Jack, and any pocket pair 66+ beats me. I only beat missed straight/flush draws or hands playing the board with a worse kicker (like QT or lower pocket pairs 22-44). Claude bet into two opponents on the Turn and led out again on the River for 700 into a pot of 2100. While the price is good (4:1 pot odds), the likelihood of Claude betting into two players on the River with a hand worse than King-high is very low. His small bet sizing (1/3 pot) typically indicates thin value (like an Ace or a 9) rather than a polarized bluff. Given that an Ace high beats me and is a very common holding in a Big Blind defense range, I cannot profitably call. Preserving my stack of ~5300 chips is preferable to making a low-probability hero call.

4-handed, UTG ranges are much wider and AdQd (AQ suited) is a premium open with strong top-pair value, nut-flush potential, and good playability in position postflop versus the blinds. Limping gives up initiative and invites multiway pots; raising lets us leverage our big stack, deny equity, and set up profitable continuation lines. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to keep the pot manageable while still applying pressure.

I am on the Button with 5s 6c (off-suit one-gapper). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader, has raised to 250. My hand is extremely weak, especially against a UTG opening range. Although I have position, calling 2.5 BB with this hand is not profitable, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky against the chip leader who can call widely or 4-bet shove. With only 8 hands left and being the shortest stack (though still >50 BB), preserving chips for better opportunities is the correct strategy. I will fold.

K5 suited in SB facing an UTG raise to 250. Getting decent pot odds (200 to win 400) with a suited king. K5s has playability - can flop flush draws, top pair, or backdoor equity. While being OOP is not ideal, the price is right to see a flop. Folding is too tight here with suited broadway potential.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7-5 offsuit. FiveTwo, the chip leader, opened for 250, and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 5:1 pot odds to call the remaining 150, 7-5 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack of 95BB, there is no need to enter a marginal or losing situation with a junk hand. I will preserve my chips for better opportunities.

Flopped nothing with K5 clubs on Qs 2s 4d. No pair, no draw - my clubs don't connect with this spade board. No reason to bet as I have zero equity worth protecting. Check and likely give up if FiveTwo c-bets.

Heads-up vs Claude (SB) after we opened UTG; he checks to us. We flop top pair top kicker (AQ) on a fairly dry Q-2-4 board with some spade-draw potential. We want to bet for value vs worse Qx, pocket pairs, and spade floats, while also denying equity to random overcards. A small c-bet keeps his range wide and controls pot size in case he check-raises.

I have K5 clubs on Qs 2s 4d - complete air. No pair, no draw (my clubs don't help on this spade board). FiveTwo's 200 into 600 is a small c-bet but I have zero equity. Floating OOP with K-high and no backdoor draws is -EV. Easy fold.

At Hand 143/150 in a 4-handed game, I am in the UTG (Cutoff) position with Ten-Eight suited (Th8h). While this hand has some playability and suited connectors/gappers are often opened from this position in 4-handed play, I am currently the short stack with roughly 53 big blinds. The player on the Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader with a massive stack and has been playing actively, and the Button (Claude) has shown capability to 3-bet. Opening a marginal hand like T8s from first position risks getting 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position against deep stacks. With only a few hands remaining, preserving my stack for higher equity spots is preferable to taking marginal +EV lines that carry high variance and reverse implied odds. Therefore, I decide to fold.

On the button with Q9o after UTG folded. This is a standard steal spot with a playable hand. Q9o plays well in position against the blinds. I'll raise to 2.5x to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take it down preflop or play in position postflop.

I am holding Jack-Nine offsuit in the small blind. Claude, the button, has raised to 250. While J9o has some playability, it is a weak hand to defend from the small blind, especially being out of position for the remainder of the hand. With only a few hands left in the tournament and being in a middle-stack position, it's better to avoid marginal spots and preserve chips for more favorable opportunities.

Claude opens the button to 2.5x and we’re getting a very good price (150 to win 400), so we can profitably defend quite wide. A3o has decent equity vs a button opening range (ace-high blocker, some top-pair potential, wheel/straight possibilities) and as the big stack we don’t need to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand via a 3-bet that can get played back at. Calling keeps ranges wide, realizes equity, and preserves stack leverage for better spots.

We flop top pair but with a very weak kicker (A3) on an A-Q-x texture where the button has a strong range advantage (lots of Ax, AQ, QQ, strong broadways). Leading doesn’t get called by much worse and risks building a pot where we’re often dominated when action escalates. By checking we keep Claude’s bluffs and weaker hands in, control pot size OOP, and can evaluate versus his c-bet sizing (comfortably continue versus small/standard bets, but avoid getting stacked versus heavy pressure).

I flopped middle pair with Q9 on a 4-Q-A board. FiveTwo checked to me from the BB. The Ace is concerning but I have a decent made hand. I should bet for value and protection against draws. A half-pot bet of around 275 allows me to get value from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable if I'm behind an Ace.

Claude’s ~1/2-pot c-bet (275 into ~550) is a very standard range-bet on A-Q-x where he has lots of bluffs and broadways that missed. With top pair we’re getting strong immediate odds and we don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with A3 (raising mostly isolates ourselves versus better Ax/AQ/sets while folding out the hands we beat). Calling keeps his bluffs in, controls pot size, and lets us re-evaluate on turns—continuing versus reasonable sizing but being prepared to fold to heavy multi-street pressure on bad runouts.

We’re OOP with a marginal made hand (top pair A with a very weak kicker) but we picked up a huge equity boost on the Td because Ad gives us the nut flush draw (plus a couple outs to trips). This is a classic spot to keep Claude’s range wide and let him continue barreling his bluffs/semibluffs (KJ/JT/diamond floats) rather than donk-leading and often isolating ourselves vs better Ax/AQ/sets when action escalates. By checking, we control pot growth with a vulnerable one-pair hand while still being able to respond aggressively if he bets (call most reasonable sizings; consider a check-raise mix depending on sizing and his tendencies).

Turn brought Td which adds straight draws (JK makes a straight). I have second pair with Q9 which has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet three streets. FiveTwo called my flop bet and could have: a weak ace, a better queen, draws, or be slowplaying. The board is getting dangerous and I don't want to inflate the pot out of position strength-wise. Checking back for pot control and to see a cheap showdown is the prudent play here.

River is A♠-Q♣-4♦-T♦-T♣ and we hold A♦3h: one pair (aces) with a very weak kicker; our nut diamond draw missed. After Claude c-bets flop and then checks back the turn, his range is capped and contains a lot of give-ups (KJ/JT, some Qx) but also plenty of Ax that pot-controlled the turn (A5–AK, AQ). If we bet here, we get called by almost all better aces and only get value from hands we already beat that may just fold (many Qx), while worse aces that can call are basically only A2. Turning it into a bluff is unnecessary given we already win at showdown versus his missed broadways/Qx, and as chip leader we don’t need to force thin, high-variance spots. Best is to check and realize our showdown value, then respond if he fires.

River paired tens making board 4d-Qc-Ah-Td-Tc. I have second pair (queens) with a weak kicker. FiveTwo checked again. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet - any Ace, any Ten, or better Queen beats me. Betting would fold out worse hands and only get called by better. Checking back to realize my showdown equity is the correct play.

J9o from UTG in 4-handed play is a marginal hand. While ranges widen 4-handed, J9o is still weak from early position. With 6 hands left in the tournament and a comfortable 120BB stack, no need to take unnecessary risks with a speculative hand that plays poorly postflop OOP. Folding to preserve stack.

I am on the button with Qh 4h, which is a marginal but suited hand in a 4-handed game. Claude has folded, and I have the opportunity to steal the blinds (150 chips). Pro is the shortest stack at the table and might fold to pressure, while I will have position on the chip leader, FiveTwo, if he calls from the small blind. A standard raise to 2.5x is a good way to test the blinds without over-committing chips if someone jams.

4-handed, Button opening range is wide and we’re the big stack. Q9 suited is too strong to fold getting a good price, but flatting from the SB invites the BB in (great odds) and leaves us playing a bloated pot out of position without initiative. A polarized 3-bet uses our stack leverage, often wins preflop, and when called we have a suited, connected hand with decent playability plus a queen blocker. If Flash 4-bets big, we can comfortably fold.

I hold 3h 6s in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand. Facing a button open and a Small Blind 3-bet, I am getting poor pot odds and would be out of position against a strong range. There is no justification for entering this pot. Folding is the only correct play.

I originally opened for 250 with Qh4h from the button, but FiveTwo, the table chip leader, has 3-bet to 950 from the small blind. Qh4h is a very weak hand to defend against a large 3-bet, especially when facing the chip leader who can apply maximum pressure. With only a few hands remaining in the tournament and several players with smaller stacks than me, there is no reason to risk a significant portion of my stack with a dominated hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am dealt a pocket pair of eights (8s 8d) under the gun in a four-handed game. Pocket eights are a strong hand in this configuration. With 92 big blinds, I have plenty of room to play. My goal is to open the pot for value and to define the hand. Looking at recent history, FiveTwo (the chip leader) has been quite active and aggressive with 3-bets, but I must still play my range correctly. A standard open to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

4-handed, Flash is opening from first position to 3bb and has shown a willingness to fold to 3-bets (including vs us recently). On the Button with a big stack, J7 suited works well as a light/polarized 3-bet: we leverage position, deny the blinds a cheap entry, and can win the pot pre-flop often enough. If Flash 4-bets we comfortably fold; if called we still have playable equity and position in a 3-bet pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 4c. The action is heavy: UTG raised to 300 and the Button 3-bet to 900. Facing a raise and a re-raise, my low suited connectors are dominated and I am out of position. I am also the shortest stack with only 5 hands left in the tournament, so preserving my 5175 chips is crucial. I cannot afford to invest nearly 20% of my stack in such a speculative hand against strong ranges. The only correct play is to fold.

Holding 66 facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. With 11850 stack, calling 800 is manageable. FiveTwo has been very active and has a massive stack (32725), giving me excellent implied odds for set mining. I hit a set ~12% of the time, and in a 3-bet pot against a deep-stacked opponent, I can extract significant value when I do. If I miss the flop, I can fold to continued aggression. 4-betting would be too loose and folding is too tight given the implied odds here.

I started the action by raising to 300 with pocket eights. FiveTwo 3-bet to 900, and Claude cold-called from the Big Blind. I am now getting excellent pot odds (600 to call into a pot of 2150) to see a flop. Pocket eights have good set-mining value and the implied odds are high given that there are two other players in the pot. Cold-calling a 3-bet often indicates a range like medium pairs or strong broadways, which my eights can compete with if the flop is favorable. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate.

Missed my set on A-7-5 board. I have an underpair with marginal showdown value. The Ace hits FiveTwo's 3-bet range heavily. Checking is standard here - I can't bet for value and don't want to bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand. If action checks through, my 66 might be good. If FiveTwo c-bets (likely), I'll have to fold as I'm rarely ahead.

On this Ace-high flop (5d-7c-Ah), my pocket eights are now an underpair. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo, who 3-bet from the button. In a 3-bet pot with two opponents, the probability that someone holds an Ace is quite high. Leading out here into the 3-bettor and a cold-caller would likely only result in being called or raised by better hands. I will check to see how the action develops and hope to reach a cheap showdown or fold if facing significant pressure.

Flop is A-high in a 3-bet pot that went multiway (cold-call + caller). This texture heavily favors the callers’ continuing ranges (a lot of Ax and some 7x/PPs), so a c-bet with J7 is rarely a clean value bet and often just inflates the pot when we’re behind. With second pair + some backdoor improvement, we’re better off taking the free card, keeping our range protected with checks, and being ready to bluff-catch some small turn stabs or bet later if it checks through again.

Turn brings Ks, adding another overcard. Board is now A-K-7-5 with two overs to my 66. FiveTwo checked the flop after 3-betting, which is interesting - could be giving up with air, trapping with a big hand, or checking back a medium pair. My 66 has minimal showdown value against a 3-bettor's range on this A-K high board. Betting as a bluff makes little sense as I can't represent much and better hands won't fold. Checking keeps the pot small and gives me a chance to see a cheap showdown or fold to any aggression.

On the turn, the King of Spades is another overcard to my pocket eights. Following a check from Claude, I will also check. My hand has some showdown value against missed broadway combinations, but betting into a 3-bettor and a cold-caller on an Ace-King board is unlikely to fold out better hands or get value from worse. Checking is the best way to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown.

Turn goes check-check to us in a multiway 3-bet pot on A♠/K♠ high texture where both callers have a lot of Ax/Kx that dominate our 7x, plus some slowplays. Our hand (J♦7♦) has decent showdown value but doesn’t get called by many worse hands if we bet (most worse pairs like 88–TT/6x often just fold facing AK), while better hands can call or spring a check-raise and put us in a tough spot. With position and no pressure on us, the highest-EV line is to take the free river, keep the pot small, and be ready to bluff-catch a reasonable river stab or value-bet ourselves if it checks through again on a safe runout.

River brings Qh, making board A-K-Q-7-5. My 66 is essentially a bluff-catcher with no showdown value on this broadway-heavy board. FiveTwo 3-bet preflop then checked flop and turn - this is either a trap with a big hand (AA, KK, QQ, AK, AQ) or giving up with air/medium pairs. Bluffing here is risky because FiveTwo's 3-bet range connects heavily with A-K-Q. If I bet, strong hands call/raise and I only fold out hands I'm already beating (complete air). Checking to showdown gives me a small chance to win if everyone has air, and minimizes losses when I'm beat.

The board has three overcards to my pocket eights (Ace, King, Queen). However, the action has been checked through the flop and turn by both opponents, including the pre-flop 3-bettor (FiveTwo) and the cold-caller (Claude). This sequence suggests that nobody is particularly strong. I have some showdown value against missed draws or lower pocket pairs. Betting into two opponents on this board is unlikely to result in a fold from an Ace, King, or Queen, and I won't get called by many worse hands. Checking is the most logical path to reach a showdown and potentially win the pot with my pair.

Both opponents have checked three streets in a 3-bet pot on an A-K-Q runout, which heavily caps their ranges toward marginal showdown hands (pocket pairs like 88–JJ, some weak Ax that didn’t want to build a pot, occasional floats) rather than strong value that would often bet earlier. Our J♦7♦ has some showdown, but multiway it’s rarely the best hand and checking back leaves a lot of fold equity unrealized. A small, credible river stab leverages our preflop aggressor story (we can still have AK/AQ/KQ/slowplays after checking twice) and targets folds from the exact hands that beat our 7 (mid pairs) while also getting some folds from Qx/Kx that don’t want to face pressure. With our chip lead, we can take this low-risk pressure line and comfortably fold to any raise.

FiveTwo 3-bet preflop, checked flop and turn on A-K-7-5 board, now betting 950 into 2700 pot on Q river. This line is suspicious - either trapping with a big hand (AK, AQ, AA, KK, QQ all in 3-bet range) or bluffing after giving up. My 66 is a pure bluff-catcher on A-K-Q-7-5 board. The small sizing (35% pot) suggests thin value or blocking bet. FiveTwo's 3-bet range crushes this board - most value hands beat me. Getting 4.9:1 pot odds needing ~17% equity, but I don't think I have it often enough against this line. Better to fold and preserve stack with 5 hands left.

The board is incredibly coordinated with three overcards (Ace, King, and Queen) to my pocket eights. While the action was checked on the flop and turn, suggesting weakness from all parties, FiveTwo's river bet of 950 into a 2750 pot is a polarized move. Given that FiveTwo was the pre-flop 3-bettor, his range includes many hands that have now improved or were already ahead (like 99, TT, JJ, or any broadway hand containing an Ace, King, or Queen). Even if FiveTwo is bluffing, my eights are at the very bottom of my range here, and I would even lose to his 'bluffs' if they were medium pocket pairs like nines or tens played cautiously. With only a few hands left in the tournament and my stack at 83 big blinds, calling here with an underpair to three overcards against the chip leader's 3-bet range is a low-probability play. I will fold and preserve my stack.

4-handed UTG (effectively early position) with J2o is at the very bottom of playable holdings. With no antes and only 150 in dead money, opening this hand relies on a very high fold frequency and risks getting called/3-bet into tough, dominated spots where our equity realization is poor. With a massive chip lead, there’s little incentive to take thin, high-variance steals here.

It is late in the tournament (Hand 146/150) and I am on the Button with T8o. Action folded to me. While the Button is a prime stealing position, T8o is a marginal hand that is at the very bottom of or outside a standard opening range, especially against active blinds like Claude and Flash who have significant stacks to defend or 3-bet. With 52 BBs, I am the shortest stack and should avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots with weak holdings that play poorly post-flop even in position. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

4d7h is a weak hand, but heads-up against just the big blind with 3:1 pot odds (50 to win 150), completing is reasonable. I could fold to save 50 chips with this trash hand, but the price is good enough to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 9c. The action folded to the Small Blind (Claude), who merely completed the bet. With a hand like J9o, which has decent high-card value and connectivity, I have the option to raise to take control or check to see a flop. Since I will have position throughout the hand and we are near the end of the tournament, checking is a low-risk way to realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a larger stack.

I flopped bottom pair with 7s on an A-T-7 board. This is a marginal made hand that's vulnerable to many holdings Flash could have. An Ace is likely in their range since they just checked behind preflop. Betting into this board with bottom pair risks getting raised off my hand or called by better. Checking for pot control makes sense - I can call a small bet or check back and see a turn.

I am in position against the Small Blind (Claude) in a limped pot. The flop of Ah-Td-7c contains two high cards, including an Ace. My hand, Jd-9c, has a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8 for a straight). Claude has checked to me. While I could bet to try and take down the small pot, checking back allows me to see a turn card for free, potentially improving to a straight or a pair, while keeping the pot small in a situation where Claude might be trapping with a weak Ace or a Ten.

Turn brought Kc, putting a third overcard on the board (A-K-T all above my 7s). My bottom pair is extremely weak now. Flash checked the flop which could be weakness or slowplay. With such a marginal hand and a scary board, I should check for pot control and hope to see a free showdown. If Flash bets, I'll fold as my hand can't stand pressure.

In this limped pot, the board has become quite heavy with high cards (A, K, T). I hold Jd 9c, which gives me a gutshot draw to a broadway straight (needing a Queen).
