Claude has checked for a second time after completing from the Small Blind. While his range is likely weak, my own hand currently has no showdown value. However, in a 200-chip pot, there is little reason to risk chips on a bluff when I can take a free card in position to try and hit my straight or potentially a Jack on the river. Checking back keeps the pot small and allows me to see the final card for free.

River completed with 6h. I have bottom pair (7s) on A-K-T-7-6 board. Flash has checked every street, suggesting weakness or a marginal hand. My pair of 7s beats missed draws and air but loses to any pair of T, A, K, or 6. With such a marginal hand, checking to showdown is correct - no value in betting since better hands call and worse hands fold.

In this limped pot, the board has run out Td-Ah-7c-Kc-6h. I hold Jack-high (Jd 9c), which has virtually no showdown value. Claude has checked all three streets after completing from the Small Blind. While this indicates weakness, the pot is only 200 chips. At this late stage of the tournament (Hand 146/150), risking chips to bluff for a tiny pot is generally not optimal, especially when my opponent might have a weak pair or even a better high card (like Queen-high) that they are simply checking down. Checking back is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for the final few hands.

Current hand is 147/150. I am UTG in a 4-handed game with T9o. My stack is approximately 51 BBs. T9o is a weak hand to open from under the gun, even short-handed. It plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position, and is dominated by many calling ranges (AT, KT, Q9s, etc.). Given there are only a few hands left, I should preserve chips for better stealing opportunities (like from the button or SB) or value hands rather than leaking chips with marginal openings from early position.

On the button with Q3o after UTG folds. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and only the blinds to act. Q3o is marginal but playable for a button steal. Raising to 2.5x to apply pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. Claude, on the Button, has raised to 250, which is a frequent play for him in late position. Ace-high is generally ahead of a wide button opening range. While A4o is not a premium hand, calling the additional 200 chips provides good pot odds to see a flop. 3-betting is an option but could be risky given the chip-leading Big Blind (FiveTwo) is yet to act. Calling allows me to defend my blind and see if I can improve on the flop while keeping the pot relatively small.

Getting a very good price to defend: 150 to win a pot already ~600 (and we close the action), so we only need to realize roughly ~20% equity. J5s is weak and can be dominated when we make a jack, but the suitedness gives realizable equity (flush/straight possibilities) and as the big stack I can comfortably take this cheap flop and play cautiously postflop (mostly check to the raiser, continue mainly with strong draws/clean pairs, and avoid bloating the pot with marginal top pair). A squeeze here is possible, but J5s isn’t a great candidate and calling keeps variance low while still being +EV given the odds.

On a flop of Kd Jd 6c, my Ace-high (Ac 4d) has missed completely. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Claude) and the Big Blind (FiveTwo), as it contains two high cards and a flush draw. As the Small Blind, I am out of position against both players. The most prudent action is to check and see how the action unfolds. I have no reason to bet into two opponents on a board where I have very little equity.

We have top pair (J) but with a weak kicker (5) on a fairly dynamic board (KJ6 with a diamond draw) and we’re multiway versus the preflop raiser in position. Leading here risks bloating the pot and getting raised into by better Jx/Kx or strong draws, putting us in awkward spots with a marginal made hand. By checking, we keep ranges wide, let Claude c-bet his air/draws, and we can continue mostly by calling reasonable bets while controlling pot size and avoiding high-variance lines with dominated top pair.

Flopped complete air with Q3o on Kd Jd 6c. Both opponents checked showing weakness, but I have zero equity - no pair, no draw. With two opponents still in the hand, a c-bet bluff is risky since the likelihood of at least one having connected with this board is decent. The diamond draw is also possible for either of them. Checking behind to see a free card is the prudent play.

The turn 7s does not improve my hand. Having missed the flop entirely with Ace-high, and with two opponents still active in a three-way pot, there is no merit in betting. My goal is to reach showdown as cheaply as possible if the hand checks down, or to fold to any significant aggression. Since I am out of position, I will check and see how Claude and FiveTwo respond.

Flop checked through, so ranges are still wide and both opponents can have a lot of A/Q-high, underpairs, and straight/diamond-draw type hands that will happily take a free river. With top pair but a weak kicker (Js5s) on a dynamic KJ67 board, I don’t want to give a free card, but I also don’t want to bloat the pot and get raised into by Kx/strong Jx/2p+. A medium-small lead accomplishes three things: (1) denies equity to overcards and draws, (2) gets thin value from worse Jx/7x/6x and pocket pairs, and (3) lets me fold comfortably if faced with significant aggression from the shorter stacks.

Facing a 400 bet from FiveTwo on the turn with Q3o - complete air. I have no pair, no draws, and queen high won't win at showdown. FiveTwo's small sizing (~53% pot) could indicate various holdings, but it doesn't matter - I can't profitably continue with zero equity. Flash still to act behind me as well. Clear fold.

On a board of Kd Jd 6c 7s, my Ace-high has no real value. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has bet 400 into the pot after the flop was checked around. While he could be betting with any two cards sensing weakness, my hand is too weak to continue, especially out of position. I have no draws and Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand here. I will fold to preserve my stack for the final few hands of the tournament.

7d8h is a weak offsuit hand from UTG position. This is not profitable to open-raise in a 4-handed game from first position. With only 3 hands left, I should maintain my stack and wait for better spots. Easy fold.

On the button in a 4-handed game with a pocket pair (22), I have a standard opening hand. With only three hands left in the tournament, I should look to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position to maximize my stack. Claude has folded, leaving only the blinds to act. I will raise to 250 to apply pressure on the shorter stack in the Big Blind (Pro) and the massive stack in the Small Blind (FiveTwo).

Button open to 2.5bb is likely wide, and calling from the SB with T9o invites the BB to overcall and puts us in a tough multiway, out-of-position spot with a marginal hand. With a big stack advantage, I can apply pressure and deny equity by 3-betting. T9 has enough connectivity to continue on many boards when called, while still functioning well as a semi-bluff that wins the pot preflop a lot. If Flash 4-bets, I can comfortably fold and preserve EV.

The tournament is ending soon (Hand 148/150). I hold AJo in the Big Blind with a stack of ~50 BB. The active chip leader (FiveTwo) has put in a large 3-bet (1000) against the Button's open. FiveTwo has been aggressive recently (3-betting hands 144, 145), suggesting a wider range, but risking my entire 50 BB stack on AJo with a cold 4-bet shove is negative EV against a calling range that likely dominates me or flips. Calling 10% of my stack to play out of position in a 3-bet pot is also unfavorable with AJo. The most prudent play to maximize expected chip value is to fold and look for a better spot in the final two hands.

With pocket deuces on the button, I made a standard opening raise, but FiveTwo's 3-bet to 1000 is a significant increase. Against a chip leader who has been aggressive, 22 is in a difficult spot. To set-mine profitably, I generally want about 15-to-1 implied odds; here, I need to call 750 to potentially win my remaining 6700 plus the current pot, which offers roughly 11.7-to-1. Given that we are at Hand 148 of 150, protecting my current stack (7450 after folding) is more prudent than taking a high-variance marginal call where I will be forced to fold on most flops. I will fold and look for a better spot in the final two hands.

I am in Hand 149 of 150, currently in the UTG position with Jack of Hearts and Four of Hearts. My stack is relatively healthy at 77 BBs, while FiveTwo is the massive chip leader. J4 suited is a weak hand to open from early position, especially in a 4-handed game where players like FiveTwo and Claude have been active. Given that we are near the end of the tournament, I want to preserve my stack for the final hand rather than risking it on a marginal bluff or a weak speculative hand from under the gun. Standard play here is a fold.

With UTG folded and us on the button, this is a prime steal spot. T2s is weak but has some playability (suited/backdoor potential) and we have a massive stack edge plus position, making a small open very profitable against blinds that will often fold or play fit-or-fold postflop. Limping gives up initiative and fold equity; folding is too tight given the immediate EV of taking down 150 uncontested. I’ll use a standard small sizing to risk little while pressuring both blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 3s, which is a very weak off-suit hand (bottom 10% range). The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has opened to 2.5bb. Despite the wide range of the Button, playing 53o out of position is highly unprofitable. I have a healthy stack of 50bb and there is no strategic incentive to defend such a weak holding in this spot. Folding is the standard and correct play.

9s8c offsuit vs button open. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). This is a decent speculative hand with straight potential. FiveTwo has been active and likely has a wide button range. Closing the action in the BB makes a call profitable here. Not strong enough to 3-bet but too good to fold at these odds.

Completely missed this flop with 9s8c on K62 rainbow. No pair, no flush draw, only backdoor straight possibilities. The board is dry and favors the preflop raiser's range (they likely have more Kx hands). With no equity and position disadvantage, checking is the clear play - no reason to bluff here when I can see a free turn card.

We have bottom pair (2s) on a dry K-6-2 rainbow board after opening the button and getting a BB defend. This is a good spot to apply our preflop initiative with a small c-bet: we’ll fold out a lot of his unpaired hands/overcards (denying equity) and can sometimes get thin value/protection versus worse deuces and some small pocket pairs. Keeping the sizing small also limits damage if he check-raises (we can comfortably fold).

I have 9s8c on K62 rainbow - complete air with no pair, no flush draw, and only weak backdoor straight draws. FiveTwo bet 200 into 550 (small c-bet). While I'm getting 3.75:1 odds, I have almost no equity here. Can't profitably float with this hand out of position against an aggressive player - I'd need to hit a miracle turn to continue. Fold is the only reasonable play.

4-handed UTG plays much wider than full-ring, and 6h5h is a solid playable suited connector with good postflop equity and board coverage. As the big chip leader, I want to apply pressure and pick up blinds frequently, while keeping risk controlled versus the shorter stacks behind. A small open accomplishes that: it denies the blinds a free look, keeps the SPR high if called, and lets me comfortably fold if I face a large 3-bet from a short stack.

This is the final hand (150 of 150). My goal is to maximize my final stack. I hold 5d 4h (54o) on the Button. The player Under the Gun (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader with a massive stack, has raised to 250. 54o is a very weak hand that performs poorly against a UTG opening range, having roughly 30-35% equity. Calling would be a negative expected value (-EV) play, which would statistically decrease my final stack size rather than increase it. Since there is no strategic benefit to gambling with a trash hand in this spot solely because it is the last hand (my goal is maximizing stack, not 'winning' a binary outcome which requires a specific threshold), the disciplined and mathematically correct decision is to fold and preserve the current stack of 5,025.

Final hand of the tournament with 3s7d - one of the worst hands in poker. FiveTwo raised from UTG and has a massive 36k stack. Even with 200 to call into a 400 pot, this hand plays terribly out of position. No reason to gamble here with trash - fold and preserve my 10,600 stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h 4s. This is the final hand of the tournament (Hand #150). FiveTwo has raised to 250 from UTG. My hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a player with a massive stack who has been very active. Given it is the last hand and I have a respectable stack of 7600, there is no strategic reason to commit chips with such a poor hand and risk a significant loss in a potentially inflated pot. Folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my current tournament standing.
